```mermaid
    INV --> LOG[Logistics Module]
    LOG --> CUST[Customer Delivery]
    CUST --> FEED[Feedback Collection]
    FEED --> PLAN
    QC --> MAINT[Maintenance Module]
    MAINT --> PROC
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Notice the **closed feedback loop** — the heart of OM. Every output generates data that re-enters planning. This continuous improvement cycle is what makes OM dynamic rather than static.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Operations Management. List any three functions of OM.**

**Model Answer:**

*Definition:* Operations Management is the branch of management that deals with the design, planning, operation, and control of the transformation process that converts inputs (materials, labor, capital, information) into outputs (goods and services) to satisfy customer needs efficiently and effectively.

*Any three functions:*
1. **Planning** — Setting objectives and determining the actions required to achieve them.
2. **Organizing** — Arranging resources and tasks in a structured manner.
3. **Controlling** — Monitoring performance and taking corrective action to ensure targets are met.

*(Alternative valid functions: Staffing, Directing)*

**Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):**
- Definition: **2 marks**
- Three functions (½ mark each): **1 mark**

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between manufacturing operations and service operations. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Manufacturing Operations | Service Operations |
|:------|:------------------------|:-------------------|
| Output | Tangible (can be touched) | Intangible (cannot be touched) |
| Inventory | Can be stored | Generally cannot be stored |
| Customer contact | Low | High |
| Examples | Automobile, Textile | Hospital, Banking |

*Examples:*
- Manufacturing: **Tata Motors, Apple iPhone factory**
- Service: **Apollo Hospitals, HDFC Bank**

**Mark Distribution:**
- Four differentiation points (½ mark each): **2 marks**
- Two examples each: **1 mark**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **scope of Operations Management** in any modern organization. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **role and importance of Operations Management** in achieving competitive advantage. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a):

The scope of Operations Management can be classified into **ten core functional areas**:

**1. Product and Service Design:**
Defines what the organization will offer. Decisions on features, quality level, branding, and product life cycle begin here.

**2. Process Design and Selection:**
Determines *how* the product/service will be produced. Choice between job shop, batch, assembly line, or continuous process.

**3. Capacity Planning:**
Establishes how much the system can produce. Involves forecasting, bottleneck analysis, and expansion planning.

**4. Facility Location and Layout:**
Decides where to locate the facility and how to arrange machines, equipment, and people within it.

**5. Work Study and Ergonomics:**
Applies method study and time study to optimize human work and improve productivity.

**6. Inventory Management:**
Controls raw materials, work-in-progress, and finished goods to balance availability with cost.

**7. Supply Chain Management:**
Manages the flow of materials from suppliers through the organization to the end customer.

**8. Scheduling:**
Sequences jobs, assigns resources, and determines production timelines.

**9. Quality Management:**
Implements TQM, Six Sigma, ISO standards, and statistical quality control.

**10. Maintenance Management:**
Ensures equipment reliability through preventive, predictive, and breakdown maintenance strategies.

**Mark Distribution:** 10 points × 0.7 = **7 marks**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b):

Operations Management plays a pivotal role in achieving competitive advantage through four strategic priorities:

**1. Cost Leadership:**
By improving productivity, reducing waste (lean manufacturing), and optimizing capacity, OM enables the firm to offer products at the lowest cost in the industry. *Example: Xiaomi's operations strategy.*

**2. Quality Leadership:**
Through TQM, Six Sigma, and continuous improvement (Kaizen), OM ensures defect-free products, building customer loyalty. *Example: Toyota Production System.*

**3. Speed and Delivery Reliability:**
Efficient scheduling, supply chain coordination, and quick changeovers enable fast on-time delivery. *Example: Amazon's same-day delivery.*

**4. Flexibility and Customization:**
Modular product design and agile processes allow firms to offer customized products at scale. *Example: Dell's configure-to-order PCs.*

**5. Innovation:**
OM integrates with R&D to rapidly move new products from concept to market. *Example: Apple's iPhone launch cycle.*

**Mark Distribution:** 5 dimensions × ~1.4 marks each = **7 marks**

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`

**(a)** Explain the **various functions of Operations Management** with reference to any manufacturing organization. (7 marks)

**(b)** Distinguish between **manufacturing and service operations** in detail, highlighting at least **eight points of difference**. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a):

The five functions of Operations Management, illustrated in the context of a **car manufacturing plant**:

**1. Planning (2 marks):**
The OM team forecasts annual demand (say 50,000 cars), plans raw material orders, schedules production for each shift, and prepares capacity plans. Master Production Schedules (MPS) are drawn up.

**2. Organizing (2 marks):**
The factory is laid out in a U-shaped production line. Workstations are arranged in sequence. The operations hierarchy — Plant Manager → Production Manager → Supervisors → Operators — is structured.

**3. Staffing (1 mark):**
Hiring machine operators, quality inspectors, maintenance technicians, and supervisors. Training them on SOPs and safety.

**4. Directing (1 mark):**
Issuing daily work orders, motivating workers through incentives, resolving shop-floor conflicts, and ensuring smooth flow.

**5. Controlling (1 mark):**
Monitoring production output against targets, tracking defect rates, measuring machine downtime, and taking corrective action through root cause analysis.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b):

**Eight Points of Difference between Manufacturing and Service Operations:**

| S.No | Parameter | Manufacturing | Service |
|:----:|:----------|:--------------|:--------|
| 1 | Nature of output | Tangible | Intangible |
| 2 | Customer contact | Low | High |
| 3 | Inventory | Can be stored | Cannot be stored |
| 4 | Variability | Low | High |
| 5 | Measurement of quality | Objective (defects) | Subjective (perception) |
| 6 | Location flexibility | Can be located anywhere | Must be near customer |
| 7 | Lead time | Long | Short or instant |
| 8 | Capital intensity | Higher | Lower |
| 9 | Examples | Automobile, Steel | Hospital, Hotel |

*(Any 8 points × ~0.85 marks each = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Missing the word "transformation"** in the definition of OM — costs up to **1 full mark** in Part A.
> 2. **Confusing functions of OM with functions of management** in general — examiners expect *operations-specific* applications.
> 3. **Not providing examples** in the manufacturing vs. service question — at least 1 example per category is mandatory.
> 4. **Writing generic answers** without structure — always use headings, bullet points, and tables. KTU examiners scan for keywords.
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — productivity without "units/hour" or "%" is incomplete and may cost 0.5 marks.
> 6. **Skipping the feedback loop** in diagrams — OM is incomplete without it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Operations Management: Functions and Scope**
>
> - **Definition keywords** (must memorize): *transformation, inputs, outputs, planning, organizing, controlling, value creation.*
> - **Universal model**: Inputs → Transformation → Outputs → Feedback.
> - **Scope (10 areas)**: Product design, Process design, Capacity, Location/Layout, Work study, Inventory, SCM, Scheduling, Quality, Maintenance.
> - **Functions (5)**: Planning, Organizing, Staffing, Directing, Controlling.
> - **Decision levels (4)**: Strategic, Tactical, Operational, Day-to-day.
> - **Core formulas**: Productivity $= O/I$; Efficiency $= \text{Actual}/\text{Standard} \times 100$; Utilization $= \text{Actual}/\text{Capacity} \times 100$.
> - **Manufacturing vs. Service** differences — remember **MASS-FICS** mnemonic.
> - **Competitive priorities**: Cost, Quality, Speed, Flexibility, Innovation.
> - **OM relationship**: Marketing (demand) ↔ OM (delivery), Finance (capital) ↔ OM (utilization), HR (people) ↔ OM (largest employer).
> - **Real-world example**: A restaurant kitchen = a perfect microcosm of an operations system.
> - **Always** mention the feedback loop in any OM diagram.
> - **Always** include units in numerical answers.
> - **Always** structure long answers with headings and tables — KTU examiners reward visual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Production Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT863 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Production Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Production System** is a structured arrangement of men, machines, materials, and information designed to transform inputs (raw materials, components, information) into outputs (finished goods or services) in a controlled, value-adding manner. The **type of production system** is determined by the **volume of output**, **variety of products**, and the **degree of standardization** of the manufacturing or service process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** Production systems classify manufacturing/operational activities into distinct categories based on **Volume (V)**, **Variety (ν)**, **Flow continuity**, and **Process Layout**. The four universally accepted types in the KTU Operations Management syllabus are: **Project, Job-Shop, Batch, Mass/Assembly Line, and Continuous (Process) Production**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **roadside tea stall** versus a **Coca-Cola bottling plant**.

- The **tea stall** makes one cup at a time, customized to each customer's taste (less sugar, extra ginger). It stops and reconfigures for every order. This is **Job Production**.
- A **local bakery** bakes bread in batches of 50 loaves at a time, then switches to cookies. This is **Batch Production**.
- A **Coca-Cola plant** produces 100,000 identical bottles per hour on a single, unchanging line. This is **Mass/Assembly Line Production**.
- An **oil refinery** runs 24/7/365 without ever stopping, producing standardized outputs from raw crude. This is **Continuous Production**.
- **Building a dam** or a **one-of-a-kind ship** is a **Project Production** — it takes years, has a fixed end date, and never repeats identically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight for Students:** Production systems exist on a spectrum from **High Variety + Low Volume** (Projects, Jobs) to **Low Variety + High Volume** (Mass, Continuous). Understanding where a system sits on this spectrum determines *everything* about its design, cost, and management style.

### 1.3 The Four Key Decision Variables

Any production system is classified using these four KTU-mandated parameters:

| Parameter | Symbol | Low End | High End |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | $V$ | One unit (Project) | Millions/year (Mass) |
| **Variety** | $\nu$ | Identical (Continuous) | Infinite (Job) |
| **Standardization** | $S$ | Custom (Project) | Fully Standardized (Mass) |
| **Flow Continuity** | $F_c$ | Intermittent (Batch) | Continuous (Flow) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently test the ability to *match* a real-world industry example to its correct production system type. Memorize the canonical example for each type.

### 1.4 The Five Canonical Production Systems — Quick Snapshot

1. **Project Production** — Building a bridge, ship, skyscraper. *Single, unique output.*
2. **Job Production** — Custom tailor, bespoke furniture, machine shop repair. *One-off or very small volume, high customization.*
3. **Batch Production** — Bakery, pharmaceutical batches, garment batches. *Grouped production runs.*
4. **Mass Production (Assembly Line)** — Automobiles, electronics, FMCG. *High volume, standardized, line flow.*
5. **Continuous Production** — Oil refinery, electricity generation, steel. *24/7 non-stop, capital intensive.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Production System Spectrum — Volume vs. Variety Framework

The **Volume–Variety frontier** is the conceptual foundation used by KTU examiners to classify production systems. As volume **increases** along the X-axis, variety **decreases** along the Y-axis, producing a classic inverse relationship.

> **Inverse Relationship (KTU High-Yield):**
> As $V \uparrow$, $\nu \downarrow$ — higher volume implies lower product variety and vice-versa.

The five production systems map onto this frontier as follows:

$$
\text{Project} \xrightarrow{\;V \uparrow, \nu \downarrow\;} \text{Job} \xrightarrow{\;V \uparrow, \nu \downarrow\;} \text{Batch} \xrightarrow{\;V \uparrow, \nu \downarrow\;} \text{Mass} \xrightarrow{\;V \uparrow, \nu \downarrow\;} \text{Continuous}
$$

### 2.2 Detailed Characteristic Analysis of Each Production System

#### A. Project Production
- **Volume:** $V = 1$ (singular output, by definition)
- **Variety:** $\nu = \text{Maximum}$
- **Duration:** Long lead time (months to years)
- **Resources:** Mobilized progressively; reverse logistics upon completion
- **Layout:** **Fixed Position Layout** — the product stays put, resources move to it
- **Examples:** Construction (bridges, dams, highways), shipbuilding, large infrastructure, NASA missions, custom software development for a single client
- **Management Focus:** Coordination, scheduling with **Critical Path Method (CPM)** and **Program Evaluation Review Technique (PERT)**

#### B. Job Production (Job Shop)
- **Volume:** $V$ = Very low (often 1 to 10 units/order)
- **Variety:** $\nu$ = Very high
- **Skill Level:** High, multi-skilled craftsmen; flexible workforce
- **Layout:** **Functional/Process Layout** — machines grouped by *function* (all lathes in one area, all mills in another)
- **Examples:** Custom tailoring, repair workshops, specialty machine shop, prototype manufacturing
- **Management Focus:** Skilled labor scheduling, job-order costing, queue management

#### C. Batch Production
- **Volume:** $V$ = Moderate (groups of 50, 100, 500 units)
- **Variety:** $\nu$ = Moderate (multiple product lines)
- **Setup:** Significant changeover time between batches — **Set-up Cost** is a critical KPI
- **Layout:** **Cellular Layout** or **Group Technology Layout** (machines grouped by *product family*)
- **Examples:** Bakeries, pharmaceutical companies (producing different tablets in batches), garment manufacturing, printing presses
- **Management Focus:** **Economic Batch Quantity (EBQ)** — balancing setup cost vs. holding cost
- **Repetition:** Repetitive but not continuous; each batch is a mini-campaign

#### D. Mass Production (Assembly Line)
- **Volume:** $V$ = Very high (thousands to millions per year)
- **Variety:** $\nu$ = Low (standardized models, limited options)
- **Layout:** **Product/Line Layout** — machines arranged in sequence of operations
- **Pacing:** Often line-paced (cycle time fixed by slowest station)
- **Examples:** Toyota cars, Samsung smartphones, Maruti Suzuki, McDonald's standardized meals
- **Management Focus:** Cycle time reduction, line balancing, takt time optimization

#### E. Continuous Production
- **Volume:** $V$ = Extremely high, uninterrupted
- **Variety:** $\nu$ = Minimal (often single product or bulk chemical)
- **Layout:** **Process Layout** with highly integrated flow
- **Capital:** **Extremely capital intensive** — once stopped, restarting is expensive
- **Examples:** Oil refinery, power plant, steel rolling mill, cement kiln, LNG plant
- **Management Focus:** Uptime maximization, preventive maintenance, throughput reliability

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following table is the **master formula/reference card** for solving any KTU numerical or theoretical problem on Types of Production Systems.

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Takt Time** | $T_t = \dfrac{T_{\text{available}}}{D}$ | Time per unit to meet demand $D$ in available time $T$ | seconds/unit |
| **Economic Batch Quantity (EBQ)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}\cdot \dfrac{1}{1 - x}}$ | Optimal batch size with scrap/shrinkage $x$ | units |
| **Simple EBQ (no scrap)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | Harris Model — $D$=demand, $S$=setup cost, $H$=holding cost | units |
| **Capacity Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Design Capacity}} \times 100\%$ | Used to assess whether system is mass, batch, or job | percent |
| **Variety-Volume Index** | $\text{VVI} = \dfrac{\nu_{\text{SKUs}}}{V_{\text{annual units}}}$ | Higher VVI $\Rightarrow$ closer to Job; lower VVI $\Rightarrow$ closer to Mass | dimensionless |
| **Setup Cost per Unit** | $S_u = \dfrac{C_s}{Q}$ | Cost amortized over batch size $Q$ | currency/unit |
| **Holding Cost per Period** | $H_p = h \cdot \dfrac{Q}{2}$ | Average inventory cost | currency |
| **Line Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{n \cdot t_{\max}} \times 100\%$ | Sum of station times / (stations × bottleneck) | percent |
| **Cycle Time (Line)** | $T_c = \max(t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n)$ | Determined by bottleneck station | seconds |
| **Workstation Min. Count** | $N_{\min} = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{T_c}$ | Theoretical minimum stations | integer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a problem gives you demand rate $D$ and time available, **always compute Takt Time first**. It instantly tells you whether the system is Job, Batch, or Mass. High takt time $\Rightarrow$ Job/Low-volume; Low takt time $\Rightarrow$ Mass/Continuous.

### 2.4 Why These Systems Differ — The Economic Logic

The choice of production system is fundamentally an **economic optimization** between three forces:

1. **Fixed Costs (Capital + Setup):** $C_f$ — favor higher volume (mass, continuous)
2. **Variable Costs (Labor + Materials per unit):** $C_v$ — favor standardization
3. **Flexibility Costs (Inventory + Changeover):** $C_{\text{flex}}$ — favor low volume, high variety

$$
\text{Total Cost} = C_f + C_v \cdot V + C_{\text{flex}} \cdot \nu
$$

Minimizing this function subject to demand $D$ and variety requirement $\nu$ yields the optimal system type. **Job shops** minimize $C_{\text{flex}}$ (they accept high $C_v$); **Mass lines** minimize $C_v$ (they accept high $C_f$).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Industrial Utility

- **Automobile Industry:** Toyota's switch to flexible mass production (mixed-model assembly) revolutionized manufacturing globally.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** FDA regulations force batch production due to validation and traceability needs.
- **Semiconductor Fabs:** Continuous-flow wafer processing for chips is the ultimate mass production.
- **Construction:** Project management is essentially project production at industrial scale — every building is unique.
- **Service Operations:** Hospitals (job), call centers (batch), ATMs (mass), cloud servers (continuous) — same typology applies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Economic Batch Quantity (EBQ) — The Harris Model

The **Economic Batch Quantity (EBQ)** is the foundational formula in Batch Production. KTU frequently tests this in Part B (14-mark) questions.

#### Step 1: Define the Cost Components

- **Setup Cost per batch:** $S$ (currency)
- **Holding Cost per unit per period:** $H$ (currency/unit/period)
- **Annual Demand:** $D$ (units/year)
- **Batch size:** $Q$ (units/batch)
- **Number of batches per year:** $N_b = \dfrac{D}{Q}$

#### Step 2: Express Annual Costs

The **Annual Setup Cost** is the cost incurred for each batch multiplied by the number of batches:

$$
C_{\text{setup}} = S \cdot \frac{D}{Q}
$$

The **Annual Holding Cost** is the cost of carrying average inventory. Since inventory builds from $0$ to $Q$ and depletes to $0$ within a batch cycle, the average inventory is $\dfrac{Q}{2}$:

$$
C_{\text{holding}} = H \cdot \frac{Q}{2}
$$

#### Step 3: Write Total Annual Cost Function

$$
TC(Q) = C_{\text{setup}} + C_{\text{holding}} = S \cdot \frac{D}{Q} + H \cdot \frac{Q}{2}
$$

#### Step 4: Minimize TC(Q) with Respect to Q

To find the optimum, take the first derivative and set it to zero:

$$
\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{SD}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0
$$

#### Step 5: Solve for Q

Rearranging the equation:

$$
\frac{H}{2} = \frac{SD}{Q^2}
$$

$$
Q^2 = \frac{2SD}{H}
$$

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2SD}{H}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Classical Harris Formula (1913)**. The EBQ is the batch size that **minimizes** the sum of setup and holding costs.

#### Step 6: Verify Minimum Using Second Derivative

$$
\frac{d^2(TC)}{dQ^2} = \frac{2SD}{Q^3} > 0
$$

Since the second derivative is positive, $TC(Q)$ is convex, confirming that $Q^*$ is a **minimum**.

#### Step 7: Minimum Total Cost at Optimum

Substituting $Q^*$ back into $TC$:

$$
TC^* = S \cdot \frac{D}{Q^*} + H \cdot \frac{Q^*}{2}
$$

$$
TC^* = S \cdot \frac{D}{\sqrt{2SD/H}} + H \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2SD/H}}{2}
$$

$$
TC^* = \sqrt{\frac{SDH}{2}} + \sqrt{\frac{SDH}{2}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{SDH}{2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;TC^* = \sqrt{2SDH}\;}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Takt Time — The Heart of Mass Production

Takt time is the **maximum allowable time per unit** to satisfy customer demand. It is derived directly from the demand–capacity balance.

#### Step 1: Define Parameters

- **Available Production Time per day:** $T_a$ (seconds/day)
  - Typically: $T_a = (\text{Shift hours}) \times 3600 - (\text{Breaks})$
- **Customer Demand per day:** $D$ (units/day)
- **Net Availability Factor:** $f \in [0,1]$ accounting for breaks, downtime

#### Step 2: Effective Available Time

$$
T_{\text{eff}} = T_a \cdot f
$$

#### Step 3: Compute Takt Time

$$
\boxed{\;T_t = \frac{T_{\text{eff}}}{D}\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** If a line has $T_t = 60$ seconds, the customer is buying a new unit every 60 seconds. The line's cycle time must be $\leq T_t$ to keep up with demand.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Line Balancing — Minimum Number of Workstations

Given a set of tasks with times $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n$ and a desired cycle time $T_c$, the theoretical minimum number of workstations is:

$$
N_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{T_c} \right\rceil
$$

where $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ is the ceiling function (round up to the nearest integer).

**Line Efficiency** (how well tasks are distributed):

$$
\eta = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{N \cdot T_c} \times 100\%
$$

where $N$ is the actual number of workstations used (must be $\geq N_{\min}$).

**Balance Delay** (the inefficiency):

$$
\text{BD} = 100\% - \eta
$$

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — The KTU Comparison Table

This is the **single most important comparative table** for KTU exams. Memorize it.

| Attribute | Project | Job Shop | Batch | Mass (Line) | Continuous |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | 1 unit | Very low | Moderate | High | Very high |
| **Variety** | Infinite | Very high | Moderate | Low | Minimal |
| **Standardization** | None | Low | Medium | High | Complete |
| **Cycle Time** | Months/years | Days–weeks | Hours–days | Minutes | Seconds |
| **Capital Intensity** | High | Low | Medium | High | Very high |
| **Skill Level** | Multi-skilled | Craftsman | Semi-skilled | Low-medium | Operator/automated |
| **Inventory** | None (in transit) | Low | Moderate (WIP) | Low (JIT) | Pipeline only |
| **Layout** | Fixed Position | Functional | Cellular | Product Line | Process/Flow |
| **Planning** | CPM/PERT | Job order | Batch scheduling | Line balancing | Flow balancing |
| **Flexibility** | Max | High | Medium | Low | Very low |
| **Examples** | Bridge, ship | Tailor, repair | Bakery, pharma | Cars, phones | Oil, power, steel |
| **Cost Focus** | Project mgmt cost | Labor cost | Setup vs. holding | Cycle time | Uptime |
| **KPI** | Schedule variance | Job completion | EBQ, utilization | Line efficiency | OEE |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** If a question says *"A factory produces 50,000 identical pens per day using a 6-station line,"* the answer is **Mass Production**. If it says *"20 different styles of shoes, 500 pairs each, made together,"* the answer is **Batch Production**. Always read the *volume + variety* first.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — EBQ with Setup/Holding Trade-off

**Problem (KTU Style):** A company produces a product with annual demand $D = 10{,}000$ units. Setup cost $S = \text{Rs. } 500$ per batch. Holding cost $H = \text{Rs. } 20$ per unit per year. Find the EBQ, number of batches per year, and minimum total cost.

**Step 1:** Apply Harris Formula.

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10000 \times 500}{20}} = \sqrt{\frac{10^7}{20}} = \sqrt{500000} \approx 707.1
$$

Round to **707 units per batch**.

**Step 2:** Number of batches per year.

$$
N_b = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{10000}{707.1} \approx 14.14
$$

So approximately **14–15 batches per year**.

**Step 3:** Minimum total cost.

$$
TC^* = \sqrt{2 \times 10000 \times 500 \times 20} = \sqrt{2 \times 10^8} = \sqrt{200000000} \approx 14142.14
$$

$$
\boxed{\;TC^* \approx \text{Rs. } 14{,}142\;}
$$

**Step 4:** Verification by testing $Q = 700$ vs. $Q = 720$.

For $Q = 700$:

$$
TC = 500 \times \frac{10000}{700} + 20 \times \frac{700}{2} = 7142.86 + 7000 = 14142.86
$$

For $Q = 720$:

$$
TC = 500 \times \frac{10000}{720} + 20 \times \frac{720}{2} = 6944.44 + 7200 = 14144.44
$$

Both are very close to $TC^* \approx 14142.14$, confirming our optimum. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart — Identifying Production System Type

The following Mermaid diagram provides the **decision-tree logic** a student should follow to classify any production system described in a KTU exam question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Q1: Is it a single\none-of-a-kind output?}
    Q2{Q2: Is volume very low\nand each unit customized?}
    Q3{Q3: Are units produced\nin discrete groups?}
    Q4{Q4: Is output continuous\n24x7 with no stoppage?}
    Q5{Q5: Is it standardized\nhigh volume line flow?}

    A1[PROJECT PRODUCTION\nExample: Dam Construction]
    A2[JOB PRODUCTION\nExample: Custom Tailoring]
    A3[BATCH PRODUCTION\nExample: Bakery, Pharma]
    A4[CONTINUOUS PRODUCTION\nExample: Oil Refinery]
    A5[MASS PRODUCTION\nExample: Car Assembly]

    Q1 -- Yes --> A1
    Q1 -- No --> Q2
    Q2 -- Yes --> A2
    Q2 -- No --> Q3
    Q3 -- Yes --> A3
    Q3 -- No --> Q4
    Q4 -- Yes --> A4
    Q4 -- No --> A5
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Production System

The following block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a generic production system, showing the inputs, transformation processes, and outputs that apply across all five types.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[INPUTS]
        direction TB
        IN1[Raw Materials]
        IN2[Capital Equipment]
        IN3[Human Resources]
        IN4[Information and Data]
    end

    subgraph TR[TRANSFORMATION PROCESS]
        direction TB
        T1[Receiving and Inspection]
        T2[Storage and Warehousing]
        T3[Processing Stage 1]
        T4[Processing Stage 2]
        T5[Assembly Stage]
        T6[Quality Control]
    end

    subgraph OUT[OUTPUTS]
        direction TB
        OUT1[Finished Goods]
        OUT2[Services Delivered]
        OUT3[By-products and Waste]
        OUT4[Feedback Loop Data]
    end

    IN1 --> T1
    IN2 --> T3
    IN3 --> T3
    IN4 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> T4
    T4 --> T5
    T5 --> T6
    T6 --> OUT1
    T6 --> OUT2
    T6 --> OUT3
    T6 --> OUT4
    OUT4 -.-> T1
```

### 4.3 Volume-Variety Positioning Diagram (Mermaid Representation)

This diagram shows where each of the five production systems sits on the Volume–Variety frontier.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HIGH_V[High Volume - Low Variety Region]
        direction TB
        N1[Continuous Production]
        N2[Mass Production]
    end

    subgraph MID[Medium Volume - Medium Variety Region]
        direction TB
        N3[Batch Production]
    end

    subgraph LOW_V[Low Volume - High Variety Region]
        direction TB
        N4[Job Production]
        N5[Project Production]
    end

    N1 --- N2
    N2 --- N3
    N3 --- N4
    N4 --- N5
```

### 4.4 Layout Architecture — Mapping Systems to Plant Layouts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Fixed Position Layout]
    L2[Functional Process Layout]
    L3[Cellular Group Layout]
    L4[Product Line Layout]
    L5[Continuous Process Layout]

    P1[Used by: Project Production]
    P2[Used by: Job Production]
    P3[Used by: Batch Production]
    P4[Used by: Mass Production]
    P5[Used by: Continuous Production]

    L1 --> P1
    L2 --> P2
    L3 --> P3
    L4 --> P4
    L5 --> P5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Learning Aid:** In the KTU exam, drawing even a rough Mermaid-style or hand-drawn sketch of these mappings can fetch **2–3 extra marks** under "diagrammatic representation" in the rubric.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Distinguish between **Job Production** and **Batch Production** with suitable examples. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Job Production | Batch Production |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | Very low (1 to few units) | Moderate (groups of 50–500) |
| **Variety** | Very high — every unit may differ | Moderate — limited product range |
| **Example** | Bespoke tailoring, machine shop repair | Bakery producing 100 loaves then 100 cookies |
| **Setup Frequency** | Setup for every new order | Setup for each new batch |

> **Mark Split:** Definition (1) + 2 distinguishing points with examples (2).

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** What is meant by **Continuous Production**? Give two industrial examples. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Continuous Production is a manufacturing system where goods are produced **non-stop, 24/7**, with **no distinct units or batches**, using highly automated, capital-intensive equipment. The output is typically a **bulk commodity** (liquid, gas, or granular material).

**Key Features (0.5 marks):**
- Extremely high capital investment
- Output is measured in tons, liters, or cubic meters
- Stopping production is very expensive

**Examples (1 mark — 0.5 each):**
1. **Oil Refinery** — crude oil processed continuously into petrol, diesel, kerosene.
2. **Thermal Power Plant** — electricity generated continuously from coal/nuclear fuel.

> **Mark Split:** Definition (1.5) + Features (0.5) + 2 examples (1).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Major Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the **five major types of production systems** with their characteristics, layout used, and at least one industrial example for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A manufacturing company has an annual demand $D = 8{,}000$ units. The setup cost per batch is $S = \text{Rs. } 400$ and the holding cost per unit per year is $H = \text{Rs. } 25$. Calculate the **Economic Batch Quantity (EBQ)**, the **number of batches per year**, and the **minimum total annual cost**. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

**1. Project Production (1.4 marks):**
- **Volume:** $V = 1$ unique unit.
- **Characteristics:** Long duration, fixed end-date, resources move to the product.
- **Layout:** Fixed Position Layout.
- **Example:** Construction of the **Kochi Metro** or a custom-built **aircraft carrier**.

**2. Job Production (1.4 marks):**
- **Volume:** Very low; 1 to 10 units per order.
- **Characteristics:** High variety, skilled craftsmen, every order is custom.
- **Layout:** Functional / Process Layout.
- **Example:** A **bespoke furniture workshop** in Kerala making a single dining table to order.

**3. Batch Production (1.4 marks):**
- **Volume:** Moderate; 50–500 units per batch.
- **Characteristics:** Production in groups; significant setup time between batches.
- **Layout:** Cellular / Group Technology Layout.
- **Example:** A **pharmaceutical company** producing 5,000 tablets of Paracetamol in one batch, then switching to Ibuprofen.

**4. Mass Production (1.4 marks):**
- **Volume:** Very high; thousands to millions per year.
- **Characteristics:** Standardized products, line flow, fixed cycle time.
- **Layout:** Product / Line Layout.
- **Example:** **Maruti Suzuki assembly line** producing identical Swift cars.

**5. Continuous Production (1.4 marks):**
- **Volume:** Extremely high, uninterrupted.
- **Characteristics:** 24/7 operation, capital intensive, output is bulk.
- **Layout:** Process Layout with continuous flow.
- **Example:** **BPCL Kochi Refinery** — refined petroleum products.

---

### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $D = 8{,}000$ units/year
- $S = \text{Rs. } 400$ per batch
- $H = \text{Rs. } 25$ per unit per year

**Step 1: Apply Harris EBQ formula (2 marks):**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8000 \times 400}{25}}
$$

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{6{,}400{,}000}{25}} = \sqrt{256{,}000} = 505.96
$$

$$
\boxed{\;Q^* \approx 506 \text{ units per batch}\;}
$$

**Step 2: Number of batches per year (2 marks):**

$$
N_b = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{8000}{505.96} \approx 15.81
$$

$$
\boxed{\;N_b \approx 16 \text{ batches per year}\;}
$$

**Step 3: Minimum total annual cost (2 marks):**

$$
TC^* = \sqrt{2SDH} = \sqrt{2 \times 400 \times 8000 \times 25}
$$

$$
TC^* = \sqrt{160{,}000{,}000} = 12{,}649.11
$$

$$
\boxed{\;TC^* \approx \text{Rs. } 12{,}649\;}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation (1 mark):**
The company should produce **506 units per batch** and run **16 batches annually** to minimize its combined setup and holding cost to approximately **Rs. 12,649 per year**. This is a clear **Batch Production** system with optimal batch sizing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Some students write $\sqrt{256000}$ without computing the final number — *always show the arithmetic step*.
> 2. **Forgetting to round $Q^*$:** If a student leaves $Q^*$ as 505.96 and uses it everywhere, marks may be deducted for not showing the rounded integer.
> 3. **Confusing EBQ with EOQ:** The Economic Batch Quantity is for **Batch Production** (production runs). The Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) is for **purchasing/inventory** — they have the same formula but different application context.
> 4. **Forgetting the interpretation step:** A bare numerical answer without interpretation loses 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Compare **Mass Production** and **Continuous Production** systems under the following heads: Volume, Variety, Layout, Capital Investment, Examples, and Inventory. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A factory operates **8 hours per day**, **250 days per year**. The daily demand is **400 units**. Determine the **Takt Time**. If the line is reorganized into 4 workstations with task times $t_1 = 50$s, $t_2 = 70$s, $t_3 = 60$s, $t_4 = 80$s, find the **Cycle Time**, **Line Efficiency**, and **Balance Delay**. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

| Basis | Mass Production | Continuous Production |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume (1 mark)** | High but discrete units (thousands/day) | Extremely high, uninterrupted bulk flow |
| **Variety (1 mark)** | Low — limited models/options | Almost nil — single product type |
| **Layout (1.5 marks)** | Product/Line Layout | Process Layout with continuous flow |
| **Capital (1.5 marks)** | High (machinery, robots) | Extremely high (refineries, pipelines) |
| **Examples (1 mark)** | Toyota car assembly, iPhone manufacturing | Oil refinery, LNG plant, power plant |
| **Inventory (1 mark)** | Low (JIT-friendly) | Pipeline inventory only (in-process) |

> **Mark Split:** 6 heads × ~1.17 marks each (rounded allocation above).

---

### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Compute available time per day (1 mark):**

$$
T_a = 8 \text{ hours} \times 3600 \text{ s/hour} = 28{,}800 \text{ seconds/day}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Takt Time (1.5 marks):**

$$
T_t = \frac{T_a}{D} = \frac{28{,}800}{400} = 72 \text{ seconds/unit}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;T_t = 72 \text{ seconds per unit}\;}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Cycle Time (1 mark):**

The cycle time of a line is determined by the **slowest (bottleneck) station**:

$$
T_c = \max(t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4) = \max(50, 70, 60, 80) = 80 \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;T_c = 80 \text{ seconds}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** Since $T_c = 80$ s > $T_t = 72$ s, the line **CANNOT meet demand**. Either reduce $T_c$ (rebalance), add a station, or add a shift.

**Step 4: Compute Line Efficiency (2 marks):**

Total task time:

$$
\sum t_i = 50 + 70 + 60 + 80 = 260 \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{\sum t_i}{N \cdot T_c} \times 100\% = \frac{260}{4 \times 80} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{260}{320} \times 100\% = 81.25\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\eta = 81.25\%\;}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Balance Delay (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{BD} = 100\% - \eta = 100\% - 81.25\% = 18.75\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{BD} = 18.75\%\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls for Q.B(b):**
> 1. **Takt Time vs. Cycle Time confusion:** Takt Time is set by *customer demand*; Cycle Time is set by the *bottleneck station*. They are not the same.
> 2. **Not comparing $T_c$ and $T_t$:** Many students compute both but fail to note the **imbalance** ($T_c > T_t$). This insight is worth 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the percentage sign on efficiency:** Marks deducted if written as `0.8125` instead of `81.25%`.
> 4. **Wrong cycle time:** If a student uses *average* task time instead of *maximum*, they get $T_c = 65$ s — **wrong**. Always use the bottleneck.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's General Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas Across All Questions on this Topic:**
> 1. **No examples given:** KTU explicitly tests "with suitable example" — giving definitions without industrial examples loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong classification:** Calling Mass Production "Continuous" or Batch Production "Mass" — be precise.
> 3. **Mixing layout and system:** "Assembly line" is a *layout*; "Mass Production" is a *system*. Many students confuse the two.
> 4. **Numerical errors in EBQ:** Forgetting to square root, or squaring when not required.
> 5. **No diagram:** A labeled diagram of Volume-Variety spectrum scores 2+ extra marks.
> 6. **Forgetting units:** Always write `seconds`, `units/year`, `Rs./unit`, etc.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Card — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ☐ **5 Production Systems:** Project → Job → Batch → Mass → Continuous (in order of increasing volume, decreasing variety).
- ☐ **Volume-Variety Inverse Law:** $V \uparrow \Rightarrow \nu \downarrow$ — always.
- ☐ **Layout ↔ System Match:**
  - Project → Fixed Position
  - Job → Functional
  - Batch → Cellular
  - Mass → Product/Line
  - Continuous → Process
- ☐ **EBQ Formula (Harris):** $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — for Batch Production.
- ☐ **Minimum Total Cost:** $TC^* = \sqrt{2SDH}$ — for Batch Production.
- ☐ **Takt Time:** $T_t = T_{\text{eff}}/D$ — for Mass Production.
- ☐ **Cycle Time:** $T_c = \max(t_i)$ — bottleneck, not average.
- ☐ **Line Efficiency:** $\eta = \frac{\sum t_i}{N \cdot T_c} \times 100\%$.
- ☐ **Balance Delay:** $\text{BD} = 100\% - \eta$.
- ☐ **Capital Intensity Rises:** Project < Job < Batch < Mass < Continuous (generally).
- ☐ **Skill Level Falls:** Project > Job > Batch > Mass > Continuous (generally).
- ☐ **Memorize Examples:** Bridge (Project), Bespoke tailor (Job), Bakery (Batch), Maruti car (Mass), BPCL refinery (Continuous).
- ☐ **Decision Rule:** If $T_c > T_t$, demand cannot be met; add stations or shifts.
- ☐ **KTU Buzzwords to Use in Answers:** "Volume-Variety frontier," "Economic Batch Quantity," "Takt Time," "Line Balancing," "Critical Path Method," "Cellular Layout," "Just-In-Time (JIT)."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
