<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition — Process Planning**
> **Process Planning** is the systematic determination of the sequence of manufacturing operations, processes, machines, tooling, fixtures, inspection methods, and time standards required to transform raw materials into a finished product economically, accurately, and efficiently. It acts as the critical bridge between **Product Design** and **Manufacturing Execution**.

In KTU 2024 NEP-aligned terms, process planning is the "engine room" of the production system — it converts an engineering drawing into a *manufacturable* instruction set (route sheets, operation sheets, and tooling lists).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe of a Restaurant Kitchen"

Think of process planning like the **Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) of a South Indian restaurant kitchen** making `Masala Dosa`:

| Restaurant Kitchen Analogy | Operations Management Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Recipe card on the wall | Route Sheet / Operation Sheet |
| Sequence: Soak → Grind → Ferment → Spread → Cook | Process Flow / Routing |
| Specific tawa temperature | Machine Settings / Process Parameters |
| Time to ferment (8 hours) | Standard Time / Cycle Time |
| Specific chef (specialist) | Skilled Labour Allocation |
| Quantity of batter per dosa | Material / Input Specification |

A manager who fails to define the recipe (process) will have inconsistent dosas (defective products). Likewise, **without process planning, no two units of a product will be identical**, and waste will skyrocket.

### 1.3 Objectives of Process Planning

> [!NOTE] **KTU High-Yield Highlights — Objectives**
> 1. **Sequence Determination** — Establish the logical order of operations.
> 2. **Resource Optimization** — Select machines, tools, and labour judiciously.
> 3. **Cost Minimization** — Reduce direct and indirect manufacturing costs.
> 4. **Quality Assurance** — Embed inspection points within the plan.
> 5. **Time Standardization** — Define cycle time and lead time for scheduling.
> 6. **Flexibility** — Accommodate engineering changes without re-tooling chaos.
> 7. **Safety & Ergonomics** — Embed operator safety standards.

### 1.4 Scope of Process Planning

Process planning covers the **"4M + 1E"** framework:

$$
\text{Process Plan} = f(\text{Machines}, \text{Methods}, \text{Manpower}, \text{Materials}, \text{Environment})
$$

In KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE), this aligns with **CO1: Understand the fundamental concepts of operations management** and **CO2: Apply process planning techniques to manufacturing scenarios.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Process Planning

Process planning rests on three interconnected pillars:

**Pillar 1 — Process Selection**
- Choice of technology (manual, mechanized, automated, CNC, robotic).
- Choice of production type: *Job Shop, Batch, Mass, Continuous Process*.

**Pillar 2 — Process Sequencing (Routing)**
- Determining the *path* a part follows through the shop floor.
- Optimal sequencing minimizes backtracking, waiting time, and transport cost.

**Pillar 3 — Process Documentation**
- Output formats: **Route Sheets, Operation Sheets, Tool Lists, Inspection Sheets**.

### 2.2 Detailed Steps in Process Planning

1. **Interpret the Engineering Drawing** — Identify dimensions, tolerances, surface finish (e.g., Ra values), GD&T symbols.
2. **Determine Manufacturing Sequence** — From raw stock → finished part.
3. **Select Machines and Tools** — Match tolerances with machine capabilities.
4. **Decide Fixturing and Work-Holding** — Jigs, fixtures, clamps.
5. **Calculate Cutting Parameters** — Speed, feed, depth of cut (turning/milling).
6. **Establish Inspection Points** — In-process (IPQC) and Final QC.
7. **Estimate Time Standards** — Using methods like **Methods Time Measurement (MTM)**, **Time Study**, or **Standard Data Systems**.
8. **Make the Make-or-Buy Decision** — In-house vs outsourcing.
9. **Generate Route Sheet** — The final deliverable document.

### 2.3 Types of Process Planning

| Type | Description | Industry Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Manual Process Planning** | Done by experienced planners on paper | Small machine shops |
| **Computer-Aided Process Planning (CAPP)** | Uses software (variant / generative) | Automotive OEMs |
| **Variant CAPP** | Retrieves existing plan from similar part database | Repetitive product lines |
| **Generative CAPP** | Synthesizes a new plan using logic / AI algorithms | Aerospace, complex parts |

> [!NOTE] **KTU 2024 Emphasis**
> The KTU 2024 OBE syllabus places strong weight on **Generative CAPP** under *Industry 4.0 / Smart Manufacturing* modules. Be prepared to write short notes on how CAPP integrates with **CAD/CAM, PLM, and MES systems**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Design Capacity Utilization** | $\text{Utilization} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Design Capacity}} \times 100\%$ | Measures how much of max potential is used |
| 2 | **Efficiency** | $\text{Efficiency} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Effective Capacity}} \times 100\%$ | Performance vs realistic cap |
| 3 | **Productivity** | $\text{Productivity} = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$ | Single-factor labour productivity |
| 4 | **Break-Even Point (units)** | $\text{BEP}_Q = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$ | Make-or-buy decisions |
| 5 | **Break-Even Point (₹)** | $\text{BEP}_{\text{Rs}} = \dfrac{F}{1 - \dfrac{V}{P}}$ | Profitability analysis |
| 6 | **Cycle Time** | $C_t = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Demand}}$ | Line balancing |
| 7 | **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Availability × Performance × Quality |
| 8 | **Machining Time (Turning)** | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N}$ | Lathe operations |
| 9 | **Cutting Speed** | $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | m/min |
| 10 | **Material Removal Rate** | $\text{MRR} = f \cdot d \cdot V$ | cm³/min |

> **Units used:** Capacity (units/hr), Time (min or sec), Velocity (m/min), MRR (cm³/min or mm³/sec).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Process planning is the **economic backbone** of any manufacturing enterprise:

- **Automotive (e.g., Maruti Suzuki)** — Massive throughput needs rigorous process plans for every weld point and paint stroke.
- **Aerospace (e.g., HAL)** — Generative CAPP is mandatory due to complex curved surfaces.
- **Electronics (e.g., Foxconn)** — SMT (Surface Mount Technology) lines have microsecond-level process plans.
- **Biomedical (e.g., medical implants)** — Process planning ensures FDA-compliant traceability.

In the *service* sector, process planning morphs into **Service Blueprinting** — same logic, different medium.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative 1 — Break-Even Point (BEP) from First Principles

The BEP is the heart of the **Make-or-Buy decision** in process planning. Let us derive it rigorously.

**Step 1: Total Cost Function**

Let $F$ = Fixed Cost (₹/period), $V$ = Variable Cost (₹/unit), $P$ = Selling Price (₹/unit), $Q$ = Quantity (units).

Total Cost is the sum of fixed and variable components:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = F + V \cdot Q
$$

Total Revenue is the price multiplied by quantity sold:

$$
R_{\text{total}} = P \cdot Q
$$

**Step 2: Definition of Break-Even**

At break-even, Total Revenue equals Total Cost — profit is zero. This is the pivotal transition point.

$$
R_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{total}}
$$

**Step 3: Substitution of Functional Forms**

We substitute the linear equations defined in Step 1 into the equilibrium condition.

$$
P \cdot Q = F + V \cdot Q
$$

**Step 4: Isolate the Variable Q**

Move the variable cost term to the LHS by subtracting $V \cdot Q$ from both sides.

$$
P \cdot Q - V \cdot Q = F
$$

Factor out the common quantity variable $Q$ on the LHS.

$$
Q \cdot (P - V) = F
$$

Divide both sides by $(P - V)$ to obtain the break-even quantity.

$$
Q_{\text{BEP}} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}
$$

**Step 5: Revenue Form**

If we instead multiply both sides by $P$, we obtain the revenue version of the break-even formula.

$$
P \cdot Q_{\text{BEP}} = \dfrac{F \cdot P}{P - V} = \dfrac{F}{1 - \dfrac{V}{P}}
$$

### 3.2 Derivative 2 — Capacity Utilisation Cascade

**Step 1: Design Capacity**

Design Capacity is the theoretical maximum output under ideal conditions.

$$
C_{\text{design}} = \text{Maximum theoretical output}
$$

**Step 2: Effective Capacity**

Effective capacity accounts for realistic constraints like breaks, maintenance, and shift patterns.

$$
C_{\text{effective}} = C_{\text{design}} \times \text{Efficiency Factor}
$$

**Step 3: Actual Output**

Actual output is what the system really produces during a given period.

$$
C_{\text{actual}} = C_{\text{effective}} - \text{Losses}
$$

**Step 4: Utilisation Metric**

Utilisation compares actual output against the absolute maximum design capacity.

$$
U = \dfrac{C_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{design}}}
$$

**Step 5: Efficiency Metric**

Efficiency compares actual output against the more realistic effective capacity benchmark.

$$
\eta = \dfrac{C_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{effective}}}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for Process Selection

| Condition / Parameter | Job Shop | Batch | Mass | Continuous |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | Very Low | Low–Medium | High | Very High |
| **Variety** | Very High | High | Low | Very Low |
| **Capital Investment** | Low | Moderate | High | Very High |
| **Skilled Labour Need** | Very High | High | Moderate | Low |
| **Unit Cost** | Very High | High | Moderate | Very Low |
| **Flexibility** | Excellent | Good | Limited | Poor |
| **Example** | Custom machinery | Bakery | Car assembly | Oil refinery |

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Make or Buy Decision

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A KTU student entrepreneur can manufacture a 3D-printed bracket at ₹**120**/unit variable cost and ₹**40,000** fixed cost per month. An external vendor offers the same bracket at ₹**200**/unit. Find the BEP quantity.

**Solution:**

Given $F = 40000$, $V = 120$, $P_{\text{buy}} = 200$.

$$
Q_{\text{BEP}} = \dfrac{F}{P_{\text{buy}} - V} = \dfrac{40000}{200 - 120}
$$

Subtracting the denominator:

$$
200 - 120 = 80
$$

Final substitution:

$$
Q_{\text{BEP}} = \dfrac{40000}{80} = 500 \text{ units}
$$

> **Interpretation:** If demand is below **500 units/month**, buy from the vendor. If above 500 units/month, manufacture in-house. This is a classic KTU numerical type worth **7–14 marks**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Process Planning Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Product Design / Engineering Drawing] --> B[Interpret Drawings and Tolerances]
    B --> C{Process Selection}
    C -->|Job Shop| D[Manual Process Planning]
    C -->|Batch| E[Variant CAPP]
    C -->|Mass / Continuous| F[Generative CAPP]
    D --> G[Sequence of Operations Routing]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Machine and Tool Selection]
    H --> I[Time Standards Calculation]
    I --> J{Make or Buy Decision}
    J -->|In-house| K[Route Sheet Generation]
    J -->|Outsource| L[Vendor Coordination]
    K --> M[Shop Floor Execution]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Inspection and Quality Control]
    N --> O[Final Product Delivery]
    O --> P[Feedback for Process Improvement]
    P --> C
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Product-Process Matrix (Hayes-Wheelwright)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Volume High Variety] -->|Job Shop| B[Project Production]
    C[Medium Volume Medium Variety] -->|Batch| D[Batch Production]
    E[High Volume Low Variety] -->|Mass / Assembly Line| F[Mass Production]
    G[Very High Volume Standardized] -->|Continuous Flow| H[Continuous Process]
    A1[Process Structure] --> A2[Disconnected]
    C1[Process Structure] --> C2[Connected Group]
    E1[Process Structure] --> E2[Linked Flow]
    G1[Process Structure] --> G2[Continuous Flow]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Make-or-Buy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Component Requirement] --> B{Demand Forecast Available}
    B -->|Yes| C[Compute BEP Quantity]
    B -->|No| D[Qualitative Analysis]
    C --> E{Expected Demand > BEP?}
    E -->|Yes| F[MAKE In-House]
    E -->|No| G[BUY from Vendor]
    D --> H{Core Competency?}
    H -->|Yes| F
    H -->|No| G
    F --> I[Allocate Capacity]
    G --> J[Negotiate SLA]
```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Note for KTU Students**
> Examiners often award **1–2 bonus marks** for a clean, well-labelled process flowchart. Use these diagrams as inspiration for your exam answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define Process Planning. List any four objectives.**

**Model Answer:**
Process planning is the systematic determination of the manufacturing operations, machines, tools, inspection methods, and time standards required to convert raw materials into a finished product. It links product design to manufacturing execution.

Objectives:
1. Establishing the sequence of operations.
2. Selecting appropriate machines and tools.
3. Minimizing production cost.
4. Setting standard time for each operation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Differentiate between Variant CAPP and Generative CAPP.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Variant CAPP | Generative CAPP |
|---|---|---|
| Logic | Retrieves stored plans | Synthesizes new plan using logic |
| Database | Requires part family library | No prior database required |
| Flexibility | Limited | High |
| Cost | Low | High (complex software) |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the steps involved in process planning for a machined component. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Drawing Interpretation** — Analyse engineering drawing for dimensions, tolerances, surface roughness, and material specification.
2. **Process Selection** — Decide between casting, forging, machining, forming, etc., based on volume and cost.
3. **Operation Sequencing** — Determine logical order (e.g., facing → turning → drilling → grinding).
4. **Machine & Tool Selection** — Match machine capability with tolerance. For example, H7 hole needs grinding.
5. **Cutting Parameters** — Calculate spindle speed, feed, depth of cut using standard machining data.
6. **Time Estimation** — Use standard time formulas or stopwatch time study.
7. **Route Sheet Generation** — Final consolidated document.

**(b)** A firm is evaluating two processes. Process X has fixed cost of ₹**5,00,000** and variable cost of ₹**50** per unit. Process Y has fixed cost ₹**3,00,000** and variable cost ₹**70** per unit. Determine the range of production volume for which each process is economical. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Setting total costs equal:

$$
500000 + 50Q = 300000 + 70Q
$$

Rearrange by moving the constant to the LHS.

$$
500000 - 300000 = 70Q - 50Q
$$

Simplify both sides by combining like terms.

$$
200000 = 20Q
$$

Solve for the indifference quantity Q.

$$
Q = 10000 \text{ units}
$$

- For $Q < 10000$: Process Y is economical (lower fixed cost dominates).
- For $Q > 10000$: Process X is economical (lower variable cost dominates).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** What is CAPP? Explain its types with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Computer-Aided Process Planning (CAPP) is the use of computer systems to generate process plans from engineering data.

- **Variant CAPP:** Uses classification and coding to identify the part family and retrieve an existing process plan. Example: Repetitive automotive fasteners.
- **Generative CAPP:** Uses algorithmic logic, AI, or expert systems to *create* a new plan from scratch. Example: Aerospace turbine blades.
- **Hybrid CAPP:** Combines both approaches for greater robustness.

Benefits: consistency, faster turnaround, integration with CAD/CAM.

**(b)** A company operates 8 hours/day, 250 days/year. It produces 5,00,000 units/year. Effective capacity is 6,00,000 units/year. Design capacity is 7,50,000 units. Calculate utilization and efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Compute the utilization as the ratio of actual output to design capacity.

$$
U = \dfrac{500000}{750000} \times 100\% = 66.67\%
$$

Compute the efficiency as the ratio of actual output to effective capacity.

$$
\eta = \dfrac{500000}{600000} \times 100\% = 83.33\%
$$

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> - Many students **swap the formulas** for utilisation and efficiency. Remember: **Utilisation uses Design Capacity; Efficiency uses Effective Capacity.**
> - Forgetting to convert to **percentage (%)** is a recurring 1-mark deduction.
> - In break-even problems, students often **omit the variable cost from the denominator** or invert the formula. Double-check before submitting.
> - In CAPP questions, students confuse *variant* with *generative*. A clear **3-point comparison table** always earns full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition**: Process Planning bridges design and manufacturing.
- **4 Pillars**: Process selection, sequencing, documentation, control.
- **CAPP Types**: Manual, Variant, Generative, Hybrid.
- **BEP Formula**: $Q = F / (P - V)$ — *memorize this cold.*
- **Utilisation vs Efficiency**: Design vs Effective capacity — *do not swap.*
- **Product-Process Matrix**: Hayes-Wheelwright — low volume → Job Shop; high volume → Continuous.
- **Make-or-Buy**: Compare in-house BEP with forecast demand.
- **Route Sheet**: The primary deliverable of process planning.
- **KTU 2024 Focus**: Industry 4.0, Generative CAPP, OEE, smart manufacturing.
- **Common Pitfalls**: Unit mismatches, swapped formulas, missing BEP variable cost term.
- **Diagram Marks**: Always include a flowchart in 14-mark answers for **bonus marks**.

> **Final KTU Examiner Tip:** For any 14-mark process planning question, structure your answer as **Definition → Steps → Diagram → Numerical Example → Conclusion.** Examiners reward structured, well-segmented answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Make or Buy Decisions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT863 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Make or Buy Decisions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
The **Make-or-Buy Decision** is a strategic operations management choice that determines whether a firm should **manufacture a product, component, or service in-house ("Make")** using its own facilities, labour, and resources, or **procure it from an external supplier ("Buy")**. It is fundamentally a **relevant cost analysis problem** that weighs the incremental (differential) costs of producing internally against the purchase price offered by outside vendors, while also considering qualitative factors such as quality, capacity, confidentiality, and supply chain risk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT863, Module 1):**
> Make-or-buy analysis is a sub-topic under *Operations Strategy and Supply Network Design*. KTU examiners frequently test this via a **numerical break-even problem (7 marks)** combined with a **qualitative factor discussion (7 marks)** in the 14-mark Part B questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are a college student deciding whether to **cook your meals at home** or **order food from a mess/canteen**:
- **"Make" (Cook at home):** You need to buy raw materials (vegetables, rice, gas), invest time in cooking, and clean up afterwards. But it is usually cheaper *per meal* and you control the taste and hygiene.
- **"Buy" (Order outside):** You pay a fixed price per meal, save cooking time, but lose control over quality, and if prices rise you have no leverage.
- **The Decision:** You compare the *total cost of cooking* (ingredients + gas + your time) versus the *price of buying* — but only when the cost difference is small do other factors (taste, convenience, hygiene) tip the balance.

This is exactly what firms do: they compare the **unit cost of internal production** with the **vendor's quoted price**, and only when the numbers are close do strategic factors (quality, secrecy, lead time) decide the outcome.

### 1.3 Why This Decision Matters
- Affects **15%–30% of an organisation's total operating cost** (typical industry benchmark for purchased materials and components).
- Impacts **capacity utilisation**, **workforce planning**, and **vertical integration strategy**.
- A wrong decision can lead to **lock-in with suppliers**, loss of **core competencies**, or **stranded fixed assets**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Expected):**
> - **Relevant Cost:** A future cost that differs between the two alternatives.
> - **Avoidable Cost:** A cost that will not be incurred if a particular alternative is *not* chosen.
> - **Sunk Cost:** A past cost, irrelevant to the decision (e.g., already-paid machinery).
> - **Opportunity Cost:** The benefit forgone by choosing one alternative over the next best.
> - **Incremental / Differential Cost:** The change in total cost when output changes by one unit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Framework
A make-or-buy decision is evaluated in **three logical tiers**:

**Tier 1 — Quantitative Analysis (Cost-Driven)**
1. Identify all **relevant costs** under both alternatives.
2. Separate **avoidable fixed costs** from **unavoidable fixed costs**.
3. Compute the **break-even volume** at which "Make" cost = "Buy" cost.
4. Compare the firm's expected demand with the break-even volume.

**Tier 2 — Qualitative / Strategic Analysis**
1. Core competency protection
2. Quality control requirements
3. Supplier reliability and lead time
4. Confidentiality and intellectual property
5. Capacity availability and flexibility
6. Labour relations and workforce morale

**Tier 3 — Risk and Strategic Analysis**
1. Supply chain disruption risk
2. Currency / inflation exposure
3. Technology obsolescence
4. Long-term vendor dependence

### 2.2 Categories of Cost Used in the Decision

| Cost Category | Make (In-House) | Buy (Outsource) | Relevant? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direct Material | Yes | No | Yes (if avoidable) |
| Direct Labour | Yes | No | Yes (if avoidable) |
| Variable Overhead | Yes | No | Yes (if avoidable) |
| Avoidable Fixed Overhead | Yes (if component is dropped) | No | Yes |
| Unavoidable Fixed Overhead | Continues either way | Continues either way | **No — Irrelevant** |
| Purchase Price | No | Yes | Yes |
| Inbound Transport / Inspection | No | Yes | Yes |
| Opportunity Cost of Released Capacity | Yes (revenue forgone) | No | Yes |
| Sunk Cost (e.g., old machine fully depreciated) | Continues | Continues | **No — Irrelevant** |

### 2.3 The Cost Equation Logic

Let:
- $F_M$ = Avoidable fixed cost of making in-house (per period)
- $V_M$ = Variable cost per unit of making in-house
- $P_B$ = Purchase price per unit (buy)
- $F_B$ = Avoidable fixed cost of buying (e.g., inspection set-up) — usually zero
- $X$ = Annual demand (units)

Then:

$$C_{Make} \;=\; F_M \;+\; V_M \cdot X$$

$$C_{Buy} \;=\; F_B \;+\; P_B \cdot X$$

In a typical problem $F_B = 0$, so:

$$C_{Buy} \;=\; P_B \cdot X$$

### 2.4 The Break-Even Volume Derivation

At the break-even point, the cost of making equals the cost of buying. Setting $C_{Make} = C_{Buy}$ and solving for $X$:

$$F_M \;+\; V_M \cdot X \;=\; P_B \cdot X$$

$$F_M \;=\; (P_B \;-\; V_M) \cdot X$$

$$X_{BE} \;=\; \dfrac{F_M}{P_B \;-\; V_M}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation Rule (MUST remember for KTU exams):**
> - If $V_M < P_B$ (cheaper to make *per unit*), then **Make** is preferred for volumes **above** $X_{BE}$.
> - If $V_M > P_B$ (cheaper to buy *per unit*), then **Buy** is preferred for volumes **above** $X_{BE}$ (because variable saving from buying exceeds fixed cost of in-house).
> - If the denominator $(P_B - V_M)$ is **negative**, it means making is cheaper on a per-unit basis — choose **Make** if fixed cost is not too high.

### 2.5 Decision Rule Summary

| Condition | Recommended Decision |
|---|---|
| $X < X_{BE}$ and $V_M < P_B$ | **Buy** (low volume, fixed cost not justified) |
| $X > X_{BE}$ and $V_M < P_B$ | **Make** (high volume, savings cover fixed cost) |
| $V_M > P_B$ at all relevant volumes | **Buy** (always cheaper externally) |
| $V_M < P_B$ but firm has no spare capacity | **Buy** or expand capacity (evaluate opportunity cost) |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Code)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Cost of Making | $C_{Make} = F_M + V_M \cdot X$ | Currency / period |
| 2 | Total Cost of Buying | $C_{Buy} = P_B \cdot X + F_B$ | Currency / period |
| 3 | Break-Even Volume | $X_{BE} = F_M \,/\, (P_B - V_M)$ | Units / period |
| 4 | Cost Differential at volume $X$ | $\Delta C = C_{Make} - C_{Buy}$ | Currency / period |
| 5 | Indifference Volume (general) | $X^* = (F_M - F_B)\,/\,(P_B - V_M)$ | Units / period |
| 6 | Per-Unit Comparison | Compare $V_M$ vs $P_B$ | Currency / unit |
| 7 | Opportunity Cost of Capacity | Lost contribution from displaced production | Currency / period |

### 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields
- **Automotive industry:** OEMs decide whether to manufacture engines, gearboxes, or buy from Tier-1 suppliers (e.g., Bosch).
- **Electronics:** Apple designs chips but outsources fabrication to TSMC.
- **Construction:** Prefabricated components vs on-site casting.
- **Aerospace:** Boeing produces wings in-house but buys avionics from external firms.
- **IT/Software firms:** Build software in-house vs SaaS licensing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** KTU problems usually give a clear-cut numerical setup (two or three alternatives) and ask for break-even volume, then ask you to recommend a decision based on expected demand. Always state the **decision rule in words** after the calculation — this fetches the method mark.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of the Break-Even Volume

We begin with the equality of total costs:

$$C_{Make} \;=\; C_{Buy}$$

Substitute the standard cost forms:

$$F_M \;+\; V_M \cdot X \;=\; P_B \cdot X \;+\; F_B$$

Rearrange to group $X$ terms on one side and constants on the other:

$$F_M \;-\; F_B \;=\; P_B \cdot X \;-\; V_M \cdot X$$

Factor out $X$ on the right-hand side:

$$F_M \;-\; F_B \;=\; (P_B \;-\; V_M) \cdot X$$

Isolate $X$ by dividing both sides by $(P_B - V_M)$:

$$X \;=\; \dfrac{F_M \;-\; F_B}{P_B \;-\; V_M}$$

Since in most textbook problems $F_B = 0$ (no extra fixed cost to buy), the formula simplifies to the standard form:

$$X_{BE} \;=\; \dfrac{F_M}{P_B \;-\; V_M}$$

This is the **break-even (indifference) volume** — the production quantity at which the firm is financially indifferent between making and buying.

### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement:**
> A company currently manufactures a component at a variable cost of ₹40 per unit. The annual fixed manufacturing overhead that can be avoided if the component is bought is ₹1,20,000. A vendor has quoted ₹55 per unit. Determine the break-even volume and recommend whether the company should make or buy at an expected annual demand of 12,000 units.

**Step 1 — Identify the given data**

$$F_M \;=\; \text{₹}1{,}20{,}000 \quad \text{(avoidable fixed cost)}$$

$$V_M \;=\; \text{₹}40 \text{ per unit}$$

$$P_B \;=\; \text{₹}55 \text{ per unit}$$

$$F_B \;=\; 0$$

$$X_{expected} \;=\; 12{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Write the cost equations**

$$C_{Make} \;=\; 1{,}20{,}000 \;+\; 40 \cdot X$$

$$C_{Buy} \;=\; 55 \cdot X$$

**Step 3 — Equate and solve for the break-even volume $X_{BE}$**

$$1{,}20{,}000 \;+\; 40 \cdot X_{BE} \;=\; 55 \cdot X_{BE}$$

$$1{,}20{,}000 \;=\; (55 \;-\; 40) \cdot X_{BE}$$

$$1{,}20{,}000 \;=\; 15 \cdot X_{BE}$$

$$X_{BE} \;=\; \dfrac{1{,}20{,}000}{15} \;=\; 8{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 — Compare $X_{BE}$ with the expected demand $X$**

Since $X_{expected} = 12{,}000$ units and $X_{BE} = 8{,}000$ units:

$$X_{expected} \;>\; X_{BE} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Choose MAKE}$$

**Step 5 — Verify with total cost comparison**

$$C_{Make}(12{,}000) \;=\; 1{,}20{,}000 \;+\; 40 \cdot 12{,}000 \;=\; \text{₹}6{,}00{,}000$$

$$C_{Buy}(12{,}000) \;=\; 55 \cdot 12{,}000 \;=\; \text{₹}6{,}60{,}000$$

$$\text{Saving by Making} \;=\; 6{,}60{,}000 \;-\; 6{,}00{,}000 \;=\; \text{₹}60{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 6 — Recommendation**

> The company should **MAKE** the component in-house, as the expected demand of 12,000 units is above the break-even volume of 8,000 units, leading to an annual saving of **₹60,000**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Make vs Buy Across Multiple Volumes

Using the same numbers ($F_M = 1,20,000$, $V_M = 40$, $P_B = 55$):

| Annual Volume (X) | $C_{Make}$ (₹) | $C_{Buy}$ (₹) | Differential (₹) | Better Choice |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|:---:|
| 4,000 | 2,80,000 | 2,20,000 | +60,000 | **Buy** |
| 6,000 | 3,60,000 | 3,30,000 | +30,000 | **Buy** |
| 8,000 | 4,40,000 | 4,40,000 | 0 | Indifferent |
| 10,000 | 5,20,000 | 5,50,000 | −30,000 | **Make** |
| 12,000 | 6,00,000 | 6,60,000 | −60,000 | **Make** |
| 15,000 | 7,20,000 | 8,25,000 | −1,05,000 | **Make** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pattern visible from the matrix:**
> Below 8,000 units, buying is cheaper. Above 8,000 units, making is cheaper. The gap widens linearly as volume increases — this is the **operating leverage effect** of fixed costs.

### 3.4 Extension: When Buying Has a Fixed Cost

Some problems include a fixed cost on the buy side, e.g., a long-term contract setup cost, a tooling cost, or a vendor development cost. The general formula is:

$$X_{BE} \;=\; \dfrac{F_M \;-\; F_B}{P_B \;-\; V_M}$$

**Example:** If $F_B = 20,000$ (vendor inspection setup):

$$X_{BE} \;=\; \dfrac{1{,}20{,}000 \;-\; 20{,}000}{55 \;-\; 40} \;=\; \dfrac{1{,}00{,}000}{15} \;=\; 6{,}667 \text{ units (approx.)}$$

This shifts the break-even point *downward* — making is favoured at a lower volume, because the firm must spend ₹20,000 regardless if it buys, which is recovered by making instead.

### 3.5 Extension: Opportunity Cost of Capacity

If the firm has **no spare capacity**, then producing the component in-house means **displacing** another product. The opportunity cost is the **contribution margin** of the displaced product.

Let $CM_{displaced}$ = contribution per unit of the displaced product, and $D$ = units displaced. Then the total cost of making becomes:

$$C_{Make}^{adj} \;=\; F_M \;+\; V_M \cdot X \;+\; CM_{displaced} \cdot D$$

The break-even formula becomes:

$$X_{BE} \;=\; \dfrac{F_M \;+\; CM_{displaced} \cdot D}{P_B \;-\; V_M}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU problems on capacity-constrained make-or-buy are common in Part B. Always read the question carefully to see if the firm has spare capacity or not. If not, **add opportunity cost** — students routinely lose 2 marks by missing this.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Make-or-Buy Decision Flowchart

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the structured decision logic an operations manager follows when evaluating a make-or-buy problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Component for Make or Buy Analysis] --> B[Estimate Annual Demand X]
    B --> C[Compute Avoidable Fixed Cost F_M]
    C --> D[Compute Variable Cost per Unit V_M]
    D --> E[Obtain Vendor Quote P_B]
    E --> F[Compute Total Cost of Make C_M = F_M + V_M times X]
    F --> G[Compute Total Cost of Buy C_B = P_B times X]
    G --> H{Is C_M less than C_B at expected X?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Recommend MAKE In House]
    H -- No --> J{Is C_B less than C_M at expected X?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Recommend BUY from Vendor]
    J -- No --> L[Perform Qualitative and Strategic Analysis]
    L --> M[Consider Core Competency Protection]
    L --> N[Consider Quality and Reliability]
    L --> O[Consider Capacity Availability]
    L --> P[Consider Confidentiality and IP Risk]
    M --> Q[Final Strategic Recommendation]
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    I --> R[Implement Decision and Monitor]
    K --> R
    Q --> R
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Cost Components in Make-or-Buy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAKECOST[COST OF MAKING IN HOUSE]
        FM[Avoidable Fixed Cost F_M] --> TM[Total Cost of Make]
        VM[Variable Cost per Unit V_M times X] --> TM
        OM[Opportunity Cost if Capacity Constrained] --> TM
    end
    subgraph BUYCST[COST OF BUYING FROM VENDOR]
        PB[Purchase Price P_B times X] --> TB[Total Cost of Buy]
        FB[Vendor Fixed Cost F_B e.g. Inspection] --> TB
        TR[Inbound Transport and Inspection] --> TB
    end
    TM --> CMP{Cost Comparison Engine}
    TB --> CMP
    CMP --> XBE[Compute Break Even Volume X_BE]
    XBE --> DEC{Decision Module}
    DEC --> REC1[MAKE if X greater than X_BE]
    DEC --> REC2[BUY if X less than X_BE]
    DEC --> REC3[STRATEGIC REVIEW if Costs are Close]
```

### 4.3 Qualitative Factor Categorisation Diagram

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Make or Buy Qualitative Factors))
    Strategic
      Core Competency
      Vertical Integration
      Confidentiality
      Long Term Supplier Power
    Operational
      Capacity Availability
      Lead Time
      Quality Control
      Flexibility
    Financial
      Cash Flow Impact
      Investment in Equipment
      Working Capital Tied Up
    Risk
      Supply Disruption
      Currency Fluctuation
      Technology Obsolescence
      Vendor Reliability
    Human
      Workforce Morale
      Labour Relations
      Skill Retention
```

### 4.4 Make-or-Buy Decision Matrix (Summary Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Decision Trigger: New Component or Capacity Crunch] --> B{Firm Has Spare Capacity?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Pure Cost Comparison]
    B -- No --> D[Add Opportunity Cost of Displaced Product]
    C --> E{Qualitative Factors Favour Make or Buy Strongly}
    D --> E
    E -- Make --> F[Choose MAKE]
    E -- Buy --> G[Choose BUY]
    E -- Mixed --> H[Hybrid Strategy: Make Critical, Buy Non Critical]
    F --> I[Monitor and Review Annually]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The block diagrams above translate the abstract cost logic into a step-by-step process flow. In KTU viva or theory exams, drawing a similar 4–5 block flow chart of the decision process fetches 2–3 easy marks even without the numerical work.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1.** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*
**What is a "Make or Buy" decision? List any four factors that influence it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *Make or Buy* decision is a strategic choice in operations management that determines whether a firm should manufacture a product or component in-house using its own facilities, or purchase it from an external vendor. The decision is based on a comparison of the **relevant (avoidable) costs** of both alternatives, supplemented by qualitative factors.

**Four influencing factors (½ mark each):**
1. **Cost** — difference between in-house production cost and vendor's quoted price.
2. **Capacity availability** — whether the firm has spare production capacity.
3. **Quality requirements** — tolerance levels and consistency of the component.
4. **Confidentiality / Intellectual Property** — secrecy of design or process.

---

**Question A2.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*
**Distinguish between "Avoidable Cost" and "Sunk Cost" in the context of a make-or-buy decision. Why is a sunk cost irrelevant?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Avoidable Cost | Sunk Cost |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | A future cost that can be eliminated by choosing one of the alternatives. | A past cost that has already been incurred and cannot be recovered. |
| Example | Salary of a dedicated operator if the product is bought instead of made. | Depreciation on an already-purchased machine, written-down value of obsolete tooling. |
| Relevance to Make-or-Buy | **Relevant** — included in differential cost analysis. | **Not relevant** — incurred regardless of the decision. |

A sunk cost is irrelevant because the decision is about **future actions**, and a past expenditure cannot be altered by choosing "Make" or "Buy" today. Including it would distort the comparison and lead to an incorrect decision (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question B1 (Option A).** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]*
**(a) A firm manufactures a sub-assembly at a variable cost of ₹180 per unit. The fixed manufacturing overhead that can be avoided if production is stopped is ₹3,60,000 per year. An outside vendor has offered to supply the sub-assembly at ₹240 per unit. Determine the break-even volume. If the expected annual demand is 5,000 units, should the firm make or buy? Show all calculations.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss any seven qualitative factors that a manager should consider before finalising the make-or-buy decision.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B1(a) — Numerical Part (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the data (1 mark):**

$$F_M = \text{₹}3{,}60{,}000, \quad V_M = \text{₹}180, \quad P_B = \text{₹}240, \quad X = 5{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Write the cost equations (1 mark):**

$$C_{Make} = 3{,}60{,}000 + 180X$$

$$C_{Buy} = 240X$$

**Step 3 — Compute break-even volume (2 marks):**

$$3{,}60{,}000 + 180X_{BE} = 240X_{BE}$$

$$3{,}60{,}000 = (240 - 180) \cdot X_{BE}$$

$$X_{BE} = \frac{3{,}60{,}000}{60} = 6{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with expected demand (1 mark):**

$$X = 5{,}000 < X_{BE} = 6{,}000 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Buy is cheaper}$$

**Step 5 — Verify with total costs (1 mark):**

$$C_{Make}(5{,}000) = 3{,}60{,}000 + 180 \times 5{,}000 = \text{₹}12{,}60{,}000$$

$$C_{Buy}(5{,}000) = 240 \times 5{,}000 = \text{₹}12{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Saving by Buying} = \text{₹}60{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 6 — Recommendation (1 mark):**

> Since the expected demand (5,000 units) is **below** the break-even volume (6,000 units), the firm should **BUY** the sub-assembly from the vendor, saving **₹60,000 per year**.

---

#### Solution to B1(b) — Theory Part (7 marks, 1 mark each)

1. **Availability of Capacity:** If the firm has idle capacity, in-house production adds minimal opportunity cost; if capacity is constrained, outsourcing frees up capacity for higher-margin products.
2. **Quality and Reliability:** Critical components may demand in-house production for tighter quality control; routine standardised parts can be safely outsourced.
3. **Confidentiality and Intellectual Property:** If the design or process involves proprietary technology, the firm prefers to keep production in-house to protect trade secrets.
4. **Supplier Reliability and Lead Time:** Dependence on a single vendor creates risk; firms may prefer to make critical items and outsource non-critical ones.
5. **Cost Stability and Inflation Hedge:** Long-term purchase contracts can lock in prices but expose the firm to vendor price hikes; in-house production gives better cost control.
6. **Labour Relations and Workforce Morale:** Outsourcing can lead to layoffs, affecting morale and union relations — a significant consideration in manufacturing-heavy economies.
7. **Strategic Flexibility:** Outsourcing converts fixed costs into variable costs, providing flexibility to scale up or down with market demand.

---

**Question B1 (Option B — Alternative for Internal Choice).** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]*
**(a) ABC Industries can purchase a component at ₹90 per unit from a vendor. If it manufactures the component in-house, the avoidable fixed cost is ₹2,00,000 per year and the variable cost is ₹60 per unit. Determine the break-even volume. The firm expects to use 6,000 units per year. Advise whether to make or buy. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the role of opportunity cost in make-or-buy analysis. How does it affect the break-even volume when the firm has no spare capacity? (7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B1-B(a) — Numerical Part (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Data (1 mark):**

$$F_M = \text{₹}2{,}00{,}000, \quad V_M = \text{₹}60, \quad P_B = \text{₹}90, \quad X = 6{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Cost equations (1 mark):**

$$C_{Make} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 60X$$

$$C_{Buy} = 90X$$

**Step 3 — Break-even volume (2 marks):**

$$2{,}00{,}000 + 60X_{BE} = 90X_{BE}$$

$$2{,}00{,}000 = 30 \cdot X_{BE}$$

$$X_{BE} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{30} = 6{,}667 \text{ units (approx.)}$$

**Step 4 — Decision (1 mark):**

$$X = 6{,}000 < X_{BE} = 6{,}667 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Buy is recommended}$$

**Step 5 — Cost verification (1 mark):**

$$C_{Make}(6{,}000) = 2{,}00{,}000 + 60 \times 6{,}000 = \text{₹}5{,}60{,}000$$

$$C_{Buy}(6{,}000) = 90 \times 6{,}000 = \text{₹}5{,}40{,}000$$

$$\text{Saving by Buying} = \text{₹}20{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 6 — Recommendation (1 mark):**

> The firm should **BUY** the component, as the expected demand (6,000 units) is **below** the break-even volume (6,667 units).

---

#### Solution to B1-B(b) — Theory Part (7 marks)

**Definition of Opportunity Cost (2 marks):**
Opportunity cost is the **value of the next-best alternative foregone** when a particular choice is made. In make-or-buy analysis, it arises when in-house production requires using capacity that could otherwise generate revenue from another product.

**Effect on Break-Even Volume (3 marks):**
When the firm has **spare capacity**, opportunity cost of in-house production is zero, and the break-even formula is the standard one:

$$X_{BE} = \frac{F_M}{P_B - V_M}$$

When the firm has **no spare capacity**, producing in-house means displacing an existing product. The opportunity cost (lost contribution margin of the displaced product) must be added to the cost of making. If $CM_d$ is the contribution per unit of the displaced product and $D$ is the displaced volume, then:

$$X_{BE} = \frac{F_M + (CM_d \times D)}{P_B - V_M}$$

The break-even volume **increases** (becomes harder to justify making), which pushes the firm towards buying.

**Numerical Illustration (2 marks):**
If $F_M = 2,00,000$, $V_M = 60$, $P_B = 90$, $CM_d = 40$, and $D = 1,000$ units, then:

$$X_{BE} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000 + (40 \times 1{,}000)}{90 - 60} = \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{30} = 8{,}000 \text{ units}$$

Compared with the earlier 6,667 units, the break-even point has shifted up — making has become less attractive.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Including sunk costs** (e.g., book value of old machinery) → loses 1 mark. Only *avoidable future* costs are relevant.
> 2. **Forgetting opportunity cost** when there is no spare capacity → loses 2 marks in Part B theory/numerical hybrid.
> 3. **Not stating the decision rule in words** after the calculation → loses 1 method mark. Always conclude: *"Since $X < X_{BE}$, the firm should BUY."*
> 4. **Wrong units in the answer** (writing 8,000 instead of 8,000 units) → minor deduction. Always write "8,000 units" or "8,000 components/year."
> 5. **Conflating total cost with variable cost** in cost equations → a frequent 1-mark error. Be clear: $C_{Make}$ has both fixed and variable components.
> 6. **Ignoring qualitative factors** in the 7-mark theory sub-part → KTU deducts up to 3 marks. Always list at least 5–7 factors with one-line explanations.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Make-or-buy is a strategic choice between in-house manufacture and external procurement of a component or service.
- **Core Analytical Tool:** Relevant cost analysis using break-even volume.
- **Break-Even Formula:** $X_{BE} = F_M \,/\, (P_B - V_M)$ when $F_B = 0$.
- **Generalised Formula:** $X_{BE} = (F_M - F_B) \,/\, (P_B - V_M)$.
- **Decision Rule:** Make if expected demand $X > X_{BE}$ and $V_M < P_B$ (per-unit savings justify fixed cost).
- **Three Tiers of Analysis:** Quantitative (cost) → Qualitative (strategic) → Risk (supply chain, technology, financial).
- **Avoidable vs Unavoidable:** Only avoidable fixed costs are relevant; unavoidable ones continue either way.
- **Sunk Costs:** Always irrelevant — exclude them.
- **Opportunity Cost:** Relevant when the firm lacks spare capacity — add the displaced product's contribution margin to the cost of making.
- **Qualitative Factors to Memorise:** Core competency, capacity, quality, confidentiality, supplier reliability, lead time, flexibility, labour relations, strategic alignment.
- **Common Numerical Setup:** 1 avoidable fixed cost + 1 variable cost per unit (Make) vs 1 purchase price per unit (Buy). Annual demand given; compute $X_{BE}$, compare, recommend.
- **Verification Step:** Always plug the expected demand back into both cost equations to verify the decision and show the absolute saving.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** The 14-mark question is almost always split as a 7-mark numerical (with break-even calculation) plus a 7-mark theory (qualitative factors or opportunity cost discussion). Practice both styles.
- **Memory Hook for Formula:** *"Fixed cost saved on top, Price minus Variable in the bottom."* $\;\;X_{BE} = \dfrac{F_M}{P_B - V_M}$
- **Sanity Check:** If $P_B < V_M$, buying is cheaper per unit regardless of fixed cost → always BUY.
- **If Break-Even Doesn't Exist** (e.g., $P_B = V_M$): The decision depends entirely on fixed costs and qualitative factors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Reengineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT863 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Reengineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Business Process Reengineering (BPR) — The Hammer & Champy Definition**
> *"Business Process Reengineering is the fundamental rethinking and radical redesign of business processes to achieve dramatic improvements in critical, contemporary measures of performance, such as cost, quality, service, and speed."*
> — **Michael Hammer & James Champy, *Reengineering the Corporation* (1993)**

In KTU 2024 Scheme Operations Management terminology, **Process Reengineering** is an organizational change management philosophy that demands the **complete overthrow of legacy, function-based workflows** in favour of **streamlined, value-adding process-based structures**. It is *not* automation, *not* downsizing, and *not* incremental improvement — it is **obliteration-and-recreation**.

### The Four Anchor Keywords (KTU High-Frequency Vocabulary)
| Keyword | Meaning in BPR Context |
|---|---|
| **Fundamental** | Question why the process exists at all |
| **Radical** | Reconstruct from ground zero, ignore all existing rules |
| **Dramatic** | Target order-of-magnitude (10x) improvements, not 10% |
| **Process** | End-to-end workflow across functions, not departmental tasks |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** BPR is positioned in Module 1 because it sets the strategic foundation for *Operations Strategy*. It connects directly to **Course Outcome CO1**: *"Understand the concepts of operations management and its applications in manufacturing and service organisations."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine your kitchen sink is clogged. You have **three options**:

1. **Use more force on the plunger** → *Continuous Improvement / Kaizen* (small, incremental fix).
2. **Buy a stronger chemical drain cleaner** → *TQM / Quality Tools* (better tools for the existing process).
3. **Knock down the entire sink wall, redesign the plumbing, and install a modern drainage system** → ***Business Process Reengineering.***

The third option ignores the current sink entirely. It asks: *"Why do we even have a sink here? What if we moved the entire kitchen?"* That radical questioning is the essence of BPR.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Example KTU Examiners Love:** **Ford Motor Company's Accounts Payable Department.** In the 1980s, Ford's 500 AP clerks processed invoices against purchase orders. After BPR, they reduced the headcount to **125 clerks** — not by firing people, but by redesigning the workflow so that **80% of invoices never required human intervention**. The *process* was the problem, not the *people*.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Reengineering Strategy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process = A Structured Set of Activities Designed to Produce a Specific Output for a Particular Customer or Market.**
> — Davenport & Short, 1990 (MIT Sloan)

BPR rests on three pillars that distinguish it from all other change methodologies:

- **Pillar 1 — Process Orientation:** Focus shifts from *vertical functions* (sales, finance, HR) to *horizontal processes* (order-to-cash, procure-to-pay, concept-to-launch).
- **Pillar 2 — Customer Value:** Every step must add value from the *customer's perspective*. Non-value-adding steps are eliminated.
- **Pillar 3 — Organizational Disruption:** Reporting hierarchies, job roles, IT systems, and KPIs are all redesigned around the process — not the other way around.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principles of Business Process Reengineering

According to **Hammer & Champy (1993)**, BPR is guided by **seven core principles**. These are *extremely high-yield* for KTU Part A 3-mark questions.

| # | Principle | Operational Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Organize around outcomes, not tasks** | Combine fragmented tasks into unified process teams |
| 2 | **Have those who use the output perform the process** | Move work to the workers (deskilling) |
| 3 | **Subsume information-processing work into the work that produces the information** | Eliminate separate data-entry roles |
| 4 | **Treat geographically dispersed resources as though they were centralized** | Leverage IT to dissolve distance |
| 5 | **Link parallel process activities instead of just integrating their results** | Coordinate concurrently, not sequentially |
| 6 | **Put the decision point where the work is performed** | Empower frontline workers; reduce hierarchy |
| 7 | **Capture information once, at the source** | Eliminate redundant data capture across functions |

---

## 2.2 BPR vs. Other Change Philosophies (Critical KTU Comparison Table)

> [!NOTE]
> This comparison is a **guaranteed 14-mark Part B question** on KTU examinations. Memorize the contrasts.

| Dimension | BPR | TQM | Continuous Improvement (Kaizen) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | End-to-end process | Department / function | Task / workstation |
| **Magnitude of Change** | **Radical / Breakthrough** | Incremental | Small, gradual |
| **Time Horizon** | Months to years | Years (ongoing) | Continuous |
| **Leadership** | Top-down (CEO-led) | Top-down + bottom-up | Bottom-up |
| **Trigger** | Crisis or competitive threat | Customer dissatisfaction | Employee suggestion |
| **Risk Level** | **High** | Moderate | Low |
| **Focus** | Process redesign | Quality of output | Waste reduction |
| **Originator** | Hammer & Champy (USA, 1993) | Deming, Juran, Crosby (USA/Japan) | Toyota Production System (Japan) |
| **Typical Improvement** | **10x – 100x** | 1.2x – 2x | 1.05x – 1.2x |
| **Use of IT** | Pivotal (enabler) | Supporting | Minimal |

---

## 2.3 The BPR Methodology — Phase-by-Phase Framework

The most widely accepted **BPR Roadmap** (Hammer & Champy, adapted by KTU) consists of **five sequential phases**:

### Phase 1 — Envision (Strategic Vision)
- Senior leadership identifies the *strategic intent*.
- Identify **processes critical to strategic goals** (e.g., cost, customer satisfaction).
- Form a **steering committee** with CEO sponsorship.

### Phase 2 — Define / Identify (Process Mapping)
- Map existing ("AS-IS") processes using techniques like:
  - **Flowcharts**
  - **IDEF0 models**
  - **SIPOC diagrams** (Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers)
- Identify **Value-Added (VA)** vs **Non-Value-Added (NVA)** steps.

### Phase 3 — Analyze (Root-Cause Analysis)
- Apply **gap analysis**, **Fishbone diagrams**, **Pareto analysis**.
- Calculate the **Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE)** — the most important BPR metric.

### Phase 4 — Redesign ("TO-BE" Design)
- Brainstorm radical alternatives without legacy constraints.
- Prototype the new process; conduct simulations.
- Design new **organizational structure**, **job roles**, and **IT architecture**.

### Phase 5 — Implement & Continuous Improvement
- Pilot the redesigned process.
- Roll out organization-wide using **change management models** (e.g., Kotter's 8-Step).
- Lock in gains; transition to **Kaizen** mode.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Metrics Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master Metric of BPR: Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE)**

$$
\text{PCE} = \frac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} \times 100\%
$$

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE)** | $\text{PCE} = \dfrac{\text{VA Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} \times 100\%$ | World-class processes target **>25%**; typical firms operate at **5–15%** |
| **Reengineering Improvement Ratio (RIR)** | $\text{RIR} = \dfrac{\text{Performance}_{\text{NEW}}}{\text{Performance}_{\text{OLD}}}$ | BPR targets **RIR $\geq$ 10** (i.e., 10x improvement) |
| **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{prevention}} + C_{\text{appraisal}} + C_{\text{internal failure}} + C_{\text{external failure}}$ | Used to size the *opportunity* before BPR |
| **Activity-Based Cost (ABC) of Process** | $\text{Cost}_{\text{process}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(\text{Cost Driver}_i \times \text{Rate}_i)$ | Allocates overhead accurately to processes (not functions) |
| **Throughput Time (Lead Time)** | $\text{LT} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(\text{Process Time}_i + \text{Wait Time}_i + \text{Transport Time}_i)$ | The total elapsed time from start to finish |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Trick:** When asked *"Why is BPR necessary?"* — always lead your answer with PCE. Most Indian manufacturing firms have a **PCE of < 5%**, meaning **>95% of process time is pure waste** (waiting, transporting, inspecting, reworking). This single statistic justifies radical redesign.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Industry

- **Manufacturing:** Toyota's transition from batch production to the *Toyota Production System* (lean) is itself a reengineered process.
- **IT Services:** DevOps replaced the sequential "Waterfall" SDLC — a textbook BPR case.
- **Banking:** ATMs replaced *teller-withdrawal* workflows; UPI eliminated *cashier-queue* workflows.
- **Healthcare:** Cleveland Clinic reengineered cardiac surgery scheduling, reducing average wait from 8 weeks to 4 days.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — Computing Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE)

**Problem Statement:** An order-fulfilment process at a Kerala-based spice-export firm has the following timeline:

| Step | Activity | Time (hours) | Classification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Order receipt via email | 0.5 | Value-Added (VA) |
| 2 | Manual data entry into ERP | 2.0 | Non-Value-Added (NVA) |
| 3 | Credit verification (waits for manager approval) | 24.0 | NVA (Wait) |
| 4 | Pick & pack in warehouse | 3.0 | VA |
| 5 | Move to staging area | 1.5 | NVA (Transport) |
| 6 | Quality inspection | 1.0 | VA |
| 7 | Wait for courier pickup | 12.0 | NVA (Wait) |
| 8 | Dispatch & documentation | 0.5 | VA |

**Compute the Process Cycle Efficiency.**

### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1 — Classify each activity as VA or NVA**

$$
\text{VA Activities} = \{ \text{Step 1, Step 4, Step 6, Step 8} \}
$$

$$
\text{NVA Activities} = \{ \text{Step 2, Step 3, Step 5, Step 7} \}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the Value-Added Time**

$$
T_{\text{VA}} = t_1 + t_4 + t_6 + t_8
$$

$$
T_{\text{VA}} = 0.5 + 3.0 + 1.0 + 0.5
$$

$$
T_{\text{VA}} = 5.0 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the Total Cycle Time**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{8} t_i
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} = (0.5 + 2.0 + 24.0 + 3.0 + 1.5 + 1.0 + 12.0 + 0.5)
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 44.5 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the PCE Formula**

$$
\text{PCE} = \frac{T_{\text{VA}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{PCE} = \frac{5.0}{44.5} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{PCE} \approx 11.24\%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the Result**

A PCE of **11.24%** indicates that **88.76% of the cycle time is pure waste** — primarily the 36 hours spent waiting for approvals and courier pickup. This is the diagnostic evidence that justifies BPR.

**Step 6 — Project the Post-BPR Scenario**

If the firm eliminates Steps 2, 3, 5, and 7 through process redesign (e.g., automated credit scoring, direct ERP integration, in-line quality checks):

$$
T_{\text{total, NEW}} = 5.0 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\text{PCE}_{\text{NEW}} = \frac{5.0}{5.0} \times 100\% = 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Reengineering Improvement Ratio (RIR)} = \frac{44.5}{5.0} = 8.9
$$

**Step 7 — Realistic World-Class Benchmark**

In practice, world-class firms achieve **PCE of 25–40%**, not 100%. A more realistic reengineered scenario retains ~1.5 hours of essential NVA (e.g., courier handover):

$$
T_{\text{total, realistic}} = 5.0 + 1.5 = 6.5 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\text{PCE}_{\text{realistic}} = \frac{5.0}{6.5} \times 100\% \approx 76.92\%
$$

$$
\text{RIR}_{\text{realistic}} = \frac{44.5}{6.5} \approx 6.85
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Insight:** Always show the **three tiers of PCE** — *current*, *ideal*, and *realistic*. Examiners award full marks only when the student demonstrates engineering judgment, not just arithmetic.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — Reengineering Improvement Ratio (RIR) vs. ROI Justification

**Problem Statement:** A KTU automobile service centre spends ₹15,00,000 per month on its 11-step warranty-claim process. The process takes **9 days** on average. After BPR, the new 4-step process takes **1.5 days** and costs **₹4,00,000 per month**. Calculate the RIR and the monthly cost savings.

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the Time-Based RIR**

$$
\text{RIR}_{\text{time}} = \frac{T_{\text{OLD}}}{T_{\text{NEW}}}
$$

$$
\text{RIR}_{\text{time}} = \frac{9.0 \text{ days}}{1.5 \text{ days}} = 6.0
$$

This indicates a **6x improvement** in cycle time — just shy of the 10x BPR benchmark.

**Step 2 — Compute the Cost-Based RIR**

$$
\text{RIR}_{\text{cost}} = \frac{C_{\text{OLD}}}{C_{\text{NEW}}}
$$

$$
\text{RIR}_{\text{cost}} = \frac{15,00,000}{4,00,000} = 3.75
$$

A **3.75x** cost reduction is substantial for a service operation.

**Step 3 — Compute the Composite RIR (Geometric Mean)**

$$
\text{RIR}_{\text{composite}} = \sqrt{\text{RIR}_{\text{time}} \times \text{RIR}_{\text{cost}}}
$$

$$
\text{RIR}_{\text{composite}} = \sqrt{6.0 \times 3.75} = \sqrt{22.5} \approx 4.74
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Monthly Cost Savings**

$$
\text{Savings} = C_{\text{OLD}} - C_{\text{NEW}}
$$

$$
\text{Savings} = 15,00,000 - 4,00,000 = ₹11,00,000 \text{ per month}
$$

**Step 5 — Annualised Savings & ROI Calculation**

$$
\text{Savings}_{\text{annual}} = 11,00,000 \times 12 = ₹1,32,00,000
$$

Assuming a one-time BPR implementation cost of **₹40,00,000** (consultants + IT + training):

$$
\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{Savings}_{\text{annual}} - \text{Implementation Cost}}{\text{Implementation Cost}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{ROI} = \frac{1,32,00,000 - 40,00,000}{40,00,000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{ROI} = \frac{92,00,000}{40,00,000} \times 100\% = 230\%
$$

$$
\text{Payback Period} = \frac{\text{Implementation Cost}}{\text{Monthly Savings}} = \frac{40,00,000}{11,00,000} \approx 3.64 \text{ months}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When a question gives both cost and time data, *always* compute the **composite RIR** using the geometric mean. It prevents a single metric from masking poor performance on the other dimension.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — BPR Success Factors vs. Failure Triggers

| Dimension | Success Factors (Hammer & Stanton) | Failure Triggers (Common KTU Case Studies) |
|---|---|---|
| **Leadership** | CEO as process champion | BPR delegated to mid-management |
| **Vision** | Clear strategic intent | Vague "improve efficiency" mandate |
| **Scope** | End-to-end process focus | Patching individual tasks |
| **Methodology** | Phased, disciplined rollout | "Big Bang" implementation |
| **IT Enablement** | IT as redesign driver, not afterthought | IT used only to automate old process |
| **Change Management** | Continuous communication, training | Underestimating people resistance |
| **Measurement** | Baseline PCE + post-BPR PCE tracked | No metrics defined upfront |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block 1 — The BPR Lifecycle (5-Phase Roadmap)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Phase 1: Envision"] --> B["Phase 2: Identify Processes"]
    B --> C["Phase 3: Analyze AS-IS"]
    C --> D["Phase 4: Design TO-BE"]
    D --> E["Phase 5: Implement and Sustain"]
    E --> F["Continuous Improvement Mode"]
    F --> C
    A1["CEO Sponsorship"] -.-> A
    A2["Strategic Intent Defined"] -.-> A
    B1["Process Mapping SIPOC"] -.-> B
    C1["Calculate PCE Baseline"] -.-> C
    D1["Prototype and Simulate"] -.-> D
    E1["Pilot and Roll Out"] -.-> E
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block 2 — BPR vs. Kaizen vs. TQM (Comparative Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph RE["Radical Change"]
        R1["BPR"]
        R2["Hammer and Champy 1993"]
        R3["10x to 100x improvement"]
        R4["Process Level"]
    end
    subgraph IN["Incremental Change"]
        I1["Kaizen"]
        I2["Toyota 1950s"]
        I3["1.05x to 1.2x improvement"]
        I4["Task Level"]
    end
    subgraph QR["Quality Focused"]
        Q1["TQM"]
        Q2["Deming and Juran 1980s"]
        Q3["1.2x to 2x improvement"]
        Q4["Function Level"]
    end
    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4
    I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> I4
    Q1 --> Q2 --> Q3 --> Q4
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Reengineered Process

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE BPR - Functional Silos"]
        CUST1["Customer"] --> S1["Sales Department"]
        S1 --> F1["Finance Department"]
        F1 --> O1["Operations Department"]
        O1 --> D1["Dispatch Department"]
        D1 --> CUST1
    end
    subgraph AFTER["AFTER BPR - Process Team"]
        CUST2["Customer"] --> PT["Unified Process Team"]
        PT --> CUST2
        PT -.-> IT["Shared IT Platform"]
        PT -.-> DEC["Empowered Decision Rights"]
        PT -.-> KPI["Single KPI Owner"]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block 4 — Decision Flow for Selecting BPR Candidate Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Identify Candidate Process"] --> q1{"Is process broken beyond incremental repair?"}
    q1 -->|No| keep["Apply Kaizen Instead"]
    q1 -->|Yes| q2{"Is it strategically critical?"}
    q2 -->|No| defer["Defer to TQM Program"]
    q2 -->|Yes| q3{"Is top management committed?"}
    q3 -->|No| stop["Do not proceed with BPR"]
    q3 -->|Yes| launch["Launch BPR Project with CEO Sponsorship"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Strategy:** When asked *"When should a firm choose BPR over TQM?"* — point to the decision tree above. BPR is reserved for **strategically critical** processes that are **fundamentally broken** and where **top-management commitment** is secured. Using BPR for minor inefficiencies is a classic *strategic misapplication*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define Business Process Reengineering. List any THREE principles of BPR.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** BPR is the *fundamental rethinking and radical redesign* of business processes to achieve *dramatic improvements* in critical performance measures like cost, quality, service, and speed (Hammer & Champy, 1993).

**Any Three Principles (2 Marks — 0.5 each + 0.5 for presentation):**

1. **Organize around outcomes, not tasks** — Combine fragmented, specialist tasks into unified process teams that deliver complete outputs.
2. **Have those who use the output perform the process** — Eliminate intermediary roles; frontline workers handle the entire process.
3. **Put the decision point where the work is performed** — Empower workers with decision rights; flatten the hierarchy.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Distinguish between BPR and TQM in any THREE dimensions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 per dimension):**

| Dimension | BPR | TQM |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Cross-functional end-to-end process | Within a department or function |
| **Magnitude** | Radical, breakthrough (10x+) | Incremental, gradual (1.2x–2x) |
| **Time Horizon** | One-time transformation project | Continuous, ongoing philosophy |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

> **QUESTION A.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Module 1 Choice A]`
>
> **(a)** Explain the **five phases** of the Business Process Reengineering methodology in detail. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compute the **Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE)** for the following loan-approval process at a Kerala bank, and recommend **two specific BPR interventions**. **(7 Marks)**

**Loan-Approval Process Data:**

| Step | Activity | Time (hrs) | VA/NVA |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Customer submits application form | 0.5 | VA |
| 2 | Branch staff manually enters data into CBS | 2.0 | NVA |
| 3 | Application awaits branch manager queue | 18.0 | NVA |
| 4 | Manager reviews and approves | 1.0 | VA |
| 5 | Document sent to regional office by physical courier | 24.0 | NVA |
| 6 | Credit-analyst reviews at regional office | 4.0 | VA |
| 7 | Sanction letter prepared and printed | 1.5 | VA |
| 8 | Wait for customer to collect letter | 36.0 | NVA |

### Model Solution for Question A(a) — The Five Phases of BPR (7 Marks)

**1. Phase 1 — Envision (2 Marks):**
The CEO and senior leadership identify the **strategic intent** (e.g., "become the fastest loan-sanctioning bank in Kerala"). They recognize that the existing process is fundamentally incapable of meeting this goal. A **steering committee** is formed with cross-functional authority and a dedicated **process champion** (usually a senior VP). The output is a *BPR Charter* defining scope, objectives, and budget.

**2. Phase 2 — Identify / Define (1.5 Marks):**
The team maps all current processes using **flowcharts**, **SIPOC diagrams**, and **swim-lane diagrams**. They prioritize processes using criteria like strategic impact, customer pain, and cost. For our loan case, the "AS-IS" map would show 8 sequential steps with 4 hand-offs across branches and regional offices.

**3. Phase 3 — Analyze (1 Mark):**
Quantitative analysis is performed — particularly the **PCE** calculation. Qualitative root-cause analysis (Fishbone, 5-Why) identifies systemic causes like manual handoffs, sequential approvals, and lack of IT integration.

**4. Phase 4 — Redesign (1.5 Marks):**
The team creates the **"TO-BE"** process. Radical alternatives are brainstormed without legacy bias. Common redesign patterns include: *automation, elimination, integration, and parallelization*. For our loan case, redesigns include online application submission and automated credit scoring.

**5. Phase 5 — Implement & Sustain (1 Mark):**
The redesigned process is **piloted**, refined, and rolled out. **Change management** (Kotter's 8-step model) addresses people resistance. New KPIs (e.g., sanction turnaround time) replace old functional metrics. Gains are locked in through transition to **Kaizen** mode.

---

### Model Solution for Question A(b) — PCE Calculation & BPR Recommendations (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify VA Activities:**
VA = {Step 1, Step 4, Step 6, Step 7}

**Step 2 — Sum Value-Added Time (1 Mark):**

$$
T_{\text{VA}} = 0.5 + 1.0 + 4.0 + 1.5 = 7.0 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum Total Cycle Time (1 Mark):**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 0.5 + 2.0 + 18.0 + 1.0 + 24.0 + 4.0 + 1.5 + 36.0 = 87.0 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply PCE Formula (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{PCE} = \frac{7.0}{87.0} \times 100\% \approx 8.05\%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret Result (1 Mark):**
**91.95% of cycle time is waste** — primarily waiting (78 of 87 hours). The process is severely inefficient and is a prime candidate for BPR.

**Step 6 — Two Specific BPR Interventions (2 Marks):**

1. **Digitize the application process (eliminate Steps 2, 3, 5):** Implement an online loan portal where customers upload documents. This eliminates manual data entry (2 hrs), manager approval queue (18 hrs), and courier transport (24 hrs) — saving **44 hours** per loan.

2. **Empower branch managers with digital decision tools (consolidate Steps 4, 6, 7):** Provide a real-time dashboard that integrates credit scoring, eligibility check, and sanction letter generation. Branch managers can approve and generate sanction letters in **30 minutes**, eliminating the 36-hour wait for customer pickup through instant digital delivery.

**Projected New PCE:**

$$
T_{\text{VA, NEW}} = 7.0 \text{ hrs}, \quad T_{\text{total, NEW}} \approx 8.0 \text{ hrs}
$$

$$
\text{PCE}_{\text{NEW}} = \frac{7.0}{8.0} \times 100\% = 87.5\%
$$

$$
\text{RIR} = \frac{87.0}{8.0} \approx 10.88
$$

This exceeds the **BPR benchmark of 10x improvement**.

---

> **QUESTION B.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Module 1 Choice B]`
>
> **(a)** Compare and contrast **Business Process Reengineering (BPR)** with **Total Quality Management (TQM)** and **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** across **at least five dimensions**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the **Ford Motor Company's Accounts Payable** case study in detail. What specific **BPR principles** were applied, and what were the measurable outcomes? **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B(a) — BPR vs. TQM vs. Kaizen (7 Marks)

**(For each dimension, allocate 1.4 marks)**

**1. Scope of Change:**
- BPR operates at the **end-to-end process level**, often crossing organizational boundaries.
- TQM operates at the **functional/departmental level**, focusing on quality of outputs.
- Kaizen operates at the **task/workstation level**, focusing on individual worker improvements.

**2. Magnitude of Improvement:**
- BPR targets **dramatic, order-of-magnitude improvements (10x to 100x)**.
- TQM delivers **moderate improvements (1.2x to 2x)** sustained over years.
- Kaizen delivers **marginal improvements (1.05x to 1.2x)** but compounded continuously.

**3. Approach to Existing Process:**
- BPR **discards** the existing process entirely and starts from scratch.
- TQM **preserves** the process and improves its quality outputs.
- Kaizen **preserves** the process and improves its efficiency step-by-step.

**4. Risk and Implementation:**
- BPR carries **high risk** due to radical disruption; failure rate is 50–70%.
- TQM carries **moderate risk**; well-established methodology.
- Kaizen carries **low risk** due to small, reversible changes.

**5. Leadership and Culture:**
- BPR is **top-down**, CEO-driven, with external consultants typically leading.
- TQM is **top-down + bottom-up**, requiring organization-wide cultural shift.
- Kaizen is **bottom-up**, driven by frontline workers and team leaders.

**6. Use of Information Technology:**
- BPR uses IT as a **strategic enabler** and redesign driver.
- TQM uses IT for **measurement, SPC, and quality data analysis**.
- Kaizen uses IT **minimally**, focusing on manual observation and worker creativity.

---

### Model Solution for Question B(b) — Ford Accounts Payable Case (7 Marks)

**Context (2 Marks):**
In the 1980s, Ford Motor Company's North American Accounts Payable (AP) department employed **over 500 clerks** in 4 regional offices. The clerks processed **over 1 million invoices per year** from more than 30,000 suppliers. Each invoice required manual matching against purchase orders and goods-received notes — a tedious, error-prone process with high rework rates.

**Why BPR Was Needed (1 Mark):**
Management consultant **Michael Hammer** observed that Ford's AP process was not labour-productive by itself — **the standard practice of "matching three documents" was the actual problem**. Simply adding more clerks would not help.

**BPR Principles Applied (2.5 Marks):**

1. **Capture information once, at the source:** Instead of suppliers mailing paper invoices, Ford implemented a **Supplier Invoice Management (SIM) system**. Suppliers were given access to submit invoices electronically, with data captured once at the point of entry.
2. **Have those who use the output perform the process:** Instead of AP clerks matching all three documents, Ford redesigned the process so that **80% of invoices never needed human matching** — the system auto-matched them against existing purchase orders.
3. **Subsume information-processing work into the actual work:** The data entry was integrated into the supplier's own system, eliminating redundant entry at Ford's end.
4. **Treat geographically dispersed resources as centralized:** The 4 regional offices were consolidated into a single **virtual AP department** using shared IT systems.

**Measurable Outcomes (1.5 Marks):**

| Metric | Before BPR | After BPR | Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of Clerks** | ~500 | ~125 | **75% reduction** |
| **Productivity** | Baseline | 4x increase | **400% gain** |
| **Accuracy** | ~85% | ~98% | **13 pp increase** |
| **Headcount Reassignment** | — | Clerks moved to higher-value roles | Not fired |

**Conclusion:** Ford's AP case is the **canonical BPR example** because it demonstrated that *the process, not the people, was the root cause of inefficiency*. The savings were reinvested into value-adding activities.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in BPR questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing BPR with downsizing** — BPR is a *process redesign* philosophy; headcount reduction is a *consequence*, not a goal. (–1 mark typical deduction)
> 2. **Writing only the definition without elaboration** — In 14-mark questions, the definition alone is worth only **1 mark**. You MUST add principles, phases, examples, and metrics.
> 3. **Ignoring the PCE calculation in numerical questions** — Even if the question doesn't explicitly ask, computing PCE demonstrates *first-principles thinking* and earns **+1 grace mark**.
> 4. **Mixing up BPR with Kaizen** — These are *opposite* philosophies. If the question asks about "radical redesign," DO NOT mention Kaizen as the methodology.
> 5. **Forgetting to compute the post-BPR PCE** — In problems, always show **before-and-after metrics**. The *delta* is what the examiner wants to see.
> 6. **Writing about IT only** — IT is the *enabler*, not the *driver*. The driver is strategic intent. Always lead with the business case, not the technology.
> 7. **Skipping the practical realism** — Don't claim PCE can reach 100%. Always acknowledge that *some* NVA (regulatory checks, hand-offs) is unavoidable.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Reengineering — Quick-Fire Revision (Pin This Section)**

- **Definition (Hammer & Champy):** Fundamental + Radical + Dramatic + Process.
- **Core Question BPR Asks:** *"Why do we do this at all?"* — not *"How do we do this faster?"*
- **Seven Principles:** Outcomes-not-tasks · User-performs-process · Subsume-info · Centralize-dispersed · Link-parallel-activities · Decision-at-work · Capture-once.
- **Five Phases:** Envision → Identify → Analyze → Redesign → Implement.
- **The Master Formula:**
  $$\text{PCE} = \frac{T_{\text{VA}}}{T_{\text{Total}}} \times 100\%$$
- **BPR Benchmark:** RIR ≥ 10x (i.e., order-of-magnitude improvement).
- **World-Class PCE Target:** 25–40% (not 100% — some NVA is unavoidable).
- **BPR ≠ Downsizing** (people are not the problem; the process is).
- **BPR ≠ Kaizen** (BPR is radical; Kaizen is incremental).
- **BPR ≠ IT automation** (IT is the enabler; strategic intent is the driver).
- **Iconic Case Study:** Ford Accounts Payable — 500 clerks → 125 clerks, 4x productivity.
- **Indian Example:** HDFC Bank's loan origination, IRCTC's tatkal booking reengineering.
- **Failure Rate:** 50–70% of BPR projects fail — *change management is the differentiator.*
- **Success Prerequisites:** CEO sponsorship, strategic intent, IT readiness, change management, baseline metrics.
- **KTU Exam Weightage:** This topic typically carries **1 question in Part A (3 marks)** + **1 question in Part B (14 marks)** = **~17 marks out of 100** in the Operations Management paper.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Wisdom:** When in doubt, structure your BPR answer in **four paragraphs**: (1) Definition + Origin, (2) Key Principles, (3) Phased Methodology, (4) Real-World Example. This scaffolding guarantees a full-mark answer even under exam pressure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
