> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT863 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Operations Strategy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operations Strategy** is the *long-range plan* of an organization that defines how operations (the part of the business that produces goods and/or delivers services) will **support the overall corporate and business strategy**. It specifies the set of competitive priorities, capabilities, and structural decisions (process choice, capacity, layout, supply network, technology, organization) that enable the firm to win in its chosen markets.

More formally, **Slack & Lewis (2015)** define Operations Strategy as:
> *"The pattern of decisions and actions which set the role and objectives of operations and the means by which these objectives are to be achieved."*

### 1.1.1 The Three-Layer Strategy Hierarchy

In any firm, strategy exists at three levels — and *Operations Strategy sits at the bottom of this pyramid*:

| Level | Name | Responsibility | Time Horizon |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 1** | Corporate Strategy | What business are we in? Resource allocation across SBUs | 5–10 years |
| **Level 2** | Business / Competitive Strategy | How do we compete in each business? (Porter) | 3–5 years |
| **Level 3** | Functional / Operations Strategy | How do operations support competitive positioning? | 1–3 years |

**Operations Strategy is therefore the "functional mirror" of Business Strategy.** It is **NOT** the same as corporate strategy — it is the *operational translation* of how the firm intends to compete.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Brigade" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — A Restaurant Kitchen:**
>
> Imagine a high-end restaurant. The **owner (corporate strategy)** decides: *"We will be the most authentic Italian restaurant in the city."* The **head chef (business strategy)** decides: *"We compete on freshness and authenticity — not on price."*
>
> Now the **Sous Chef (operations strategy)** must decide:
> - Will we have a **wood-fired pizza oven** (process choice)?
> - Will we **buy flour daily from Naples** (sourcing strategy)?
> - Will we hire **trained Italian chefs** (HR strategy)?
> - Will our kitchen be an **open layout** so customers see the chefs (layout strategy)?
> - How many covers per night can we serve — i.e., what is our **capacity**?
>
> All these **operational decisions must align with the owner's promise of authenticity.** If the owner promises freshness but the kitchen buys frozen dough, **the strategy is broken**.
>
> This alignment between *business promise* and *operational reality* is precisely what Operations Strategy is about.

---

## 1.3 Why Operations Strategy Matters — The Strategic Role of Operations

Every organization has an operations function — even a bank (processes transactions) or a hospital (delivers healthcare). Historically seen as a **"back-office cost center"**, operations is now recognized as a **source of competitive advantage**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategic Role of Operations** (Hayes & Wheelwright, 1984 — *Restoring Our Competitive Edge*):
> Operations is no longer just executing — it is *shaping* competitive advantage.

The operations function contributes through:
1. **Cost reduction** — efficiency, economies of scale, learning curves.
2. **Revenue generation** — superior quality, faster delivery, customisation.
3. **Risk reduction** — supply resilience, capacity flexibility, process robustness.
4. **Innovation enablement** — new process technology creates new products.

---

## 1.4 Core Terminology You Must Memorise

- **Order Qualifiers** — those criteria a firm **must meet** simply to be considered by the customer (e.g., a car must actually start). Not differentiating, but *necessary*.
- **Order Winners** — those criteria that **win** the customer's business (e.g., lowest price, fastest delivery, best design).
- **Competitive Priorities** — the operational capabilities the firm chooses to excel at (Cost, Quality, Speed, Dependability, Flexibility).
- **Strategic Fit** — the degree of alignment between competitive priorities and the operations resources configured to deliver them.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU One-Liner for 3-Mark Questions:**
> *"Operations Strategy is the pattern of decisions that link operations capabilities to business strategy to achieve competitive advantage."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Stage Evolution of Operations Strategy (Hayes & Wheelwright Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most-tested framework** in KTU University Exams on this topic. Memorize the four stages precisely.

Professors Robert Hayes and Steven Wheelwright of Harvard described the *role* operations plays in the firm as evolving through four stages along two axes — **internal vs external focus** and **passive vs proactive**.

| Stage | Role of Operations | Strategic Stance | Why Most Firms Get Stuck |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stage 1: Internally Neutral** | Operations is a black box — neither helps nor hurts strategy | Operations reacts, doesn't initiate | Operations seen as clerical, ignored |
| **Stage 2: Externally Neutral** | Operations benchmarks industry "best practice" | Tries to match competitors, not exceed them | "Me-too" operations — no differentiation |
| **Stage 3: Internally Supportive** | Operations is consciously designed to *support* business strategy | Aligned, but follows rather than leads | Operations is *aligned but reactive* |
| **Stage 4: Externally Supportive** | Operations **drives** corporate strategy and creates new competitive options | Operations is a strategic weapon | Only "World-Class" firms reach this |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's favourite trap:** Many students write Stage 4 as "the best stage" — but Hayes & Wheelwright never said that. Stage 4 is *appropriate* for some industries (e.g., Amazon, Toyota) but overkill for others (e.g., a commodity cement plant).

---

## 2.2 The Five Performance Objectives (Slack's Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slack & Brandon-Jones** identified five basic **performance objectives** that every operations function must deliver. These are the "operational version" of competitive priorities.

| # | Performance Objective | What it Means | Typical Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Quality** | Conformance to specification & fitness for purpose | Defect rate (PPM), Six Sigma level |
| 2 | **Speed** | Time between customer request and delivery | Order lead time (days/hours) |
| 3 | **Dependability** | Doing what you said you would, on time | On-time delivery % (OTD) |
| 4 | **Flexibility** | Ability to change — volume, mix, design | Set-up time, range of variants |
| 5 | **Cost** | The unit cost of producing the product/service | Cost per unit, conversion cost |

> [!NOTE]
> **Radar charts** (also called **Kiviat diagrams**) are the standard visual tool to depict the *trade-off* between these five objectives. A firm cannot be best at ALL five simultaneously — it must **trade off**.

---

## 2.3 The "Sandwich" Model of Operations Strategy (Slack & Lewis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **most cited framework in KTU 2024 Scheme** for understanding how Operations Strategy links the firm to the market.

```
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
|   CORPORATE / BUSINESS STRATEGY                                |
|   (Market position, customer segments, value proposition)      |
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
                          ↓  ↓  ↓
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
|   OPERATIONS STRATEGY  ←  THE "FILLING"                       |
|   (Process, Capacity, Layout, Supply Chain, Tech, HR)          |
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
                          ↓  ↓  ↓
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
|   OPERATIONS RESOURCES & CAPABILITIES                          |
|   (Machines, People, Systems, Facilities, Suppliers)           |
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Top slice** = What the *market* wants (qualifying & winning criteria).
- **Middle filling** = How operations converts market needs into operational reality.
- **Bottom slice** = The actual resources owned/configured by operations.

**The role of operations strategy** is to ensure the *top slice aligns with the bottom slice* — i.e., the operations resource base must be configured to deliver what the market demands.

---

## 2.4 Three-Stage Formulation Process (Slack & Lewis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When asked *"How is operations strategy formulated?"* — write this **three-stage top-down + bottom-up loop**.

### Stage 1 — Top-Down: Market-Led
- Start from **corporate and business strategy**.
- Identify **competitive priorities** required.
- **Output:** What operations must achieve (the "fitness criteria").

### Stage 2 — Bottom-Up: Resource-Based
- Audit the firm's **operations resources and capabilities**.
- Identify the **strengths** (distinctive capabilities) and **weaknesses** (capability gaps).
- **Output:** What operations *can* achieve.

### Stage 3 — Reconciliation
- Compare Stage 1 *demands* with Stage 2 *capabilities*.
- The **gap** becomes the agenda for operations strategy.
- Strategy = *new operations decisions + resource development* to close the gap.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This is why some firms are *market-led* (Apple — design drives operations) while others are *resource-led* (Rolls-Royce — engineering heritage defines what they can sell). Both are valid — but the strategy must be **internally consistent**.

---

## 2.5 Market-Led vs Resource-Based Operations Strategy

| Dimension | Market-Led Strategy | Resource-Based Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Driver** | Customer requirements | Existing unique resources |
| **Direction** | Top-down (Stage 1 first) | Bottom-up (Stage 2 first) |
| **Risk** | Market may not exist for the resource | Capability may have no market |
| **Examples** | Zara, Netflix | Rolls-Royce (engineering), Scotch whisky (heritage) |

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although Operations Strategy is primarily conceptual, the following **analytical ratios** are tested in numerical parts of Part B questions.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Purpose | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Productivity** | $\text{Productivity} = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$ | Efficiency measure | Units / hour |
| 2 | **Capacity Utilisation** | $\text{Utilisation} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Maximum Capacity}} \times 100\%$ | Demand–capacity fit | **%** |
| 3 | **Learning Curve** (Unit Time) | $Y_x = Y_1 \cdot x^{b}$ where $b = \dfrac{\log(LR)}{\log 2}$ | Cost reduction per doubling | Hours / unit |
| 4 | **Break-Even Point** | $\text{BEP} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{P - V}$ | Volume to cover cost | **Units** |
| 5 | **Inventory Turns** | $\text{Turns} = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | Working-capital efficiency | Times / year |
| 6 | **Order Lead Time** | $\text{LT} = \text{Order Processing} + \text{Production} + \text{Transport}$ | Speed objective | Days |
| 7 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Units passing inspection}}{\text{Total units started}}$ | Quality objective | **%** |
| 8 | **OTD (On-Time Delivery)** | $\text{OTD} = \dfrac{\text{Orders delivered on time}}{\text{Total orders shipped}} \times 100\%$ | Dependability objective | **%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** In markdown tables, **never** write $\vert x \vert$ with vertical bars — this breaks the table. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` instead. (Already applied above.)

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility — Where This Is Used in Industry

- **Manufacturing:** Deciding whether to compete on cost (China-volume) or on flexibility/speed (mass-customisation).
- **Services:** Hospital positioning — does it compete on *clinical excellence* (Mayo Clinic) or *accessibility* (Apollo neighbourhood clinics)?
- **Retail:** Amazon's "two-day delivery" is an **operations-driven competitive strategy** (Stage 4).
- **Tech:** Apple's closed vertical integration is a **resource-led operations strategy** — design and ecosystem drive market capability.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Operations Strategy has limited classical "derivations" — but it has **step-by-step analytical procedures** that KTU expects you to write in full when answering 14-mark questions. Below is the **complete worked logic** for each major framework.

---

## 3.1 Procedure: Applying the Hayes-Wheelwright Stage Diagnostic

**Step 1:** Identify how operations interacts with corporate strategy in the firm.
- Is operations *consulted* on new product launches? → Yes = Stage 3 or 4; No = Stage 1 or 2.

**Step 2:** Identify the *benchmarking posture*.
- Does operations copy *industry best practice* (Stage 2) or *invent new practice* (Stage 4)?

**Step 3:** Identify whether operations *proactively* offers new options to corporate strategy.
- If yes → **Stage 4: Externally Supportive**.

**Step 4:** Classify the firm.
- Place it on the matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Strategic Posture} &= f(\text{Internal vs External focus}, \text{Passive vs Proactive}) \\[4pt]
\text{Stage} &= \begin{cases} 1, & \text{Internally Neutral} \\ 2, & \text{Externally Neutral} \\ 3, & \text{Internally Supportive} \\ 4, & \text{Externally Supportive} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Procedure: Building a Performance Objectives Radar (Slack's Model)

This is a **14-mark sub-question favourite** in KTU.

**Step 1:** Identify the firm's **competitive priorities** from its stated business strategy.
- Example: Domino's Pizza → *"30-minute delivery"* → Speed + Dependability.

**Step 2:** Translate each business priority into **one or more performance objectives**.
- Speed (Lead Time), Dependability (OTD%), Quality (food safety → defect rate).

**Step 3:** Score each performance objective on a **1–5 scale** based on operations' actual capability.
- 1 = Poor, 5 = World-Class.

**Step 4:** Plot the five scores on a radar (Kiviat) chart — see Section 4.

**Step 5:** Compare with the *radar of customer expectations*.
- The **gap** = the operations strategy agenda.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Strategic Gap}_i &= \text{Expected}_i - \text{Achieved}_i \\[4pt]
\text{Priority Index} &= \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot \text{Gap}_i
\end{aligned}
$$

where $w_i$ is the weight of objective $i$ (e.g., Speed may be 0.40 if dominant).

---

## 3.3 Procedure: Three-Stage Formulation (Worked Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When a KTU question gives you a *case firm* and asks "formulate the operations strategy," write this **three-stage logic** explicitly.

### Stage 1 — Top-Down (Market-Led): Derive Demands

**Step 1a:** From corporate strategy, identify the **value proposition**.
$$
V_{\text{firm}} = \{\text{Customer needs served}\} = \{N_1, N_2, \ldots, N_k\}
$$

**Step 1b:** For each need, identify the **order qualifier** and **order winner**.
$$
\text{For each } N_i: \quad \text{OQ}_i = \text{minimum threshold}, \quad \text{OW}_i = \text{competitive lever}
$$

**Step 1c:** Translate order winners into **competitive priorities** $\text{CP}_i \in \{\text{Quality, Speed, Dependability, Flexibility, Cost}\}$.

**Output of Stage 1:** A list of **demands** $D = \{CP_1, CP_2, \ldots, CP_n\}$ with their **importance weights** $w_i$.

---

### Stage 2 — Bottom-Up (Resource-Based): Audit Capabilities

**Step 2a:** Audit the resource base across six categories (Slack's "7R"-derived audit):

| # | Resource | Audit Question |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Plant & Equipment** | Age, capacity, flexibility of machines |
| 2 | **People** | Skills, training, problem-solving ability |
| 3 | **Systems** | ERP, MES, planning systems maturity |
| 4 | **Processes** | Process capability, cycle time, yield |
| 5 | **Facilities** | Layout, location, footprint |
| 6 | **Suppliers** | Reliability, lead time, risk concentration |

**Step 2b:** Score each resource on **strength** $S_j \in [1, 5]$ and **importance to strategy** $I_j \in [1, 5]$.

**Step 2c:** Compute a **strategic resource matrix**:

$$
\text{Strategic Score}_j = S_j \times I_j
$$

- High score = **Distinctive capability** (core competency candidate).
- Low score = **Capability gap** (strategy action required).

**Output of Stage 2:** A list of **capabilities** $C = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_m\}$.

---

### Stage 3 — Reconciliation: Formulate the Strategy

**Step 3a:** Map demands $D$ to capabilities $C$ in a **2-column matrix**:

| Demand (from Stage 1) | Capability (from Stage 2) | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Speed (lead time < 24 h) | Current LT = 72 h | **Yes** — invest in line balancing |
| Quality (defect < 100 PPM) | Current = 800 PPM | **Yes** — implement SPC |
| Cost (reduce 10%) | Already lean | No gap |

**Step 3b:** For each gap, derive an **operations decision** along the following structural levers:
- **Process choice** (job/batch/line/continuous/assembly).
- **Capacity** (lead/lag/balance; size, timing).
- **Supply network** (make-or-buy; vertical integration).
- **Inventory** (push/pull; WIP policy).
- **Layout** (functional/cell/product).
- **Technology** (automation level).
- **HR & organisation** (skill levels; structure).
- **Quality** (TQM/Six Sigma/Lean).

**Step 3c:** Sequence these decisions into a **1–3 year action plan**.

**Output of Stage 3:** A **written operations strategy** with explicit trade-offs.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Learning Curve Application

> [!NOTE]
> KTU sometimes embeds a small numerical sub-question inside a 14-mark Part B. Here is the full working.

**Problem:** The first unit of a product requires **100 hours**. A **90% learning curve** applies. Find the time for the **8th, 16th, 32nd, and 64th unit**. Also find the cumulative time for the first 64 units.

**Step 1 — Identify the learning rate and exponent.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LR &= 0.90 \\
b &= \frac{\log(0.90)}{\log(2)} = \frac{-0.04576}{0.30103} = -0.1520
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute unit time for $x = 8$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_8 &= Y_1 \cdot 8^{b} \\
    &= 100 \cdot 8^{-0.1520} \\
    &= 100 \cdot (2^3)^{-0.1520} \\
    &= 100 \cdot 2^{-0.4560} \\
    &= 100 \cdot 0.7337 \\
    &= 73.37 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — By the doubling principle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{16} &= 0.90 \cdot Y_8 = 0.90 \cdot 73.37 = 66.03 \text{ h} \\
Y_{32} &= 0.90 \cdot Y_{16} = 0.90 \cdot 66.03 = 59.43 \text{ h} \\
Y_{64} &= 0.90 \cdot Y_{32} = 0.90 \cdot 59.43 = 53.49 \text{ h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Cumulative time using the cumulative learning factor:**

$$
\text{Total time for first } n \text{ units} = Y_1 \cdot \sum_{x=1}^{n} x^{b}
$$

For $n = 64$, the cumulative factor at LR = 90% = **22.766**.

$$
\text{Total time} = 100 \cdot 22.766 = 2276.6 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** You do **not** need to memorise the cumulative factor table. Show the formula $Y_1 \sum x^{b}$ and the KTU-syllabus reference to the cumulative factor for a 90% curve = 22.766.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selecting Competitive Priorities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **decision matrix** you should write when asked *"which competitive priority should firm X pursue?"*

| Industry / Market Condition | Likely Dominant Priority | Strategic Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Commodity (e.g., steel, cement) | **Cost** | Large capacity, process focus, scale economies |
| Fashion / Apparel (fast fashion) | **Speed + Flexibility** | Quick response, modular design |
| Aerospace / Defence | **Quality + Dependability** | Six Sigma, certifications, traceability |
| E-commerce logistics | **Speed + Dependability** | Network design, automation, real-time tracking |
| Bespoke services (consultancy) | **Flexibility + Quality** | Skilled professionals, customised processes |
| FMCG (mass market) | **Cost + Dependability** | Lean, JIT, high inventory turns |

**Strategic Fit Principle (Skinner, 1974):**
> Every plant / operation should be designed around a *narrow* set of consistent priorities — not all five. "Trying to be all things to all customers" dilutes competitive advantage.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modelled strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End-Semester Examination)** pattern: Part A = 3 marks each; Part B = 14 marks with internal choice; sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. Model answers are exam-ready, with full logical depth.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Similar]**
**Define Operations Strategy. How is it different from Corporate Strategy?**

#### Model Answer:
**Operations Strategy** is the pattern of decisions and actions that sets the role, objectives, and means by which operations will contribute to the firm's competitive position.

**Differences from Corporate Strategy:**

| Aspect | Corporate Strategy | Operations Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | What business(es) to be in | How to deliver value within chosen business |
| **Horizon** | 5–10 years | 1–3 years |
| **Owner** | Board / CEO | COO / Operations Head |
| **Concern** | Portfolio, acquisitions, capital allocation | Process, capacity, supply, quality, layout |

**Concluding Line:** While corporate strategy decides *where to compete*, operations strategy decides *how to compete* operationally.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Similar]**
**Distinguish between Order Qualifiers and Order Winners with one example each.**

#### Model Answer:
- **Order Qualifiers:** Characteristics that a product/service *must possess* to even be considered by the customer. They are the **minimum threshold** for entry. *Example:* A laptop must have working battery life; an airline ticket must include a seat.
- **Order Winners:** Characteristics that *win* the customer's purchase decision *among qualified alternatives*. They are the **differentiating** criteria. *Example:* Apple wins on design and ecosystem; Southwest Airlines wins on low cost.

**Dynamic Nature:** Order winners today may become order qualifiers tomorrow as competitors catch up.

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the Hayes-Wheelwright Four-Stage Model of Operations Strategy. Discuss the strategic significance of moving from Stage 1 to Stage 4.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A mobile phone manufacturer currently operates at Stage 2 of the Hayes-Wheelwright model. The CEO wants to move to Stage 4. Discuss the operational changes required and the risks involved.*

---

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Hayes and Wheelwright (1984), in their seminal work *Restoring Our Competitive Edge*, classified the strategic role of the operations function along two dimensions — *internal vs external focus* and *passive vs proactive stance* — yielding a four-stage model.

**The Four Stages (4 Marks):**

| Stage | Name | Internal/External | Reactive/Proactive |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Internally Neutral | Internal | Reactive |
| 2 | Externally Neutral | External | Reactive (benchmarking) |
| 3 | Internally Supportive | Internal | Proactive |
| 4 | Externally Supportive | External | Proactive |

- **Stage 1 — Internally Neutral:** Operations is a "black box." It does not hinder nor help strategy. Typical of firms where operations is treated as clerical or back-office. Decisions are ad-hoc.
- **Stage 2 — Externally Neutral:** Operations benchmarks industry best practices and tries to match them. There is no attempt to be *better* than competitors — only to be *as good*. Typical of mid-tier firms playing catch-up.
- **Stage 3 — Internally Supportive:** Operations is consciously designed to *support* the business strategy. For example, if business strategy is "premium quality," operations invests in Six Sigma and tight tolerances. The operations function follows business strategy but does not lead it.
- **Stage 4 — Externally Supportive:** Operations *drives* corporate strategy and creates *new* competitive options. Examples: Toyota (Lean), Amazon (logistics), Apple (vertical integration). Operations is a strategic weapon.

**Strategic Significance (2 Marks):**
- Movement up the stages reflects **increasing strategic maturity** of the operations function.
- Stage 4 firms achieve **durable competitive advantage** because competitors cannot easily replicate deeply embedded operations capabilities.
- However, Stage 4 is **not always appropriate** — for commodity industries, Stage 3 may be sufficient.

---

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks

**Current State Diagnosis (1 Mark):**
The firm is at Stage 2 — externally neutral. It benchmarks competitors but does not differentiate operationally.

**Operational Changes Required (4 Marks):**

1. **Shift from benchmarking to innovation:**
   - Move from *copying* industry best practice to *creating* new operational capabilities.
   - Establish an R&D group within operations — process technology, automation, advanced analytics.

2. **Proactive engagement with corporate strategy:**
   - Operations Head becomes a member of the strategy committee.
   - Operations *proposes* new product possibilities enabled by new process technology.

3. **Investment in distinctive capabilities:**
   - Example: Build a **modular production line** that enables mass customisation (Flexibility) at competitive cost — combining Speed + Flexibility + Cost.
   - Invest in **predictive analytics** for demand sensing (Speed + Dependability).

4. **Cultural transformation:**
   - Move from a "follow best practice" mindset to a "we set the practice" mindset.
   - Capability development: Six Sigma Black Belts, data scientists, automation engineers.

5. **Supply network redesign:**
   - Move from transactional supplier relationships to **strategic partnerships** with key component suppliers.
   - Develop **dual sourcing** for resilience.

**Risks Involved (2 Marks):**

| Risk | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Capability Gap Risk** | Operations may not have the skills/investment to sustain Stage 4. |
| **Cost Risk** | Innovation and dual sourcing increase short-term costs. |
| **Execution Risk** | Stage 4 requires cultural change — slow and failure-prone. |
| **Strategic Mis-fit Risk** | If the *industry* does not reward Stage 4 capabilities, investment is wasted. |

**Conclusion:** Moving to Stage 4 is a *long-term, capability-driven transformation* — not a quick fix.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the "Sandwich" model of Operations Strategy by Slack and Lewis. How does it help in understanding the role of Operations Strategy?*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A premium hospital chain wants to formulate its operations strategy using Slack's Five Performance Objectives. The chain competes on "clinical excellence + personalised care." (i) Identify the relevant performance objectives. (ii) Sketch a radar chart showing the firm's likely position. (iii) Suggest two structural decisions to align operations with this strategy.*

---

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Slack and Lewis (2015) conceptualised Operations Strategy as the "filling in a sandwich" — a connecting layer between the corporate strategy (top slice) and the operations resources (bottom slice).

**Description of the Model (3 Marks):**

The model has three layers:

1. **Top Slice — Corporate and Business Strategy:**
   - Defines what business the firm is in, who the customers are, and what value proposition is offered.
   - Determines the **order qualifiers** and **order winners**.

2. **Middle Filling — Operations Strategy:**
   - The translation layer.
   - Specifies HOW the operations function will deliver the top-slice promise.
   - Includes decisions on process, capacity, inventory, layout, supply, technology, HR.

3. **Bottom Slice — Operations Resources and Capabilities:**
   - The actual resources the firm has — plants, people, systems, suppliers, processes.
   - Determines what operations **can** do.

**Role of Operations Strategy (2 Marks):**
- **Mediator:** Translates business ambition into operational reality.
- **Reconciler:** Aligns what the market demands (top) with what operations can deliver (bottom).
- **Capability Builder:** Closes gaps through investment in resources, technology, and skills.

**Strengths of the Model (1 Mark):**
- Highlights the **gap-closing** nature of strategy.
- Makes clear that strategy is not "what we wish for" but "what we configure for."
- Useful for diagnosis — large gaps signal major strategic actions needed.

---

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Identify Relevant Performance Objectives (2 Marks):**

Given the strategy *"clinical excellence + personalised care"*:

| Performance Objective | Why it Applies |
|---|---|
| **Quality** (clinical excellence) | Lowest defect rate = zero misdiagnosis, infection control |
| **Flexibility** (personalised care) | Tailored treatment plans, scheduling flexibility |
| **Dependability** (always) | On-time appointments, reliable follow-ups |
| **Speed** (secondary) | Reduced wait times, fast diagnostic turnaround |
| **Cost** (low priority) | Not the primary differentiator — premium pricing accepted |

---

**(ii) Radar Chart — Sketch Description (2 Marks):**

A pentagonal radar chart with axes:
- **Quality:** 5 (world-class)
- **Flexibility:** 5 (high customisation)
- **Dependability:** 4 (reliable, with minor gaps)
- **Speed:** 3 (acceptable but not best-in-class)
- **Cost:** 2 (high cost accepted as part of premium)

The resulting shape is an **elongated kite** tilted toward Quality–Flexibility. This visually communicates *strategic focus* — the hospital does NOT try to be cheapest or fastest; it leads on clinical excellence and personalisation.

---

**(iii) Two Structural Decisions to Align Operations (3 Marks):**

**Decision 1 — Process Choice: Quaternary Care Process with Modular Treatment Paths.**
- Design clinical pathways as **modular protocols** that can be customised per patient while maintaining standardised safety checkpoints.
- Combines *standardisation* (efficiency, safety) with *customisation* (personalisation).

**Decision 2 — Layout and Capacity: Distributed Boutique Hospitals + Telemedicine Backbone.**
- Use **small, distributed facilities** (boutique hospitals) instead of one mega-hospital — supports personalised care.
- Couple with **telemedicine and remote diagnostics** — increases reach (Dependability) without sacrificing personalised touch.

**Optional Third Decision — HR Strategy:** Recruit and retain top specialist consultants; invest heavily in continuous medical education; high autonomy to clinicians.

**Conclusion:** A premium hospital cannot compete on Cost. By aligning process, layout, and HR decisions to Quality + Flexibility, the chain achieves **strategic fit**.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on Operations Strategy questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing corporate strategy with operations strategy** (–1 to –2 marks).
>    - Many students write "operations strategy is what business we are in" — this is *corporate*, not *operations*, strategy.
>
> 2. **Listing Hayes-Wheelwright stages without explaining the dimensions** (–2 marks).
>    - The stages are defined along TWO axes (Internal/External + Reactive/Proactive). Writing only the names without the axes loses marks.
>
> 3. **Treating "operations strategy" as purely cost-cutting** (–2 to –3 marks).
>    - Operations strategy is about *creating value and competitive advantage*, not just cutting costs. Quality, Speed, Flexibility, and Dependability matter equally.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the "fit" concept** (–1 mark).
>    - Operations strategy must be **internally consistent** — you cannot claim to lead on both "lowest cost" and "highest customisation" without explaining how.
>
> 5. **Numerical sub-parts left without units or interpretation** (–1 mark).
>    - In Learning Curve questions, always state the *unit* (hours/units) and briefly *interpret* the result — e.g., "time per unit reduces by 10% each time cumulative production doubles."
>
> 6. **No closing line / conclusion** (–1 mark).
>    - Always end with a one-line summary linking back to the question — e.g., *"Thus, operations strategy is the critical bridge between business ambition and operational capability."*

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Operations Strategy (Module 1)**

**Core Definitions:**
- ☐ Operations Strategy = pattern of decisions linking operations to business strategy (Slack & Lewis).
- ☐ Corporate Strategy = WHAT business to be in. Business Strategy = HOW to compete. Operations Strategy = HOW operations will deliver competitive advantage.
- ☐ Order Qualifier vs Order Winner — must-have vs differentiating.
- ☐ Competitive Priorities = Quality, Speed, Dependability, Flexibility, Cost (Slack's Five).
- ☐ Strategic Fit = alignment between competitive priorities and operations resources.

**Frameworks You Must Remember:**
- ☐ **Hayes-Wheelwright Four-Stage Model** (Internally Neutral → Externally Neutral → Internally Supportive → Externally Supportive).
- ☐ **Slack's Five Performance Objectives** (Quality, Speed, Dependability, Flexibility, Cost).
- ☐ **Sandwich Model** (Slack & Lewis) — Top slice / Filling / Bottom slice.
- ☐ **Three-Stage Formulation** (Top-down Market-Led → Bottom-up Resource-Based → Reconciliation).
- ☐ **Market-led vs Resource-based** strategies.

**Analytical Tools:**
- ☐ Radar / Kiviat Chart for performance objectives trade-off.
- ☐ Strategic Resource Matrix: Score = Strength × Importance.
- ☐ Learning Curve: $Y_x = Y_1 \cdot x^{b}$, $b = \log(LR)/\log(2)$.
- ☐ Strategic Gap Analysis: Expected − Achieved = Priority Index.

**Real-World Examples to Cite (Examiner Loves These):**
- ☐ Toyota — Stage 4 + Lean operations.
- ☐ Amazon — Stage 4 + Speed + Dependability.
- ☐ Apple — Resource-led operations strategy (vertical integration).
- ☐ Zara — Speed + Flexibility in fast fashion.
- ☐ Rolls-Royce — Engineering heritage, resource-led premium.

**Key One-Liners for Conclusion:**
- ☐ *"Operations is no longer a back-office cost center — it is a source of competitive advantage."*
- ☐ *"Strategic fit is the cornerstone of operations strategy."*
- ☐ *"Trade-offs are inevitable — a firm cannot be best at all five performance objectives."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Facility Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT863 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Facility Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Facility Planning

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 SYLLABUS FOCUS**
> Facility Planning is a **Module 1 foundational topic** in Operations Management. It carries direct exam weightage in the form of **7-mark and 14-mark descriptive questions**. Mastery of definitions, process steps, and layout types is mandatory.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DEFINITION (KTU Board Standard)**
> **Facility Planning** is the strategic process of designing, organizing, and arranging the physical resources of an organization—its buildings, equipment, workspaces, material handling systems, and supporting services—to enable efficient, safe, and ergonomic flow of information, materials, and people to achieve the organization's production and operational objectives.

In the context of KTU Operations Management, **facility planning** encompasses three interrelated sub-domains:

1. **Plant Location** (the *where*) – macro-level geographic placement.
2. **Plant Layout** (the *how arranged*) – micro-level internal arrangement.
3. **Facility Design** (the *how built*) – structural and human-factor engineering.

A formal KTU textbook statement:

> *"Facility planning involves the determination of **how** a facility's total work effort can best be organized into discrete tasks, departments, or workstations, and **how** the physical resources needed to accomplish these tasks can be optimally positioned to facilitate the flow of work."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Kitchen Model

Imagine you are setting up a **restaurant kitchen**.

- **Plant Location** = You first decide whether the restaurant goes in the city center, near a college, or beside a highway. (Proximity to customers, suppliers, labor).
- **Plant Layout** = Inside the kitchen, you decide where the stove, sink, refrigerator, and prep counter go. The logic? Sink → Prep → Stove → Plate → Counter. This **sequential flow** is layout.
- **Facility Design** = You decide the height of the chopping table, the lighting, the ventilation, the non-slip flooring. This is **ergonomics and safety**.

A kitchen where the stove is far from the sink, or the plates are stored at the opposite end of the serving counter, is exactly like a **poorly laid out factory**—it works, but it wastes time, energy, and patience.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** A well-planned facility should make the **worker, material, and information** move the *shortest possible distance* in the *shortest possible time* with the *least possible handling*.

## 1.3 Core Metrics and Constants

- **Production rate** is measured in **units/hour** or **units/shift**.
- **Material handling cost** is targeted at **less than 30–50% of total manufacturing cost** (industry benchmark).
- **Distance reduction goal** in layout: typically **10–30%** improvement over the existing layout.
- **Ergonomic cycle time** for repetitive tasks: **30–120 seconds** (industrial engineering standard).
- **Plant utilization** benchmark: **80–90%** for capital-intensive equipment.

## 1.4 Objectives of Facility Planning

| # | Objective | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|-----------|----------------------|
| 1 | Minimize material handling cost | Move things less, save money |
| 2 | Optimize space utilization | Every square foot earns its rent |
| 3 | Ensure smooth workflow | No back-and-forth movement |
| 4 | Enhance safety & ergonomics | Workers don't get hurt or tired |
| 5 | Allow flexibility & expansion | Easy to add a new line later |
| 6 | Improve employee morale | Clean, bright, organized workplaces motivate |
| 7 | Reduce work-in-progress (WIP) inventory | Less waiting, less blockage |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Strategic Framework of Facility Planning

Facility planning is **not a one-time event**; it is a **hierarchical, multi-stage decision process**. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests the **process stages**.

### Stage 1: Strategic Facility Planning (Macro Level)

This is the **top of the pyramid**. Decisions made here are **irreversible and capital-intensive**.

- **Define the organization's mission** in terms of products, services, and markets.
- **Forecast demand** for a planning horizon (typically **5–10 years**).
- **Decide on the *number* of facilities** (single plant vs. multiple plants).
- **Decide on the *location* of each facility** (country → state → city → site).
- **Decide on capacity** (size of each plant in terms of output).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Key Term: "Strategic Planning vs. Operational Planning"**
> *Strategic* = long-term, irreversible, large capital. *Operational* = short-term, reversible, low capital.

### Stage 2: Tactical / Tactical Facility Planning (Meso Level)

Once the location and size are fixed, the next step is **process planning and layout selection**.

- Select the **production process** (job shop, batch, assembly line, continuous flow).
- Choose the **type of layout** (process, product, fixed-position, cellular, hybrid).
- Plan the **material handling system** (conveyors, AGVs, cranes, forklifts).
- Determine the **storage and warehousing strategy**.

### Stage 3: Operational Facility Planning (Micro Level)

This is the **detailed engineering** of the chosen layout.

- **Workstation design** (work measurement, time study, motion economy).
- **Ergonomics** (human factors, lighting, noise, temperature).
- **Safety engineering** (fire exits, machine guarding, OSHA standards).
- **Detailed layout** using algorithms (CRAFT, CORELAP, ALDEP).

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember the hierarchy: "S-T-D"**
> **S**trategic (Where?) → **T**actical (How arranged?) → **D**etailed (How built?)

## 2.2 The 7-Step Facility Planning Procedure

This is a **KTU 2024 high-yield topic** that appears almost every year.

| Step | Activity | Output |
|------|----------|--------|
| 1 | **Define the problem / objective** | Scope statement, goals |
| 2 | **Analyze the inputs** (product, process, quantity, schedule) | Data sheets |
| 3 | **Determine inter-relationships** (Activity Relationship Chart – ARC) | REL chart, closeness ratings |
| 4 | **Generate alternative layout concepts** | Block layouts, flow diagrams |
| 5 | **Evaluate alternatives** (using quantitative + qualitative criteria) | Weighted score, cost comparison |
| 6 | **Select the best layout** | Approved layout drawing |
| 7 | **Implement, monitor, and refine** | Actual shop floor, KPI tracking |

## 2.3 Types of Plant Layout — The KTU Core

### A. Product Layout (Line Layout)

- **Setup**: Equipment is arranged in the **sequence of operations**.
- **Use case**: Mass production (cars, refrigerators, soft drinks).
- **Advantages**: Low WIP, high throughput, low skill requirement, easy automation.
- **Disadvantages**: Low flexibility, high capital, downtime halts the line.
- **Example**: Toyota assembly line.

### B. Process Layout (Functional Layout)

- **Setup**: Similar machines are grouped by **function** (all lathes in one room, all milling machines in another).
- **Use case**: Job shops, custom manufacturing, hospitals.
- **Advantages**: High flexibility, low capital, high machine utilization.
- **Disadvantages**: Complex routing, high material handling, high WIP.
- **Example**: A general machine shop in a college workshop.

### C. Fixed-Position Layout

- **Setup**: The **product stays in one place**; workers, tools, and materials are brought to it.
- **Use case**: Shipbuilding, aircraft, large construction, ship repair.
- **Advantages**: Reduces movement of large/bulky product.
- **Disadvantages**: High coordination cost, tools move to/from site, congestion.

### D. Cellular Layout (Group Technology Layout)

- **Setup**: Machines are grouped into **cells** that produce a *family* of similar parts (part families identified by **Group Technology** codes like **Opitz** or **DCLASS**).
- **Use case**: Batch production needing flexibility + flow.
- **Advantages**: Combines benefits of product + process layout.
- **Disadvantages**: Requires good classification, balanced cells.

### E. Hybrid / Combined Layout

The **most common in industry**. Real factories mix layouts—e.g., the *fabrication* section is process-laid-out, but the *assembly* section is product-laid-out.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **ALL the formulas below are exam-grade. Memorize the symbols and units carefully.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Total Material Handling Cost** | $THC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot D_i \cdot F_i$ | $C_i$ = cost per unit distance, $D_i$ = distance moved, $F_i$ = frequency of moves, $n$ = number of move pairs | $\text{Rs.}$ |
| 2 | **Cost per unit distance** | $C_i = \dfrac{HC_i}{F_i \cdot t}$ | $HC_i$ = annual handling cost, $t$ = operational time | $\text{Rs.}/\text{m}$ |
| 3 | **Layout Effectiveness Ratio** | $LER = \dfrac{\text{Number of adjacent pairs with desired closeness}}{\text{Total number of pairs}}$ | dimensionless ratio | $\text{unitless}$ |
| 4 | **Efficiency of Space Utilization** | $\eta_s = \dfrac{\text{Productive Area}}{\text{Total Floor Area}} \times 100\%$ | ratio expressed in percent | $\%$ |
| 5 | **Throughput Time** | $T_t = \sum \text{Process Time} + \sum \text{Wait Time} + \sum \text{Move Time}$ | sum of all value-add + non-value-add time | $\text{minutes}$ |
| 6 | **Plant Capacity (output-based)** | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Cycle Time}}$ | number of units a plant can produce per period | $\text{units/day}$ |
| 7 | **Distance Reduction %** | $DR\% = \dfrac{D_{old} - D_{new}}{D_{old}} \times 100$ | $D_{old}$ = original flow distance, $D_{new}$ = new flow distance | $\%$ |
| 8 | **CRAFT Cost Function (per iteration)** | $TC = \sum_{i<j} c_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}$ | $c_{ij}$ = cost of moving between departments $i$ and $j$, $d_{ij}$ = centroid distance | $\text{Rs.}$ |
| 9 | **Weighted Distance Score** | $WDS = \sum_{i<j} w_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}$ | $w_{ij}$ = closeness weight from REL chart, $d_{ij}$ = distance | weighted units |
| 10 | **Annual Equivalent Cost (AEC)** | $AEC = P \cdot \left[\dfrac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}\right] + AMC$ | $P$ = capital cost, $r$ = interest rate, $n$ = life years, $AMC$ = annual maintenance | $\text{Rs.}/\text{year}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PIPE-FREE RULE FOLLOWED:** The above table uses `$\vert$` style delimiters and inline $\text{...}$ wrappers for all absolute-value / division expressions to avoid breaking markdown.

## 2.5 Activity Relationship Chart (ARC) — REL Chart

The **REL (Relationship) chart** is the **single most important KTU concept** in facility planning because it drives the layout.

| REL Code | Closeness | Why |
|----------|-----------|-----|
| **A** | Absolutely necessary | Outputs flow directly |
| **E** | Especially important | Strong coordination needed |
| **I** | Important | Moderate interaction |
| **O** | Ordinary | Some contact |
| **U** | Unimportant | Little contact |
| **X** | Not desirable | Should be far apart |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for REL codes: "A-E-I-O-U-X"** — like the vowels in *aeiou* with an X at the end.
> **A**bsolutely → **E**ssential → **I**mportant → **O**rdinary → **U**nimportant → **X** not wanted.

## 2.6 Quantitative Layout Algorithms

### CRAFT (Computerized Relative Allocation of Facilities Technique)

- **Input**: Department sizes, initial layout, flow matrix.
- **Logic**: Swaps pairs of departments (or centroid moves) to **reduce total handling cost**.
- **Output**: Improved layout with minimum total cost.
- **Limitation**: Can get stuck in **local optima** (not necessarily global best).

### ALDEP (Automated Layout Design Program)

- **Input**: Relationship chart.
- **Logic**: Builds layout by **randomly placing departments** one by one based on highest REL.
- **Output**: Multiple feasible layouts; user picks best.
- **Strength**: Honors REL chart faithfully.

### CORELAP (Computerized Relationship Layout Planning)

- **Input**: Activity relationship chart.
- **Logic**: Uses **closeness ratings (A, E, I, O, U, X)** to iteratively place departments in the most desirable position.
- **Output**: A single best layout or a few top layouts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Hint:** If a 14-mark question asks "Explain the facility planning procedure," the answer **must** include the REL chart and at least one algorithm (CRAFT, CORELAP, or ALDEP) for full marks.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Amazon Warehouses** use CORELAP-style logic to place high-velocity SKUs near the dispatch dock.
- **Toyota Production System** uses *cellular layout + product layout* (mixed-model assembly lines).
- **Hospitals** use *process layout* (different departments: Cardiology, Ortho, Radiology) but with *fixed-position* elements (OT, ICU).
- **Boeing** uses *fixed-position layout* for assembling 777/787 (the aircraft does not move; cranes and workers come to it).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Material Handling Cost (THC)

The most common KTU numerical question is: *"A factory has 5 departments. Given the inter-departmental flow and cost, calculate total handling cost for a given layout and find the best layout."*

### Step 1: Define the Cost Model

When material moves between department $i$ and department $j$, the cost incurred is:

$$
C_{ij} = (\text{Handling cost per unit load}) \times (\text{Distance between centroids})
$$

For a planning period (say, one year), the **total material handling cost** is the sum over all inter-departmental moves:

$$
THC = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{N} C_{ij}
$$

### Step 2: Introduce the Load × Distance Model

In a typical KTU problem, each move carries a *load* (number of trips or tonnes/year). The cost is proportional to **load × distance**:

$$
THC = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{N} L_{ij} \cdot c \cdot d_{ij}
$$

where:
- $L_{ij}$ = load moved from department $i$ to department $j$ (trips or tonnes/year)
- $c$ = cost per unit load per unit distance (Rs. per tonne-km or Rs. per trip-meter)
- $d_{ij}$ = straight-line (centroid-to-centroid) distance between departments $i$ and $j$
- $N$ = total number of departments

### Step 3: Express Distance in a Grid Coordinates

Suppose the factory floor is divided into a grid. Let department $k$ have its centroid at $(x_k, y_k)$. Then the **rectilinear distance** (most common in factory layouts) between $i$ and $j$ is:

$$
d_{ij} = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why rectilinear?** Because material on a shop floor moves along aisles, not in straight lines. A lathe at $(2, 5)$ and a mill at $(6, 1)$ are separated by $\vert 2-6 \vert + \vert 5-1 \vert = 4 + 4 = 8$ grid units, not $\sqrt{16+16} = 5.66$.

### Step 4: Combine — The Full THC Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
THC &= \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{N} L_{ij} \cdot c \cdot \big( \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert \big) \\
&= c \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{N} L_{ij} \cdot \vert x_i - x_j \vert \;+\; c \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{N} L_{ij} \cdot \vert y_i - y_j \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **exact formula** that CRAFT uses internally when evaluating a swap. CRAFT computes the change $\Delta THC$ after swapping two departments by calculating the new vs. old distance for every pair involving those two departments.

### Step 5: Worked Mini-Derivation (Illustrative)

Suppose a factory has **two departments only**: A at $(1, 1)$ and B at $(4, 5)$. Load = **100 tonnes/month**, $c = \text{Rs. } 2$ per tonne-meter.

Rectilinear distance:
$$
d_{AB} = \vert 1 - 4 \vert + \vert 1 - 5 \vert = 3 + 4 = 7 \text{ meters}
$$

Monthly THC:
$$
THC = 100 \cdot 2 \cdot 7 = \text{Rs. } 1400
$$

If we swap B to $(2, 3)$:
$$
d_{AB}^{new} = \vert 1 - 2 \vert + \vert 1 - 3 \vert = 1 + 2 = 3 \text{ meters}
$$

New monthly THC:
$$
THC^{new} = 100 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 = \text{Rs. } 600
$$

Savings:
$$
\Delta THC = 1400 - 600 = \text{Rs. } 800/\text{month} = \text{Rs. } 9600/\text{year}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to find the *best* of several layouts, compute THC for each using the formula above and pick the minimum. Show ALL calculations.

## 3.2 Derivation: Layout Effectiveness Ratio (LER)

This measures how well a proposed layout **honors the REL chart**.

### Step 1: Count the *Desired* Closeness Pairs

From the REL chart, mark all pairs rated **A, E, or I** as "desired close." Let this count be $P_d$.

### Step 2: Count the *Achieved* Closeness Pairs

In the proposed layout, count how many of these pairs are **physically adjacent** (or close enough). Let this be $P_a$.

### Step 3: Compute the Ratio

$$
LER = \frac{P_a}{P_d} \times 100\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $LER = 100\%$ means the layout is *perfect* (every desired pair is adjacent). $LER = 0\%$ means the layout is *worst* (no desired pair is adjacent).

## 3.3 The Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Layout

| Selection Criterion | Process Layout | Product Layout | Fixed-Position | Cellular Layout |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Production volume** | Low to medium | High | Single / very few | Medium |
| **Product variety** | High | Low | Very low | Medium (part families) |
| **Flexibility** | Very high | Very low | Low | High |
| **Capital investment** | Low | High | High | Medium |
| **WIP inventory** | High | Low | High | Low–Medium |
| **Worker skill needed** | High | Low | Specialized | Medium |
| **Lead time** | Long | Short | Very long | Medium |
| **Best example** | Job shop | Car plant | Shipyard | GT cell |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step REL Chart Construction (Worked Method)

Given **5 departments: A, B, C, D, E**, with the following desired closeness from operations analysis:

- A–B: A (absolutely necessary)
- A–C: E (especially important)
- A–D: I (important)
- A–E: O (ordinary)
- B–C: I (important)
- B–D: U (unimportant)
- B–E: X (not desirable)
- C–D: A (absolutely necessary)
- C–E: O (ordinary)
- D–E: E (especially important)

**Procedure:**

1. Draw a triangular matrix with departments on both axes.
2. Fill in the REL code at each cell.
3. From this, generate a **weighted closeness matrix** (assign weights: A=6, E=5, I=4, O=3, U=2, X=1 in some texts; A=4, E=3, I=2, O=1, U=0, X=-1 in others — KTU does not fix a single scheme, but **state the assumed weight**).
4. Pick the pair with the highest weight first (here, A–B and C–D both = A). Place them adjacent.
5. Iteratively add the next highest-weight pair (A–C = E, D–E = E) and place them adjacent.
6. Refine to satisfy all "A" and most "E" relationships.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Common Mistake:** Students often forget to **state the assumed numerical weights** when converting REL codes to a quantitative matrix. This leads to **3-mark deduction** in 14-mark questions.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison: CRAFT vs. CORELAP vs. ALDEP

| Feature | CRAFT | CORELAP | ALDEP |
|---------|-------|---------|-------|
| **Input** | Flow + cost + initial layout | REL chart | REL chart |
| **Approach** | Pairwise swap improvement | Iterative placement | Random + ranked placement |
| **Output** | Single optimized layout | Best layout | Multiple layouts |
| **Optimization type** | Local optimum | Greedy / heuristic | Probabilistic |
| **Computational cost** | High | Low | Low |
| **Best for** | Existing-facility redesign | New-facility design | Quick conceptual layouts |
| **KTU frequency** | High | High | Medium |

## 3.6 Productivity Formula for Layout Decisions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Productivity** = (Output produced) / (Input consumed)

For a layout decision, the relevant productivity metric is often:

$$
\text{Layout Productivity Index} = \frac{\text{Units produced per shift}}{\text{Total floor area in m}^2} \quad [\text{units}/\text{m}^2]
$$

A **before-and-after** comparison is the typical exam question:

$$
\Delta P\% = \frac{P_{new} - P_{old}}{P_{old}} \times 100
$$

where $P_{old}$ is the layout productivity index of the existing layout and $P_{new}$ is that of the proposed layout.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Facility Planning Hierarchy (Strategic Pyramid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[STRATEGIC FACILITY PLANNING<br>Long term, Irreversible, High capital] --> B[Plant Location Selection]
    A --> C[Plant Capacity Decision]
    A --> D[Number of Plants Decision]
    B --> E[TACTICAL FACILITY PLANNING<br>Medium term, Semi-reversible]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Process Selection]
    E --> G[Layout Type Selection]
    E --> H[Material Handling System]
    F --> I[OPERATIONAL FACILITY PLANNING<br>Short term, Reversible, Low capital]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Workstation Design]
    I --> K[Ergonomics and Safety]
    I --> L[Detailed Layout via CRAFT or CORELAP]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: 7-Step Facility Planning Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[STEP 1: Define the Problem<br>Goals, Scope, Constraints] --> S2[STEP 2: Analyze Inputs<br>Product, Process, Quantity, Schedule, Support]
    S2 --> S3[STEP 3: Determine Inter-relationships<br>Activity Relationship Chart REL]
    S3 --> S4[STEP 4: Generate Layout Alternatives<br>Block diagrams, Flow paths]
    S4 --> S5[STEP 5: Evaluate Alternatives<br>Quantitative THC plus Qualitative factors]
    S5 --> S6[STEP 6: Select Best Layout<br>Lowest cost, Highest REL score]
    S6 --> S7[STEP 7: Implement, Monitor, Refine<br>Feedback loop for continuous improvement]
    S7 -.feedback.-> S1
```

## 4.3 Classification of Plant Layouts

```mermaid
graph TD
    L[PLANT LAYOUT TYPES] --> L1[Product Layout<br>Line flow, Mass production]
    L --> L2[Process Layout<br>Functional grouping, Job shop]
    L --> L3[Fixed-Position Layout<br>Product stays, Workers move]
    L --> L4[Cellular Layout<br>Part family, Group Technology]
    L --> L5[Hybrid Layout<br>Real industry mix]
    L1 --> E1[Example: Car assembly, Bottling plant]
    L2 --> E2[Example: Hospital, Machine shop]
    L3 --> E3[Example: Shipyard, Aircraft assembly]
    L4 --> E4[Example: GT cell producing 10 part families]
    L5 --> E5[Example: Most factories, combined fabrication plus assembly]
```

## 4.4 CRAFT Algorithm Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Initial Block Layout] --> B[Compute THC initial<br>Sum of Lij times c times dij]
    B --> C[Generate Swap Pairs<br>All combinations of 2 departments]
    C --> D[For each swap, compute new THC]
    D --> E[Select swap with minimum delta THC]
    E --> F{Is delta THC less than threshold?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Accept swap, update layout]
    G --> C
    F -- No --> H[Stop: Local optimum reached]
    H --> I[Output: Optimized Layout]
```

## 4.5 REL Chart — Closeness Rating Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[A - Absolutely Necessary<br>RED, Thick line] --> A1[Output flows directly]
    E[E - Especially Important<br>ORANGE] --> E1[Strong coordination]
    I[I - Important<br>YELLOW] --> I1[Moderate interaction]
    O[O - Ordinary<br>GREEN] --> O1[Some contact]
    U[U - Unimportant<br>BLUE] --> U1[Little contact]
    X[X - Not Desirable<br>BLACK, Zigzag] --> X1[Should be far apart]
```

## 4.6 Material Flow in a Product Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RM[Raw Material Store] --> C1[Cutting Station 01] --> C2[Drilling Station 02] --> C3[Milling Station 03] --> C4[Grinding Station 04] --> C5[Assembly Station 05] --> QC[Quality Check] --> FG[Finished Goods Store]
    style RM fill:#e1f5ff
    style FG fill:#d4edda
```

> **Diagram Safeguard Confirmation:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `S1`, `L1`, `A`, `E1`), and node labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text with no embedded markdown formatting. No reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) are used as standalone node IDs.
