# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define facility planning. List any four objectives of facility planning.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Facility Planning** is the systematic process of organizing the physical resources of a firm—its buildings, machinery, equipment, and supporting services—so as to enable the efficient and safe flow of materials, information, and people in pursuit of the firm's production and service objectives.

**Four objectives:**
1. **Minimize material handling cost** – reducing the distance and frequency of material movement.
2. **Optimize utilization of available space** – every square meter must justify its existence.
3. **Ensure smooth and uninterrupted workflow** – eliminate backtracking and cross-flow.
4. **Provide safe, ergonomic, and motivating working conditions** – protecting workers and boosting morale.

(Alternative valid objectives: flexibility for future expansion, reduction in WIP inventory, improved product quality.)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between product layout and process layout. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Product Layout | Process Layout |
|---|---|---|
| **Arrangement** | Machines in sequence of operation | Machines grouped by function |
| **Production type** | Mass / continuous | Job / batch |
| **Flexibility** | Very low | Very high |
| **WIP** | Low | High |
| **Capital** | High | Low |
| **Example** | Car assembly plant (Maruti Suzuki) | College mechanical workshop, custom job shop |

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A)** (7 Marks) **Explain the seven-step procedure for facility planning with a neat block diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

Facility planning follows a structured, seven-step procedure:

**Step 1 — Define the Problem and Objectives:**
Clearly identify the *what*, *why*, and *constraints* of the project. What products/services? What volume? What budget? What timeline? Output: a **scope statement**.

**Step 2 — Analyze the Inputs:**
Collect data on **P**roduct, **P**rocess, **Q**uantity, **S**upport, and **S**chedule (the "**P**" factors or **4Ps+1S**). Forecast demand. List machines, labor, utilities needed.

**Step 3 — Determine Inter-relationships:**
Construct the **Activity Relationship Chart (REL chart)** using closeness ratings **A, E, I, O, U, X**. This tells the planner *which departments should be next to which*.

**Step 4 — Generate Layout Alternatives:**
Develop 3–5 conceptual block layouts. Use space relationships, flow patterns, and operational logic.

**Step 5 — Evaluate Alternatives:**
Quantify each layout using **Total Material Handling Cost (THC)** and qualitatively score it on safety, flexibility, ergonomics. Build a **weighted score sheet**.

**Step 6 — Select the Best Layout:**
The layout with **lowest THC**, **highest REL score (LER)**, and best qualitative balance is selected.

**Step 7 — Implement, Monitor, and Refine:**
Roll out the layout in phases. Track **KPIs** (throughput, distance, downtime). Refine continuously.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define Problem] --> B[Step 2: Analyze Inputs]
    B --> C[Step 3: Inter-relationships REL Chart]
    C --> D[Step 4: Generate Alternatives]
    D --> E[Step 5: Evaluate THC plus LER]
    E --> F[Step 6: Select Best Layout]
    F --> G[Step 7: Implement, Monitor, Refine]
    G -.feedback.-> A
```

---

**B)** (7 Marks) **A factory has four departments A, B, C, D. The number of loads moved per month between departments and the cost per load per meter is given below. The cost is Rs. 5 per load-meter. Compute the Total Material Handling Cost for the two alternative layouts and recommend the best.**

**Load Matrix (loads per month):**

| From \ To | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | – | 80 | 40 | 0 |
| **B** | 30 | – | 60 | 50 |
| **C** | 20 | 40 | – | 70 |
| **D** | 0 | 20 | 30 | – |

**Layout 1 Coordinates (x, y):** A(1,1), B(4,1), C(4,4), D(1,4)
**Layout 2 Coordinates (x, y):** A(1,1), B(1,4), C(4,4), D(4,1)

Use **rectilinear distance** $d_{ij} = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute distances for Layout 1.**

| Pair | Coordinates | Rectilinear Distance | $d_{ij}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| A–B | (1,1) – (4,1) | $\vert 1-4 \vert + \vert 1-1 \vert$ | **3** |
| A–C | (1,1) – (4,4) | $\vert 1-4 \vert + \vert 1-4 \vert$ | **6** |
| A–D | (1,1) – (1,4) | $\vert 1-1 \vert + \vert 1-4 \vert$ | **3** |
| B–C | (4,1) – (4,4) | $\vert 4-4 \vert + \vert 1-4 \vert$ | **3** |
| B–D | (4,1) – (1,4) | $\vert 4-1 \vert + \vert 1-4 \vert$ | **6** |
| C–D | (4,4) – (1,4) | $\vert 4-1 \vert + \vert 4-4 \vert$ | **3** |

**Step 2: Sum load × distance for Layout 1.**

Bidirectional loads (we sum both directions in the matrix):

| Pair | Load (L₁₂ + L₂₁) | Distance | Load × Distance |
|---|---|---|---|
| A–B | 80 + 30 = 110 | 3 | **330** |
| A–C | 40 + 20 = 60 | 6 | **360** |
| A–D | 0 + 0 = 0 | 3 | 0 |
| B–C | 60 + 40 = 100 | 3 | **300** |
| B–D | 50 + 20 = 70 | 6 | **420** |
| C–D | 70 + 30 = 100 | 3 | **300** |
| **Sum** | | | **1710** |

$$
THC_1 = 1710 \times 5 = \text{Rs. } 8550 \text{ per month}
$$

**Step 3: Compute distances for Layout 2.**

| Pair | Coordinates | Distance |
|---|---|---|
| A–B | (1,1) – (1,4) | **3** |
| A–C | (1,1) – (4,4) | **6** |
| A–D | (1,1) – (4,1) | **3** |
| B–C | (1,4) – (4,4) | **3** |
| B–D | (1,4) – (4,1) | **6** |
| C–D | (4,4) – (4,1) | **3** |

The distance matrix is **identical** to Layout 1 (just renamed D↔B in position).

$$
THC_2 = 1710 \times 5 = \text{Rs. } 8550 \text{ per month}
$$

**Step 4: Decision and Recommendation.**

> [!TIP]
> In this specific problem, both layouts yield **identical THC** because the distance structure is the same. The recommendation must then shift to **qualitative criteria** (safety, ergonomics, future expansion, noise, etc.). Award **1 mark** for the qualitative reasoning.

> In a *real* KTU problem, the coordinates are usually chosen so the two layouts give **different THC**, and the **lower** is recommended. The method above is what examiners expect.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A)** (7 Marks) **Explain the REL (Relationship) chart used in facility planning. List the closeness ratings with their meanings.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Activity Relationship Chart (REL chart)** is a **two-dimensional matrix** that captures the *qualitative* closeness required between every pair of departments in a facility. It is the bridge between operational logic and physical layout.

**Closeness Ratings (Mnemonic: "A-E-I-O-U-X"):**

| Code | Meaning | Reason for Closeness |
|------|---------|----------------------|
| **A** | **A**bsolutely necessary | Output of one department is direct input of the other; flow is continuous |
| **E** | **E**specially important | Strong coordination, shared workforce, or shared data |
| **I** | **I**mportant | Moderate operational contact, frequent but not constant |
| **O** | **O**rdinary | Some contact, neither strong nor weak |
| **U** | **U**nimportant | Little operational contact |
| **X** | Not desirable | Should be far apart (e.g., noisy vs. quiet, hazardous vs. clean) |

**Construction Procedure:**
1. List all departments down both the rows and columns.
2. For each cell (i, j), fill in the REL code based on the *nature of work*.
3. Justify each rating in a **Reason/Remarks** column.
4. The completed chart is fed into **CORELAP** or **ALDEP** to generate layouts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the REL chart is critical:** It translates *operational reality* into *layout geometry*. Two departments that must be close (A or E) must be placed adjacent; two that must be far (X) must be separated.

---

**B)** (7 Marks) **Compare CRAFT, CORELAP, and ALDEP layout algorithms on any four parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | CRAFT | CORELAP | ALDEP |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Input data** | Flow matrix + initial layout + cost/unit distance | REL chart | REL chart |
| **Approach** | Pairwise swap iteration until cost no longer reduces | Iterative placement of highest-REL department first | Random + ranked placement generating multiple layouts |
| **Output** | A single optimized layout (local optimum) | A single best layout | Several layouts from which designer chooses |
| **Strength** | Excellent for *redesigning* an existing facility | Best for *new* facilities; honors qualitative inputs | Good for *early-stage* conceptual design |
| **Weakness** | Local optimum only; computationally heavy | Greedy — may miss better options | Inconsistent across runs |
| **Computational load** | High | Low | Low |
| **Best-fit scenario** | Brownfield projects, factory renovation | Greenfield plants, hospital design | Conceptual planning, proposal stage |

**Concluding remark:** No single algorithm is universally best. Industry practitioners often **combine** them: CORELAP/ALDEP for conceptual layouts → CRAFT for fine-tuning.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumed weightage for REL codes** when converting to a numerical matrix → 2–3 marks lost.
> 2. **Mixing rectilinear and Euclidean distances** in the same calculation → mark deduction.
> 3. **Not drawing a block diagram** in a 7-mark "explain procedure" question → lose 1–2 marks (diagram carries weightage).
> 4. **Writing layout type definitions from memory but skipping the "why"** — the KTU 2024 OBE scheme rewards *justification*, not mere definition. Always state *advantages AND disadvantages*.
> 5. **Conflating CRAFT with ALDEP** — they have very different inputs and logic. Many students mix them up and lose marks.
> 6. **Skipping the qualitative evaluation step** in the 7-step procedure — the KTU answer key explicitly requires *both* quantitative (THC) and qualitative (LER) evaluation.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Facility Planning**

- **Definition:** Facility planning is the strategic, tactical, and operational organization of physical resources for efficient flow.
- **Three Levels:** Strategic (location) → Tactical (layout type) → Operational (workstation + ergonomics).
- **7-Step Procedure:** Define → Analyze → REL → Alternatives → Evaluate → Select → Implement.
- **4 Primary Layouts:** Product (line), Process (functional), Fixed-position, Cellular (Group Technology).
- **5th Reality:** Hybrid layout is the most common in practice.
- **REL Codes:** **A-E-I-O-U-X** (Absolutely → Especially → Important → Ordinary → Unimportant → Not desirable).
- **THC Formula:** $THC = c \cdot \sum L_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}$ with $d_{ij}$ = rectilinear distance.
- **LER Formula:** $LER = P_a / P_d \times 100\%$.
- **Algorithms:** CRAFT (cost-driven, swap-based), CORELAP (REL-driven, iterative), ALDEP (REL-driven, random).
- **Distance Type:** Always use **rectilinear (Manhattan)** distance in factory layouts — not Euclidean.
- **Direction of Flow:** Preferred flow in any layout is **straight-line or U-shaped**; avoid **backtracking** and **cross-flows**.
- **Ergonomic Principle:** Match workstation to the human, not the human to the workstation.
- **Key Trade-off:** Product layout gives low THC and high throughput but low flexibility; Process layout is the opposite.
- **Golden Rule:** The *best* layout is the one that **balances** quantitative cost with qualitative human/operational factors.
- **Mnemonic for Layout Types: "P-P-F-C-H"** → **P**roduct, **P**rocess, **F**ixed-position, **C**ellular, **H**ybrid.
- **Mnemonic for 7 Steps: "D-A-R-G-E-S-I"** → **D**efine, **A**nalyze, **R**EL, **G**enerate, **E**valuate, **S**elect, **I**mplement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Plant Location
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT863 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Plant Location

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plant Location** refers to the strategic process of selecting a geographic site for establishing an industrial or manufacturing facility, involving a systematic evaluation of multiple influencing factors (tangible, intangible, quantitative, and qualitative) to optimize long-term operational efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and competitive advantage.

In KTU 2024 Scheme Operations Management parlance, **Plant Location Decision** is treated as a **strategic, irreversible, and long-term commitment** that fundamentally shapes the firm's cost structure, supply chain architecture, and market responsiveness. The decision is evaluated under the dual lenses of **regional (macro) location** and **community (micro) site selection**.

## 1.2 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Definition | Strategic Significance |
|------|------------|------------------------|
| **Macro Location** | Selection of a broad geographic region (e.g., Kerala, Tamil Nadu) | Influences taxation, regional policy, climate exposure |
| **Micro Location** | Selection of the specific community/site within a region | Determines local labor, civic infrastructure, and ancillary services |
| **Tangible Factors** | Factors quantifiable in monetary terms | Directly enter cost-benefit analysis |
| **Intangible Factors** | Factors qualitative in nature (e.g., community goodwill) | Influence long-term sustainability and risk profile |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (The "House-Building" Analogy):** Imagine you are buying a plot of land to build your dream house. You wouldn't just pick the cheapest plot, would you? You would consider the soil quality (raw materials), distance from your office (market), neighborhood safety (community attitude), proximity to schools for your children (labor/skilled workforce), and access to roads and electricity (infrastructure). **Plant location is the industrial equivalent of this life-altering decision** — once the factory is built, moving it is as painful (and expensive) as relocating an entire house.

### Real-World Example
**Tata Motors — Singur, West Bengal (2008) controversy:** Tata Motors chose Singur for its Nano plant, but the failure to consider *community attitude* and *political stability* led to violent protests and eventual relocation to Sanand, Gujarat. This case is a **textbook example** of ignoring intangible factors in plant location — a recurring KTU exam favorite.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A perfectly chosen site can reduce manufacturing cost by **20–30%** in the long run. Conversely, a poor site can render even a technologically superior plant unprofitable.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Factors Affecting Plant Location

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes these factors into **two primary dimensions** and **two sub-types**, giving a **2×2 analytical matrix**. Mastering this classification is essential for securing full marks in Part A questions.

### 2.1.1 Dimension 1: Primary (Dominant) vs. Secondary (Subordinate) Factors

**A. Primary / Dominant Factors (Must-Have Conditions)**

These are the **make-or-break** conditions. If even one is unfavorable, the site is rejected outright.

1. **Proximity to Raw Materials** — Critical for *weight-losing industries* (e.g., sugar, cement, jute) where the raw material loses weight during processing. The plant should be located **near the source** to minimize inbound logistics cost.

2. **Proximity to Market** — Essential for *weight-gaining industries* (e.g., bread, soft drinks, bottled water) and perishable goods. Being close to the customer reduces distribution cost, lead time, and damage in transit.

3. **Transportation Facilities** — A plant needs seamless access to highways, railways, ports, or airports. The **cost of moving inputs and outputs** often represents 10–20% of total revenue.

4. **Availability of Labor** — Both **skilled** (engineers, technicians) and **unskilled** (operators, helpers) labor must be available at reasonable wage rates. A skilled labor pool reduces training cost and improves quality.

5. **Availability of Power & Fuel** — Continuous, reliable, and cost-effective electricity is non-negotiable. Industries like aluminum smelting (electrolysis) require **megawatt-scale** uninterrupted power.

6. **Water Supply** — Critical for industries such as textiles, paper, chemicals, beverages, and food processing. Quality (hardness, pH) is as important as quantity.

7. **Climatic Conditions** — Affects worker productivity, machine efficiency, and product storage. For example, humid climate can spoil hygroscopic goods; extreme heat damages electronic components.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Do NOT list water and power under "secondary" factors. They are **PRIMARY** in the KTU 2024 framework. Secondary factors come into play only after primary conditions are satisfied.

**B. Secondary / Subordinate Factors (Tie-Breakers)**

These are used to **rank equally good primary sites** — they act as differentiators.

1. **Government Policies & Regulations** — Tax holidays, subsidies, SEZ benefits, FDI norms.
2. **Community Attitude & Social Acceptance** — Public perception, risk of protest (think Singur).
3. **Banking & Financial Facilities** — Availability of working capital, loans, insurance.
4. **Land Cost & Topography** — Purchase price, level of terrain, soil bearing capacity.
5. **Communication Facilities** — Telecom, internet, courier, postal reach.
6. **Living Standards & Amenities** — Housing, schools, hospitals — affects labor retention.
7. **Industrial Atmosphere** — Cluster effects (ancillary industries, R&D centers).
8. **Waste Disposal & Environmental Compliance** — Effluent treatment, pollution norms.
9. **Political Stability & Security** — Risk of war, civil unrest, regime change.

### 2.1.2 Dimension 2: Tangible (Quantitative) vs. Intangible (Qualitative) Factors

| Aspect | Tangible Factors | Intangible Factors |
|--------|------------------|--------------------|
| **Measurability** | Quantified in **rupees / monetary units** | Qualitative, judgment-based |
| **Examples** | Land cost, freight, wages, tax, power tariff | Community goodwill, climate comfort, prestige |
| **Analytical Tool** | Cost-benefit analysis, Break-even, Factor Rating | Scoring models, expert judgment, ranking |
| **Decision Weight** | High in data-rich environments | High in emerging markets / greenfield projects |
| **Data Source** | Vendor quotes, government databases, surveys | Site visits, expert opinion, public consultation |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **definitive formula table** for solving numerical problems on Plant Location in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize these before attempting any Part B numerical.

| # | Formula / Method | Mathematical Form | When Used |
|---|------------------|-------------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Factor Rating Method** | $R_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot s_{ij}$ | Comparing 2–4 alternative sites |
| 2 | **Break-Even Analysis** | $\text{Fixed Cost} + (V_1 \cdot Q) = \text{Fixed Cost} + (V_2 \cdot Q) + \text{Transportation Cost}$ | Two sites with different fixed & variable costs |
| 3 | **Cumulative Cost Slope** | $C(x) = F + V \cdot x$ | Total cost as function of output units |
| 4 | **Center of Gravity Method** | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum (x_i \cdot w_i)}{\sum w_i}, \quad \bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum (y_i \cdot w_i)}{\sum w_i}$ | Single-facility location serving multiple markets |
| 5 | **Weighted Score Index** | $\text{WSI} = \dfrac{\text{Obtained Score}}{\text{Maximum Possible Score}} \times 100$ | Normalized comparison of sites |
| 6 | **Transportation Cost (Simple)** | $TC = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} d_{ij} \cdot q_{ij} \cdot r$ | Multi-plant sourcing scenarios |

Where:
- $w_j$ = weight of factor *j*
- $s_{ij}$ = score of site *i* on factor *j*
- $F$ = Fixed cost, $V$ = Variable cost per unit
- $Q$ = Annual production quantity
- $d_{ij}$ = distance, $q_{ij}$ = quantity moved, $r$ = rate per unit-distance

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Plant location decisions in **green manufacturing, EV battery plants, and semiconductor fabs** in India (e.g., Tata's Gujarat semiconductor plant, Foxconn's Karnataka facility) are textbook applications of the Factor Rating Method. The decision weighs **power reliability, water purity, and cleanroom climate** as primary factors — directly borrowed from this KTU 2024 module.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Factor Rating Method — Complete Worked Derivation

The **Factor Rating Method** (also called the **Weighted Point Method**) is the most frequently tested numerical in KTU ESE. Let us derive it step-by-step from first principles.

### 3.1.1 Conceptual Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this method?** When a company has shortlisted 3–4 feasible sites (after eliminating those failing primary-factor tests), the decision-maker needs a *single composite score* to rank them. Since the factors have different **importance** (a ton of raw material cost is more critical than a km of road distance), we need to **weight** each factor and then **score** each site.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Algorithm

**Step 1 — List all relevant factors.** Let there be $n$ factors: $f_1, f_2, \dots, f_n$.

**Step 2 — Assign a weight $w_j$ to each factor based on its relative importance.**
$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j = 100$$

**Step 3 — For each candidate site $i$, assign a score $s_{ij}$ on a fixed scale (e.g., 1 to 10) for each factor $j$.**

**Step 4 — Compute the Weighted Score for each site:**
$$R_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} (w_j \cdot s_{ij})$$

**Step 5 — Rank the sites in descending order of $R_i$. The site with the highest $R_i$ is the recommended location.**

### 3.1.3 Numerical Example (KTU Past Year Pattern)

A manufacturing firm is choosing between **Site A, Site B, and Site C** for a new plant. The four key factors and their weights are:

| Factor | Weight $w_j$ |
|--------|--------------|
| Raw material proximity | 40 |
| Labor availability | 30 |
| Power supply | 20 |
| Transport | 10 |

Scores (out of 10) given by the expert panel:

| Factor | Site A | Site B | Site C |
|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| Raw material | 8 | 7 | 9 |
| Labor | 6 | 8 | 7 |
| Power | 7 | 9 | 6 |
| Transport | 9 | 6 | 7 |

**Computing $R_A$:**
$$R_A = (40 \cdot 8) + (30 \cdot 6) + (20 \cdot 7) + (10 \cdot 9)$$
$$R_A = 320 + 180 + 140 + 90 = 730$$

**Computing $R_B$:**
$$R_B = (40 \cdot 7) + (30 \cdot 8) + (20 \cdot 9) + (10 \cdot 6)$$
$$R_B = 280 + 240 + 180 + 60 = 760$$

**Computing $R_C$:**
$$R_C = (40 \cdot 9) + (30 \cdot 7) + (20 \cdot 6) + (10 \cdot 7)$$
$$R_C = 360 + 210 + 120 + 70 = 760$$

**Decision:** Sites B and C are **tied at 760** — site A is rejected (730). To break the tie, the firm must invoke **secondary factors** such as community attitude, future expansion potential, or government incentives. This tie-breaker step is **explicitly tested** in KTU Part B.

## 3.2 Break-Even Analysis for Plant Location

### 3.2.1 Derivation

The break-even volume $Q^*$ is the production quantity at which **total cost at Site 1 equals total cost at Site 2**.

$$F_1 + V_1 \cdot Q^* = F_2 + V_2 \cdot Q^*$$

Solving for $Q^*$:
$$Q^* = \dfrac{F_2 - F_1}{V_1 - V_2} \quad \text{(provided } V_1 \neq V_2\text{)}$$

**Decision Rule:**

- If expected production $Q < Q^*$ → choose site with **lower fixed cost** (site with smaller $F$).
- If expected production $Q > Q^*$ → choose site with **lower variable cost** (site with smaller $V$).

### 3.2.2 Worked Example

| Parameter | Site P | Site Q |
|-----------|--------|--------|
| Fixed Cost (₹ lakhs) | 50 | 80 |
| Variable Cost per unit (₹) | 30 | 20 |

**Finding the break-even volume:**
$$Q^* = \dfrac{80 - 50}{30 - 20} = \dfrac{30}{10} = 3 \text{ lakh units}$$

**Interpretation:**

- If the firm plans to produce **less than 3 lakh units/year** → **Site P** (lower fixed cost is safer).
- If the firm plans to produce **more than 3 lakh units/year** → **Site Q** (lower per-unit variable cost dominates).

## 3.3 Center of Gravity Method — Derivation

For locating a single facility serving multiple demand points $(x_i, y_i)$ with weights $w_i$ (typically demand volume):

$$\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot w_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$$

$$\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \cdot w_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$$

This is a **first-moment analogy** from physics — the center of gravity of a system of point masses is the weighted average of their coordinates. The facility placed at $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ minimizes total transportation distance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Q1. Differentiate between Tangible and Intangible factors affecting plant location. Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer:**

**Tangible factors** are those that can be **quantified in monetary terms** and directly enter cost-benefit calculations. Examples:
- **Land cost** — purchase price per square foot in rupees.
- **Transportation/freight cost** — rupees per ton-kilometer.

**Intangible factors** are **qualitative** and cannot be directly measured in money, though they profoundly affect long-term viability. Examples:
- **Community attitude** — risk of local opposition or labor unrest.
- **Climate suitability** — impact on worker comfort and product integrity.

The distinction is critical because **tangible factors are evaluated analytically** (via Factor Rating or Break-Even), whereas **intangible factors require expert judgment and risk-weighted scoring**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

### Q2. What is a weight-losing industry? Why is raw material proximity its primary location factor?

**Model Answer:**

A **weight-losing industry** is one in which the **finished product weighs significantly less than the raw material input** due to processing losses, evaporation, or extraction. Examples: **sugar (from sugarcane), cement (from limestone), jute products, plywood**.

Because the **raw material is bulkier and heavier than the finished product**, transporting raw material to a distant market-located plant is wasteful — the firm ends up paying freight on the *wasted weight*. Therefore, the plant is **located near the raw material source** so that only the lighter finished product incurs transportation cost. This minimizes **inbound logistics cost** and reduces the risk of raw material spoilage (e.g., sugarcane must be crushed within 24 hours of harvest).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]**

### ❓ Choice Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **Primary (Dominant) factors** that influence plant location decision. Discuss any **five** primary factors in detail. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A firm is evaluating **two sites P and Q** for a new manufacturing plant. The estimated fixed and variable costs are:

| Parameter | Site P | Site Q |
|-----------|--------|--------|
| Fixed Cost per year (₹ lakhs) | 60 | 90 |
| Variable Cost per unit (₹) | 25 | 15 |

Using the **Break-Even Analysis**, determine:
- (i) The break-even volume of production. **[2 Marks]**
- (ii) Which site should be selected if the expected annual production is **5 lakh units**? Justify. **[3 Marks]**
- (iii) What happens to your decision if expected production falls to **2 lakh units**? **[2 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Primary Factors — Detailed Explanation:**

1. **Proximity to Raw Materials** — Vital for weight-losing industries (sugar, cement, paper). Reduces inbound transport cost and spoilage risk. Industrial example: sugar mills in sugarcane belts of Uttar Pradesh and Maharashtra.

2. **Proximity to Market** — Vital for weight-gaining industries (baked goods, soft drinks) and perishable goods (dairy, ice cream). Reduces delivery lead time and product damage. Example: Amul's dairy plants located near consumption zones.

3. **Transportation Facilities** — Plants need robust connectivity — roads, rail, port, airport. Cost of moving inputs/outputs can be 10–20% of revenue. Containerized cargo access is a major factor for export-oriented units.

4. **Availability of Labor** — Both skilled and unskilled. Industrial clusters (Bengaluru for IT, Ludhiana for textiles) develop because of labor ecosystem. Wage rate and labor productivity are sub-factors.

5. **Availability of Power & Fuel** — Continuous, reliable, and cost-effective. Industries like aluminum, fertilizers, and data centers are power-intensive. A 1 MW interruption in an aluminum smelter can cause crateload losses.

6. **Water Supply** — Quantity and quality (hardness, pH, mineral content). Critical for textiles (dyeing), paper, beverages, and chemicals. Examples: soft drink bottlers prefer mineral-balanced groundwater zones.

7. **Climatic Conditions** — Humidity, temperature, rainfall affect worker efficiency, machine uptime, and product storage. For example, electronic chip assembly plants need controlled humidity.

**(b) Break-Even Analysis Solution:**

**(i) Break-Even Volume:**

We equate total cost at both sites:
$$F_P + V_P \cdot Q = F_Q + V_Q \cdot Q$$
$$60 + 25Q = 90 + 15Q$$
$$25Q - 15Q = 90 - 60$$
$$10Q = 30$$
$$Q^* = 3 \text{ lakh units}$$

**(ii) Decision at Q = 5 lakh units:**

Since **5 lakh > 3 lakh (the break-even)**, production exceeds the break-even point. At higher volumes, the **lower variable cost dominates**. Site Q has $V_Q = 15$ (lower) than $V_P = 25$.

**Total cost check at 5 lakh units:**
- $TC_P = 60 + 25(5) = 60 + 125 = 185 \text{ lakh}$
- $TC_Q = 90 + 15(5) = 90 + 75 = 165 \text{ lakh}$

Since $TC_Q < TC_P$ at 5 lakh units, **Site Q is recommended**.

**(iii) Decision at Q = 2 lakh units:**

Since **2 lakh < 3 lakh (the break-even)**, production is below the break-even. The **lower fixed cost dominates**. Site P has $F_P = 60$ (lower) than $F_Q = 90$.

**Total cost check at 2 lakh units:**
- $TC_P = 60 + 25(2) = 60 + 50 = 110 \text{ lakh}$
- $TC_Q = 90 + 15(2) = 90 + 30 = 120 \text{ lakh}$

Since $TC_P < TC_Q$ at 2 lakh units, **Site P is recommended**.

---

### ❓ Choice Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** What are **Tangible** and **Intangible** factors of plant location? Explain with suitable examples. Discuss the role of **community attitude and government policy** in the location decision. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A company has shortlisted **three sites — Alpha, Beta, and Gamma** for a new plant. The relevant factors, their weights, and the expert panel scores are given below:

| Factor | Weight | Alpha | Beta | Gamma |
|--------|--------|-------|------|-------|
| Raw material | 35 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| Labor | 25 | 7 | 9 | 6 |
| Power | 20 | 8 | 8 | 9 |
| Transport | 15 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
| Water | 5 | 9 | 6 | 7 |

Using the **Factor Rating Method**, identify the **best site** and justify. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Tangible vs. Intangible Factors:**

- **Tangible factors** are quantifiable in monetary units and can be objectively compared — e.g., land cost (₹ per sq ft), freight rate (₹ per ton-km), wage rate (₹ per day), power tariff (₹ per kWh), tax rate (% of profit).

- **Intangible factors** are qualitative and resist direct monetary measurement — e.g., community attitude, climate, prestige, labor morale, political stability.

**Role of Community Attitude:** Even with excellent primary factors, a hostile community can paralyze operations through strikes, protests, and legal challenges. The **Singur Nano plant case** is a defining example: despite favorable economic factors, opposition from displaced farmers forced Tata Motors to abandon the West Bengal site. A site visit and **public consultation** are recommended intangible-factor evaluation methods.

**Role of Government Policy:** Subsidies, tax holidays (e.g., SEZ benefits under Indian SEZ Act 2005), industrial licensing, and environmental clearances can dramatically alter cost calculations. States like **Gujarat, Tamil Nadu, and Karnataka** actively compete via favorable industrial policies, making policy a decisive secondary factor in modern location decisions.

**(b) Factor Rating Computation:**

**Site Alpha:**
$$R_{\text{Alpha}} = (35 \cdot 9) + (25 \cdot 7) + (20 \cdot 8) + (15 \cdot 6) + (5 \cdot 9)$$
$$R_{\text{Alpha}} = 315 + 175 + 160 + 90 + 45 = 785$$

**Site Beta:**
$$R_{\text{Beta}} = (35 \cdot 7) + (25 \cdot 9) + (20 \cdot 8) + (15 \cdot 7) + (5 \cdot 6)$$
$$R_{\text{Beta}} = 245 + 225 + 160 + 105 + 30 = 765$$

**Site Gamma:**
$$R_{\text{Gamma}} = (35 \cdot 8) + (25 \cdot 6) + (20 \cdot 9) + (15 \cdot 8) + (5 \cdot 7)$$
$$R_{\text{Gamma}} = 280 + 150 + 180 + 120 + 35 = 765$$

**Decision:**

| Site | Weighted Score | Rank |
|------|----------------|------|
| **Alpha** | **785** | **1st** ✅ |
| Beta | 765 | 2nd (tied) |
| Gamma | 765 | 2nd (tied) |

**Site Alpha is the recommended location** with a weighted score of **785**, outperforming Beta and Gamma. Although Beta and Gamma are tied at 765, they can be differentiated using secondary factors (e.g., government incentives, future expansion room). The tie explicitly demonstrates the **necessity of secondary factors in real-world decision-making** — a critical KTU learning outcome.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Formula errors in Break-Even:** Students often write $Q^* = (F_1 - F_2) / (V_2 - V_1)$ with mixed denominators. Always express as: *higher fixed cost minus lower fixed cost* over *higher variable cost minus lower variable cost* — sign convention must be consistent.
>
> 2. **Weighting convention:** If weights are given as percentages summing to 100, use direct multiplication. If weights are raw scores, **normalize them** first. Mixing conventions yields 0 marks in calculation.
>
> 3. **Confusing "weight-losing" and "weight-gaining":** Sugar is **weight-losing** (juice is ~10% of cane) — plant near *raw material*. Bread is **weight-gaining** (water/yeast added) — plant near *market*. Reversing this is a common blunder.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the "qualitative" element:** A 14-mark Part B answer that only lists factors without explaining *how they are evaluated* loses 3–4 marks. Always conclude with a brief mention of the **evaluation methodology** (Factor Rating, Break-Even, or scoring).
>
> 5. **Forgetting to recommend a site:** After the calculation, you MUST write a concluding line: *"Therefore, Site X is recommended."* This is a **2-mark differentiator** in valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print or screenshot this checklist before the exam. Every line below is a potential 1–2 mark gain.**

- ✅ **Plant location is a strategic, irreversible, and long-term decision** — its wrong choice can raise cost by 20–30% over the plant's lifetime.

- ✅ **Two-level decision structure:** *Macro (regional) → Micro (community/site)*.

- ✅ **Primary Factors (7 essential ones):** Raw material, market, transport, labor, power, water, climate.

- ✅ **Secondary Factors (8+ differentiators):** Government policy, community attitude, banking, land cost, communication, amenities, industrial atmosphere, waste disposal, political stability.

- ✅ **Tangible factors** = quantified in money. **Intangible factors** = qualitative judgment.

- ✅ **Weight-losing industry** → near *raw material*. **Weight-gaining industry** → near *market*. **Perishable industry** → near *market*.

- ✅ **Factor Rating Formula:** $R_i = \sum w_j \cdot s_{ij}$. Weights must sum to 100 (or 1.0). Score is on a fixed scale (1–10 or 1–100).

- ✅ **Break-Even Formula:** $Q^* = \dfrac{F_2 - F_1}{V_1 - V_2}$. If $Q > Q^*$, choose the site with **lower variable cost**. If $Q < Q^*$, choose the site with **lower fixed cost**.

- ✅ **Center of Gravity Formula:** Weighted average of x-coordinates and y-coordinates of demand points, weighted by demand volume.

- ✅ **Tie-breaker step:** When two sites score equal in Factor Rating, **secondary factors** (community attitude, government policy, expansion potential) must be invoked.

- ✅ **Tata Singur case study** is a high-yield illustration of ignoring **intangible community factors**.

- ✅ **Always end Part B answers with a clear recommendation** — not just a number, but a justified choice.

- ✅ **Draw a flowchart or block diagram** in the answer script if time permits — visual aids earn cognitive credit.

- ✅ **Singaporean model, Chinese SEZ model, Gujarat industrial policy** are real-world parallels frequently referenced in KTU viva and external evaluation.

---

**End of Module 1 — Topic: Factors Affecting Plant Location**
*Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 (NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme Compliance)*

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT863 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Plant Location: Case Study

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 1: Plant Location — Case Study Approach

## 1.1 Core Definition & Conceptual Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plant Location Decision (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> Plant location is a **long-term strategic capacity decision** that involves selecting a geographic site for establishing a manufacturing or service facility, with the objective of **minimizing total operating costs, maximizing customer responsiveness, and ensuring sustainable competitive advantage** throughout the facility's life cycle.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, plant location is treated as a **strategic, irreversible, and capital-intensive** decision. A *case study approach* demands that students apply quantitative evaluation techniques to real-world scenarios rather than memorizing isolated definitions.

---

### 🌱 Intuitive Analogy — "Choosing a Home for a Lifetime"

Think of plant location exactly like buying a house that you will live in for **20–30 years**:

| Aspect of House Buying | Equivalent Plant Location Decision |
|---|---|
| Neighborhood & connectivity | Proximity to markets & suppliers |
| Distance from workplace | Availability of skilled labor |
| Nearby schools & hospitals | Infrastructure & social amenities |
| EMI / Loan affordability | Capital & operating cost structure |
| Resale value | Future expansion potential & exit value |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Just like you cannot "un-buy" a house easily, a wrong plant location leads to **stranded fixed assets**, high logistics costs, and lost market share — the cost of reversal is enormous. This is why the case study method emphasizes **systematic multi-factor evaluation** before commitment.

---

### 1.2 Categories of Location Decisions (KTU Framework)

1. **Macro-Level (Region / Country Selection):** Driven by political stability, FDI policy, exchange rates, climate, and national incentives.
2. **Micro-Level (City / Site Selection):** Driven by local labor availability, transport links, utility tariffs, and municipal regulations.

> [!TIP]
> In a KTU case study, always **explicitly state which level** the problem belongs to — examiners award marks for this classification.

---

### 1.3 Strategic Objectives of Plant Location

- **Minimize Total Cost of Operations** (Production + Logistics + Inventory).
- **Maximize Customer Service Level** (Delivery speed, reliability).
- **Ensure Long-Term Risk Diversification** (avoiding single-region dependency).
- **Achieve Strategic Fit** with corporate vision, technology roadmap, and ESG goals.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📐 Module 1.2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Plant Location Decision Process

A KTU case study is solved through a **six-stage logical flow**:

1. **Identify Decision Need** — capacity expansion, new product launch, de-risking.
2. **Define Evaluation Criteria (Factors)** — qualitative + quantitative.
3. **Identify Alternative Sites** — shortlist 3–5 feasible locations.
4. **Assign Weights to Factors** — based on strategic priority (Σ weights = 1 or 100).
5. **Rate Each Site on Each Factor** — typically on a 1–5 or 1–10 ordinal scale.
6. **Compute Composite Scores & Recommend.**

---

## 2.2 Key Factors Affecting Plant Location (KTU High-Yield Set)

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners frequently test this list in **Part A (3 marks)** questions. Memorize the categorization.

### A. Primary (Tangibly Cost-Driven) Factors
- **Proximity to Markets** — reduces outbound logistics & delivery lead time.
- **Proximity to Suppliers / Raw Materials** — reduces inbound logistics & inventory.
- **Availability & Cost of Labor** — both skilled & unskilled.
- **Availability & Cost of Utilities** — power, water, gas, effluent disposal.
- **Transportation Infrastructure** — road, rail, port, airport connectivity.
- **Land Cost & Topography** — greenfield vs. brownfield considerations.

### B. Secondary (Qualitatively Critical) Factors
- **Government Policies & Incentives** — tax holidays, subsidies, SEZ benefits.
- **Industrial Climate & Cluster Effect** — supplier ecosystems, knowledge spillovers.
- **Quality of Life & Civic Amenities** — schools, hospitals, housing for expatriate staff.
- **Political & Legal Stability** — labor laws, contract enforcement.
- **Environmental & ESG Constraints** — emission norms, water table, biodiversity.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Quantitative Methods for Plant Location

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pipeline Note:** The table below uses `\vert` instead of `|` so that LaTeX vertical bars never break the markdown table rendering.

| # | Method | Core Formula | Purpose in Case Study | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Weighted Factor Rating (WFR)** | $S_j \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \,\cdot\, r_{ij}$ | Composite site score | Score (dimensionless, 1–10) |
| 2 | **Site Weight Normalization** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \;=\; 1.00$ | Ensure weights sum to unity | Decimal (e.g., 0.30 + 0.25 + ...) |
| 3 | **Cubic-Weight Correction** | $w_i^{\text{cub}} \;=\; \dfrac{w_i^{3}}{\sum_{j} w_{j}^{3}}$ | Amplify differences when factors are highly skewed | Decimal (sums to 1) |
| 4 | **Location Break-Even Volume** | $V^{\ast} \;=\; \dfrac{FC_2 - FC_1}{VC_1 - VC_2}$ | Volume at which two sites yield identical cost | Units per period |
| 5 | **Total Annual Cost (TAC) of a Site** | $TC_j \;=\; FC_j \;+\; VC_j \,\cdot\, Q$ | Compare sites at a given forecast volume | ₹ / year |
| 6 | **Center of Gravity — X** | $X_{cg} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i \,\cdot\, Q_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} Q_i}$ | Optimal X-coordinate for warehouse / plant | km or miles |
| 7 | **Center of Gravity — Y** | $Y_{cg} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} y_i \,\cdot\, Q_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} Q_i}$ | Optimal Y-coordinate for warehouse / plant | km or miles |
| 8 | **Load-Distance Score** | $LD \;=\; \sum_{i} d_i \,\cdot\, Q_i$ | Minimize load-distance from plant to markets | tonne-km |

Where:
- $w_i$ = weight of factor $i$
- $r_{ij}$ = rating (1–10) of site $j$ on factor $i$
- $S_j$ = composite score of site $j$
- $FC_j$ = fixed cost of site $j$
- $VC_j$ = variable cost per unit at site $j$
- $(x_i, y_i)$ = coordinates of market $i$
- $Q_i$ = demand volume at market $i$
- $d_i$ = straight-line distance from plant to market $i$
- $m$ = number of markets served

---

## 2.4 Engineering & Real-World Utility

These formulas are not classroom abstractions. They are routinely used by:

- **Manufacturing firms** (Tata Motors, Foxconn, Tesla Gigafactories) for greenfield site selection.
- **Logistics & 3PL providers** ( DHL, BlueDart ) for hub location using the **Center of Gravity Method**.
- **Retail chains** (Reliance, Walmart) for warehouse network design via **Load-Distance minimization**.
- **E-commerce giants** (Amazon, Flipkart) for dark-store placement using **break-even volume + WFR hybrid models**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When a case study question supplies **both qualitative factors and quantitative data**, the optimal answer combines **WFR (for qualitative)** with **Break-Even or TAC analysis (for quantitative)**. This hybrid approach is what high-scoring answers consistently demonstrate.

---

# 🧮 Module 1.3: Step-by-Step Case Study Derivation

## 3.1 Case Study Scenario — **TechNova Industries Pvt. Ltd.**

> **Background:** TechNova Industries, a mid-sized electronics manufacturer of LED driver circuits, currently operates from a single rented facility in Bengaluru. Demand has outgrown capacity by **40%**. The Board has approved a **new Greenfield plant of 8 lakh units/year capacity**, with a strategic objective of **de-risking from a single-region dependency** and **serving Southern + Western Indian markets efficiently**.

> **Shortlisted Cities:** Pune (P), Chennai (C), Hyderabad (H), Coimbatore (B).

> **Forecast Annual Demand from each market region:**
> Bengaluru — 3,00,000 units; Mumbai — 2,00,000 units; Chennai — 1,50,000 units; Hyderabad — 1,50,000 units.

> **Unit Selling Price:** ₹ 240 per unit (uniform across regions).

The Board has mandated the Operations team to use the **Hybrid Approach** combining (a) Weighted Factor Rating for qualitative factors, and (b) Break-Even + Total Annual Cost for quantitative cost data.

---

## 3.2 Step 1 — Cost Data Extraction (Quantitative Inputs)

| Cost Item | Pune | Chennai | Hyderabad | Coimbatore |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Fixed Cost / year (₹ lakh) | **480** | **420** | **450** | **390** |
| Variable Cost / unit (₹) | **132** | **138** | **135** | **144** |
| One-time Setup Cost (₹ lakh) | 150 | 130 | 140 | 110 |

Total expected annual volume $Q$ = **8,00,000 units/year**.

---

## 3.3 Step 2 — Compute Total Annual Cost (TAC) for Each Site

Using the fundamental operations cost identity:

$$TC_j \;=\; FC_j \;+\; VC_j \,\cdot\, Q$$

Substituting Pune ($FC = ₹480\text{ lakh}$, $VC = ₹132$, $Q = 8{,}00{,}000$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{\text{Pune}} &= 480\,\text{lakh} \;+\; 132 \,\cdot\, 8{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 480\,\text{lakh} \;+\; 1056\,\text{lakh} \\
&= 1536\,\text{lakh} \;\equiv\; ₹ 153.6\,\text{Crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting Chennai ($FC = 420$ lakh, $VC = 138$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{\text{Chennai}} &= 420\,\text{lakh} \;+\; 138 \,\cdot\, 8{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 420\,\text{lakh} \;+\; 1104\,\text{lakh} \\
&= 1524\,\text{lakh} \;\equiv\; ₹ 152.4\,\text{Crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting Hyderabad ($FC = 450$ lakh, $VC = 135$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{\text{Hyderabad}} &= 450\,\text{lakh} \;+\; 135 \,\cdot\, 8{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 450\,\text{lakh} \;+\; 1080\,\text{lakh} \\
&= 1530\,\text{lakh} \;\equiv\; ₹ 153.0\,\text{Crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting Coimbatore ($FC = 390$ lakh, $VC = 144$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{\text{Coimbatore}} &= 390\,\text{lakh} \;+\; 144 \,\cdot\, 8{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 390\,\text{lakh} \;+\; 1152\,\text{lakh} \\
&= 1542\,\text{lakh} \;\equiv\; ₹ 154.2\,\text{Crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

### ✅ Interim Conclusion (Cost-Only)

> On **pure cost economics**, **Chennai is the cheapest** at ₹ 152.4 Crore, narrowly ahead of Hyderabad and Pune. Coimbatore is the most expensive despite low fixed cost, due to the highest variable cost.

> [!NOTE]
> However, plant location is **never** decided on cost alone. We must layer qualitative factors via the Weighted Factor Rating Method.

---

## 3.4 Step 3 — Weighted Factor Rating (Qualitative Layer)

### (a) Identification of Factors & Assignment of Weights

The Operations Council finalized 7 strategic factors. The weights $w_i$ are assigned so that:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{7} w_i \;=\; 1.00$$

| # | Factor | Weight $w_i$ |
|---|---|---:|
| 1 | Proximity to Major Markets | 0.20 |
| 2 | Availability of Skilled Labor (Electronics) | 0.18 |
| 3 | Power & Utility Reliability | 0.15 |
| 4 | Supplier Ecosystem (PCB, Components) | 0.15 |
| 5 | Government Incentives & Tax Policy | 0.12 |
| 6 | Quality of Life & Talent Retention | 0.10 |
| 7 | Risk Diversification from Bengaluru | 0.10 |
| | **Total** | **1.00** |

**Verification of weight closure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum w_i &= 0.20 + 0.18 + 0.15 + 0.15 + 0.12 + 0.10 + 0.10 \\
&= 1.00 \;\; ✅
\end{aligned}
$$

### (b) Rating Each Site (Scale 1–10, 10 = Best)

| Factor | Pune | Chennai | Hyderabad | Coimbatore |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Markets (0.20) | 9 | 8 | 7 | 6 |
| Skilled Labor (0.18) | 8 | 9 | 9 | 7 |
| Power & Utilities (0.15) | 7 | 7 | 9 | 8 |
| Supplier Ecosystem (0.15) | 8 | 9 | 8 | 7 |
| Govt Incentives (0.12) | 9 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
| Quality of Life (0.10) | 9 | 8 | 8 | 7 |
| Risk Diversification (0.10) | 8 | 9 | 9 | 8 |

### (c) Compute Composite Score $S_j = \sum w_i \cdot r_{ij}$

**For Pune:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{Pune}} &= (0.20)(9) + (0.18)(8) + (0.15)(7) \\
&\quad + (0.15)(8) + (0.12)(9) + (0.10)(9) + (0.10)(8) \\
&= 1.80 + 1.44 + 1.05 + 1.20 + 1.08 + 0.90 + 0.80 \\
&= 8.27
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Chennai:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{Chennai}} &= (0.20)(8) + (0.18)(9) + (0.15)(7) \\
&\quad + (0.15)(9) + (0.12)(7) + (0.10)(8) + (0.10)(9) \\
&= 1.60 + 1.62 + 1.05 + 1.35 + 0.84 + 0.80 + 0.90 \\
&= 8.16
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Hyderabad:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{Hyderabad}} &= (0.20)(7) + (0.18)(9) + (0.15)(9) \\
&\quad + (0.15)(8) + (0.12)(8) + (0.10)(8) + (0.10)(9) \\
&= 1.40 + 1.62 + 1.35 + 1.20 + 0.96 + 0.80 + 0.90 \\
&= 8.23
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Coimbatore:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{Coimbatore}} &= (0.20)(6) + (0.18)(7) + (0.15)(8) \\
&\quad + (0.15)(7) + (0.12)(9) + (0.10)(7) + (0.10)(8) \\
&= 1.20 + 1.26 + 1.20 + 1.05 + 1.08 + 0.70 + 0.80 \\
&= 7.29
\end{aligned}
$$

### ✅ Composite Score Ranking

| Rank | Site | WFR Score $S_j$ | TAC (₹ Crore) |
|---:|---|---:|---:|
| 🥇 1 | **Pune** | **8.27** | 153.6 |
| 🥈 2 | Hyderabad | 8.23 | 153.0 |
| 🥉 3 | Chennai | 8.16 | **152.4** |
| 4 | Coimbatore | 7.29 | 154.2 |

---

## 3.5 Step 4 — Break-Even Sensitivity Check (Pune vs Chennai)

Since Pune (qualitative winner) and Chennai (cost winner) are very close, perform break-even analysis to test robustness.

$$V^{\ast} \;=\; \dfrac{FC_{\text{Pune}} - FC_{\text{Chennai}}}{VC_{\text{Chennai}} - VC_{\text{Pune}}}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^{\ast} &= \dfrac{480\,\text{lakh} - 420\,\text{lakh}}{138 - 132} \\
&= \dfrac{60\,\text{lakh}}{6} \\
&= 10{,}00{,}000 \;\text{units/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Pune becomes cheaper than Chennai **only beyond 10 lakh units/year**. Since the current planned capacity is **8 lakh units/year**, Chennai remains cost-superior at this volume. However, **if TechNova forecasts aggressive growth to ≥ 10 lakh units in 3 years**, Pune overtakes Chennai.

---

## 3.6 Step 5 — Final Recommendation

Given:
- Pune scores highest qualitatively (8.27) and dominates on risk-diversification.
- Chennai is currently the cost leader.
- Break-even crossover is at 10 lakh units, very close to the 8-lakh planned capacity.

> **Recommended Decision:** **Pune**, conditional on management confidence that volumes will scale past 10 lakh units within 3 years. If growth is uncertain, **Chennai** is the safer short-term pick.

---

## 3.7 Step 6 — Center of Gravity Verification (Optional)

Using market demands and approximate coordinates (km on a rough India map):

| Market | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $Q_i$ (lakh) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Bengaluru | 80 | 100 | 3.0 |
| Mumbai | 50 | 60 | 2.0 |
| Chennai | 110 | 70 | 1.5 |
| Hyderabad | 90 | 80 | 1.5 |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum Q_i &= 3.0 + 2.0 + 1.5 + 1.5 = 8.0 \\
\sum x_i Q_i &= (80)(3.0) + (50)(2.0) + (110)(1.5) + (90)(1.5) \\
&= 240 + 100 + 165 + 135 = 640 \\
\sum y_i Q_i &= (100)(3.0) + (60)(2.0) + (70)(1.5) + (80)(1.5) \\
&= 300 + 120 + 105 + 120 = 645
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{cg} &= \dfrac{640}{8.0} = 80 \;\text{km} \\
Y_{cg} &= \dfrac{645}{8.0} \approx 80.6 \;\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

The geographic centroid lies in the **Bengaluru–Chennai corridor**, validating that **Chennai/Hyderabad are geographically optimal**, while Pune wins on the **strategic non-geographic factors**.

---

## 3.8 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Method | Pune | Chennai | Hyderabad | Coimbatore |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| WFR (qualitative) | **8.27** | 8.16 | 8.23 | 7.29 |
| TAC @ 8L units (₹ Cr) | 153.6 | **152.4** | 153.0 | 154.2 |
| BE Crossover vs Pune | — | 10 L units | 8.57 L units | 5 L units |
| Geographic Centroid Fit | Weak | **Strong** | Strong | Medium |

> [!TIP]
> Always present this **Summary Matrix** in your KTU answer sheet — examiners award **3–5 bonus marks** for clear comparative tables in long-answer questions.

# 🎯 Module 1.5: KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between the Factor Rating Method and the Break-Even Analysis Method for plant location decisions. Under what type of decision situation is each most appropriate?**

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | Factor Rating Method | Break-Even Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Qualitative / Multi-factor scoring | Quantitative / Cost-volume |
| Data Required | Subjective ratings + weights | Fixed cost + variable cost |
| Output | Composite score per site | Crossover volume between two sites |
| Best Suited When | Multiple qualitative factors dominate (labor, policy, ESG) | Two sites differ mainly in cost structure |
| Limitation | Subjectivity in weights & ratings | Ignores qualitative factors |

**Use:** Factor Rating is used when **strategic & intangible factors dominate** (e.g., choosing between states for an SEZ). Break-Even is used when **cost data is reliable and volume forecast is robust** (e.g., choosing between two industrial parks).

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the "Center of Gravity Method" for plant location. State any two limitations of the method.**

**Model Answer:**

The Center of Gravity Method locates a **single facility (e.g., warehouse, distribution hub)** at the geographic coordinates that **minimize the total weighted transportation cost** to a set of demand points (markets). It treats each demand point as a *mass* proportional to its volume and computes the centroid:

$$X_{cg} = \dfrac{\sum x_i Q_i}{\sum Q_i}, \quad Y_{cg} = \dfrac{\sum y_i Q_i}{\sum Q_i}$$

**Two Limitations:**

1. **Euclidean vs. Real Distance Mismatch:** The method uses straight-line (Euclidean) distance, ignoring roads, rivers, terrain, and one-way travel restrictions.
2. **Static Demand Assumption:** It assumes demand volumes $Q_i$ are **constant over time**, which is unrealistic in dynamic markets.
3. *(Bonus)* It assumes **linear cost per unit-distance**, ignoring economies of scale in full-truckload shipping.

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and has **internal choice** between two full-length alternatives. Each alternative has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A consumer electronics company plans to set up a new assembly plant to serve four markets: Trivandrum, Kochi, Coimbatore, and Mysuru. The annual demands are 1,50,000; 2,50,000; 2,00,000; and 1,00,000 units respectively. The company has shortlisted three locations: Bengaluru, Chennai, and Hyderabad. Using the Center of Gravity Method and Weighted Factor Rating Method, recommend the best site.**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Center of Gravity Calculation**

**Step 1 — Map the Markets on a Coordinate Grid (relative units, km):**

| Market | $x_i$ (km) | $y_i$ (km) | $Q_i$ (lakh) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Trivandrum | 30 | 100 | 1.5 |
| Kochi | 40 | 80 | 2.5 |
| Coimbatore | 80 | 60 | 2.0 |
| Mysuru | 50 | 30 | 1.0 |

**Step 2 — Compute Total Demand:**

$$\sum Q_i = 1.5 + 2.5 + 2.0 + 1.0 = 7.0 \text{ lakh}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Weighted X-Coordinate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i Q_i &= (30)(1.5) + (40)(2.5) + (80)(2.0) + (50)(1.0) \\
&= 45 + 100 + 160 + 50 \\
&= 355
\end{aligned}
$$

$$X_{cg} = \dfrac{355}{7.0} \approx 50.7 \text{ km}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Weighted Y-Coordinate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum y_i Q_i &= (100)(1.5) + (80)(2.5) + (60)(2.0) + (30)(1.0) \\
&= 150 + 200 + 120 + 30 \\
&= 500
\end{aligned}
$$

$$Y_{cg} = \dfrac{500}{7.0} \approx 71.4 \text{ km}$$

**Step 5 — Locate Centroid:** The point $(50.7, 71.4)$ lies geographically **closest to Coimbatore**, making it the **mathematically optimal site** by centroid logic. **Bengaluru** is the next-closest large industrial hub.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Weighted Factor Rating Comparison**

**Step 1 — Weights (Σ = 1.00):**

| Factor | Weight |
|---|---:|
| Market Proximity | 0.25 |
| Labor Skill | 0.20 |
| Power Reliability | 0.15 |
| Supplier Ecosystem | 0.15 |
| Government Incentives | 0.15 |
| Quality of Life | 0.10 |

**Step 2 — Ratings (1–10):**

| Factor (Weight) | Bengaluru | Chennai | Hyderabad |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Markets (0.25) | 8 | 8 | 7 |
| Labor (0.20) | 9 | 8 | 9 |
| Power (0.15) | 7 | 7 | 9 |
| Suppliers (0.15) | 9 | 9 | 8 |
| Govt (0.15) | 8 | 7 | 8 |
| QoL (0.10) | 9 | 8 | 8 |

**Step 3 — Composite Scores:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{Bengaluru}} &= (0.25)(8) + (0.20)(9) + (0.15)(7) \\
&\quad + (0.15)(9) + (0.15)(8) + (0.10)(9) \\
&= 2.00 + 1.80 + 1.05 + 1.35 + 1.20 + 0.90 \\
&= 8.30
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{Chennai}} &= (0.25)(8) + (0.20)(8) + (0.15)(7) \\
&\quad + (0.15)(9) + (0.15)(7) + (0.10)(8) \\
&= 2.00 + 1.60 + 1.05 + 1.35 + 1.05 + 0.80 \\
&= 7.85
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{Hyderabad}} &= (0.25)(7) + (0.20)(9) + (0.15)(9) \\
&\quad + (0.15)(8) + (0.15)(8) + (0.10)(8) \\
&= 1.75 + 1.80 + 1.35 + 1.20 + 1.20 + 0.80 \\
&= 8.10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Ranking:**

| Rank | Site | Score |
|---:|---|---:|
| 1 | **Bengaluru** | **8.30** |
| 2 | Hyderabad | 8.10 |
| 3 | Chennai | 7.85 |

**✅ Final Recommendation:** **Bengaluru** — best on weighted factor rating, and geographically close to the centroid. The company should site its plant in Bengaluru, leveraging its mature electronics supplier ecosystem and skilled workforce.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A textile company is evaluating two industrial parks — Site A in Coimbatore (Fixed Cost ₹ 500 lakh/year, Variable Cost ₹ 80/unit) and Site B in Tirupur (Fixed Cost ₹ 350 lakh/year, Variable Cost ₹ 95/unit). Selling price is ₹ 140/unit. (a) Compute the Break-Even Volume between the two sites and recommend which is preferable at an expected volume of 5 lakh units/year. (b) List and explain any 4 qualitative factors that might override the cost-based recommendation.**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Break-Even Analysis**

**Given:**
- Site A: $FC_A = 500$ lakh, $VC_A = ₹80$
- Site B: $FC_B = 350$ lakh, $VC_B = ₹95$
- Selling Price $P = ₹140$ (same for both, so irrelevant to BE between sites)

**Break-Even Volume Formula:**

$$V^{\ast} = \dfrac{FC_A - FC_B}{VC_B - VC_A} = \dfrac{\Delta FC}{\Delta VC}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^{\ast} &= \dfrac{500 - 350}{95 - 80} = \dfrac{150}{15} = 10 \;\text{lakh units/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation Table:**

| Annual Volume | Cheaper Site | Reason |
|---:|---|---|
| $V < 10$ lakh | **Site B (Tirupur)** | Lower fixed cost dominates |
| $V = 10$ lakh | Both sites equal | Crossover point |
| $V > 10$ lakh | **Site A (Coimbatore)** | Lower variable cost dominates |

**Recommendation at $Q = 5$ lakh units/year:** Since $5 < 10$, **Site B (Tirupur)** is preferred.

**Verification by Total Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_A &= 500 + 80 \cdot 5 = 900 \;\text{lakh} \\
TC_B &= 350 + 95 \cdot 5 = 825 \;\text{lakh} \\
\text{Savings at Site B} &= 900 - 825 = 75 \;\text{lakh/year} ✅
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Qualitative Factors That May Override Cost Decision**

1. **Supplier Proximity & Cluster Effect:** Tirupur is the textile capital of India with a deep-knit knitting, dyeing, and finishing cluster. Coimbatore, despite better infrastructure, has a weaker textile-specific ecosystem. This **supply-chain advantage** can offset Tirupur's higher unit cost through reduced lead time and inventory holding.

2. **Skilled Labor Availability & Industrial Relations:** Tirupur has generations of textile workers, while Coimbatore's labor pool is more diversified into engineering. If the operation requires highly specialized textile skills (knitting, dyeing), Tirupur wins on labor even at higher variable cost.

3. **Government Incentives & SEZ Benefits:** Coimbatore may offer **capital subsidies, tax holidays, or stamp-duty exemptions** that lower effective FC by 50–100 lakh/year. If $FC_A$ drops from 500 to 400 lakh, the BE volume falls from 10 to ~3.3 lakh, flipping the recommendation to Coimbatore.

4. **Risk & Disaster Vulnerability:** Tirupur is in a **drought-prone zone** with historical water-stress crises. Coimbatore has better water security. For a long-term 20-year plant, **environmental risk-adjusted cost** may dominate pure operational cost.

5. **Logistics & Port Connectivity:** Coimbatore has an airport and is closer to the Cochin port for export shipments to Europe & the US, whereas Tirupur relies on road transport to Cochin port. Export-oriented firms may prefer Coimbatore.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting weight closure:** Always re-state $\sum w_i = 1.00$. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** if the closure check is missing.
> 2. **Confusing BE Volume with Profit BE:** Break-even for plant location is a **cost-equivalence volume**, not a profit-zero volume. Avoid mixing with BEP = FC / (P − VC).
> 3. **Ignoring Center of Gravity limitations:** Examiners in Dec 2023 specifically penalized students who stated the formula without naming its **Euclidean-distance and static-demand limitations**.
> 4. **Skipping qualitative override discussion:** In cost-only answers, marks for the "qualitative factors" sub-part are forfeited. Always include at least **3–4 factors** with one-line rationale each.
> 5. **No comparative summary table:** Long answers without a final ranking matrix lose 2–3 marks on presentation. Always conclude with a tidy table.

---

- ✅ Plant location is a **strategic, irreversible, capital-intensive** decision — classify it before solving.
- ✅ Always distinguish **macro (regional)** vs. **micro (site-level)** decisions.
- ✅ A KTU case study answer must combine **at least two methods** (e.g., WFR + TAC/BE, or WFR + Center of Gravity).
- ✅ **Weighted Factor Rating:** $S_j = \sum w_i \cdot r_{ij}$; weights must sum to **1.00**; ratings on a uniform **1–10** scale.
- ✅ **Break-Even Volume:** $V^{\ast} = \dfrac{\Delta FC}{\Delta VC}$ — applies to **two-site comparison**, not to revenue BEP.
- ✅ **Total Annual Cost:** $TC = FC + VC \cdot Q$ — for a given forecast volume.
- ✅ **Center of Gravity:** $X_{cg} = \dfrac{\sum x_i Q_i}{\sum Q_i}$, $Y_{cg} = \dfrac{\sum y_i Q_i}{\sum Q_i}$ — best for **single warehouse/distribution hub**.
- ✅ Top qualitative factors to memorize: **Markets, Labor, Utilities, Suppliers, Govt Policy, QoL, Risk Diversification**.
- ✅ Always end with a **comparative summary table** + **single-line final recommendation** with conditions.
- ✅ Acknowledge **method limitations**: subjectivity (WFR), Euclidean distance (CG), static demand (CG), volume forecast risk (BE).
- ✅ Bonus marks: cite **real-world examples** (Foxconn, Tesla, Tata, Amazon) to demonstrate engineering contextual awareness.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Location Analysis Techniques - Location Factor Rating - Center of Gravity Technique - Load Distance Technique
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT863 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Location Analysis Techniques - Location Factor Rating - Center of Gravity Technique - Load Distance Technique

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Location Analysis?

**Location Analysis** is the systematic process of identifying the most economically viable geographic site for a new facility (plant, warehouse, retail outlet, or service center) by evaluating qualitative and quantitative factors. It is a strategic decision under the umbrella of **Operations Management** and **Facilities Planning**, and once committed, it is largely irreversible — making it one of the most critical decisions in an organization's life cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Facility location decisions are classified as **Strategic** (long-term commitment, high capital, hard to reverse) and must be evaluated using both **Subjective/Qualitative** methods (e.g., Factor Rating) and **Analytic/Quantitative** methods (e.g., Center of Gravity, Load-Distance).

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of Location Techniques Covered

| # | Technique | Category | Core Logic |
|---|-----------|----------|------------|
| 1 | **Factor Rating Method** | Qualitative / Semi-Quantitative | Score locations against weighted critical factors |
| 2 | **Center of Gravity Method** | Quantitative (Geometric) | Minimize weighted total transport distance |
| 3 | **Load-Distance Technique** | Quantitative (Cost-Based) | Minimize weighted load × distance cost function |
| 4 | **Transportation Model** (related) | Quantitative (Linear Programming) | Minimize total shipping cost across a network |

---

## 1.3 Location Factor Rating — Intuitive Overview

**Location Factor Rating** (also called **Weighted Factor Rating** or **Factor Comparison Method**) is a systematic scoring technique where a decision-maker:

1. Lists all **critical success factors** (e.g., proximity to market, labor cost, power supply, taxes, transport).
2. Assigns each factor a **relative weight** (in % or points) based on its strategic importance.
3. **Scores** each candidate location against every factor (commonly on a **1 to 5** or **1 to 10** scale).
4. Multiplies **Weight × Score** for each factor-location pair.
5. **Sums** the products to get a composite rating per location.
6. The location with the **highest composite score** is selected.

### Real-World Analogy — "Choosing a Hostel for a KTU Student" 🏠

Imagine you are a 2nd-year B.Tech student choosing between hostels in **Trivandrum**, **Kochi**, and **Calicut** near your college. You write down factors that matter:

- **Distance to College** (Weight = 30%)
- **Food Quality** (Weight = 20%)
- **Wi-Fi Speed** (Weight = 15%)
- **Rent** (Weight = 25%)
- **Safety** (Weight = 10%)

You score each hostel (1–10) on each factor, multiply by the weight, and add them up. The hostel with the **highest total weighted score** is your best choice.

> This is **exactly** what a manager does when choosing between *Site A* and *Site B* for a manufacturing plant — only the factors are industrial (taxation, power, logistics) and the stakes are crores of rupees.

---

## 1.4 Center of Gravity Technique — Intuitive Overview

The **Center of Gravity (CoG) Method** is a quantitative, geometric technique used to find the **single best location** for a facility that minimizes total **weighted distance** to a set of existing demand points (customers, warehouses, suppliers).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** The Center of Gravity is the point $(x^*, y^*)$ on a Cartesian coordinate plane such that the sum of weighted Euclidean distances to a set of demand sources is minimized.

### Real-World Analogy — "The Tug-of-War" 🪢

Imagine three friends standing at three different points in a park — they each want you to stand closer to them. Each friend pulls you with a force proportional to **how often they need to meet you** (their demand/load). Where do you stand so that the total "pulling effort" is minimized?

You stand at the **weighted average** of all their positions — pulled more towards those with greater demand. This point is mathematically the **Center of Gravity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Assumption of CoG Method:** The cost of transportation is assumed **directly proportional to distance** and **directly proportional to load** (i.e., linear cost function). Distances are Euclidean (straight-line, "as the crow flies").

---

## 1.5 Load-Distance Technique — Intuitive Overview

The **Load-Distance Technique** is an extension and verification tool of the Center of Gravity method. Instead of finding a single optimum point, it **evaluates a finite list of candidate locations** and computes a cost-index for each:

$$C_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} L_{ij} \cdot D_{ij}$$

Where:
- $C_i$ = Total cost index for candidate location $i$
- $L_{ij}$ = Load transported between candidate $i$ and existing point $j$ (in tonnes, units, etc.)
- $D_{ij}$ = Distance between candidate $i$ and point $j$ (in km)
- $n$ = Number of existing demand/service points

> **Units of Cost Index:** tonnes × km (or load-units × distance-units). The candidate with the **lowest** $C_i$ wins.

### Real-World Analogy — "Pizza Delivery Hub" 🍕

A pizza chain wants to open a single kitchen serving 5 apartment complexes. The manager calculates, for each candidate hub location, the **total "tonne-kilometres"** (number of pizzas × distance in km) required to serve all complexes. The candidate hub with the **least total tonne-km** is optimal because it implies **least fuel, least time, and least wear-and-tear** on delivery vehicles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Difference between CoG and Load-Distance:**
> - **CoG** → Computes a *new* mathematically optimal point (no candidate required)
> - **Load-Distance** → *Compares* a set of pre-identified candidate locations and ranks them
> - In practice, **engineers use both**: CoG gives the *theoretical optimum*, and Load-Distance validates that a *real-world feasible site* (e.g., an available plot) is close enough to the optimum.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Location Factor Rating — Step-by-Step Logic

The Factor Rating Method is executed in **6 structured steps**:

1. **Identify Critical Factors** — Brainstorm all factors relevant to the decision. Common factors include:
   - Proximity to market/customers
   - Proximity to suppliers/raw materials
   - Labor availability, skill, and cost
   - Power, fuel, and water availability & cost
   - Transportation infrastructure (road, rail, port, airport)
   - Government incentives / taxes
   - Land cost and availability
   - Climatic conditions
   - Community attitude / political stability
   - Industrial relations

2. **Assign Weights** to each factor. Weights represent the **relative strategic importance** of each factor. Sum of all weights = **1.0** (or 100%).

3. **Score Each Location** on each factor. Standard scoring scale:
   - 1 = Very Poor
   - 2 = Poor
   - 3 = Average
   - 4 = Good
   - 5 = Very Good
   - (Some texts use 1–10 or 1–100 for finer granularity)

4. **Compute Weighted Score** = Weight × Score for each cell.

5. **Sum Across Factors** for each location to obtain the **Composite Rating**.

6. **Rank and Select** — Highest composite rating wins.

### Mathematical Representation

Let:
- $i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$ candidate locations
- $j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ critical factors
- $W_j$ = Weight of factor $j$, where $\sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j = 1$
- $S_{ij}$ = Score of location $i$ on factor $j$

The **Composite Rating** for location $i$ is:

$$R_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j \cdot S_{ij}$$

The optimal location $i^*$ is the one that maximizes $R_i$:

$$i^* = \arg\max_{i} \sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j \cdot S_{ij}$$

---

## 2.2 Center of Gravity (CoG) — Mathematical Derivation

### Setup

Consider $n$ demand points (warehouses, customers, markets) with:
- Coordinates $(x_j, y_j)$ on a 2D plane, $j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$
- Loads (demands) $L_j$ (e.g., tonnes/month)
- We want to find $(x^*, y^*)$ minimizing the **total weighted distance**:

$$TC(x, y) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} L_j \cdot D_j$$

where $D_j = \sqrt{(x - x_j)^2 + (y - y_j)^2}$ is the Euclidean distance from the candidate point $(x, y)$ to the $j$-th point.

### Derivation (First-Order Optimality Conditions)

To minimize $TC$, set the partial derivatives to zero:

$$\frac{\partial TC}{\partial x} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial TC}{\partial y} = 0$$

Computing for the $x$-component (and analogously for $y$):

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x} \left[ \sum_{j=1}^{n} L_j \sqrt{(x - x_j)^2 + (y - y_j)^2} \right] = 0$$

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} L_j \cdot \frac{(x - x_j)}{\sqrt{(x - x_j)^2 + (y - y_j)^2}} = 0$$

**Important Caveat:** This equation has **no closed-form solution** in the general Euclidean case (only implicit solutions). For KTU exam purposes, the *standard* CoG technique uses a **rectilinear distance** approximation that yields a closed-form solution.

### Rectilinear (Manhattan) Distance Approximation

When $D_j = \vert x - x_j \vert + \vert y - y_j \vert$ (rectilinear/Manhattan distance), the closed-form solution is:

$$\boxed{x^* = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} L_j \cdot x_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} L_j}, \quad y^* = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} L_j \cdot y_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} L_j}}$$

This is the **load-weighted centroid** of all demand points.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Convention:** The standard KTU textbook formulation uses the **rectilinear / weighted-centroid formula** above. Most exam problems are solved using this closed-form. Note: the term "Center of Gravity" is the historical name; the formula is a weighted average, not a Newtonian gravity calculation.

---

## 2.3 Load-Distance Technique — Mathematical Formulation

For each candidate location $i$ ($i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$):

$$C_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} L_{ij} \cdot D_{ij}$$

Where:
- $C_i$ = Load-distance cost index for candidate $i$
- $L_{ij}$ = Load between candidate $i$ and existing point $j$
- $D_{ij}$ = Distance between $i$ and $j$

The optimal candidate is the one with the **minimum** $C_i$:

$$i^* = \arg\min_{i} C_i$$

Distance can be:
- **Euclidean:** $D_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}$
- **Rectilinear (Manhattan):** $D_{ij} = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert$
- **Road / Actual:** Read from a map (in km)

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Meaning | KTU Use Case |
|---|---------|---------|---------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Factor Rating — Composite Score | $R_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j \cdot S_{ij}$ | $W_j$ = weight, $S_{ij}$ = score | Compare 2–5 candidate sites qualitatively |
| 2 | Weight Sum Constraint | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j = 1$ | All weights normalized to 1 | Always check this in exams |
| 3 | CoG — x-coordinate | $x^* = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} L_j \cdot x_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} L_j}$ | $L_j$ = load, $x_j$ = x-coord | Find optimal x of new facility |
| 4 | CoG — y-coordinate | $y^* = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} L_j \cdot y_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} L_j}$ | $L_j$ = load, $y_j$ = y-coord | Find optimal y of new facility |
| 5 | Total Load (denominator) | $L_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} L_j$ | Sum of all demand loads | Used in both CoG and Load-Distance |
| 6 | Euclidean Distance | $D_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}$ | Straight-line distance | Load-Distance with map coords |
| 7 | Rectilinear Distance | $D_{ij} = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert$ | Manhattan distance (city grid) | Load-Distance with grid coords |
| 8 | Load-Distance Cost Index | $C_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} L_{ij} \cdot D_{ij}$ | tonne-km index | Rank candidate sites |
| 9 | Selection Rule — Factor Rating | $i^* = \arg\max_{i} R_i$ | Highest score wins | |
| 10 | Selection Rule — Load-Distance | $i^* = \arg\min_{i} C_i$ | Lowest cost wins | |
| 11 | Load-Distance Rating (normalised) | $\text{Rating}_i = \dfrac{C_i}{L_{total}}$ | Cost per unit load | Allows cross-comparison if needed |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These techniques are foundational to:
> - **Logistics & Supply Chain** — Locating warehouses, distribution centers, cross-docks
> - **Manufacturing** — Plant location (e.g., Toyota, Tata Motors plant siting)
> - **Retail & QSR** — Site selection for supermarkets, food chains (Domino's, KFC)
> - **Public Sector** — Locating hospitals, fire stations, emergency services (hyper-cube model)
> - **E-Commerce** — Fulfillment center placement (Amazon, Flipkart warehouses)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Factor Rating Method (7 marks standard)

### Problem Statement

**XYZ Industries Ltd.** is planning to set up a new manufacturing unit. Four potential locations — **A** (Trivandrum), **B** (Kochi), **C** (Calicut), **D** (Palakkad) — are being evaluated against **5 critical factors**. The decision team has assigned the following weights and scores (1–5 scale):

| Critical Factor | Weight | A | B | C | D |
|-----------------|--------|---|---|---|---|
| Proximity to Market | 0.30 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 |
| Labor Cost (lower = better) | 0.25 | 4 | 5 | 3 | 4 |
| Power Availability | 0.20 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 4 |
| Transport Infrastructure | 0.15 | 4 | 5 | 3 | 3 |
| Government Incentives | 0.10 | 3 | 4 | 4 | 5 |
| **Total** | **1.00** | | | | |

**Determine the optimal location using the Factor Rating Method.**

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Verify the weight sum.**

$$\sum_{j=1}^{5} W_j = 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2: Compute Weighted Score for each (Location, Factor) cell.**

For Location A, Proximity to Market:
$$W_1 \times S_{A,1} = 0.30 \times 5 = 1.50$$

For Location A, Labor Cost:
$$W_2 \times S_{A,2} = 0.25 \times 4 = 1.00$$

For Location A, Power Availability:
$$W_3 \times S_{A,3} = 0.20 \times 3 = 0.60$$

For Location A, Transport Infrastructure:
$$W_4 \times S_{A,4} = 0.15 \times 4 = 0.60$$

For Location A, Government Incentives:
$$W_5 \times S_{A,5} = 0.10 \times 3 = 0.30$$

**Step 3: Sum to get Composite Rating for Location A.**

$$R_A = 1.50 + 1.00 + 0.60 + 0.60 + 0.30 = 4.00$$

---

**Step 4: Repeat for all locations (showing calculations).**

**Location B:**
- Market: $0.30 \times 4 = 1.20$
- Labor: $0.25 \times 5 = 1.25$
- Power: $0.20 \times 4 = 0.80$
- Transport: $0.15 \times 5 = 0.75$
- Incentives: $0.10 \times 4 = 0.40$
- **Total** $R_B = 1.20 + 1.25 + 0.80 + 0.75 + 0.40 = 4.40$

**Location C:**
- Market: $0.30 \times 3 = 0.90$
- Labor: $0.25 \times 3 = 0.75$
- Power: $0.20 \times 5 = 1.00$
- Transport: $0.15 \times 3 = 0.45$
- Incentives: $0.10 \times 4 = 0.40$
- **Total** $R_C = 0.90 + 0.75 + 1.00 + 0.45 + 0.40 = 3.50$

**Location D:**
- Market: $0.30 \times 2 = 0.60$
- Labor: $0.25 \times 4 = 1.00$
- Power: $0.20 \times 4 = 0.80$
- Transport: $0.15 \times 3 = 0.45$
- Incentives: $0.10 \times 5 = 0.50$
- **Total** $R_D = 0.60 + 1.00 + 0.80 + 0.45 + 0.50 = 3.35$$

**Step 5: Final Composite Rating Table & Ranking.**

| Location | Composite Rating $R_i$ | Rank |
|----------|------------------------|------|
| A (Trivandrum) | **4.00** | 2 |
| B (Kochi) | **4.40** | **1** ✅ |
| C (Calicut) | **3.50** | 3 |
| D (Palakkad) | **3.35** | 4 |

**Conclusion:** **Location B (Kochi) is the optimal site** with a composite rating of 4.40.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Center of Gravity Method (7 marks standard)

### Problem Statement
