A logistics company wants to set up a central warehouse to serve **4 distribution centers**. The locations and monthly loads are given below. Find the **optimal location** of the warehouse using the Center of Gravity method.

| Distribution Center | x-coordinate (km) | y-coordinate (km) | Monthly Load (tonnes) |
|---------------------|-------------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| DC-1 | 5  | 10 | 1,000 |
| DC-2 | 15 | 5  | 1,500 |
| DC-3 | 10 | 20 | 2,000 |
| DC-4 | 20 | 15 | 500  |

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the weighted x-product** $L_j \cdot x_j$ for each DC.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 \cdot x_1 &= 1000 \times 5  = 5{,}000 \\
L_2 \cdot x_2 &= 1500 \times 15 = 22{,}500 \\
L_3 \cdot x_3 &= 2000 \times 10 = 20{,}000 \\
L_4 \cdot x_4 &= 500  \times 20 = 10{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the weighted y-product** $L_j \cdot y_j$ for each DC.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 \cdot y_1 &= 1000 \times 10 = 10{,}000 \\
L_2 \cdot y_2 &= 1500 \times 5  = 7{,}500 \\
L_3 \cdot y_3 &= 2000 \times 20 = 40{,}000 \\
L_4 \cdot y_4 &= 500  \times 15 = 7{,}500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Total Load (denominator).**

$$L_{total} = 1000 + 1500 + 2000 + 500 = 5000 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 4: Sum the weighted x-products (numerator for $x^*$).**

$$\sum L_j \cdot x_j = 5{,}000 + 22{,}500 + 20{,}000 + 10{,}000 = 57{,}500$$

**Step 5: Sum the weighted y-products (numerator for $y^*$).**

$$\sum L_j \cdot y_j = 10{,}000 + 7{,}500 + 40{,}000 + 7{,}500 = 65{,}000$$

**Step 6: Apply the CoG formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x^* &= \frac{\sum L_j \cdot x_j}{\sum L_j} = \frac{57{,}500}{5000} = 11.50 \text{ km} \\
y^* &= \frac{\sum L_j \cdot y_j}{\sum L_j} = \frac{65{,}000}{5000} = 13.00 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The optimal location of the new warehouse is at **(11.50 km, 13.00 km)** in the given coordinate system. The site is **pulled towards DC-3** (the highest load of 2,000 tonnes at (10, 20)), as expected.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Load-Distance Technique (7 marks standard)

### Problem Statement

Building on Example 2, the company has identified **3 feasible candidate sites** for the warehouse. Using the **Load-Distance technique**, find the best site. Use **rectilinear distance** (Manhattan distance, $D_{ij} = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert$).

| Candidate Site | x (km) | y (km) |
|----------------|--------|--------|
| S-1 | 12 | 14 |
| S-2 | 10 | 10 |
| S-3 | 14 | 12 |

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute Distance Matrix $D_{ij}$ for each (Site, DC) pair using Manhattan distance.**

**Site S-1 (12, 14):**
- $D_{S1,1} = \vert 12 - 5 \vert + \vert 14 - 10 \vert = 7 + 4 = 11$ km
- $D_{S1,2} = \vert 12 - 15 \vert + \vert 14 - 5 \vert = 3 + 9 = 12$ km
- $D_{S1,3} = \vert 12 - 10 \vert + \vert 14 - 20 \vert = 2 + 6 = 8$ km
- $D_{S1,4} = \vert 12 - 20 \vert + \vert 14 - 15 \vert = 8 + 1 = 9$ km

**Site S-2 (10, 10):**
- $D_{S2,1} = \vert 10 - 5 \vert + \vert 10 - 10 \vert = 5 + 0 = 5$ km
- $D_{S2,2} = \vert 10 - 15 \vert + \vert 10 - 5 \vert = 5 + 5 = 10$ km
- $D_{S2,3} = \vert 10 - 10 \vert + \vert 10 - 20 \vert = 0 + 10 = 10$ km
- $D_{S2,4} = \vert 10 - 20 \vert + \vert 10 - 15 \vert = 10 + 5 = 15$ km

**Site S-3 (14, 12):**
- $D_{S3,1} = \vert 14 - 5 \vert + \vert 12 - 10 \vert = 9 + 2 = 11$ km
- $D_{S3,2} = \vert 14 - 15 \vert + \vert 12 - 5 \vert = 1 + 7 = 8$ km
- $D_{S3,3} = \vert 14 - 10 \vert + \vert 12 - 20 \vert = 4 + 8 = 12$ km
- $D_{S3,4} = \vert 14 - 20 \vert + \vert 12 - 15 \vert = 6 + 3 = 9$ km

**Step 2: Compute Load-Distance Product $L_{ij} \cdot D_{ij}$ for each cell.**

**Site S-1:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{S1,1} \cdot D_{S1,1} &= 1000 \times 11 = 11{,}000 \\
L_{S1,2} \cdot D_{S1,2} &= 1500 \times 12 = 18{,}000 \\
L_{S1,3} \cdot D_{S1,3} &= 2000 \times 8  = 16{,}000 \\
L_{S1,4} \cdot D_{S1,4} &= 500  \times 9  = 4{,}500 \\
C_{S1} &= 11{,}000 + 18{,}000 + 16{,}000 + 4{,}500 = 49{,}500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Site S-2:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{S2,1} \cdot D_{S2,1} &= 1000 \times 5  = 5{,}000 \\
L_{S2,2} \cdot D_{S2,2} &= 1500 \times 10 = 15{,}000 \\
L_{S2,3} \cdot D_{S2,3} &= 2000 \times 10 = 20{,}000 \\
L_{S2,4} \cdot D_{S2,4} &= 500  \times 15 = 7{,}500 \\
C_{S2} &= 5{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 7{,}500 = 47{,}500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Site S-3:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{S3,1} \cdot D_{S3,1} &= 1000 \times 11 = 11{,}000 \\
L_{S3,2} \cdot D_{S3,2} &= 1500 \times 8  = 12{,}000 \\
L_{S3,3} \cdot D_{S3,3} &= 2000 \times 12 = 24{,}000 \\
L_{S3,4} \cdot D_{S3,4} &= 500  \times 9  = 4{,}500 \\
C_{S3} &= 11{,}000 + 12{,}000 + 24{,}000 + 4{,}500 = 51{,}500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Tabulate the Cost Indices and Select the Minimum.**

| Site | x | y | Cost Index $C_i$ (tonne-km) | Rank |
|------|---|---|------------------------------|------|
| S-1  | 12 | 14 | 49,500 | 2 |
| S-2  | 10 | 10 | **47,500** | **1** ✅ |
| S-3  | 14 | 12 | 51,500 | 3 |

**Conclusion:** **Site S-2 (10, 10)** is the optimal candidate with a load-distance cost of **47,500 tonne-km**. Note that the **CoG optimum (11.5, 13.0)** lies very close to S-2, validating S-2 as a near-optimal practical site.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Factor Rating | Center of Gravity | Load-Distance |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Type** | Qualitative / Semi-quantitative | Quantitative (Geometric) | Quantitative (Cost) |
| **Data Required** | Subjective weights + scores | Coordinates + loads | Coordinates + loads + candidate list |
| **Output** | Ranking of candidate sites | Single optimal coordinate | Ranking of candidate sites |
| **Distance Type** | Not applicable | Rectilinear (closed-form) | Euclidean or Rectilinear |
| **Complexity** | Low | Low–Medium | Medium |
| **Considers Multiple Sites** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (computes one) | ✅ Yes |
| **Subjectivity** | High | None | None |
| **Use Case** | Initial screening of many sites | Finding theoretical optimum | Validating feasible sites near optimum |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | Very High | Very High | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flow — Which Location Technique to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Facility Location Decision] --> B{Is the decision\nqualitative or\nquantitative?}
    B -- Qualitative / Strategic --> C[Factor Rating Method]
    B -- Quantitative / Cost-Based --> D{Do we have a set\nof candidate sites?}
    D -- No candidates yet --> E[Center of Gravity Method]
    D -- Yes, finite candidates --> F[Load-Distance Technique]
    E -- Gives theoretical optimum --> G[Convert to real site via\nLoad-Distance on feasible plots]
    F --> H[Compare C_i values]
    C --> I[Compare R_i scores]
    G --> H
    H --> J[Select Minimum C_i]
    I --> K[Select Maximum R_i]
    J --> L[Optimal Site Selected]
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Factor Rating Method — Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Step 1: List Critical Factors] --> stepB[Step 2: Assign Weights Wj]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Score Each Location Sij]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Compute Weighted Score Wj x Sij]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Sum Across Factors Ri]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6: Select Max Ri]
    stepB -.Verify.-> stepG[Sum Wj = 1]
```

## 4.3 Center of Gravity — Mathematical Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input[Input: Set of Demand Points j=1..n\nwith coordinates xj, yj\nand loads Lj] --> sumX[Compute Sum of Lj x xj]
    input --> sumY[Compute Sum of Lj x yj]
    input --> sumL[Compute Sum of Lj]
    sumX --> formulaX[x-star = Sum Lj xj / Sum Lj]
    sumY --> formulaY[y-star = Sum Lj x yj / Sum Lj]
    sumL --> formulaX
    sumL --> formulaY
    formulaX --> output[Optimal Coordinates x-star, y-star]
    formulaY --> output
```

## 4.4 Load-Distance Technique — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    candidates[Input: Candidate Sites i=1..m] --> distMatrix[Build Distance Matrix Dij]
    dc[Input: Existing Points j=1..n\nwith loads Lj] --> distMatrix
    candidates --> loadMatrix[Load Matrix Lij]
    dc --> loadMatrix
    distMatrix --> multiply[Element-wise Multiply Lij x Dij]
    loadMatrix --> multiply
    multiply --> sumRow[Row-wise Sum: Ci = Sum Lij x Dij]
    sumRow --> rank[Rank Candidates by Ci]
    rank --> select[Select Candidate with Minimum Ci]
```

## 4.5 Integrated Methodological Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph QUAL[Qualitative Phase]
        FR[Factor Rating]
    end
    subgraph QUANT[Quantitative Phase]
        COG[Center of Gravity]
        LD[Load-Distance]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Decision Output]
        OS[Optimal Site]
    end
    FR -->|Short-list top 2-3 sites| LD
    COG -->|Theoretical optimum point| LD
    LD -->|Compare feasible candidates| OS
    FR -.Independent.-> OS
```

> [!TIP]
> **Board-Examiner Insight:** The diagram above (4.5) is a **high-yield KTU answer structure**. If a 14-mark question asks *"Discuss the various techniques for facility location with their inter-relationships,"* this integrated framework plus brief explanations scores full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**State any three critical factors that influence plant location decisions and briefly explain how the *Factor Rating Method* evaluates them.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

Three critical factors are: **(i) Proximity to market**, **(ii) Labor cost and availability**, and **(iii) Power and fuel supply**.

The Factor Rating Method evaluates them through the following steps:

1. Each factor is assigned a **relative weight** $W_j$ based on its strategic importance (with $\sum W_j = 1$).
2. Each candidate location is **scored** $S_{ij}$ on a numerical scale (e.g., 1 to 5).
3. A **weighted score** is computed: $W_j \times S_{ij}$ for each cell.
4. The **composite rating** for each location is the sum: $R_i = \sum_j W_j \cdot S_{ij}$.
5. The location with the **highest $R_i$** is selected.

This allows decision-makers to combine **qualitative judgement** (weights) with **objective scoring** (ratings) for a structured comparison.

> **Marking Note:** Definition (1m) + Any 3 factors (1m) + 5-step process summary (1m). Total 3 marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Distinguish between the Center of Gravity method and the Load-Distance method of facility location. State one key limitation of each.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Aspect | Center of Gravity | Load-Distance Method |
|--------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Output** | Computes a *new theoretical optimal point* $(x^*, y^*)$ | *Compares* a set of pre-identified candidate sites |
| **Input** | Coordinates + loads of demand points | Coordinates of candidates + demand points + loads |
| **Selection** | Closed-form formula | Ranking by lowest $C_i$ |
| **Use** | Identifies theoretical best | Validates feasible real-world sites |

**Limitations:**
- **Center of Gravity:** Does not guarantee the computed optimum corresponds to a *practically feasible* site (it may fall in a river, on a protected forest, or on a rocky hill).
- **Load-Distance:** Requires *prior identification* of candidate sites and cannot itself generate a new candidate if the existing list is poor.

> **Marking Note:** 2 differences (2m) + 1 limitation each or both (1m). Total 3 marks.

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module Internal Choice — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

A pharmaceutical company plans to set up a new manufacturing plant. Four candidate locations — **P, Q, R, S** — are evaluated against five factors. Use the **Factor Rating Method** to determine the optimal location.

| Factor | Weight | P | Q | R | S |
|--------|--------|---|---|---|---|
| Proximity to Raw Material | 0.20 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 2 |
| Labor Availability & Cost | 0.25 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 3 |
| Power & Utilities | 0.20 | 3 | 5 | 4 | 4 |
| Market Access | 0.25 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 5 |
| Government Incentives | 0.10 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 4 |

#### Sub-part (a): Compute the composite rating for each location and rank them. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Verify weight sum** $= 0.20+0.25+0.20+0.25+0.10 = 1.00 \checkmark$

**Step 2: Compute weighted scores row-wise.**

**Location P:**
- Raw Material: $0.20 \times 4 = 0.80$
- Labor: $0.25 \times 5 = 1.25$
- Power: $0.20 \times 3 = 0.60$
- Market: $0.25 \times 4 = 1.00$
- Incentives: $0.10 \times 3 = 0.30$
- **Composite $R_P = 0.80 + 1.25 + 0.60 + 1.00 + 0.30 = 3.95$**

**Location Q:**
- Raw Material: $0.20 \times 3 = 0.60$
- Labor: $0.25 \times 4 = 1.00$
- Power: $0.20 \times 5 = 1.00$
- Market: $0.25 \times 4 = 1.00$
- Incentives: $0.10 \times 4 = 0.40$
- **Composite $R_Q = 0.60 + 1.00 + 1.00 + 1.00 + 0.40 = 4.00$**

**Location R:**
- Raw Material: $0.20 \times 5 = 1.00$
- Labor: $0.25 \times 3 = 0.75$
- Power: $0.20 \times 4 = 0.80$
- Market: $0.25 \times 4 = 1.00$
- Incentives: $0.10 \times 5 = 0.50$
- **Composite $R_R = 1.00 + 0.75 + 0.80 + 1.00 + 0.50 = 4.05$**

**Location S:**
- Raw Material: $0.20 \times 2 = 0.40$
- Labor: $0.25 \times 3 = 0.75$
- Power: $0.20 \times 4 = 0.80$
- Market: $0.25 \times 5 = 1.25$
- Incentives: $0.10 \times 4 = 0.40$
- **Composite $R_S = 0.40 + 0.75 + 0.80 + 1.25 + 0.40 = 3.60$**

**Final Ranking:**

| Location | Composite Rating $R_i$ | Rank |
|----------|------------------------|------|
| P | 3.95 | 3 |
| Q | 4.00 | 2 |
| **R** | **4.05** | **1** ✅ |
| S | 3.60 | 4 |

**Optimal location: R**

---

#### Sub-part (b): Discuss the **limitations** of the Factor Rating Method and explain how it can be **integrated** with the Center of Gravity method in a real-world plant location study. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Limitations of the Factor Rating Method (4 marks):**

1. **Subjectivity in Weights:** Weights assigned to factors depend on the judgement of decision-makers, which can introduce bias.
2. **Subjectivity in Scores:** Scoring is also subjective; different experts may assign different scores to the same location.
3. **No Cost Consideration:** The method does not directly account for actual cost differences (transportation, land, labor) between locations.
4. **No Distance/Geometry:** It does not consider the physical positioning of locations relative to markets, suppliers, and transport routes.
5. **Equal Weight of Factors:** All factors are aggregated linearly, ignoring potential interactions between factors.

**Integration with Center of Gravity Method (3 marks):**

In real-world plant location, the two methods are used **sequentially** to complement each other:

1. **Phase 1 — Factor Rating (Qualitative Shortlisting):** The Factor Rating Method is first used to evaluate a large pool of candidate regions (e.g., different states in India), shortlisting the **top 2–3 regions** based on qualitative factors such as tax benefits, government policy, labor climate, and community attitude.
2. **Phase 2 — Center of Gravity (Quantitative Optimum):** Within the shortlisted region, the Center of Gravity Method is applied using the precise geographic coordinates of all key demand points (markets, warehouses, suppliers) and their respective loads. This yields the **mathematically optimal point** for the new facility.
3. **Phase 3 — Feasibility Check:** The theoretical CoG optimum is checked against **real-world constraints** (land availability, zoning laws, road access). The nearest feasible plot is selected.
4. **Phase 4 — Final Validation:** The **Load-Distance Technique** is applied to the feasible candidate plots to verify which is closest to the theoretical optimum.

> This integrated approach combines **strategic judgement** (Factor Rating), **mathematical rigor** (CoG), and **practical feasibility** (Load-Distance) — yielding a robust, defensible, and optimal plant location decision.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

An e-commerce company wants to set up a central fulfillment center to serve **5 delivery hubs**. The data is given below. Use the **Center of Gravity method** to find the optimal location. Then, given **3 candidate sites**, use the **Load-Distance technique** to recommend the best feasible site.

| Hub | x (km) | y (km) | Daily Load (tonnes) |
|-----|--------|--------|---------------------|
| H-1 | 4  | 8  | 800  |
| H-2 | 12 | 6  | 1,200 |
| H-3 | 8  | 14 | 1,500 |
| H-4 | 18 | 10 | 600  |
| H-5 | 14 | 18 | 900  |

**Candidate Sites:** S-A (10, 12), S-B (12, 10), S-C (9, 11)

#### Sub-part (a): Find the optimal coordinates using the **Center of Gravity method**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Compute weighted products $L_j \cdot x_j$ and $L_j \cdot y_j$.**

| Hub | $L_j$ | $x_j$ | $y_j$ | $L_j \cdot x_j$ | $L_j \cdot y_j$ |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|-----------------|-----------------|
| H-1 | 800  | 4  | 8  | 3,200   | 6,400   |
| H-2 | 1,200 | 12 | 6  | 14,400  | 7,200   |
| H-3 | 1,500 | 8  | 14 | 12,000  | 21,000  |
| H-4 | 600  | 18 | 10 | 10,800  | 6,000   |
| H-5 | 900  | 14 | 18 | 12,600  | 16,200  |
| **Sum** | **5,000** | | | **53,000** | **56,800** |

**Step 2: Apply the CoG formulas.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x^* &= \frac{\sum L_j \cdot x_j}{\sum L_j} = \frac{53{,}000}{5{,}000} = 10.60 \text{ km} \\
y^* &= \frac{\sum L_j \cdot y_j}{\sum L_j} = \frac{56{,}800}{5{,}000} = 11.36 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The theoretical optimum is at **(10.60 km, 11.36 km)**.

---

#### Sub-part (b): Compute the **Load-Distance cost index** for each of the 3 candidate sites using **rectilinear distance** and recommend the best site. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Compute Distance Matrix using $D_{ij} = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert$.**

**Site S-A (10, 12):**
- $D_{A1} = \vert 10 - 4 \vert + \vert 12 - 8 \vert = 6 + 4 = 10$
- $D_{A2} = \vert 10 - 12 \vert + \vert 12 - 6 \vert = 2 + 6 = 8$
- $D_{A3} = \vert 10 - 8 \vert + \vert 12 - 14 \vert = 2 + 2 = 4$
- $D_{A4} = \vert 10 - 18 \vert + \vert 12 - 10 \vert = 8 + 2 = 10$
- $D_{A5} = \vert 10 - 14 \vert + \vert 12 - 18 \vert = 4 + 6 = 10$

**Site S-B (12, 10):**
- $D_{B1} = \vert 12 - 4 \vert + \vert 10 - 8 \vert = 8 + 2 = 10$
- $D_{B2} = \vert 12 - 12 \vert + \vert 10 - 6 \vert = 0 + 4 = 4$
- $D_{B3} = \vert 12 - 8 \vert + \vert 10 - 14 \vert = 4 + 4 = 8$
- $D_{B4} = \vert 12 - 18 \vert + \vert 10 - 10 \vert = 6 + 0 = 6$
- $D_{B5} = \vert 12 - 14 \vert + \vert 10 - 18 \vert = 2 + 8 = 10$

**Site S-C (9, 11):**
- $D_{C1} = \vert 9 - 4 \vert + \vert 11 - 8 \vert = 5 + 3 = 8$
- $D_{C2} = \vert 9 - 12 \vert + \vert 11 - 6 \vert = 3 + 5 = 8$
- $D_{C3} = \vert 9 - 8 \vert + \vert 11 - 14 \vert = 1 + 3 = 4$
- $D_{C4} = \vert 9 - 18 \vert + \vert 11 - 10 \vert = 9 + 1 = 10$
- $D_{C5} = \vert 9 - 14 \vert + \vert 11 - 18 \vert = 5 + 7 = 12$

**Step 2: Compute $L_{ij} \cdot D_{ij}$ and Sum.**

**Site S-A:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_A &= 800(10) + 1200(8) + 1500(4) + 600(10) + 900(10) \\
&= 8{,}000 + 9{,}600 + 6{,}000 + 6{,}000 + 9{,}000 = 38{,}600
\end{aligned}
$$

**Site S-B:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_B &= 800(10) + 1200(4) + 1500(8) + 600(6) + 900(10) \\
&= 8{,}000 + 4{,}800 + 12{,}000 + 3{,}600 + 9{,}000 = 37{,}400
\end{aligned}
$$

**Site S-C:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_C &= 800(8) + 1200(8) + 1500(4) + 600(10) + 900(12) \\
&= 6{,}400 + 9{,}600 + 6{,}000 + 6{,}000 + 10{,}800 = 38{,}800
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Comparison and Recommendation.**

| Site | Coordinates | Cost Index $C_i$ (tonne-km) | Rank |
|------|-------------|------------------------------|------|
| S-A  | (10, 12) | 38,600 | 2 |
| **S-B** | **(12, 10)** | **37,400** | **1** ✅ |
| S-C  | (9, 11)  | 38,800 | 3 |

**Recommendation:** **Site S-B (12, 10)** is the optimal candidate with the lowest cost index of **37,400 tonne-km**. The CoG theoretical optimum (10.60, 11.36) lies very close to all three sites, validating the practical usefulness of this candidate set.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Weight Sum Not Verified:** Always verify $\sum W_j = 1$ in Factor Rating. Examiners deduct ½ to 1 mark if the sum is wrong but you used the weights blindly.
> 2. **Confusing CoG with Geometric Centroid:** The CoG is a **load-weighted** average, not a simple average. Using unweighted averages gives the **wrong answer** and zero marks.
> 3. **Wrong Distance Formula:** Mixing Euclidean and Rectilinear in the same solution loses full credit. State the formula clearly at the start.
> 4. **Forgetting the Final Selection Sentence:** Always conclude with a bold statement: *"The optimal location is ___ with composite rating R = ___."* Examiners reward a clear decision.
> 5. **Unit Errors:** Always mention units — *tonne-km* for Load-Distance, *km* for coordinates, *points* or *%* for weights.
> 6. **Arithmetic in Distance Matrix:** Careless arithmetic in $D_{ij}$ (e.g., $\vert 12 - 15 \vert = 3$ written as $-3$ instead of $3$) cascades into wrong $C_i$ and loses all 7 marks in the sub-part.
> 7. **Not Showing Tabulation:** For 7-mark problems, present a clean **summary table** at the end — examiners award step-marks for tabulated final answers.

---

## 🚀 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Location Analysis** is a **strategic** decision — long-term, capital-intensive, and largely irreversible.
- [ ] **Factor Rating Method** is **qualitative/semi-quantitative**, using **weighted scores** $R_i = \sum W_j \cdot S_{ij}$ with $\sum W_j = 1$.
- [ ] **Higher score wins** in Factor Rating: $i^* = \arg\max_i R_i$.
- [ ] **Center of Gravity Method** is **quantitative**, computing a **single optimal point** $(x^*, y^*)$ as the **load-weighted centroid** of all demand points.
- [ ] **CoG Formulas:** $x^* = \frac{\sum L_j x_j}{\sum L_j}$ and $y^* = \frac{\sum L_j y_j}{\sum L_j}$.
- [ ] **Load-Distance Method** **ranks** pre-identified candidate sites using the cost index $C_i = \sum L_{ij} \cdot D_{ij}$.
- [ ] **Lower cost wins** in Load-Distance: $i^* = \arg\min_i C_i$.
- [ ] **Distance Types:** **Euclidean** (straight-line, $\sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$) vs. **Rectilinear/Manhattan** ($\vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert$) — state the formula used.
- [ ] **Cost Index Units:** **tonne-km** (or load-units × distance-units) — always include units.
- [ ] **Integration:** Factor Rating (qualitative shortlist) → CoG (theoretical optimum) → Load-Distance (feasible site validation) is the **gold-standard 3-phase framework** for plant location.
- [ ] **Limitations:** CoG may yield infeasible points (river, mountain); Load-Distance requires good candidate set; Factor Rating is subjective.
- [ ] **Always Verify:** Weight sum = 1; distance formula stated; arithmetic checked; final recommendation sentence written; units mentioned.
- [ ] **KTU Pattern:** Factor Rating (1 part × 7 marks) + Discussion (1 part × 7 marks) is the most common 14-mark structure. CoG + Load-Distance combined is the second most common.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacities and Facilities: Capacity Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT863 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Capacities and Facilities: Capacity Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capacity** is defined as the upper limit or maximum rate of output that a production system, facility, process, or machine is physically capable of producing in a given period of time under normal operating conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Capacity Planning** is the systematic, long-term strategic process undertaken by operations managers to determine the **aggregate production capacity** required (in terms of manpower, machinery, workspace, and supporting infrastructure) to meet the *forecasted demand* of products or services, while optimizing cost, quality, and delivery timelines.

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned Operations Management framework, capacity planning sits at the intersection of **strategic planning** and **operations execution**, forming a critical link between demand forecasting (Module 1) and aggregate/master scheduling (Module 3).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen**
>
> Imagine a small restaurant kitchen as a production system:
> - The **number of stoves, ovens, and chefs** is the *design capacity* (the maximum possible output).
> - Due to breaks, cleaning, and ingredient shortages, the kitchen realistically serves only **80%** of the theoretical maximum — this is *effective capacity*.
> - On a busy Saturday, the kitchen serves about **60 dishes/hour** — this is *actual output*.
> - The owner must decide whether to **add another stove** (capacity expansion) before the festival season — this is *capacity planning*.
>
> The *gap* between demand and capacity decides whether the restaurant makes money or loses customers. **Capacity planning is the art of sizing this kitchen perfectly.**

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Vocabulary

The following bolded metrics constitute the **canonical KTU vocabulary** for this module:

- **Design Capacity (Maximum Capacity)**: The theoretical maximum output a system can produce in a given time under ideal conditions.
- **Effective Capacity**: The maximum output achievable given realistic constraints such as product mix, scheduling, maintenance, and quality considerations. *Always less than design capacity.*
- **Actual Output**: The real, realized output of the system during a period. *Often less than effective capacity due to disruptions, absenteeism, breakdowns.*
- **Capacity Cushion**: The reserve capacity kept as a buffer against demand surges and supply disruptions. Expressed as a *percentage of capacity*.
- **Utilization**: How much of design capacity is actually being used.
- **Efficiency**: How much of effective capacity is actually being used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy of Capacity Values (KTU Standard Order):**
> **Design Capacity ≥ Effective Capacity ≥ Actual Output**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structured Theoretical Breakdown

#### A. Dimensions of Capacity

Capacity is measured along **three dimensions**, which KTU examiners frequently test:

1. **Output Quantities** — e.g., 5,000 cars/month, 10,000 tonnes/year.
2. **Input Quantities** — e.g., 200 labour-hours/day, 500 machine-hours/week.
3. **Throughput (Flow Rate)** — units produced per unit time on a continuous basis.

#### B. Determinants of Effective Capacity

The following factors jointly determine the effective capacity of any system:

- **Facility-related**: Design, layout, location, environmental conditions.
- **Product/Service-related**: Product mix, complexity, degree of customization.
- **Process-related**: Process technology, automation level, sequence of operations.
- **Human-related**: Skill level, training, motivation, absenteeism rate.
- **Policy-related**: Quality standards, safety norms, working hours, shift patterns.
- **External**: Government regulations, supplier reliability, supply chain constraints.

#### C. Types of Capacity Planning (Time Horizons)

| Horizon | Time Frame | Decision Type | Typical Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Long-Range Capacity Planning** | > 1 year | Strategic — facilities, technology | New plant, major expansion |
| **Intermediate-Range (Aggregate)** | 3–18 months | Tactical — workforce, inventory, subcontracting | Hire/fire, overtime |
| **Short-Range (Capacity Scheduling)** | < 3 months | Operational — daily/weekly scheduling | Job assignments, sequencing |

#### D. Three Capacity Strategies (Heizer & Render Framework)

- **Lead Strategy**: Capacity is added *in advance* of demand (aggressive). Avoids stockouts, builds market share, but risks idle capacity.
- **Lag Strategy**: Capacity is added *after* demand materializes (conservative). Minimizes idle capacity, but risks lost sales and customer dissatisfaction.
- **Match (Tracking) Strategy**: Capacity is added in *small increments* matching demand growth. Balanced approach — most widely used.

#### E. Steps in Capacity Planning (Sequential)

1. Estimate future capacity requirements (using forecasting techniques).
2. Identify gaps between current capacity and required capacity.
3. Develop alternative plans to fill the gaps.
4. Evaluate each alternative on financial and qualitative criteria.
5. Select and implement the best alternative.
6. Monitor performance and revise the plan.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Capacity Planning Cheat Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) numericals. Master each one.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Utilization** | $Utilization = \dfrac{Actual\ Output}{Design\ Capacity} \times 100$ | Expressed as a **percentage (%)** |
| 2 | **Efficiency** | $Efficiency = \dfrac{Actual\ Output}{Effective\ Capacity} \times 100$ | Expressed as a **percentage (%)** |
| 3 | **Capacity Cushion** | $Capacity\ Cushion = 100\% - Utilization\ Rate$ | Always in **%** of capacity |
| 4 | **Effective Capacity** (in units) | $Effective\ Capacity = Design\ Capacity \times Efficiency_{system}$ | Units of output |
| 5 | **Break-Even Point (BEP)** | $BEP_{(units)} = \dfrac{Fixed\ Cost}{(Price - Variable\ Cost)}$ | Units of product |
| 6 | **BEP in Rupees** | $BEP_{(₹)} = \dfrac{Fixed\ Cost}{1 - \frac{Variable\ Cost}{Price}}$ | Indian Rupees (₹) |
| 7 | **Contribution Margin** | $CM = Price - Variable\ Cost\ per\ unit$ | ₹ per unit |
| 8 | **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** | $EMV = \sum (Probability_i \times Payoff_i)$ | ₹ |
| 9 | **Number of Machines Required** | $N = \dfrac{Demand\ (units)}{Capacity\ per\ machine \times Utilization}$ | Whole number (round up) |
| 10 | **Worker Requirement** | $Workers = \dfrac{Standard\ Hours\ Required}{Hours\ available\ per\ worker}$ | Whole number |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: Capacity planning principles are applied in factory floor design (mechanical), hospital bed management (biomedical), data centre server sizing (CS), and construction site scheduling (civil). The **utilization–efficiency–cushion** triad forms the foundation of every OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) study in Six Sigma and Lean Manufacturing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative Relationship: Utilization vs Efficiency

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Logic**: Every capacity metric in operations management can be derived from the fundamental *Throughput Equation*. Below is the rigorous derivation linking utilization, efficiency, and capacity cushion — a frequent **Part B (7-mark)** derivation question in KTU exams.

Starting from first principles, let:

- $DC$ = Design Capacity (units/period)
- $EC$ = Effective Capacity (units/period)
- $AO$ = Actual Output (units/period)
- $U$ = Utilization (%)
- $E$ = Efficiency (%)
- $CC$ = Capacity Cushion (%)

By definition:

$$
U = \frac{AO}{DC} \times 100
$$

$$
E = \frac{AO}{EC} \times 100
$$

Dividing the two:

$$
\frac{U}{E} = \frac{AO / DC}{AO / EC} = \frac{EC}{DC}
$$

$$
EC = DC \times \frac{U}{E}
$$

Multiplying both sides by $E$ and dividing by $100$:

$$
AO = EC \times \frac{E}{100} = DC \times \frac{U}{100}
$$

The **Capacity Cushion** is defined as the unused portion of design capacity:

$$
CC = 100\% - U
$$

Substituting the value of $U$:

$$
CC = 100 - \left( \frac{AO}{DC} \times 100 \right) = \frac{DC - AO}{DC} \times 100
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation**: The Capacity Cushion represents the **safety margin** a firm keeps unused to absorb demand spikes or supply disruptions. A cushion of **20%–25%** is generally considered healthy in manufacturing.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — Number of Machines Required

**Problem (KTU Board Pattern):**
A small-scale industry expects demand of **12,000 units/year** for product P. Each machine can produce **50 units/day** operating on a single 8-hour shift, 250 working days per year. The machines are operated at **85% utilization**. How many machines should be purchased?

**Step 1 — Annual Capacity of One Machine:**

$$
\text{Annual Capacity per Machine} = 50 \times 250 = 12{,}500\ units
$$

**Step 2 — Effective Capacity (factoring utilization):**

$$
\text{Effective Capacity per Machine} = 12{,}500 \times 0.85 = 10{,}625\ units
$$

**Step 3 — Number of Machines Required:**

$$
N = \frac{Demand}{Effective\ Capacity\ per\ Machine} = \frac{12{,}000}{10{,}625} = 1.129
$$

**Step 4 — Rounding (KTU Convention):**

Since we cannot buy 0.129 of a machine, we **always round UP** to the next whole integer:

$$
N = 2\ machines
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — Break-Even Analysis for Capacity Decision

**Problem:**
A firm is considering installing an additional machine costing **₹5,00,000 (fixed)** with a variable cost of **₹40/unit** and selling price of **₹100/unit**. The expected demand is **8,000 units/year**.

**(a)** Compute the break-even point in units and rupees.

**(b)** Is the investment justified at the forecasted demand?

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — BEP in Units:**

$$
BEP_{(units)} = \frac{Fixed\ Cost}{Price - Variable\ Cost}
$$

$$
BEP_{(units)} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{100 - 40} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{60} = 8{,}333.33 \approx 8{,}334\ units
$$

**Part (a) — BEP in Rupees:**

$$
BEP_{(₹)} = \frac{Fixed\ Cost}{1 - \frac{Variable\ Cost}{Price}}
$$

$$
BEP_{(₹)} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{1 - \frac{40}{100}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{0.6} = ₹8{,}33{,}333
$$

**Part (b) — Investment Decision:**

Demand = 8,000 units, which is **less than** the BEP of 8,334 units.

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion**: Since the forecasted demand (8,000 units) **does not exceed** the break-even point (8,334 units), the firm will incur a **loss of ₹20,040** at the projected demand. The capacity addition is **NOT financially justified** under current forecasts. The firm should either reduce fixed costs, raise the price, or wait for higher demand.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Capacity Strategies

| Criterion | Lead Strategy | Lag Strategy | Match Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Risk of stockout | **Low** | **High** | Moderate |
| Risk of idle capacity | **High** | **Low** | Moderate |
| Capital intensity | High | Low | Moderate |
| Market share growth | Aggressive | Reactive | Balanced |
| Suitability for | High-growth, predictable demand | Mature, declining markets | Most industries |
| KTU Exam Hint | "Aggressive" / "Proactive" | "Conservative" / "Reactive" | "Balanced" / "Tracking" |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Capacity Hierarchy Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below visualizes the relationship between the three levels of capacity — a frequently asked **2-mark conceptual question** in KTU exams.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["DESIGN CAPACITY - Maximum Theoretical Output"] --> B["Subtract Planned Losses - Maintenance, Breaks, Setups"]
    B --> C["EFFECTIVE CAPACITY - Realistic Maximum Output"]
    C --> D["Subtract Unplanned Losses - Breakdowns, Absenteeism"]
    D --> E["ACTUAL OUTPUT - Realized Production"]
    E --> F["Compute Utilization vs Design"]
    E --> G["Compute Efficiency vs Effective"]
    F --> H["CAPACITY CUSHION - 100% minus Utilization"]
```

### 4.2 Capacity Planning Process Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["Step 1: Forecast Long-Term Demand"] --> stepB["Step 2: Compute Current Effective Capacity"]
    stepB --> stepC["Step 3: Identify Capacity Gap"]
    stepC --> stepD{"Step 4: Gap Exists?"}
    stepD -- Yes --> stepE["Step 5a: Generate Alternatives - Expand, Subcontract, Overtime"]
    stepD -- No --> stepF["Step 5b: Maintain Status Quo"]
    stepE --> stepG["Step 6: Evaluate Financials - BEP, NPV, EMV"]
    stepG --> stepH["Step 7: Select Best Alternative"]
    stepH --> stepI["Step 8: Implement and Monitor"]
    stepF --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ["Step 9: Feedback and Revise Forecast"]
    stepJ --> stepA
```

### 4.3 Three-Strategy Decision Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["Capacity Decision Required"] --> q1{"Demand Trend?"}
    q1 -- "Rapidly Growing" --> lead["LEAD STRATEGY - Add capacity early"]
    q1 -- "Stable or Declining" --> lag["LAG STRATEGY - Wait and add later"]
    q1 -- "Gradual Growth" --> match["MATCH STRATEGY - Incremental additions"]
    lead --> r1["Pro: Capture market share"]
    lead --> r2["Con: High idle cost risk"]
    lag --> r3["Pro: Minimal idle cost"]
    lag --> r4["Con: Lost sales risk"]
    match --> r5["Pro: Balanced risk-reward"]
    match --> r6["Con: Requires accurate forecasting"]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Capacity Planning System

> [!NOTE]
> **Adapted Architecture Mapping**: For industries where physical diagrams are infeasible, the following block diagram maps the functional interactions between demand forecasting, capacity analysis, financial evaluation, and execution.

```mermaid
graph LR
    input["INPUT MODULE - Forecast Demand, Current Capacity"] --> processor["PROCESSING MODULE - Gap Analysis, BEP, EMV"]
    processor --> decision["DECISION MODULE - Choose Lead or Lag or Match"]
    decision --> execute["EXECUTION MODULE - Hire or Buy or Subcontract"]
    execute --> monitor["MONITORING MODULE - Track Utilization and Efficiency"]
    monitor --> feedback["FEEDBACK CHANNEL - Update Forecast"]
    feedback --> input
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
Define the term **"Capacity"** and distinguish between **Design Capacity** and **Effective Capacity**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Capacity** is the maximum output rate that a production facility, system, or process can produce in a given period under normal operating conditions.
>
> - **Design Capacity** is the *theoretical maximum* output of a system under *ideal* conditions — it ignores breakdowns, delays, and quality losses.
> - **Effective Capacity** is the capacity that the system can realistically achieve given product mix, available labour, scheduling constraints, and standard maintenance.
>
> *Design Capacity is always greater than or equal to Effective Capacity.*

*(Allocation: 1 mark definition, 2 marks distinction)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What is a **Capacity Cushion**? Why is it kept by organizations?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Capacity Cushion** is the *unused reserve capacity* maintained by an organization as a safety buffer against demand surges and supply disruptions. It is calculated as:
>
> $$\text{Capacity Cushion} = 100\% - \text{Utilization Rate}$$
>
> Organizations retain a cushion to (i) handle unforeseen demand spikes, (ii) absorb machine breakdowns, (iii) meet delivery commitments, and (iv) avoid customer dissatisfaction. A typical manufacturing cushion ranges between **15% and 25%**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**: Each Module carries a **14-mark question** with an *internal choice* between two sub-options. Both sub-questions must be attempted for full marks. Each 14-mark question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

A precision engineering firm manufactures industrial valves. The firm operates **10 CNC machines**, each with a design capacity of **2,000 valves/month**. Due to maintenance, breakdowns, and operator breaks, the effective capacity is **80% of design capacity**. In a particular month, the actual production was **14,500 valves**.

**(a)** Calculate the **Utilization** and **Efficiency** of the firm. (7 marks)

**(b)** If the demand forecast for next year is **2,50,000 valves/year**, how many additional CNC machines should be purchased to maintain a **25% capacity cushion**? Assume each machine works **12 months/year** at the current efficiency. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Utilization and Efficiency**

**Step 1 — Compute Design Capacity:**

$$
DC = 10\ machines \times 2{,}000 = 20{,}000\ valves/month
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Effective Capacity (given as 80% of design):**

$$
EC = 20{,}000 \times 0.80 = 16{,}000\ valves/month
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Utilization:**

$$
U = \frac{AO}{DC} \times 100 = \frac{14{,}500}{20{,}000} \times 100 = 72.5\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Efficiency:**

$$
E = \frac{AO}{EC} \times 100 = \frac{14{,}500}{16{,}000} \times 100 = 90.625\%
$$

*(Allocation: Step 1 — 1 mark, Step 2 — 2 marks, Step 3 — 2 marks, Step 4 — 2 marks)*

---

**Part (b) — Additional Machines Required with 25% Cushion**

**Step 1 — Annual Demand:**

$$
Demand = 2{,}50{,}000\ valves/year
$$

**Step 2 — Required Annual Capacity with 25% Cushion:**

$$
Required\ Capacity = \frac{Demand}{1 - Cushion} = \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{1 - 0.25} = \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{0.75} = 3{,}33{,}333.33\ valves/year
$$

**Step 3 — Annual Capacity per Machine at 90.625% efficiency:**

$$
Capacity\ per\ Machine = 2{,}000 \times 12 \times 0.90625 = 21{,}750\ valves/year
$$

**Step 4 — Total Machines Needed:**

$$
Total = \frac{3{,}33{,}333.33}{21{,}750} = 15.33 \approx 16\ machines\ (round\ up)
$$

**Step 5 — Additional Machines:**

$$
Additional = 16 - 10 = 6\ machines
$$

*(Allocation: 1 mark each for Steps 1–4, 3 marks for Step 5 with justification)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Retest Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **three capacity strategies** — Lead, Lag, and Match — with their merits and limitations. (7 marks)

**(b)** A company is evaluating two capacity alternatives using **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** analysis:

| State of Nature | Probability | Payoff of Alternative X (₹) | Payoff of Alternative Y (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| High Demand | 0.4 | 8,00,000 | 5,00,000 |
| Moderate Demand | 0.5 | 5,00,000 | 4,50,000 |
| Low Demand | 0.1 | -2,00,000 | 1,00,000 |

Which alternative should be selected? (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Three Capacity Strategies (7 marks)**

1. **Lead Strategy** (3 marks):
   - Capacity is added *ahead* of demand.
   - *Merit*: Captures early market share, avoids stockouts.
   - *Limitation*: High idle-capacity risk, large capital lock-up.

2. **Lag Strategy** (2 marks):
   - Capacity is added *after* demand materializes.
   - *Merit*: Low idle cost, conservative capital use.
   - *Limitation*: Risk of lost sales, customer dissatisfaction.

3. **Match (Tracking) Strategy** (2 marks):
   - Capacity is expanded *incrementally* to track demand.
   - *Merit*: Balanced risk-reward, adaptable.
   - *Limitation*: Requires accurate forecasting; moderately complex.

---

**Part (b) — EMV Analysis (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — EMV of Alternative X:**

$$
EMV_X = (0.4 \times 8{,}00{,}000) + (0.5 \times 5{,}00{,}000) + (0.1 \times -2{,}00{,}000)
$$

$$
EMV_X = 3{,}20{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 - 20{,}000 = ₹5{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — EMV of Alternative Y:**

$$
EMV_Y = (0.4 \times 5{,}00{,}000) + (0.5 \times 4{,}50{,}000) + (0.1 \times 1{,}00{,}000)
$$

$$
EMV_Y = 2{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}25{,}000 + 10{,}000 = ₹4{,}35{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Decision:**

$$
EMV_X\ (₹5{,}50{,}000) > EMV_Y\ (₹4{,}35{,}000)
$$

> **Decision**: Alternative **X** should be selected since it has the higher Expected Monetary Value.

*(Allocation: 3 marks for X computation, 3 marks for Y computation, 1 mark for decision with comparison statement)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Capacity Planning questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Utilization with Efficiency** — Utilization uses *Design Capacity* in the denominator; Efficiency uses *Effective Capacity*. A single-line error here costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to round UP machine count** — Always round up to the next integer; rounding down makes the design infeasible.
> 3. **Missing the Cushion in BEP/Decision Questions** — If a cushion is specified, divide the demand by $(1 - Cushion)$, not by 1.
> 4. **Skipping units in EMV computation** — Always write the ₹ sign and the probability explicitly. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for sloppy notation.
> 5. **Writing only the formula without substitution** — KTU awards **1 mark** for the correct formula and **remaining marks** for substitution and arithmetic. Never skip the substitution step.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Capacity Planning**

- ☐ **Definition**: Capacity = maximum output rate; Capacity Planning = sizing the system to meet forecasted demand.
- ☐ **Hierarchy**: Design Capacity ≥ Effective Capacity ≥ Actual Output.
- ☐ **Utilization** uses Design Capacity; **Efficiency** uses Effective Capacity.
- ☐ **Capacity Cushion** = $100\% - Utilization$; healthy cushion = 15%–25%.
- ☐ **Three Horizons**: Long-range (facility), Intermediate (workforce), Short-range (schedule).
- ☐ **Three Strategies**: Lead (aggressive), Lag (conservative), Match (balanced).
- ☐ **BEP formula**: $\dfrac{Fixed\ Cost}{(Price - Variable\ Cost)}$ — units; $\dfrac{Fixed\ Cost}{(1 - VC/Price)}$ — rupees.
- ☐ **EMV** = weighted average of payoffs; choose alternative with **highest EMV**.
- ☐ **Machine count**: always **round UP** to next whole integer.
- ☐ **Cushion Adjustment**: When cushion is given, multiply demand by $\dfrac{1}{1 - Cushion}$.
- ☐ **Determinants of Effective Capacity**: Facility, product, process, human, policy, external factors — *memorize all six for Part A definition questions.*
- ☐ **Common Tools**: Break-Even Analysis, Decision Trees, Waiting-Line Analysis, Simulation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Facility Layout - Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT863 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Facility Layout - Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Facility Layout** is the physical arrangement of industrial facilities (machines, equipment, workstations, storage areas, service points, and auxiliary departments) within a production environment, designed to enable a smooth, efficient, and cost-effective flow of materials, information, and personnel from raw material input to finished product output.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Facility Layout is treated as a **strategic operational decision** that determines the conversion logic of how inputs (raw materials, labor, capital) are transformed into outputs (goods, services) within the *Conversion Subsystem* of an operating system. It directly influences **throughput, cycle time, work-in-process (WIP) inventory, and operating cost**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Layout" Intuition

Imagine your kitchen at home:
- If your **refrigerator**, **sink**, **stove**, and **dining table** are placed randomly, you will walk back and forth repeatedly while cooking, spill food, waste time, and tire yourself.
- But if you arrange them in the **flow order** — Fridge → Counter → Sink → Stove → Serving Counter → Dining Table — cooking becomes effortless.

> **The kitchen IS a facility. The items inside ARE your resources. The cook IS the flow of work.**
> A *Facility Layout* is simply this kitchen-design principle scaled up to a factory, hospital, warehouse, or office.

## 1.3 Why Study the "Objectives" of Facility Layout?

> [!NOTE]
> A layout is not an end in itself — it is a **means to achieve specific operational goals**. KTU examiners frequently frame questions around *why* a layout decision is made rather than *what* a layout looks like. Hence, mastering the **Objectives of Facility Layout** is the most heavily tested concept in this module.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU High-Weightage Terms)

The following metrics are used to evaluate whether a layout has achieved its objectives:

| **Metric** | **Symbol / Unit** | **Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| Material Handling Cost | **MHC (₹/unit)** | Cost of moving materials between workstations |
| Backtracking Distance | **BD (m)** | Unproductive travel against the flow direction |
| Floor Space Utilization | **FSU (%)** | Percentage of total floor area effectively used |
| Throughput Time | **TPT (hours)** | Time taken for one unit to traverse the system |
| Work-in-Process Inventory | **WIP (units)** | Items waiting between operations |
| Production Cycle Efficiency | **PCE (%)** | Ratio of value-added time to total cycle time |
| Safety Incident Rate | **SIR (per 1000 hrs)** | Frequency of accidents per labor-hour |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Strategic Significance of Facility Layout

The decision of *where to put what* consumes only **1%–2% of the total project cost**, yet influences **50% or more of the operating cost** over the facility's lifetime. Therefore, the **Objectives of Facility Layout** act as the **decision criteria** used by industrial engineers to evaluate layout alternatives.

## 2.2 The 10 Canonical Objectives of Facility Layout (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners award marks when students enumerate objectives **with both the objective AND its operational justification**. Memorizing only the list is insufficient — the *reason* behind each objective is what scores.

Below is the analytically structured objective matrix:

### Objective 1 — Minimize Material Handling Cost
- **Logic:** Material handling is a **non-value-adding activity** (it does not change the form, fit, or function of the product).
- **How achieved:** Place high-volume / frequently-used workstations **adjacent** to each other.
- **Real-world utility:** In a car assembly plant, the *paint shop* is kept next to the *final assembly line* because every car must travel that route.

### Objective 2 — Reduce Production Delays and Bottlenecks
- **Logic:** A bottleneck = the slowest workstation → it caps the entire system's throughput (Little's Law).
- **How achieved:** Balance line capacities by giving bottlenecks **more space, buffer inventory, and easier material access**.
- **KTU Insight:** This objective connects directly to **Line Balancing Theory** (covered in Module 3).

### Objective 3 — Achieve Optimum Utilization of Available Space (Cubic Space Utilization)
- **Logic:** Floor area is 2D; space is **3D**. Using vertical height (e.g., mezzanines, racks) multiplies effective capacity.
- **Formula Concept:**

$$\text{Cubic Space Utilization (CSU)} = \frac{\text{Volume effectively used}}{\text{Total available volume}} \times 100\%$$

### Objective 4 — Promote Easy Supervision, Control & Communication
- **Logic:** A linear or U-shaped layout enables a single supervisor to oversee many workers.
- **How achieved:** Keep reporting hierarchies within **sight-line distance**; co-locate communication-intensive departments.

### Objective 5 — Ensure Worker Safety, Comfort, and Convenience
- **Logic:** Accidents cause direct cost (compensation) and indirect cost (downtime, morale).
- **KTU-Mandated Standards:** Ventilation, lighting (≥ **300 lux** for precision work), ergonomic reach distances, and emergency egress paths.

### Objective 6 — Maintain Flexibility for Future Changes
- **Logic:** Product mix and volumes change every 3–5 years. A *rigid* layout becomes obsolete.
- **How achieved:** Use modular equipment, standardized foundations, and adequate aisle widths.

### Objective 7 — Minimize Investment in Equipment and Capital
- **Logic:** Moving costs (₹ per machine-shift) for relocation can be **30%–50%** of original equipment cost.
- **How achieved:** Decide layout *once, decisively*, using systematic methods (CRAFT, CORELAP, ALDEP).

### Objective 8 — Provide Effective Integration of Support Services
- **Logic:** Tool rooms, inspection areas, and maintenance bays must be reachable *without crossing the main flow*.
- **How achieved:** Position support services on **peripheries** with **dedicated access corridors**.

### Objective 9 — Reduce Work-in-Process (WIP) Inventory
- **Logic:** WIP ties up working capital and hides quality defects.
- **Formula Linkage (Little's Law):**

$$WIP = \text{Throughput Rate} \times \text{Throughput Time}$$

- Therefore, **shorter physical flow distance** ⇒ **lower TPT** ⇒ **lower WIP** for the same throughput.

### Objective 10 — Improve Product Quality and Customer Satisfaction
- **Logic:** Long material flows cause scratches, contamination, and damage.
- **How achieved:** Compress the value stream and reduce exposure to ambient hazards.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Revision Table

> [!NOTE]
> Facility Layout is largely a **qualitative + semi-quantitative** topic, but the following formulas appear in numerical sub-parts of KTU exam questions.

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|
| $TPT = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i + \sum_{j=1}^{m} M_{hj}$ | Sum of operation times plus material handling times | Total production time per unit |
| $WIP = R \times T$ | Little's Law (R = throughput rate, T = flow time) | Estimating inventory between stages |
| $CSU = \dfrac{V_{used}}{V_{total}} \times 100$ | Cubic space utilization % | Assessing vertical layout efficiency |
| $MHC = \sum_{i<j} F_{ij} \times D_{ij} \times C_{ij}$ | Material handling cost: sum of (load × distance × unit handling cost) over all department pairs | Comparing two layout alternatives |
| $PCE = \dfrac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{TPT} \times 100$ | Production cycle efficiency | Layout effectiveness benchmark |
| $Backtracking \% = \dfrac{D_{backward}}{D_{total}} \times 100$ | Percentage of flow against natural direction | Layout flow quality |

> **Critical Notation Rule:** All distance variables $D_{ij}$ represent the shortest walking path between department $i$ and department $j$, NOT straight-line distance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Mathematical Derivation — Material Handling Cost (MHC) for Layout Comparison

This derivation is **the most frequently asked 7-mark numerical** in KTU Module 2 questions.

### Step 1 — Define the Decision Variables

Let there be $n$ departments in a facility. Between every pair $(i, j)$ of departments:
- $F_{ij}$ = Number of loads moved per period between department $i$ and department $j$ (loads/month).
- $D_{ij}$ = Distance between department $i$ and department $j$ in meters.
- $C_{ij}$ = Cost of moving **one load across one meter** in ₹/load-meter.

### Step 2 — Establish the Cost Equation for a Single Pair

The handling cost for material moving only between department $i$ and department $j$ is:

$$\text{Cost}_{ij} = F_{ij} \times D_{ij} \times C_{ij}$$

**Conversion Logic:** We multiply because:
- $F_{ij}$ tells us *how many times* the movement happens.
- $D_{ij}$ tells us *how far* each movement travels.
- $C_{ij}$ is the *unit price* per load per meter.

Their product therefore gives the **total rupee value** of handling between just those two departments.

### Step 3 — Extend to All Department Pairs

Since every pair of departments exchanges materials, we sum over all unique pairs (avoiding double counting, hence the factor ½):

$$\text{Total MHC} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} F_{ij} \times D_{ij} \times C_{ij}, \quad i \neq j$$

### Step 4 — Compare Two Alternative Layouts

Suppose Layout **A** has distance matrix $[D^{A}_{ij}]$ and Layout **B** has $[D^{B}_{ij}]$.

Then the **savings** from switching from Layout A to Layout B are:

$$\Delta \text{MHC} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i \neq j} F_{ij} \cdot C_{ij} \cdot (D^{A}_{ij} - D^{B}_{ij})$$

If $\Delta \text{MHC} > 0$, Layout B is cheaper → adopt B.
If $\Delta \text{MHC} < 0$, Layout B is costlier → retain A.

### Step 3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

A factory has **3 departments (A, B, C)**. The load matrix is:

$$F = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 20 & 30 \\ 20 & 0 & 10 \\ 30 & 10 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \text{ loads/day}$$

The unit handling cost $C_{ij} = \text{₹}0.50$ per load-meter (assumed identical for simplicity).

**Layout P** distances (meters):

$$D^P = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 10 & 25 \\ 10 & 0 & 15 \\ 25 & 15 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Layout Q** distances (meters):

$$D^Q = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 8 & 20 \\ 8 & 0 & 12 \\ 20 & 12 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step A — Compute MHC for Layout P:**

$$\text{MHC}^P = \frac{1}{2} \big[ (20 \cdot 10) + (30 \cdot 25) + (20 \cdot 10) + (10 \cdot 15) + (30 \cdot 25) + (10 \cdot 15) \big] \times 0.50$$

Compute each product:
- $F_{AB} \cdot D^P_{AB} = 20 \times 10 = 200$
- $F_{AC} \cdot D^P_{AC} = 30 \times 25 = 750$
- $F_{BA} \cdot D^P_{BA} = 20 \times 10 = 200$
- $F_{BC} \cdot D^P_{BC} = 10 \times 15 = 150$
- $F_{CA} \cdot D^P_{CA} = 30 \times 25 = 750$
- $F_{CB} \cdot D^P_{CB} = 10 \times 15 = 150$

Sum = $200 + 750 + 200 + 150 + 750 + 150 = 2200$ load-meters.

Apply factor ½ and unit cost:

$$\text{MHC}^P = \frac{1}{2} \times 2200 \times 0.50 = \text{₹}550 \text{ per day}$$

**Step B — Compute MHC for Layout Q:**

- $F_{AB} \cdot D^Q_{AB} = 20 \times 8 = 160$
- $F_{AC} \cdot D^Q_{AC} = 30 \times 20 = 600$
- $F_{BA} \cdot D^Q_{BA} = 20 \times 8 = 160$
- $F_{BC} \cdot D^Q_{BC} = 10 \times 12 = 120$
- $F_{CA} \cdot D^Q_{CA} = 30 \times 20 = 600$
- $F_{CB} \cdot D^Q_{CB} = 10 \times 12 = 120$

Sum = $160 + 600 + 160 + 120 + 600 + 120 = 1760$ load-meters.

$$\text{MHC}^Q = \frac{1}{2} \times 1760 \times 0.50 = \text{₹}440 \text{ per day}$$

**Step C — Compute Savings:**

$$\Delta \text{MHC} = 550 - 440 = \text{₹}110 \text{ per day}$$

**Step D — Annualize and Conclude:**

$$\text{Annual Savings} = 110 \times 300 = \text{₹}33{,}000$$

Hence, **Layout Q is preferred** as it directly satisfies the objective *"Minimize Material Handling Cost"*.

## 3.2 Analytical Objective Trade-off Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Real-world layout design involves **conflicting objectives**. Engineers must optimize *simultaneously*. The matrix below shows how objectives interact — KTU examiners award extra marks when students mention these trade-offs.

| **If we optimize for this objective…** | **It may worsen this other objective** | **Trade-off Reason** |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum MHC (close departments) | Worker safety & ventilation | Crowding near machines causes heat, noise, dust |
| Maximum space utilization | Flexibility for future changes | Cramped layout cannot absorb new machines |
| Minimum WIP | Worker safety | Zero buffers mean workers must rush, increasing accidents |
| Easy supervision (open plan) | Confidentiality / quality control | No enclosures → contamination risk in pharma/food |

> **Engineering Insight:** No single objective dominates. The "best" layout is the one that achieves an **acceptable balance** of all objectives within the firm's **strategic priorities**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy Diagram — Classification of Facility Layouts

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Facility Layout] --> nodeB[Product Layout]
    nodeA[Facility Layout] --> nodeC[Process Layout]
    nodeA[Facility Layout] --> nodeD[Fixed Position Layout]
    nodeA[Facility Layout] --> nodeE[Cellular Layout]
    nodeA[Facility Layout] --> nodeF[Combined Layout]
    nodeB[Product Layout] --> nodeB1[Line flow, high volume, low variety]
    nodeC[Process Layout] --> nodeC1[Functional grouping, job shop, high variety low volume]
    nodeD[Fixed Position Layout] --> nodeD1[Product stays put, resources move: ships, aircraft, buildings]
    nodeE[Cellular Layout] --> nodeE1[Group technology, U shaped cells, family of parts]
    nodeF[Combined Layout] --> nodeF1[Hybrid of two or more layouts]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow Diagram — How Objectives Drive Layout Design

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1[Inputs from Marketing and Strategy] --> node2[Identify Operating Objectives]
    node2 --> node3[Generate Layout Alternatives]
    node3 --> node4[Evaluate Each Alternative]
    node4 --> node5[Select Optimum Layout]
    node5 --> node6[Implement and Monitor]
    node6 --> node7[Feedback Loop for Continuous Improvement]
    node7 --> node2
    node8[MHC Minimization] -. Objective 1 .- node4
    node9[Space Utilization] -. Objective 3 .- node4
    node10[Worker Safety] -. Objective 5 .- node4
    node11[Flexibility] -. Objective 6 .- node4
    node12[Investment Minimization] -. Objective 7 .- node4
```

## 4.3 Mapping Diagram — Relationship Between Layout Type and Primary Objective

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph prod
        nodeP1[Product Layout] --> nodeP2[Primary Objective: Minimize MHC and Flow Time]
    end
    subgraph proc
        nodeQ1[Process Layout] --> nodeQ2[Primary Objective: Flexibility and Variety Handling]
    end
    subgraph fix
        nodeR1[Fixed Position Layout] --> nodeR2[Primary Objective: Move Resources not Product]
    end
    subgraph cell
        nodeS1[Cellular Layout] --> nodeS2[Primary Objective: Balance MHC Flexibility and WIP]
    end
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Objective Evaluation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Load Matrix Fij] --> nodeB[Distance Matrix Dij per Layout]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Cost Engine Computes MHC]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Volume Engine Computes WIP via Little Law]
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[Space Engine Computes CSU]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Multi Objective Score Aggregator]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeG[Constraint Weights from Strategy] --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeH[Ranked Layout Decision]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block architecture mirrors how commercial layout software (e.g., **Factory PLAN, VisFactory, Siemens Tecnomatix**) actually computes layout scores — converting the qualitative objectives into a single quantitative decision metric.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define the term "Facility Layout". List any four objectives of a good facility layout.**

**Model Answer:**
Facility Layout refers to the physical arrangement of machines, equipment, workstations, and service areas within a facility to achieve smooth, economical, and safe flow of work.

**Four Objectives:**
1. Minimize material handling cost.
2. Achieve optimum utilization of available floor and cubic space.
3. Ensure worker safety, comfort, and convenience.
4. Provide flexibility for future changes in product or process.

*(Word limit target: 60–80 words. Award full marks if 4 objectives are stated correctly with no justification required at this mark level.)*

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between Product Layout and Process Layout based on flow pattern.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **Product Layout** | **Process Layout** |
|---|---|---|
| Flow pattern | Straight-line, continuous | Zig-zag, intermittent |
| Equipment arrangement | As per operation sequence | Grouped by function (milling, grinding) |
| Suitable for | High volume, low variety | Low volume, high variety |
| WIP inventory | Very low | High |
| Example | Car assembly, bottling plant | Job shop, tool room |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often *list* objectives without explaining **how each objective is achieved through layout design**. You must pair each objective with its **design strategy** to score full marks.
> **Pitfall #2:** In numericals, students compute the **distance matrix product incorrectly** by forgetting the ½ factor for double counting. Always write "1/2 × ΣΣ" explicitly on the answer sheet.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Layout Selection & Objective Analysis

**Part (a) — 7 Marks [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

A small-scale industry is shifting its manufacturing unit from Location P to Location Q. The proposed layout consists of 4 departments: **Receiving (R), Machining (M), Assembly (A), and Shipping (S)**. The inter-departmental flow matrix (loads/day) is:

$$F = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 30 & 10 & 0 \\ 30 & 0 & 25 & 0 \\ 10 & 25 & 0 & 35 \\ 0 & 0 & 35 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Two alternative layouts have been proposed with the following distance matrices (meters):

$$D^P = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 12 & 24 & 36 \\ 12 & 0 & 12 & 24 \\ 24 & 12 & 0 & 12 \\ 36 & 24 & 12 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D^Q = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 8 & 16 & 24 \\ 8 & 0 & 8 & 16 \\ 16 & 8 & 0 & 8 \\ 24 & 16 & 8 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Unit handling cost = **₹0.40 per load-meter**. Compute total daily material handling cost for both layouts. Which layout satisfies the objective of *minimum material handling cost*? Show all steps.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute load-distance products for Layout P:**

| **Pair (i, j)** | **$F_{ij}$** | **$D^P_{ij}$** | **$F_{ij} \cdot D^P_{ij}$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| R-M | 30 | 12 | 360 |
| R-A | 10 | 24 | 240 |
| R-S | 0 | 36 | 0 |
| M-A | 25 | 12 | 300 |
| M-S | 0 | 24 | 0 |
| A-S | 35 | 12 | 420 |

Sum of one-way products = $360 + 240 + 0 + 300 + 0 + 420 = 1320$ load-meters.

Applying the ½ factor for double counting:

$$\text{Total L-D for P} = \frac{1}{2} \times (1320 + 1320) = 1320 \text{ load-meters (since symmetric)}$$

$$\text{MHC}^P = 1320 \times 0.40 = \text{₹}528 \text{ per day}$$

**Step 2 — Compute load-distance products for Layout Q:**

| **Pair (i, j)** | **$F_{ij}$** | **$D^Q_{ij}$** | **$F_{ij} \cdot D^Q_{ij}$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| R-M | 30 | 8 | 240 |
| R-A | 10 | 16 | 160 |
| R-S | 0 | 24 | 0 |
| M-A | 25 | 8 | 200 |
| M-S | 0 | 16 | 0 |
| A-S | 35 | 8 | 280 |

Sum = $240 + 160 + 0 + 200 + 0 + 280 = 880$ load-meters.

$$\text{MHC}^Q = 880 \times 0.40 = \text{₹}352 \text{ per day}$$

**Step 3 — Decision:**

$$\Delta \text{MHC} = 528 - 352 = \text{₹}176 \text{ per day saved by Layout Q}$$

**Conclusion:** **Layout Q is preferred** as it achieves the objective of *minimizing material handling cost* with a daily saving of ₹176 (≈ ₹52,800 annually assuming 300 working days).

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**Q: Discuss in detail any **seven** objectives of facility layout. For each objective, mention ONE design strategy to achieve it.**

**Model Answer (7 Objectives, each with a design strategy):**

| **#** | **Objective** | **Design Strategy to Achieve It** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Minimize material handling cost | Place departments with **highest inter-departmental flow** adjacent to each other (e.g., using *Trafi-Tardieu* closeness rating) |
| 2 | Achieve optimum utilization of available space (cubic space) | Use **vertical stacking**, mezzanine floors, overhead conveyors |
| 3 | Ensure worker safety and comfort | Maintain **minimum aisle width (≥ 1.0 m)**, install **emergency exits every 30 m** |
| 4 | Promote easy supervision and control | Adopt **U-shaped or straight-line** layouts for single-supervisor sight-lines |
| 5 | Reduce production delays and bottlenecks | Provide **buffer storage** near bottleneck stations; apply *line balancing* |
| 6 | Maintain flexibility for future changes | Keep **modular equipment on standardized foundations** with **spare aisles** |
| 7 | Provide effective integration of support services | Locate **tool rooms, maintenance, inspection** on facility periphery with dedicated corridors |

*(For full 7 marks, examiner expects: 7 objectives stated + 7 strategies + brief operational justification for at least 2 of them.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Part (a) — 7 Marks [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q: Explain with suitable examples how the principle of "minimum distance" and the principle of "flow" guide the arrangement of departments in a process layout.**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of Minimum Distance:**
This principle states that *workstations, departments, and service points exchanging a high volume of materials should be located as close as possible to minimize travel time and cost*.

**Example:** In a hospital, the **Operation Theatre (OT)** is located adjacent to the **ICU and Post-Operative Ward** because patient transfers between these units happen frequently and must be rapid. The laundry and kitchen, with low interaction, are placed on remote corners.

**Principle of Flow:**
This principle dictates that *the layout should permit a unidirectional, progressive movement of materials from raw input to finished output with minimum backtracking*.

**Example:** In an automobile assembly plant, the chassis moves through welding → painting → assembly → testing in a straight **U-shaped line** to avoid any reverse motion. Reverse motion would cause congestion, scratches, and wasted cycle time.

**How They Work Together:**
- *Minimum distance* decides **how close** to place any two departments.
- *Flow* decides **in what sequence** departments should be arranged.
- Together, they minimize the **total weighted flow distance** = the objective function used by CRAFT algorithm.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Q: A manufacturing unit produces 480 units/day. The current layout results in a throughput time of 8 hours. After relayout based on objectives, throughput time reduces to 5 hours. Compute:**
**(i) Current WIP and revised WIP using Little's Law.**
**(ii) Reduction in WIP inventory in percentage.**
**(iii) Capital blocked, assuming each unit has a raw material cost of ₹500.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Production rate $R = 480$ units/day.
- Old flow time $T_1 = 8$ hours = $\frac{8}{24} = \frac{1}{3}$ day.
- New flow time $T_2 = 5$ hours = $\frac{5}{24}$ day.

**(i) WIP Calculation using Little's Law $WIP = R \times T$:**

$$\text{Old WIP} = 480 \times \frac{8}{24} = 480 \times 0.3333 = 160 \text{ units}$$

$$\text{New WIP} = 480 \times \frac{5}{24} = 480 \times 0.2083 = 100 \text{ units}$$

**(ii) Percentage Reduction:**

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{160 - 100}{160} \times 100 = \frac{60}{160} \times 100 = 37.5\%$$

**(iii) Capital Blocked:**

Old capital blocked: $160 \times 500 = \text{₹}80{,}000$

New capital blocked: $100 \times 500 = \text{₹}50{,}000$

$$\text{Capital Freed} = 80{,}000 - 50{,}000 = \text{₹}30{,}000$$

**Conclusion:** The new layout satisfies **two objectives simultaneously** — *minimize WIP inventory* and *free working capital* — demonstrating how a well-designed layout contributes directly to **operational excellence**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Fire Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- [ ] **Definition (must memorize):** Facility Layout = *physical arrangement of resources to enable efficient flow of work*.
- [ ] **Top 10 Objectives (learn in order):** MHC minimization → Space utilization → Safety → Supervision → Flexibility → Capital minimization → WIP reduction → Bottleneck removal → Support integration → Quality improvement.
- [ ] **Key Formula #1:** $MHC = \frac{1}{2} \sum F_{ij} \cdot D_{ij} \cdot C_{ij}$ — always include the **½ factor**.
- [ ] **Key Formula #2:** Little's Law $WIP = R \times T$ — link it to throughput time reduction.
- [ ] **Key Formula #3:** $CSU = \dfrac{V_{used}}{V_{total}} \times 100$ — use *cubic*, not just floor, area.
- [ ] **Memorize 4 Layout Types:** Product, Process, Fixed Position, Cellular — know at least one industrial example for each.
- [ ] **Trade-off Insight:** Objectives often conflict (e.g., MHC vs. flexibility) — examiners reward students who recognize this.
- [ ] **Valuation Tip:** For 7-mark theory questions, always pair *objective + design strategy + one-line justification*.
- [ ] **Valuation Tip:** For 7-mark numericals, draw the matrix in tabular form, compute cell-by-cell, and state the **decision** explicitly at the end.
- [ ] **Pitfall:** Never confuse **flow distance** (shortest walking path) with **straight-line distance** in MHC calculations.
- [ ] **Pitfall:** When asked "list objectives," do not stop at listing — **rank** them in order of importance as per the given industry context.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Layout - Process Layout - Product Layout - Fixed Position Layout - Cellular Layout
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT863 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Layout - Process Layout - Product Layout - Fixed Position Layout - Cellular Layout

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Plant Layout — The Foundational Concept

A **Plant Layout** refers to the physical arrangement of machines, equipment, workstations, storage areas, and material handling equipment within a production facility, designed to achieve the most efficient flow of work, materials, and information. In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMRT863 — Operations Management, Module 2), the four fundamental types of facility layouts are **Process Layout, Product Layout, Fixed Position Layout, and Cellular Layout**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *Layout decisions are strategic, long-term commitments that determine the physical configuration of a production system. The choice of layout is dictated by product volume, variety, process requirements, and the level of operational flexibility required.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Kitchen" Model

Imagine a **restaurant kitchen** to understand layouts intuitively:

- **Process Layout** = A traditional kitchen where all knives are in one drawer, all spices in one rack, all pots in one cupboard. A chef (worker) moves around collecting ingredients from different "departments." *(Flexible, but slow.)*
- **Product Layout** = A McDonald's assembly line where buns-toaster → patties-grill → sauce-station are arranged in a fixed straight line. The **product moves** while workers stay in their stations. *(Fast, but inflexible.)*
- **Fixed Position Layout** = Cooking a massive wedding cake in the dining hall itself — the **cake (product) doesn't move**; bakers, decorators, and equipment all come to the cake. *(Used for very large/heavy items.)*
- **Cellular Layout** = A modern "ghost kitchen" where all equipment needed to make a specific cuisine (e.g., Italian) is grouped into a self-contained mini-station. *(Best of both worlds.)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Mnemonic — "P³FC":**
> **P**rocess, **P**roduct, **F**ixed-position, **C**ellular — remember this order; it also follows **increasing efficiency for high volume, standardized products**.

## 1.3 The Four Layouts — Formal One-Line Definitions

| # | Layout Type | One-Line Formal Definition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Process Layout** | Grouping of similar machines/workstations together based on the type of process performed (also called **functional layout**). |
| 2 | **Product Layout** | Arrangement of machines and workstations in a sequence dictated by the operations required to manufacture the product (also called **line layout**). |
| 3 | **Fixed Position Layout** | A layout where the product remains stationary at one location and resources (men, machines, materials) are brought to it. |
| 4 | **Cellular Layout** | A hybrid layout grouping dissimilar machines into cells to produce a **family of similar parts** using **Group Technology (GT)**. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **CO2 — "Design of Production Systems."** A student should be able to *select, justify, and compare layouts* for a given production scenario.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Analytical Logic Breakdown for Each Layout

### 🔹 2.1.1 Process Layout (Functional / Job-Shop Layout)

**Operating Logic:**
- The facility is divided into **departments** (e.g., Milling Department, Welding Department, Inspection Department).
- Each department contains **similar machines or processes**.
- Jobs (products) follow **variable paths** through the departments based on their unique processing requirements.

**Why this layout exists:**
- Suitable for **low-volume, high-variety** production (e.g., custom machine shop, hospital).
- General-purpose machines can perform a wide variety of operations.
- Workers become **specialists** in their department.

**How it works (operational flow):**
- A job enters → routed to Department A → then B → then D → then C (non-linear).
- Path is determined by the **routing sheet** of each job.

### 🔹 2.1.2 Product Layout (Line Layout / Assembly Line)

**Operating Logic:**
- Machines and workstations are arranged **in a fixed sequence**, matching the operations required for a **single standard product** (or a very narrow family).
- A **conveyor** or paced line moves the product from one station to the next.
- Each station performs a **limited, repetitive task**.

**Why this layout exists:**
- Suitable for **high-volume, standardized** production (e.g., automobile assembly, FMCG bottling).
- Built around the principle of **division of labor** (Adam Smith → Henry Ford).

**How it works:**
- Raw material enters Station 1 → Station 2 → … → Station *n* → Finished Goods exit.
- **Cycle Time** is the master pace setter: time between successive units coming off the line.

### 🔹 2.1.3 Fixed Position Layout (Project Layout / Site Layout)

**Operating Logic:**
- The product — typically **very large, heavy, or immobile** — is constructed **in place**.
- All resources (workers, tools, cranes, sub-assemblies) are **moved to** the product.
- Used for **one-of-a-kind projects** with long durations.

**Why this layout exists:**
- Moving the product would be **impossible or uneconomical** (a ship weighs 200,000+ tonnes; an aircraft is too wide for any road).

**How it works:**
- Layout planning focuses on **positioning of support areas** (stores, workshops, site offices) around the fixed product location.
- Path of resources is orchestrated by a **master project schedule** (CPM/PERT).

### 🔹 2.1.4 Cellular Layout (Group Technology Layout)

**Operating Logic:**
- Identifies **part families** (groups of parts sharing similar design features or manufacturing operations).
- For each family, a **dedicated cell** is created containing all machines needed to produce that family end-to-end.
- A cell is **U-shaped** or rectangular, with operators trained on **multiple machines**.

**Why this layout exists:**
- Combines the **flexibility of process layout** with the **flow efficiency of product layout**.
- Rooted in **Group Technology (GT)** classification and coding systems (e.g., Opitz, DCLASS).

**How it works:**
- Parts of Family X enter Cell X → undergo sequential operations inside the cell → exit as completed sub-assemblies.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Quantitative Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Layout design is largely **qualitative**, but KTU examiners expect students to know the **quantitative metrics** that measure layout performance.

| Metric | Formula | Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Load Distance (LD)** | $LD = \sum (n_{ij} \cdot d_{ij})$ | m-units | Minimizes total material movement cost. |
