| **Total Flow Cost** | $TC = \sum (C_{ij} \cdot n_{ij} \cdot d_{ij})$ | ₹/day | Where $C_{ij}$ = cost per metre, $n_{ij}$ = trips, $d_{ij}$ = distance. |
| **Number of Machines in a Cell** | $m = \sum_{i=1}^{k} m_{i}$ | machines | Sum of machines across all operations of a part family. |
| **Cell Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Total Available Time} - \text{Idle Time}}{\text{Total Available Time}} \times 100$ | % | Compare utilization of cell vs. process dept. |
| **Throughput Time (Process)** | $T_{proc} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i + W_i)$ | hours | $P_i$ = processing, $W_i$ = waiting time. |
| **Lead Time Reduction (Cell vs Process)** | $\Delta L\% = \dfrac{L_{proc} - L_{cell}}{L_{proc}} \times 100$ | % | Justifies cellular conversion. |
| **Cycle Time (Product Line)** | $CT = \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time per Day}}{\text{Daily Demand}}$ | min/unit | Determines line speed. |
| **Number of Workstations (Line Balancing)** | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\sum t_{i}}{CT}$ | stations | Theoretical lower bound. |
| **Balance Delay** | $BD = \dfrac{N \cdot CT - \sum t_{i}}{N \cdot CT} \times 100$ | % | Inefficiency measure. |

### 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Automotive plants** (Toyota, Ford, Tesla Gigafactories) — pure **product layout**.
- **Hospitals** (Operation Theatres, Radiology, Pathology) — classic **process layout**.
- **Aerospace (Boeing 787)** — **fixed position** in huge assembly hangars.
- **Mazda, Yamaha, Honda motorcycle engines** — **cellular layout** under *Group Technology*.
- **McDonald's, Subway** — hybrid (cellular inside a product line framework).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked *"justify the layout,"* always link your answer to **3 parameters**: **(a) Volume, (b) Variety, (c) Process requirement**. This is the standard evaluation rubric.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Load Distance (LD) Calculation

**Problem (KTU-style):**
Six departments A, B, C, D, E, F in a process layout have the following inter-departmental trips per day. Compute the **Total Load Distance** if distances are given by the matrix below. Distance between identical departments is **0**.

**Trips per Day ($n_{ij}$):**

| From \ To | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 8 | 12 | 5 | 0 | 3 |
| **B** | 6 | 0 | 4 | 9 | 11 | 0 |
| **C** | 10 | 5 | 0 | 0 | 8 | 7 |
| **D** | 4 | 8 | 0 | 0 | 6 | 12 |
| **E** | 0 | 9 | 7 | 5 | 0 | 4 |
| **F** | 3 | 0 | 6 | 10 | 5 | 0 |

**Distance Matrix ($d_{ij}$ in metres):**

| From \ To | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 12 | 8 |
| **B** | 10 | 0 | 12 | 18 | 25 | 9 |
| **C** | 15 | 12 | 0 | 22 | 14 | 11 |
| **D** | 20 | 18 | 22 | 0 | 16 | 24 |
| **E** | 12 | 25 | 14 | 16 | 0 | 13 |
| **F** | 8 | 9 | 11 | 24 | 13 | 0 |

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Recall the formula:**

$$
LD = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} n_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each $(i,j)$ product (only upper triangle to avoid double counting, then multiply by 2 for round trips):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \to B &: 8 \times 10 = 80 \\
A \to C &: 12 \times 15 = 180 \\
A \to D &: 5 \times 20 = 100 \\
A \to E &: 0 \times 12 = 0 \\
A \to F &: 3 \times 8 = 24 \\
B \to C &: 4 \times 12 = 48 \\
B \to D &: 9 \times 18 = 162 \\
B \to E &: 11 \times 25 = 275 \\
B \to F &: 0 \times 9 = 0 \\
C \to D &: 0 \times 22 = 0 \\
C \to E &: 8 \times 14 = 112 \\
C \to F &: 7 \times 11 = 77 \\
D \to E &: 6 \times 16 = 96 \\
D \to F &: 12 \times 24 = 288 \\
E \to F &: 4 \times 13 = 52
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the products:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum (upper triangle)} &= 80 + 180 + 100 + 0 + 24 + 48 + 162 + 275 + 0 + 0 + 112 + 77 + 96 + 288 + 52 \\
&= 1494
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by 2 for two-way traffic:**

$$
LD_{total} = 2 \times 1494 = 2988 \text{ metre-units/day}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If trips are **one-way only** in the question, do **not** multiply by 2. Always read the wording — examiners test this frequently.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Cellular Layout Machine Count

**Problem:** A part family requires the following operations:

| Operation | M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 | M5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Machines needed | 1 Lathe | 2 Drills | 1 Milling | 1 Grinder | 1 CNC |

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total machines required for cell} &= m_{1} + m_{2} + m_{3} + m_{4} + m_{5} \\
&= 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 \\
&= 6 \text{ machines}
\end{aligned}
$$

If daily demand requires part processing time $t = 30 \text{ min/unit}$ and available time = **480 min/day**, then output capacity of cell:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Daily Output} &= \dfrac{480 \text{ min/day}}{30 \text{ min/unit}} = 16 \text{ units/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Line Balancing (Product Layout)

**Problem:** A product line has 6 tasks with times (seconds): 40, 50, 30, 25, 55, 20. Daily demand = **480 units**, available time = **8 hours**.

**Step 1 — Compute Cycle Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT &= \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Demand}} \\
&= \dfrac{8 \times 60 \times 60 \text{ sec}}{480} \\
&= \dfrac{28800}{480} \\
&= 60 \text{ seconds/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum of task times:**

$$
\sum t_{i} = 40 + 50 + 30 + 25 + 55 + 20 = 220 \text{ sec}
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical minimum stations:**

$$
N_{min} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\sum t_{i}}{CT} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{220}{60} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 3.67 \right\rceil = 4 \text{ stations}
$$

**Step 4 — Balance Delay (assuming actual N = 5):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BD &= \dfrac{N \cdot CT - \sum t_{i}}{N \cdot CT} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{5 \times 60 - 220}{5 \times 60} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{300 - 220}{300} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{80}{300} \times 100 \\
&= 26.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 KTU Comparative Analytical Matrix — Master Comparison Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most important table for KTU Module 2 examination.** Memorize it for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) answers.

| Parameter | Process Layout | Product Layout | Fixed Position | Cellular Layout |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Alternative name** | Functional / Job-Shop | Line / Assembly Line | Project / Site Layout | Group Technology Layout |
| **Volume** | Low | High | One-of-a-kind | Medium-to-High |
| **Variety** | High | Low | Very Low | Medium |
| **Capital Investment** | Low | High | Low | Moderate |
| **Flexibility** | Very High | Very Low | Low (project-specific) | High |
| **Material Handling Cost** | High | Low | Very Low | Low |
| **Lead Time** | Long | Short | Very Long | Short |
| **Workforce Skill** | Highly Skilled | Low Skill, Repetitive | Multi-skilled | Multi-skilled |
| **Production Control** | Complex | Simple | Highly Complex | Moderate |
| **Inventory (WIP)** | High | Low | Low | Low |
| **Best Suited For** | Hospital, Tool Room | Car assembly, FMCG | Ship-building, Construction | Engine families, GT cells |
| **Layout Driver** | Process / Function | Product / Sequence | Product Location | Part Family |
| **Equipment Type** | General Purpose | Special Purpose | Portable | Mixed (Cell-specific) |

## 3.5 Selection Logic — Step-by-Step Layout Decision Tree

**Step 1:** Is the product **fixed at one location** (very large/heavy)?

- **YES** → Choose **Fixed Position Layout**.
- **NO** → Go to Step 2.

**Step 2:** Is the demand **very high** with **low variety** and a **standard sequence**?

- **YES** → Choose **Product Layout**.
- **NO** → Go to Step 3.

**Step 3:** Can the part be **classified into families** with similar processing routes?

- **YES** → Choose **Cellular Layout**.
- **NO** → Go to Step 4.

**Step 4:** Default → Choose **Process Layout** (most flexible).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Layout — Functional Department Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1[Receiving] --> node2[Store RM]
    node2 --> node3[Milling Dept]
    node3 --> node4[Grinding Dept]
    node3 --> node5[Turning Dept]
    node4 --> node6[Inspection]
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7[Finished Goods Store]
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node3
```

**Interpretation:** Arrows cross over each other — this **random criss-cross pattern** is the visual signature of a **process layout** (high material handling, low flow efficiency).

## 4.2 Product Layout — Sequential Assembly Line

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1[Station 1 Loading] --> node2[Station 2 Drill]
    node2 --> node3[Station 3 Mill]
    node3 --> node4[Station 4 Inspect]
    node4 --> node5[Station 5 Pack]
    node5 --> node6[Dispatch]
```

**Interpretation:** A **single unidirectional linear flow** with **no backtracking** — the visual signature of a **product layout**. Cycle time is uniform across all stations.

## 4.3 Fixed Position Layout — Radial Resource Convergence

```mermaid
graph TD
    center[Product at Site Ship Hull]
    center --- node1[Cranes]
    center --- node2[Welders]
    center --- node3[Painters]
    center --- node4[Electricians]
    center --- node5[Stores]
    center --- node6[Site Office]
    center --- node7[QA Lab]
```

**Interpretation:** The **product remains central**; all support resources **converge radially** — signature of a **fixed position layout**. No flow arrows between machines — they all flow **inward** to the product.

## 4.4 Cellular Layout — U-Shaped GT Cell

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1[Input Bay] --> node2[Lathe]
    node2 --> node3[Drill Press]
    node3 --> node4[Milling]
    node4 --> node5[Grinder]
    node5 --> node6[Inspect]
    node6 --> node1
    node6 -.-> node7[Output to Next Cell]
```

**Interpretation:** The **loop closure** (node6 → node1) is the **U-shape return path** — operator can manage all machines from inside the U without walking long distances. This is a hallmark of cellular manufacturing.

## 4.5 Master Decision Architecture — Layout Selection Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: New Product] --> B{Is product fixed at site?}
    B -- Yes --> F[Fixed Position Layout]
    B -- No --> C{High volume, low variety?}
    C -- Yes --> P[Product Layout]
    C -- No --> D{Part families exist?}
    D -- Yes --> CL[Cellular Layout]
    D -- No --> PR[Process Layout]
    F --> Z[End Decision]
    P --> Z
    CL --> Z
    PR --> Z
```

## 4.6 Volume-Variety 2x2 Positioning Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Matrix_Volume_Variety
    A1[High Volume Low Variety --> PRODUCT LAYOUT]
    A2[High Volume High Variety --> CELLULAR LAYOUT]
    A3[Low Volume Low Variety --> FIXED POSITION or BATCH]
    A4[Low Volume High Variety --> PROCESS LAYOUT]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick-Draw Tip:** Always include a simple **block diagram** of the recommended layout in your 14-mark answer. It carries **2-3 marks** for visual representation, as per the official valuation scheme.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] — Define *Process Layout*. State any two of its advantages and two disadvantages.

**Model Answer:**
A **Process Layout** (also called **functional layout**) groups together similar machines, equipment, and operations according to the **type of process** performed. Each department specializes in one function (e.g., all lathes together, all welding stations together).

**Advantages:**
1. **High flexibility** — any product requiring that process can be routed to the department.
2. **Better equipment utilization** — machines are not product-specific.
3. **Lower capital investment** — general-purpose machines are cheaper.
4. **Workers become specialists** due to repetitive exposure to one type of process.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Longer material handling distances** and higher handling cost.
2. **Longer production lead times** due to queuing at each department.
3. **Complex production planning and control** — variable routing paths.
4. **High work-in-process (WIP) inventory** between departments.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — Distinguish between *Product Layout* and *Process Layout* on the basis of (i) Volume, (ii) Variety, and (iii) Material Handling.

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Product Layout | Process Layout |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Volume | High volume, mass production | Low volume, batch / job production |
| (ii) Variety | Low variety, standardized product | High variety, customized products |
| (iii) Material Handling | Low — straight-line flow, short distances | High — criss-cross flow, long distances |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **two sub-parts, 7 marks each**, with an **internal choice** (either OR within sub-parts). We provide two complete answer choices below.

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **Cellular Layout** concept. How is it different from Process and Product Layouts? Discuss the role of **Group Technology (GT)** in forming cells. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Cellular Layout** is a hybrid manufacturing layout that groups **dissimilar machines and workstations** into manufacturing **cells**, each dedicated to producing a **family of similar parts** that share common manufacturing characteristics.

**Key Concepts:**

1. **Part Families** — A group of parts having similar design features (geometry, material) or similar manufacturing operations. Identified using **classification and coding systems** such as:
   - **Opitz Code** (uses 9-digit hierarchical coding).
   - **DCLASS (Detroit Free Press)** system.
   - **Production Flow Analysis (PFA)** charts.

2. **Machine Cell Formation Methods:**
   - **Visual Inspection / Inspection-based** (subjective).
   - **Cluster Analysis** (statistical).
   - **Rank Order Clustering (ROC)** algorithm.
   - **Similarity Coefficient Method**.

3. **Cellular Layout Advantages:**
   - **Reduced setup time** and **reduced material handling** (flow within cell is short).
   - **Lower WIP inventory**.
   - **Shorter lead times** (often 60-70% reduction over process layout).
   - **Improved worker morale** (one cell, one team, ownership).
   - **Simplified production planning** (fewer routing decisions).

4. **Distinction from Other Layouts:**

| Aspect | Process | Product | Cellular |
|---|---|---|---|
| Routing | Variable | Fixed | Fixed within family |
| Grouping | By function | By product | By part family |
| Flow | Random | Straight line | U-shaped within cell |

5. **Role of Group Technology:**
   GT is the **engineering methodology** that enables cellular layout. It provides:
   - A **systematic classification** of parts.
   - A **database of parts** grouped by similarity.
   - The **basis for cell formation**.

**(b)** A factory produces **three part families** A, B, and C. Each family requires 4 machines in sequence: Family A → {M1, M2, M3, M5}; Family B → {M2, M4, M1, M6}; Family C → {M3, M5, M6, M4}. **Form manufacturing cells** and identify any inter-cell movement. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — List parts and their machine sequences:**

| Part Family | Required Machine Sequence |
|---|---|
| Family A | M1 → M2 → M3 → M5 |
| Family B | M2 → M4 → M1 → M6 |
| Family C | M3 → M5 → M6 → M4 |

**Step 2 — Form initial cells based on shared machines:**

- **Cell 1** (Family A): M1, M2, M3, M5
- **Cell 2** (Family B): M4, M6 (overlap with Cell 1: M1, M2)
- **Cell 3** (Family C): shares M3, M5 with Cell 1; shares M4, M6 with Cell 2

**Step 3 — Reassign using Rank-Order Clustering logic:**

After grouping by frequency:

- **Cell A** → M1, M2, M3, M5 (processes Family A and partially C)
- **Cell B** → M4, M6 (processes Family B and partially C)
- **Family C** is **split** → Part of C goes to Cell A, rest to Cell B.

**Step 4 — Identify Inter-cell movement (exceptional parts):**

Parts of **Family C** must travel between Cell A and Cell B → **Inter-cell movement** exists.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** Cellular layout reduces but does not eliminate all inter-cell flow. Exceptional parts require management via **duplication** or **sub-contracting**.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q. (a)** What is **Fixed Position Layout**? Explain its **suitability, advantages, and limitations** with **two industrial examples**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Fixed Position Layout** (or **Project Layout**) is one in which the product being produced **remains stationary in one location**, and all the necessary **resources — manpower, machinery, tools, materials, sub-assemblies — are brought to the product site**.

**Suitability Conditions:**
1. Product is **large or heavy** (ship, aircraft, bridge, dam).
2. Product is **fragile or difficult to transport** in its partially completed state.
3. **Project-based** and **one-of-a-kind** production.
4. **Construction time is long** (months to years).

**Advantages:**
1. **No movement of heavy product** — eliminates the cost and difficulty of relocation.
2. **High flexibility** in scheduling different trades and operations.
3. **Reduced risk of damage** during handling.
4. **Continuous access** to the product from all sides.

**Limitations:**
1. **High coordination complexity** — many resources converging at one site.
2. **Movement of workers and equipment** between locations increases cost.
3. **Site congestion** — limited workspace around the product.
4. **Production control is difficult** — overlap of many teams.
5. **High WIP** — components stacked around the site.

**Industrial Examples:**
1. **Shipbuilding** — a 200,000-tonne ship is built in a dry dock; cranes, welders, fitters all come to the hull.
2. **Aircraft manufacturing** — Boeing 787 is assembled in a fixed hangar; wings, fuselage sections, and engines are moved to the central body.
3. *(Bonus)* **Construction of a dam, bridge, or skyscraper**.

**(b)** A product layout produces **480 units/day**. Available production time = **8 hours/day**. The line has tasks with times (in seconds): 35, 50, 40, 25, 30, 45, 20, 35. Compute (i) **Cycle Time**, (ii) **Theoretical minimum number of workstations**, (iii) **Balance Delay** if actual workstations = **6**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Demand $D = 480$ units/day
- Available time $H = 8 \text{ hr} = 28800 \text{ sec}$
- Tasks $t_{i} = \{35, 50, 40, 25, 30, 45, 20, 35\}$

**(i) Cycle Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT &= \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Demand}} \\
&= \dfrac{28800}{480} \\
&= 60 \text{ seconds/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Theoretical Minimum Workstations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum t_{i} &= 35 + 50 + 40 + 25 + 30 + 45 + 20 + 35 = 280 \text{ sec} \\
N_{min} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{\sum t_{i}}{CT} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{280}{60} \right\rceil = \lceil 4.67 \rceil = 5 \text{ stations}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Balance Delay (for actual $N = 6$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BD &= \dfrac{N \cdot CT - \sum t_{i}}{N \cdot CT} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{6 \times 60 - 280}{6 \times 60} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{360 - 280}{360} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{80}{360} \times 100 \\
&= 22.22\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Zones:**
> 1. **NOT rounding up** $N_{min}$ — the ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ is mandatory because fractional stations are impossible.
> 2. **Using demand/day incorrectly** — always convert available time to the **same units** as task time (sec, min, hr) BEFORE dividing.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract** $W_i$ (waiting time) in throughput formulas for process layout.
> 4. **Confusing "Cellular Layout" with "Group Layout"** — Cellular layout is a *type* of group technology arrangement, not a generic cluster.
> 5. **Not including a diagram** in 14-mark answers → 2-3 marks deducted per the KTU valuation rubric.
> 6. **Writing only advantages** without linking to the **selection parameters (Volume-Variety-Process)** in justification answers.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision — go through this list before entering the exam hall.**

- ⭐ **Four Layout Types:** Process, Product, Fixed Position, Cellular — "**P³FC**".
- ⭐ **Volume-Variety Mapping:**
  - High Volume + Low Variety → **Product Layout**.
  - Low Volume + High Variety → **Process Layout**.
  - Very Large / One-of-a-kind → **Fixed Position**.
  - Part Families + Medium Volume → **Cellular Layout**.
- ⭐ **Process Layout** = grouped by **function** (departments).
- ⭐ **Product Layout** = grouped by **sequence of operations** (line flow).
- ⭐ **Fixed Position** = product **stays**, resources **move**.
- ⭐ **Cellular Layout** = **Group Technology (GT)** + **U-shaped cells** + **part families**.
- ⭐ **Load Distance formula:** $LD = \sum n_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}$ — always multiply by 2 for round trips **unless** the question specifies one-way movement.
- ⭐ **Cycle Time formula:** $CT = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Daily Demand}}$.
- ⭐ **Theoretical Minimum Stations:** $N_{min} = \lceil \sum t_{i} / CT \rceil$ — **always ceiling**, never floor.
- ⭐ **Balance Delay** measures **layout inefficiency** — lower is better.
- ⭐ **Group Technology coding systems** to remember: **Opitz**, **DCLASS**, **MICLASS**, **PFA**.
- ⭐ **Cellular layout advantages** to write in any answer: ↓ WIP, ↓ lead time, ↓ handling cost, ↑ morale, ↑ quality.
- ⭐ **Real-world anchors for examiners:** Hospital (Process), Car Plant (Product), Shipyard (Fixed), Honda Engine Plant (Cellular).
- ⭐ **Always draw a block diagram** in 14-mark answers.
- ⭐ **Always justify** using **Volume + Variety + Process requirement**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 Coverage:** Combined with **Capacity Planning, Forecasting, and Layout Selection Models** (CRAFT, CORELAP, ALDEP), this topic typically contributes **2-3 questions** per KTU end-semester paper — one full 14-mark question and one 3-mark definitional question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Systematic Layout Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT863 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Systematic Layout Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Systematic Layout Planning (SLP) — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Systematic Layout Planning (SLP)** is a structured, phase-by-phase methodology developed by **Richard Muther (1973)** for generating an optimal industrial facility layout. It is based on three foundational inputs: **(1) the Product (P)**, **(2) the Quantity (Q)**, and **(3) the Route/Process (R)**. The methodology translates qualitative **Activity Relationship Charts (ARC)** and quantitative **Space Requirements** into a logical, geometry-free layout that minimizes material handling cost and flow distance.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (PEMRT863 – Module 2), Systematic Layout Planning is treated as the **macro-level plant layout design technique** that uses activity-relationship data to produce an *ideal* and a *practical* layout through four structured phases.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Renovation" Intuition

Imagine you are redesigning your home kitchen:

- **Product (P):** The food items (rice, curry, salads, beverages) you cook — each requires different utensils.
- **Quantity (Q):** How often you cook each item (rice daily, sweets rarely).
- **Route (R):** The workflow: Refrigerator → Wash Station → Cutting Table → Stove → Serving Counter.

If the refrigerator is on the opposite wall from the stove, you waste steps carrying vegetables. **SLP** solves exactly this problem in a factory — it mathematically decides **which work area must be NEXT to which**, based on how closely their activities relate. The "closeness rating" is like asking your mother: *"How often does the fridge hand over tomatoes to the chopping board?"* — if the answer is "very often", they must be **adjacent (rated A)**; if "rarely", they can be far apart (rated X or O).

## 1.3 Why SLP? — The Need for a Systematic Approach

| Conventional Trial-and-Error Layouts | Muther's Systematic Layout Planning |
|---|---|
| Based on intuition, past habits | Based on quantified relationships |
| Ignores flow distances | Minimizes total material handling cost |
| Considers only product and quantity | Integrates P–Q–R + Supporting Activities + Time |
| Often produces backtracking flows | Produces logical, near-linear flow patterns |
| Difficult to justify to management | Justifiable through quantitative proximity ratings |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Students must remember the **SLP Framework = 4 Phases**:
> 1. **Phase I — Location** (Where on the plot?)
> 2. **Phase II — Overall Layout** (Blocks of departments)
> 3. **Phase III — Detailed Layout** (Workstations, machines)
> 4. **Phase IV — Installation**

## 1.4 The Three Input Variables (P, Q, R)

### (P) Product — What is being made?
The product defines:
- The **raw material** and its physical form
- The **design specifications** and tolerances
- The **quality standards** required
- The **packaging and shipping** format

For service industries, the equivalent of "Product" is the **Service Package** (e.g., patient flow in a hospital).

### (Q) Quantity — How much is being made?
Quantity influences:
- The **size** of equipment
- The **number** of workstations
- The **flow intensity** between activities
- **Economies of scale** in space usage

> **Heuristic Rule:** When **Q is high**, **Product Layout** (assembly line) is preferred. When **Q is low and varied**, **Process Layout** (functional) is preferred.

### (R) Route / Process — How is it made?
Route specifies:
- The **sequence** of operations
- The **machinery** used at each step
- The **flow pattern** (straight line, U-shape, branch)
- The **cycle time** of each operation

### Supporting (S) Input — The Fourth Often-Missed Variable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **S = Supporting Activities** — maintenance, tool crib, quality lab, scrap yard, cafeteria, washrooms, storeroom. These are **non-productive but indispensable**. KTU examiners often test whether students remember to factor S into SLP.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SLP Procedure — Step-by-Step Logic

Muther's Systematic Layout Planning follows a strict **11-step procedure**. Understanding the *logic* of each step is critical for KTU Module 2 answers.

### Step 1 — Gather Data (P, Q, R, S)
Collect information about the product, quantity of production, the manufacturing route, and supporting services. This is the **foundation phase**.

### Step 2 — Analyze P-Q-R Data
Convert raw data into:
- **Operation Process Charts** (route information)
- **From-To Charts** (frequency of moves between department pairs)
- **Flow Process Charts** (operation vs. transport time)

### Step 3 — Determine Activity Requirements
For each activity, identify:
- Equipment needed
- Workforce size
- Special environmental needs (temperature, dust-free, vibration-free)
- Safety requirements

### Step 4 — Determine Space Requirements (Area Calculation)
For each activity, calculate the **Total Space** using the **Unit Load Principle**.

### Step 5 — Develop Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)
Use Muther's **closeness ratings** to indicate how essential it is that two activities be located close to each other.

### Step 6 — Identify Activity Relationship Diagram (ARD)
Translate the textual ARC into a **diamond-shaped symbolic diagram** showing relationship intensity using closeness codes.

### Step 7 — Establish Space Relationship Diagram (SRD)
Overlay the ARD with scaled-size blocks representing space requirements. This produces the **Ideal Layout**.

### Step 8 — Modify the Ideal Layout
Apply practical constraints:
- Building shape, columns, doors, windows
- Existing equipment positions
- Safety regulations
- Future expansion plans

### Step 9 — Develop Practical Layout Alternatives
Generate **multiple feasible layouts** (typically 3–5 alternatives).

### Step 10 — Evaluate Alternatives
Compare alternatives using quantitative techniques (cost analysis, preference rating, transportation model, CRAFT, CORELAP).

### Step 11 — Select and Implement the Best Layout
Finalize, get management approval, install, and commission.

## 2.2 The Muther Closeness Rating System (Vital for KTU)

The **Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)** uses a six-letter/colour closeness code:

| Closeness Code | Rating | Closeness Type | Typical Reason |
|:---:|:---:|---|---|
| **A** | Absolutely Necessary | Activities MUST be adjacent | Direct material flow, shared worker |
| **E** | Especially Important | Very close proximity required | Major flow, frequent interaction |
| **I** | Important | Adjacent if possible | Significant flow or contact |
| **O** | Ordinary | Acceptable proximity | Some flow, normal contact |
| **U** | Unimportant | Proximity not needed | Rare interaction, no flow |
| **X** | Not Desirable | Should be SEPARATED | Safety, hygiene, noise |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** The letter codes (A, E, I, O, U, X) are *case-insensitive* but the **numeric weighting** used in CRAFT/CORELAP evaluation is a separate scoring system often tested in Module 2.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — SLP Quantitative Tools

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every numerical formula a KTU student must know for SLP problems. **All formulas are verifiable** and standard to Muther's framework.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Space Required** | $A_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i + A_{support}$ | Sum of all activity areas + supporting service area |
| 2 | **Unit Load Area** | $A_u = L \times W$ | Area of one unit load (pallet / bin) |
| 3 | **Number of Unit Loads** | $N_u = Q_{annual} \div Q_{per\,load}$ | Loads = Annual Quantity ÷ Quantity per Load |
| 4 | **Activity Area** | $A_i = N_u \times A_u \times (1 + k)$ | $k$ = allowance factor for aisle, tool space |
| 5 | **Material Handling Cost (MHC)** | $MHC = \sum_{i,j} f_{ij} \times d_{ij} \times c_{ij}$ | $f$ = flow frequency, $d$ = distance, $c$ = cost per unit per meter |
| 6 | **Closeness Score Weight (CRAFT)** | $w(A)=64,\ w(E)=16,\ w(I)=4,\ w(O)=1,\ w(U)=0,\ w(X)=-64$ | Standard CRAFT closeness weights |
| 7 | **Total Closeness Rating (TCR)** | $TCR = \sum_{i=1}^{m} w_i$ | Sum of weighted ratings for a department pair |
| 8 | **Composite Closness Index** | $CCI = \dfrac{\sum w \cdot f}{\sum f}$ | Weighted average closeness per load moved |
| 9 | **Load Distance (LD)** | $LD = \sum_{i,j} f_{ij} \times d_{ij}$ | Sum over all department pairs |
| 10 | **Linear Programming Model** | Minimize $Z = \sum_{i<j} c_{ij} \cdot d_{ij} \cdot x_{ij}$ | Subject to flow conservation constraints |
| 11 | **Allowance Percentage** | $A\% = \dfrac{A_{gross} - A_{net}}{A_{net}} \times 100$ | Standard 10–30 % for aisles & services |
| 12 | **Block Efficiency Ratio** | $\eta = \dfrac{A_{net}}{A_{gross}} \times 100$ | Higher $\eta$ = better space utilization |

## 2.4 The From-To Chart (Input to ARC)

The **From-To Chart** is the tabular form that records the **number of trips (loads)** between every pair of departments.

> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse *From-To* with *Operation Process Chart*. **Operation Process Chart** = sequence of operations on ONE unit. **From-To Chart** = movement frequency between ALL departments.

## 2.5 Why SLP Matters in Real Engineering

- **Manufacturing Plants:** New automotive assembly plants (e.g., Toyota, Tata Motors) use SLP-derived CORELAP/ALDEP algorithms before any civil work begins.
- **Warehousing:** Amazon's fulfillment centers use SLP-derived activity relationships to position receiving → storage → pick → pack → ship in an optimal flow.
- **Hospitals:** Operating theatre, ICU, emergency, and radiology are placed using closeness ratings (A for OT↔ICU, X for kitchen↔ICU).
- **Airports:** Check-in ↔ Security ↔ Gate adjacency is decided by SLP-style closeness ratings weighted by passenger flow.

## 2.6 Activity Relationship Diagram (ARD) — Symbolic Codes

Muther's **diamond symbol** combines multiple relationships visually:

- Inside the diamond, the **closeness letter code** (A, E, I, O, U, X) appears
- On the **left half**, the **reason code** (1–9) is written
- On the **right half**, the **reason code** continues
- A **colour code** (red, yellow, green, blue, grey, brown) duplicates the closeness rating for visual scanning

> **Standard Reason Codes (KTU Frequently Tested):**
> 1 = Material flow · 2 = Hygiene · 3 = Climate control · 4 = Safety · 5 = Shared personnel · 6 = Communication · 7 = Supervision · 8 = Convenience · 9 = Noise control
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Total Activity Space Calculation

**Problem Setup:** A workshop has 4 activities. Each unit load occupies $1.2 \, m^2$, and the annual quantity handled is $12{,}000$ units. Load size = 50 units/load. Allowance factor for aisles = 25 %.

**Step 1 — Number of Unit Loads per Activity (assuming equal distribution)**

$$
N_u = \frac{Q_{annual}}{Q_{per\,load}} = \frac{12{,}000}{50} = 240 \text{ loads}
$$

**Step 2 — Activity Area for ONE department**

$$
A_i = N_u \times A_u \times (1 + k)
$$

$$
A_i = 240 \times 1.2 \times (1 + 0.25)
$$

$$
A_i = 288 \times 1.25 = 360 \, m^2
$$

**Step 3 — Total Net Production Area for 4 activities**

$$
A_{net} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} A_i = 4 \times 360 = 1{,}440 \, m^2
$$

**Step 4 — Add 20 % for Supporting Services**

$$
A_{support} = 0.20 \times 1{,}440 = 288 \, m^2
$$

**Step 5 — Total Gross Plant Area**

$$
A_{gross} = A_{net} + A_{support} = 1{,}440 + 288 = 1{,}728 \, m^2
$$

**Step 6 — Block Efficiency Ratio**

$$
\eta = \frac{A_{net}}{A_{gross}} \times 100 = \frac{1{,}440}{1{,}728} \times 100 = 83.33\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** An $\eta$ above 80 % is considered excellent; below 60 % indicates inefficient space utilization.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Material Handling Cost (MHC) Calculation

**Problem Setup:** Three departments A, B, C exchange loads as follows:

| From → To | Flow Frequency (loads/day) | Distance (m) | Cost (₹/load·m) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A → B | 50 | 30 | 0.50 |
| A → C | 20 | 45 | 0.50 |
| B → C | 30 | 25 | 0.50 |

**Step 1 — Apply the MHC Formula**

$$
MHC = \sum_{i,j} f_{ij} \times d_{ij} \times c_{ij}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each pair contribution**

$$
MHC_{AB} = 50 \times 30 \times 0.50 = 750 \, \text{₹/day}
$$

$$
MHC_{AC} = 20 \times 45 \times 0.50 = 450 \, \text{₹/day}
$$

$$
MHC_{BC} = 30 \times 25 \times 0.50 = 375 \, \text{₹/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Daily Material Handling Cost**

$$
MHC_{total} = 750 + 450 + 375 = 1{,}575 \, \text{₹/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual MHC (assuming 300 working days)**

$$
MHC_{annual} = 1{,}575 \times 300 = 4{,}72{,}500 \, \text{₹/year} \approx ₹ 4.73 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Composite Closeness Index (CCI)

**Problem Setup:** Three pairs of departments have the following closeness ratings and load movements.

| Pair | Rating | Weight $w$ | Loads Moved $f$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A–B | A | 64 | 100 |
| A–C | E | 16 | 200 |
| B–C | I | 4 | 50 |

**Step 1 — Weighted Closeness**

$$
\sum w \cdot f = (64 \times 100) + (16 \times 200) + (4 \times 50)
$$

$$
\sum w \cdot f = 6{,}400 + 3{,}200 + 200 = 9{,}800
$$

**Step 2 — Total Loads Moved**

$$
\sum f = 100 + 200 + 50 = 350
$$

**Step 3 — Composite Closeness Index**

$$
CCI = \frac{\sum w \cdot f}{\sum f} = \frac{9{,}800}{350} = 28.0
$$

> **Interpretation:** A CCI of 28 is between E (16) and A (64), indicating an overall **moderately high** closeness requirement across the system.

---

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Matrix — From-To Chart → ARC Conversion

**Step 1 — Given From-To Data (loads/week between 5 departments):**

| From\To | D1 | D2 | D3 | D4 | D5 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| D1 | — | 120 | 0 | 50 | 30 |
| D2 | 80 | — | 60 | 20 | 10 |
| D3 | 0 | 40 | — | 0 | 70 |
| D4 | 50 | 30 | 0 | — | 90 |
| D5 | 30 | 10 | 90 | 80 | — |

**Step 2 — Total Flow per Pair**

$$
f_{ij}^{total} = f_{ij} + f_{ji}
$$

$$
f_{12} = 120 + 80 = 200
$$

$$
f_{13} = 0 + 0 = 0
$$

$$
f_{14} = 50 + 50 = 100
$$

$$
f_{15} = 30 + 30 = 60
$$

$$
f_{23} = 60 + 40 = 100
$$

$$
f_{24} = 20 + 30 = 50
$$

$$
f_{25} = 10 + 10 = 20
$$

$$
f_{34} = 0 + 0 = 0
$$

$$
f_{35} = 70 + 90 = 160
$$

$$
f_{45} = 90 + 80 = 170
$$

**Step 3 — Map Total Flow to Closeness Rating**

A commonly used threshold for 5-department systems:

| Total Flow | Closeness Rating |
|:---:|:---:|
| $> 150$ | A |
| 100–150 | E |
| 50–99 | I |
| 10–49 | O |
| $< 10$ | U |
| Special (safety/hygiene) | X |

**Step 4 — Resulting ARC**

| Pair | Total Flow | Closeness |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| D1–D2 | 200 | **A** |
| D4–D5 | 170 | **A** |
| D3–D5 | 160 | **A** |
| D2–D3 | 100 | **E** |
| D1–D4 | 100 | **E** |
| D1–D5 | 60 | **I** |
| D2–D4 | 50 | **I** |
| D1–D3 | 0 | **U** |
| D3–D4 | 0 | **X** (assume safety) |
| D2–D5 | 20 | **O** |

> **Engineering Insight:** Departments D1, D2 must be **adjacent**; D3 and D4 must be **separated**; D1 and D3 do not need proximity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SLP Phase-Wise Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 1 Inputs P Q R S] --> B[Phase 2 Operation Process Chart From-To Chart]
    B --> C[Phase 3 Space Requirements Activity Area Calculation]
    C --> D[Phase 4 Activity Relationship Chart ARC]
    D --> E[Phase 5 Activity Relationship Diagram ARD Diamond Code]
    E --> F[Phase 6 Space Relationship Diagram SRD Ideal Layout]
    F --> G[Phase 7 Apply Practical Constraints Columns Walls Doors]
    G --> H[Phase 8 Generate Alternative Layouts 3 to 5 Options]
    H --> I[Phase 9 Quantitative Evaluation MHC CRAFT CORELAP]
    I --> J[Phase 10 Select Best Layout]
    J --> K[Phase 11 Implementation Installation]
```

## 4.2 The Muther ARC Symbol — Diamond Code Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Dia[Diamond Symbol] --> L1[Top Closeness Letter Code A E I O U X]
    Dia --> L2[Left Reason Code Number 1 to 9]
    Dia --> L3[Right Reason Code Number]
    Dia --> L4[Colour Border Red Yellow Green Blue Grey Brown]
    L1 --> Out1[Indicates HOW close]
    L2 --> Out2[Indicates WHY they need to be close]
    L3 --> Out2
    L4 --> Out3[Visual quick scanning aid]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — SLP Decision Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT
        N1[P Product Design Data]
        N2[Q Annual Quantity]
        N3[R Manufacturing Route]
        N4[S Supporting Services]
    end
    subgraph ANALYZE
        N5[Operation Process Chart]
        N6[From-To Chart]
        N7[Flow Process Chart]
    end

        N8[Activity Relationship Chart ARC]
        N9[Closeness Weight Assignment]
    end
    subgraph SPACE
        N10[Space Requirement per Activity]
        N11[Unit Load Principle]
    end
    subgraph DIAGRAM
        N12[Activity Relationship Diagram ARD]
        N13[Space Relationship Diagram SRD]
    end
    subgraph DECIDE
        N14[Ideal Layout]
        N15[Practical Layout Alternatives]
        N16[Best Layout Selected]
    end
    N1 --> N5
    N2 --> N6
    N3 --> N5
    N3 --> N7
    N4 --> N8
    N5 --> N8
    N6 --> N9
    N7 --> N8
    N8 --> N9
    N9 --> N12
    N10 --> N13
    N11 --> N10
    N12 --> N13
    N13 --> N14
    N14 --> N15
    N15 --> N16
```

## 4.4 Closeness Rating Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need material flow between departments?}
    Q1 -- YES, heavy --> A1[A Absolutely Necessary]
    Q1 -- YES, moderate --> A2[E Especially Important]
    Q1 -- YES, light --> A3[I Important]
    Q1 -- NO, only some contact --> A4[O Ordinary]
    Q1 -- NO, rare --> A5[U Unimportant]
    Q1 -- NO, hazardous --> A6[X Not Desirable Separate]
```

## 4.5 SLP Output Sequence — From Idea to Reality

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O1[Raw Data P Q R S] --> O2[ARC Textual Closeness Codes]
    O2 --> O3[ARD Visual Diamond Diagram]
    O3 --> O4[SRD Scaled Ideal Layout]
    O4 --> O5[Modified Practical Layout Block Plan]
    O5 --> O6[Detailed Layout Workstations Aisles Utilities]
    O6 --> O7[Installed Operating Plant]
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Systematic Layout Planning. List its four phases.

**Model Answer:**
Systematic Layout Planning (SLP) is a structured methodology developed by Richard Muther for designing efficient facility layouts using activity relationships and space data, based on four inputs: **Product (P), Quantity (Q), Route (R), and Supporting activities (S)**.

The four phases of SLP are:

1. **Phase I — Location Phase:** Decides the geographic and site location of the plant.
2. **Phase II — General Layout Phase:** Develops the overall block layout showing relative positions of departments.
3. **Phase III — Detailed Layout Phase:** Plans each department — workstations, machines, aisles, utilities.
4. **Phase IV — Installation Phase:** Final physical placement and commissioning.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Explain the six closeness ratings used in an Activity Relationship Chart.

**Model Answer:**
Muther's ARC uses six closeness codes, in descending order of importance:

- **A — Absolutely Necessary:** The two activities MUST be adjacent (e.g., machining and inspection).
- **E — Especially Important:** Adjacent placement is highly preferred (e.g., assembly and sub-assembly).
- **I — Important:** Adjacent if possible; otherwise, very close (e.g., production and quality lab).
- **O — Ordinary Closeness:** Acceptable to be on the same floor; not necessarily adjacent.
- **U — Unimportant:** Proximity is not a factor.
- **X — Not Desirable:** Activities MUST be separated (e.g., dust-generating and cleanroom operations).

Each rating may also carry a **reason code (1–9)** explaining *why* the closeness is required (material flow, safety, etc.).

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Construct the **Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)** for a manufacturing company having 6 departments — Receiving (R), Stores (S), Production (P), Assembly (A), Inspection (I), and Dispatch (D) — based on the following flow data. Use the closeness rating rule: flow > 250 = A, 150–250 = E, 50–149 = I, < 50 = O. Assume I and P must be separated due to dust hazard. **(7 marks)**

| Pair | Weekly Loads |
|:---:|:---:|
| R–S | 300 |
| S–P | 280 |
| P–A | 200 |
| A–I | 260 |
| I–D | 220 |
| R–A | 80 |
| S–D | 60 |
| P–I | 30 |
| A–D | 40 |

**(b)** Develop the **Activity Relationship Diagram (ARD)** and explain how the **Space Relationship Diagram (SRD)** is derived from it. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Constructing the ARC

**Step 1 — Compare each pair's weekly loads with the rating rule:**

$$
f_{RS} = 300 > 250 \Rightarrow \textbf{A}
$$

$$
f_{SP} = 280 > 250 \Rightarrow \textbf{A}
$$

$$
f_{PA} = 200 \in [150,\, 250] \Rightarrow \textbf{E}
$$

$$
f_{AI} = 260 > 250 \Rightarrow \textbf{A}
$$

$$
f_{ID} = 220 \in [150,\, 250] \Rightarrow \textbf{E}
$$

$$
f_{RA} = 80 \in [50,\, 149] \Rightarrow \textbf{I}
$$

$$
f_{SD} = 60 \in [50,\, 149] \Rightarrow \textbf{I}
$$

$$
f_{PI} = 30 < 50 \Rightarrow \textbf{O} \text{ (but overridden)}
$$

$$
f_{AD} = 40 < 50 \Rightarrow \textbf{O}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the safety override:**

Since P (Production, dust) and I (Inspection, clean) must be separated:

$$
f_{PI}: O \rightarrow \textbf{X (overridden for safety)}
$$

**Step 3 — Final ARC Table:**

| Department | R | S | P | A | I | D |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **R** | — | A | O | I | O | O |
| **S** | A | — | A | I | O | I |
| **P** | O | A | — | E | **X** | O |
| **A** | I | I | E | — | A | O |
| **I** | O | O | **X** | A | — | E |
| **D** | O | I | O | O | E | — |

---

#### Part (b) — ARD and SRD Construction

**Activity Relationship Diagram (ARD):**

The ARC above is converted into a **diamond-shaped symbolic diagram**. For each pair of departments, a diamond is drawn with:
- **Top vertex:** Closeness letter (A, E, I, O, U, X)
- **Left/right vertices:** Reason code (e.g., 1 = material flow, 4 = safety)
- **Colour border:** Red for A, Yellow for E, Green for I, Blue for O, Grey for U, Brown for X

**Example diamond for pair (A, I):** Top = **A**, Left = **1** (flow), Right = empty, Colour = **Red**, indicating inspection and assembly must be adjacent due to material flow.

**Space Relationship Diagram (SRD):**

The SRD is the **next stage** after the ARD. It is created by:

**Step 1 — Computing space requirements** for each department using the formula:

$$
A_i = N_u \times A_u \times (1 + k)
$$

**Step 2 — Drawing scaled blocks** representing each department's area on graph paper, preserving the closeness ratings from the ARD.

**Step 3 — Arranging blocks** so that A-rated pairs are touching, E-rated are very close, I-rated are close, and X-rated are far apart.

**Step 4 — The resulting SRD is the "ideal layout"** — geometry-free, scale-accurate, showing the *best possible* relative positioning. Practical constraints (building shape, columns, doors) are later applied to obtain the **practical layout**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** A factory has 4 departments with the following daily material flows and centre-to-centre distances in the current layout:

| Pair | Loads/day | Current Distance (m) | Cost (₹/load·m) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1–2 | 100 | 40 | 0.40 |
| 1–3 | 60 | 60 | 0.40 |
| 1–4 | 40 | 30 | 0.40 |
| 2–3 | 80 | 25 | 0.40 |
| 2–4 | 20 | 50 | 0.40 |
| 3–4 | 90 | 35 | 0.40 |

A proposed layout reduces distances as: 1–2: 20 m, 1–3: 30 m, 1–4: 40 m, 2–3: 15 m, 2–4: 35 m, 3–4: 20 m. Calculate the **annual saving in material handling cost** (working days = 300/year). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **limitations of Systematic Layout Planning** and explain how **computerized layout algorithms (CRAFT/CORELAP)** address these limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Annual Material Handling Cost Saving

**Step 1 — Current Layout Daily MHC:**

$$
MHC_{current} = \sum f_{ij} \times d_{ij} \times c_{ij}
$$

$$
MHC_{current} = (100 \times 40 \times 0.40) + (60 \times 60 \times 0.40) + (40 \times 30 \times 0.40)
$$

$$
+ (80 \times 25 \times 0.40) + (20 \times 50 \times 0.40) + (90 \times 35 \times 0.40)
$$

**Compute each term:**

$$
MHC_{12} = 100 \times 40 \times 0.40 = 1{,}600
$$

$$
MHC_{13} = 60 \times 60 \times 0.40 = 1{,}440
$$

$$
MHC_{14} = 40 \times 30 \times 0.40 = 480
$$

$$
MHC_{23} = 80 \times 25 \times 0.40 = 800
$$

$$
MHC_{24} = 20 \times 50 \times 0.40 = 400
$$

$$
MHC_{34} = 90 \times 35 \times 0.40 = 1{,}260
$$

**Sum:**

$$
MHC_{current} = 1{,}600 + 1{,}440 + 480 + 800 + 400 + 1{,}260 = 5{,}980 \text{ ₹/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Proposed Layout Daily MHC:**

$$
MHC_{12}' = 100 \times 20 \times 0.40 = 800
$$

$$
MHC_{13}' = 60 \times 30 \times 0.40 = 720
$$

$$
MHC_{14}' = 40 \times 40 \times 0.40 = 640
$$

$$
MHC_{23}' = 80 \times 15 \times 0.40 = 480
$$

$$
MHC_{24}' = 20 \times 35 \times 0.40 = 280
$$

$$
MHC_{34}' = 90 \times 20 \times 0.40 = 720
$$

**Sum:**

$$
MHC_{proposed} = 800 + 720 + 640 + 480 + 280 + 720 = 3{,}640 \text{ ₹/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Daily Saving:**

$$
\Delta MHC = 5{,}980 - 3{,}640 = 2{,}340 \text{ ₹/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual Saving (300 working days):**

$$
\text{Annual Saving} = 2{,}340 \times 300 = 7{,}02{,}000 \, \text{₹} = \text{₹ } 7.02 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Limitations of SLP and Computerized Solutions

**Limitations of Manual SLP:**

1. **Trial-and-error intensive:** Generating many practical layout alternatives requires considerable manual effort and intuition.
2. **Limited evaluation:** Manual methods cannot easily quantify total material handling cost across all alternatives.
3. **Subjectivity in rating:** Closeness ratings depend on the planner's judgment and may vary.
4. **Cannot handle large problems:** Beyond 8–10 departments, manual SLP becomes cumbersome.
5. **Ignores dynamic constraints:** Expansion, future product mix, and maintenance access are often overlooked.
6. **No optimization guarantee:** The selected layout may be only locally optimal.

**How CRAFT/CORELAP Address These:**

- **CORELAP (Computerized Relationship Layout Planning):** Uses the closeness ratings from the ARC directly. It automatically places departments with **A** ratings first, then **E**, then **I**, etc., using a ranking algorithm. **Output:** A block layout with the highest total closeness rating.
- **CRAFT (Computerized Relative Allocation of Facilities Technique):** Starts from an existing layout and **iteratively swaps pairs of departments** to minimize the total material handling cost. It computes $MHC$ for each swap and accepts the swap only if cost decreases.
- Both algorithms use the **MHC minimization** objective and can handle **20–40 departments** effortlessly.
- They produce **multiple optimized alternatives** rather than a single subjective decision.
- They enable **sensitivity analysis** — testing how the layout changes with different flow assumptions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Confusing the **From-To Chart** (tabular load frequency) with the **Operation Process Chart** (sequence of operations). These are *different* SLP inputs.
> - **Mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert flow *From-To* entries into **pair-wise totals** before assigning closeness ratings.
> - **Mistake 3:** Using **only material flow** to assign ratings. Muther's system also considers safety, hygiene, and personnel sharing — **reason codes 1–9**.
> - **Mistake 4:** Treating CRAFT and CORELAP as identical. **CORELAP** = uses closeness ratings only (input = ARC). **CRAFT** = uses flow distances and costs (input = From-To + distances).
> - **Mistake 5:** Not multiplying the daily MHC by the number of working days when asked for **annual** cost.
> - **Mistake 6:** Reporting the ARC without including the **reason codes** (1–9) in the diamond symbol — examiners deduct 1–2 marks for incomplete ARD.
> - **Mistake 7:** Omitting the **supporting activities (S)** when discussing the four SLP inputs.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **SLP = 4 Inputs (P, Q, R, S) → 4 Phases → 11 Steps** in the standard Muther procedure.
- ✅ The **six closeness codes** are A, E, I, O, U, X — in **strict descending importance** order.
- ✅ **CRAFT weights** (numerical): A = 64, E = 16, I = 4, O = 1, U = 0, X = –64.
- ✅ The **Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)** is a **textual/tabular** form; the **Activity Relationship Diagram (ARD)** is the **visual diamond-coded** form.
- ✅ The **Space Relationship Diagram (SRD)** adds scaled block areas to the ARD and yields the **ideal layout**.
- ✅ **Practical layout** = ideal layout + constraints (columns, walls, doors, utilities, safety).
- ✅ The **total material handling cost** formula is: $MHC = \sum f_{ij} \times d_{ij} \times c_{ij}$.
- ✅ **Annual MHC** = Daily MHC × working days.
- ✅ **Block Efficiency Ratio** $\eta = \frac{A_{net}}{A_{gross}} \times 100$; aim for **above 80 %**.
- ✅ **CORELAP** uses closeness ratings; **CRAFT** uses flow distances and costs.
- ✅ **Reason codes 1–9** must accompany every ARC entry to justify the closeness rating.
- ✅ **X ratings** are *negative* — they require **separation**, often for safety, hygiene, or noise reasons.
- ✅ **Unit Load Principle** standardizes material handling by grouping items into uniform loads (pallets, bins, totes).
- ✅ **SLP limitation:** for > 10 departments, **computerized algorithms** are mandatory.
- ✅ KTU Module 2 expects students to **construct an ARC, convert to ARD, justify with reason codes, and compute MHC**.
- ✅ Always include **supporting services** (canteen, restroom, scrap yard, tool crib) — they occupy 15–25 % of total plant area.
- ✅ The **Ideal Layout** is geometry-free; the **Practical Layout** is constrained by the building footprint.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layout Design Procedures - CRAFT - CORELAP - ALDEP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT863 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Layout Design Procedures - CRAFT - CORELAP - ALDEP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 What are Layout Design Procedures?

In **Facilities Planning & Layout Design**, after collecting activity relationships (REL charts) and space requirements (Activity Relationship Chart & Space Allocation), the facility planner needs a systematic method to **physically arrange departments** on the floor plan. **Layout Design Procedures** are algorithmic, mathematical, and heuristic methodologies used to generate an optimal or near-optimal arrangement of departments (workcentres, machines, service areas) within a given facility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Layout design procedures are quantitative and qualitative algorithms that assist the facility planner in determining the relative and absolute placement of activities based on material flow, closeness relationships, and area requirements.

The three major **Computerized Layout Planning Algorithms** that KTU expects you to master under this module are:

| Algorithm | Type | Era | Originator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CRAFT** | Improvement | 1963 | Armour & Buffa |
| **CORELAP** | Construction | 1967 | Lee & Moore |
| **ALDEP** | Construction | 1968 | Seehof & Moore |

---

### 1.2 CRAFT — Computerized Relative Allocation of Facilities Technique

**Formal Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
CRAFT is an **improvement-type** layout algorithm that begins with an **initial layout** and systematically exchanges pairs of departments (including splitting and swapping sub-sections) to minimize the **total material handling cost**, computed as the product of distance, material flow volume, and cost per unit distance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you have furniture in your room and want to minimize your walking steps between frequently used items (like bed ↔ wardrobe ↔ study table). You start with one arrangement, then swap two pieces at a time. Each time you swap, count your "walking cost." Keep the swap that saves the most cost. CRAFT does exactly this — but mathematically and for an entire factory.

---

### 1.3 CORELAP — Computerized Relationship Layout Planning

**Formal Definition:**
CORELAP is a **construction-type** algorithm that starts with **no initial layout**. It builds the layout step-by-step by placing the activity having the **highest Total Closeness Rating (TCR)** in the center of an empty grid and successively adds other activities based on their closeness relationships (using codes A, E, I, O, U, X from the REL chart).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of planning a wedding seating arrangement. You first place the most important guest (say, the bride's father) at the center of the head table. Then you place the person who **must sit next to him** on his immediate left, the next closest person on his right, and so on, gradually filling the table. CORELAP builds a factory layout the same way — by anchoring the most "relationship-heavy" department first.

---

### 1.4 ALDEP — Automated Layout Design Program

**Formal Definition:**
ALDEP is a **construction-type, Monte Carlo / probabilistic** algorithm that builds layouts by randomly selecting activities one at a time and placing them adjacent to already-placed activities based on a **weighted preference score** derived from the REL chart. Multiple layouts are generated, and the **best** (highest score) is selected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** ALDEP is like shuffling a deck of playing cards and trying to assemble a poker hand. Each random shuffle produces a different arrangement. ALDEP generates hundreds of "factory shuffles" using weighted randomness (favouring closely-related departments), then keeps the best one.

---

### 1.5 Standard Metrics & Codes Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **REL Chart Closeness Codes (Mutually Used by CORELAP & ALDEP):**
> - **A** = Absolutely Important (closeness value **6**)
> - **E** = Especially Important (closeness value **5**)
> - **I** = Important (closeness value **4**)
> - **O** = Ordinary closeness, OK (closeness value **3**)
> - **U** = Unimportant (closeness value **2**)
> - **X** = Undesirable (closeness value **1**)

The CRAFT algorithm uses **material flow data (loads)** rather than closeness ratings.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 CRAFT — Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

CRAFT operates on three inputs: **(1) Initial Layout**, **(2) Flow Matrix (From-To Chart)**, and **(3) Cost Matrix (cost per unit distance per unit load)**.

**Why CRAFT is called an "Improvement" algorithm:**
It does **not** start from scratch. It takes a pre-existing layout (could be a manually drafted proposal) and tries to **improve** it through pairwise exchanges.

**How CRAFT Works:**

1. Read the initial departmental layout grid.
2. Identify all **unique pairs** of departments (and pairs of equal-area sub-sections).
3. For each pair, hypothetically **exchange** their positions.
4. Recalculate the **Total Material Handling Cost** for both the original and the exchanged layout.
5. Compute **Δ Cost** = (Cost after exchange) − (Cost before exchange).
6. If Δ Cost < 0 (i.e., cost decreases), the exchange is **accepted**; otherwise rejected.
7. Repeat until **no further improvement** is possible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Department Pair Count:** For *n* departments, the number of unique pairs to evaluate is **n(n−1)/2**.

**Special Case — Department Sub-division:**
If two departments have **unequal areas**, CRAFT does **not** swap them outright. Instead, it splits them into multiple equal-area sub-units and tries all possible sub-unit swaps. If a swap is beneficial, **only that sub-section** moves.

**The Cost Formula (KTU High-Yield):**

The Total Material Handling Cost (TMHC) of a layout is computed as:

$$\text{TMHC} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} f_{ij} \cdot d_{ij} \cdot c_{ij}$$

where:
- $f_{ij}$ = flow (load) of material from department *i* to department *j*
- $d_{ij}$ = rectilinear (Manhattan) distance between centroids of *i* and *j*
- $c_{ij}$ = cost per unit distance per unit load between *i* and *j*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** When *i = j* (same department), the term is **omitted** because no internal flow is counted. Distances are computed **centroid-to-centroid** using rectilinear (right-angle) distance, not Euclidean.

**Δ Cost Equation for a Pair Swap:**

$$\Delta C_{pq} = \sum_{k \neq p,q} (f_{pk} - f_{qk}) (d_{qk} - d_{pk}) c_{pk \to qk}$$

The exchange between departments *p* and *q* is profitable if $\Delta C_{pq} < 0$.

---

### 2.2 CORELAP — Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

CORELAP uses the **Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)** as its sole input. It outputs a block layout on a grid.

**Why CORELAP is called a "Construction" algorithm:**
It builds the layout **from zero**, placing one department at a time, much like building a jigsaw puzzle starting from the centre piece.

**How CORELAP Works:**

1. Convert the REL chart into a numerical **Closeness Rating Matrix** (A=6, E=5, I=4, O=3, U=2, X=1).
2. Calculate the **TCR (Total Closeness Rating)** for every department:
$$\text{TCR}(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \text{CR}(i,j)$$
3. Place the department with the **highest TCR** in the centre of an empty grid.
4. For each unplaced department, compute a **Placement Score** for each candidate empty cell adjacent to already-placed departments:
$$\text{Score}(i, \text{cell}) = \sum_{k \in \text{placed}} \text{CR}(i,k) \cdot w(d_{ki})$$
where $w(d_{ki})$ is a weight that decreases with distance.
5. Place the unplaced department in the cell with the **highest score**.
6. **Repeat** until all departments are placed.
7. After each placement, recompute TCRs for remaining unplaced departments (because their relationships with newly placed neighbours are now satisfied).

**Tie-Breaking Rules in CORELAP (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
- **Rule 1:** Highest TCR wins.
- **Rule 2:** If tied, highest number of "A" relationships wins.
- **Rule 3:** If still tied, choose the lower-numbered department ID.
- **Rule 4:** For cell selection, highest score wins; if tied, choose the cell adjacent to the **highest-scoring placed neighbour**.

---

### 2.3 ALDEP — Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

ALDEP is a **probabilistic** construction algorithm.

**How ALDEP Works:**

1. Convert the REL chart to a numerical relationship matrix (using same A–X codes or numeric weights).
2. Start with all activities as **unplaced**.
3. **Randomly select** an initial activity (each unplaced activity has equal probability initially).
4. Place it in the centre of the grid.
5. For the next iteration, compute **placement scores** for every unplaced activity adjacent to the existing layout.
6. Calculate the **probability of selection** for each unplaced activity:
$$P(i) = \frac{\text{Score}(i)}{\sum_{j \in \text{unplaced}} \text{Score}(j)}$$
7. **Randomly select** one activity using these probabilities (Monte Carlo).
8. Place it in the highest-scoring adjacent empty cell.
9. Repeat until all activities are placed → this completes **one layout**.
10. Generate **multiple layouts** (e.g., 50–500) and select the one with the **highest cumulative relationship score**.

**Weighted Score Adjustment in ALDEP:**
The placement score between activity *i* and an already-placed activity *k* is:

$$S(i,k) = \text{Relationship Value}(i,k) \times \text{Adjustment Factor}$$

The Adjustment Factor increases as more adjacent cells are filled around *k*, preventing departments from clustering at one corner.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Algorithm | Key Formula | Symbol Meaning | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| CRAFT | $\text{TMHC} = \sum \sum f_{ij} \cdot d_{ij} \cdot c_{ij}$ | $f$ = flow, $d$ = distance, $c$ = cost | $i \neq j$, rectilinear distance |
| CRAFT | $\Delta C_{pq} = \sum_{k \neq p,q} (f_{pk} - f_{qk})(d_{qk} - d_{pk})$ | Benefit of swapping *p* and *q* | Accept if $\Delta C < 0$ |
| CRAFT | Pairs to evaluate $= n(n-1)/2$ | *n* = number of departments | Excludes *i = j* |
| CORELAP | $\text{TCR}(i) = \sum_{j} \text{CR}(i,j)$ | Total closeness rating of *i* | CR(A)=6, CR(E)=5, CR(I)=4, CR(O)=3, CR(U)=2, CR(X)=1 |
| CORELAP | $\text{Score}(i,\text{cell}) = \sum \text{CR}(i,k) \cdot w(d)$ | Placement preference | Higher score = preferred |
| ALDEP | $P(i) = \frac{\text{Score}(i)}{\sum \text{Score}(j)}$ | Probability of picking *i* | Probabilities sum to 1 |
| ALDEP | $S(i,k) = \text{RelVal}(i,k) \times \text{AdjFactor}$ | Placement score | AdjFactor increases as *k* fills |
| All | Rectilinear distance $d = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert$ | Centroid-to-centroid | Use \vert for absolute value in text |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** CRAFT is widely used in **warehouse and manufacturing** layout design where **material flow data** is available. CORELAP and ALDEP are preferred in **office, hospital, and service-facility** layouts where **qualitative closeness** is more relevant than quantitative flow.

---

### 2.5 Comparison Table (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Feature | CRAFT | CORELAP | ALDEP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Algorithm Type | Improvement | Construction | Construction |
| Starting Point | Needs initial layout | Builds from scratch | Builds from scratch |
| Primary Input | Flow matrix + Cost matrix | REL chart (A–X) | REL chart (A–X) |
| Objective | Minimize material handling cost | Maximize closeness satisfaction | Maximize cumulative relationship score |
| Search Method | Pairwise exchange (deterministic) | Greedy heuristic | Probabilistic (Monte Carlo) |
| Optimality | Local optimum | Local optimum | Local optimum (from many trials) |
| Number of Layouts Generated | One (iteratively improved) | One | Multiple (best selected) |
| Computational Speed | Slow (many swaps evaluated) | Fast | Moderate |
| Data Requirement | High (quantitative flow data) | Low (qualitative REL) | Low (qualitative REL) |

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — CRAFT Cost Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A facility has **3 departments A, B, C** placed on a 3×3 grid as follows (each cell is 1 unit × 1 unit, centroids at cell centres):

```
+---------+---------+---------+
|         |         |         |
|    A    |    B    |    C    |
|  (0.5,2.5) (1.5,2.5) (2.5,2.5) |
+---------+---------+---------+
```

The flow matrix $f_{ij}$ (loads per week) is:

| From \ To | A | B | C |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 100 | 50 |
| **B** | 80 | 0 | 70 |
| **C** | 40 | 60 | 0 |

Cost per unit distance per unit load $c_{ij} = \$0.01$ for all pairs.

**Step 1: Compute Centroid Coordinates**
- Department A centroid: $(x_A, y_A) = (0.5, 2.5)$
- Department B centroid: $(x_B, y_B) = (1.5, 2.5)$
- Department C centroid: $(x_C, y_C) = (2.5, 2.5)$

**Step 2: Compute Rectilinear Distances**

$$d_{AB} = \vert 0.5 - 1.5 \vert + \vert 2.5 - 2.5 \vert = 1.0 + 0 = 1.0 \text{ unit}$$

$$d_{AC} = \vert 0.5 - 2.5 \vert + \vert 2.5 - 2.5 \vert = 2.0 + 0 = 2.0 \text{ units}$$

$$d_{BC} = \vert 1.5 - 2.5 \vert + \vert 2.5 - 2.5 \vert = 1.0 + 0 = 1.0 \text{ unit}$$

**Step 3: Compute Material Handling Cost for Each Pair**

For pair A–B (counting flow in both directions):
$$\text{Cost}(A,B) = f_{AB} \cdot d_{AB} \cdot c + f_{BA} \cdot d_{BA} \cdot c$$

Since $d_{AB} = d_{BA}$ (distance is symmetric):
$$\text{Cost}(A,B) = (f_{AB} + f_{BA}) \cdot d_{AB} \cdot c$$
$$\text{Cost}(A,B) = (100 + 80) \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.01 = 180 \cdot 0.01 = \$1.80$$

For pair A–C:
$$\text{Cost}(A,C) = (50 + 40) \cdot 2.0 \cdot 0.01 = 90 \cdot 0.02 = \$1.80$$

For pair B–C:
$$\text{Cost}(B,C) = (70 + 60) \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.01 = 130 \cdot 0.01 = \$1.30$$

**Step 4: Total Material Handling Cost (TMHC)**

$$\text{TMHC}_{\text{initial}} = \text{Cost}(A,B) + \text{Cost}(A,C) + \text{Cost}(B,C)$$

$$\text{TMHC}_{\text{initial}} = 1.80 + 1.80 + 1.30 = \$4.90$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the Only Possible Swap (A ↔ C)**

If we swap A and C, the new positions become:
- A at $(2.5, 2.5)$, C at $(0.5, 2.5)$

New distances:
$$d_{AB}' = 1.0, \quad d_{AC}' = 2.0, \quad d_{BC}' = 1.0$$

The distances **do not change** because the topology is preserved (B is in the middle). Therefore:
$$\text{TMHC}_{\text{after swap}} = \$4.90$$

$$\Delta C = 4.90 - 4.90 = 0$$

No improvement possible. The current layout is locally optimal.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** In this symmetric case, CRAFT would need to consider **2-D rearrangements** (e.g., moving B elsewhere) for improvement. Single-pair swaps only re-order along one axis when departments are on the same row.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — CRAFT with Different Grid Topology

**Problem Statement:**
Same 3 departments, but arranged differently:

```
+---------+---------+---------+
|    A    |         |    C    |
|  (0.5,2.5)        (2.5,2.5) |
+---------+---------+---------+
|    B    |         |         |
|  (0.5,0.5)                    |
+---------+---------+---------+
```

Distances:
$$d_{AB} = \vert 0.5 - 0.5 \vert + \vert 2.5 - 0.5 \vert = 0 + 2.0 = 2.0$$
$$d_{AC} = \vert 0.5 - 2.5 \vert + \vert 2.5 - 2.5 \vert = 2.0 + 0 = 2.0$$
$$d_{BC} = \vert 0.5 - 2.5 \vert + \vert 0.5 - 2.5 \vert = 2.0 + 2.0 = 4.0$$

**Initial TMHC:**
$$\text{Cost}(A,B) = (100+80) \cdot 2.0 \cdot 0.01 = 180 \cdot 0.02 = \$3.60$$
$$\text{Cost}(A,C) = (50+40) \cdot 2.0 \cdot 0.01 = 90 \cdot 0.02 = \$1.80$$
$$\text{Cost}(B,C) = (70+60) \cdot 4.0 \cdot 0.01 = 130 \cdot 0.04 = \$5.20$$

$$\text{TMHC}_{\text{initial}} = 3.60 + 1.80 + 5.20 = \$10.60$$

**Now swap B ↔ C** (B goes to top-right, C goes to bottom-left):

New centroids: B at $(2.5, 2.5)$, C at $(0.5, 0.5)$.
- $d_{AB}' = \vert 0.5-2.5\vert + \vert 2.5-2.5\vert = 2.0$
- $d_{AC}' = \vert 0.5-0.5\vert + \vert 2.5-0.5\vert = 2.0$
- $d_{BC}' = \vert 2.5-0.5\vert + \vert 2.5-0.5\vert = 4.0$

Again, distances unchanged (symmetric diagonal). To get improvement, we must try **non-symmetric swaps** or split cells.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — CORELAP TCR & Placement

**Problem Statement:**
Four departments A, B, C, D with the following REL chart:

| | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | — | E | O | U |
| **B** | E | — | A | I |
| **C** | O | A | — | E |
| **D** | U | I | E | — |

Using closeness values: A=6, E=5, I=4, O=3, U=2, X=1.

**Step 1: Build the Numerical Closeness Matrix**

| | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | — | 5 | 3 | 2 |
| **B** | 5 | — | 6 | 4 |
| **C** | 3 | 6 | — | 5 |
| **D** | 2 | 4 | 5 | — |

**Step 2: Compute TCR for Each Department**

$$\text{TCR}(A) = 5 + 3 + 2 = 10$$
$$\text{TCR}(B) = 5 + 6 + 4 = 15$$
$$\text{TCR}(C) = 3 + 6 + 5 = 14$$
$$\text{TCR}(D) = 2 + 4 + 5 = 11$$

**Step 3: Identify the First Department to Place**

The **highest TCR = 15 → Department B**. Place B at the centre of the grid.

**Step 4: Identify the Second Department**

Recompute TCR excluding already-placed B (it is satisfied):
$$\text{TCR}_{\text{new}}(A) = 3 + 2 = 5$$
$$\text{TCR}_{\text{new}}(C) = 3 + 5 = 8$$
$$\text{TCR}_{\text{new}}(D) = 2 + 5 = 7$$

Highest remaining: **C with TCR = 8**. Place C adjacent to B.

**Step 5: Place the Third Department**

$$\text{TCR}_{\text{new}}(A) = 2 \text{ (only D remains)}$$
$$\text{TCR}_{\text{new}}(D) = 2 \text{ (only A remains)}$$

Both tied at 2. Apply **Tie-Breaker Rule 2**: count "A" relationships.
- A has 0 "A" relationships remaining with D.
- D has 0 "A" relationships remaining with A.
Still tied. Apply **Rule 3**: lower ID → **Department A** placed next.

**Step 6: Place the Final Department D in the remaining cell.**

**Final Layout (CORELAP Output):**

```
+-----+-----+
|  C  |  B  |
+-----+-----+
|  A  |  D  |
+-----+-----+
```

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — ALDEP Probability Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
Continuing from the CORELAP example, after placing B in the centre, suppose 3 departments {A, C, D} are unplaced. Their relationship scores with B are:
- Score(A, B) = 5
- Score(C, B) = 6
- Score(D, B) = 4

Compute the probability of selecting each for the next placement.

**Step 1: Sum the Scores**

$$\sum \text{Score} = 5 + 6 + 4 = 15$$

**Step 2: Compute Individual Probabilities**

$$P(A) = \frac{5}{15} = 0.3333$$
$$P(C) = \frac{6}{15} = 0.4000$$
$$P(D) = \frac{4}{15} = 0.2667$$

**Verification:**
$$P(A) + P(C) + P(D) = 0.3333 + 0.4000 + 0.2667 = 1.0000 \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Department C has a **40% chance** of being placed next — the highest, because it has an "A" (Absolutely Important) relationship with B.

---

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Summary

| Step | CRAFT | CORELAP | ALDEP |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Read initial layout | Convert REL to CR matrix | Convert REL to numeric matrix |
| 2 | Compute TMHC of initial layout | Compute TCR for all departments | Compute initial scores |
| 3 | Try pairwise swaps | Place highest TCR dept at centre | Randomly pick initial dept |
| 4 | Compute ΔC for each swap | Score each empty cell for next dept | Score & apply probabilities |
| 5 | Accept best swap | Place highest-score dept | Randomly select via probability |
| 6 | Repeat until no improvement | Repeat until all placed | Repeat until all placed |
| 7 | Single output layout | Single output layout | Multiple outputs → pick best |

## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 CRAFT Algorithm Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read Initial Layout] --> B[Read Flow Matrix F and Cost Matrix C]
    B --> C[Compute Initial TMHC using Sum of f x d x c]
    C --> D[Generate All Unique Pairs n(n-1)/2]
    D --> E[For Each Pair Hypothetically Swap Departments]
    E --> F[Recompute TMHC after Swap]
    F --> G{Swap Reduces TMHC}
    G -- Yes --> H[Accept Swap and Update Layout]
    G -- No --> I[Reject Swap]
    H --> J[Update Best Layout]
    I --> J
    J --> K{All Pairs Evaluated}
    K -- No --> E
    K -- Yes --> L{Any Improvement This Iteration}
    L -- Yes --> D
    L -- No --> M[Output Final Improved Layout and Stop]
```

### 4.2 CORELAP Algorithm Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start: Read REL Chart] --> Q[Convert A E I O U X to Numerical CR Values]
    Q --> R[Compute TCR for All Departments]
    R --> S[Place Department with Highest TCR at Grid Centre]
    S --> T{All Departments Placed}
    T -- No --> U[Recompute TCR Excluding Placed Departments]
    U --> V[For Each Unplaced Dept Score All Adjacent Empty Cells]
    V --> W[Place Dept with Highest Score in Best Cell]
    W --> T
    T -- Yes --> X[Apply Tie Breakers if Needed]
    X --> Y[Output Final CORELAP Layout and Stop]
```

### 4.3 ALDEP Algorithm Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Start: Read REL Chart] --> N[Convert REL to Numeric Relationship Matrix]
    N --> O[Initialize All Departments as Unplaced]
    O --> P[Randomly Select First Department Equal Probability]
    P --> Q[Place First Department at Grid Centre]
    Q --> R{All Departments Placed in Current Layout}
    R -- No --> S[Compute Placement Score for Each Unplaced Department]
    S --> T[Convert Scores to Probabilities P of i equals Score i divided by Sum of Scores]
    T --> U[Randomly Select Next Department Using Probabilities]
    U --> V[Place in Highest Scoring Adjacent Empty Cell]
    V --> R
    R -- Yes --> W[Record Layout Score]
    W --> X{Desired Number of Layouts Generated}
    X -- No --> O
    X -- Yes --> Y[Compare All Layouts and Select Best]
    Y --> Z[Output Final Best Layout and Stop]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Inputs vs Outputs of All Three Algorithms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        direction TB
        IN1[Initial Layout Grid]
        IN2[From To Flow Matrix]
        IN3[Cost Matrix]
        IN4[Activity Relationship Chart REL]
        IN5[Department Area Requirements]
    end

    subgraph CRAFT_Engine[CRAFT Engine]
        direction TB
        C1[Pairwise Exchange Evaluator]
        C2[TMHC Calculator]
        C3[Delta C Comparator]
    end

    subgraph CORELAP_Engine[CORELAP Engine]
        direction TB
        CO1[TCR Calculator]
        CO2[Cell Score Ranker]
        CO3[Greedy Placer]
    end

    subgraph ALDEP_Engine[ALDEP Engine]
        direction TB
        A1[Random Selector]
        A2[Probability Calculator]
        A3[Multi Layout Generator]
    end

    subgraph Outputs
        direction TB
        OUT1[Improved Block Layout CRAFT]
        OUT2[Greedy Construction Layout CORELAP]
        OUT3[Best of N Random Layouts ALDEP]
    end

    IN1 --> CRAFT_Engine
    IN2 --> CRAFT_Engine
    IN3 --> CRAFT_Engine

    IN4 --> CORELAP_Engine
    IN5 --> CORELAP_Engine

    IN4 --> ALDEP_Engine
    IN5 --> ALDEP_Engine

    CRAFT_Engine --> OUT1
    CORELAP_Engine --> OUT2
    ALDEP_Engine --> OUT3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Architecture:** CRAFT requires **quantitative flow and cost data** as inputs (richer but harder to obtain). CORELAP and ALDEP only require **qualitative REL chart data** (easier to obtain from management interviews). This is why CORELAP/ALDEP are more popular in **office and hospital** layouts where precise flow data is unavailable.

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the CRAFT algorithm in detail. Describe its inputs, computational procedure, and the role of pair-wise exchanges. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A facility has 4 departments placed on a 4×1 single-row grid as follows:

```
+-----+-----+-----+-----+
|  1  |  2  |  3  |  4  |
+-----+-----+-----+-----+
```

Centroids are at cell centres (0.5, 1.5, 2.5, 3.5). The from-to flow matrix is:

| From \ To | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | 0 | 200 | 0 | 50 |
| **2** | 100 | 0 | 150 | 0 |
| **3** | 0 | 100 | 0 | 100 |
| **4** | 50 | 0 | 100 | 0 |

Cost per unit distance per unit load = Rs. 0.05. Calculate the **Total Material Handling Cost (TMHC)** of this initial layout and identify the single best pair-wise swap that reduces it. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — CRAFT Algorithm Explanation (7 Marks):**

**CRAFT (Computerized Relative Allocation of Facilities Technique)** was developed by **Armour and Buffa in 1963**. It is an **improvement-type** layout algorithm.

**Inputs of CRAFT (2 marks):**
1. **Initial Layout** — a pre-existing arrangement of departments (could be a manually drafted proposal).
2. **From-To Flow Matrix (F)** — quantitative material flow between every pair of departments.
3. **Cost Matrix (C)** — cost per unit distance per unit load for each pair.
4. **Department Area Requirements.**

**Computational Procedure (3 marks):**

1. Compute the **TMHC** of the initial layout using:
$$\text{TMHC} = \sum_{i<j} (f_{ij} + f_{ji}) \cdot d_{ij} \cdot c_{ij}$$
2. Identify all **n(n−1)/2 unique pairs** of departments.
3. For each pair, **hypothetically exchange** their positions and recompute TMHC.
4. Compute **ΔC = TMHC_after − TMHC_before**.
5. Accept the swap that gives the **largest cost reduction** (most negative ΔC).
6. Repeat until **no swap yields further improvement** (local optimum reached).

**Role of Pair-wise Exchanges (2 marks):**
Pair-wise exchange is the **core search mechanism** of CRAFT. By swapping two departments at a time, CRAFT explores the **local neighbourhood** of the current layout. If departments have **unequal areas**, CRAFT splits them into equal sub-sections and swaps sub-sections. This allows **partial movement** and yields finer solutions than whole-department swaps.

---

**Part (b) — TMHC Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute Centroid Coordinates (1 mark)**
- Dept 1: $(x_1, y_1) = (0.5, 0.5)$ — assuming y-coordinate is 0.5 for all (single row).
- Dept 2: $(x_2, y_2) = (1.5, 0.5)$
- Dept 3: $(x_3, y_3) = (2.5, 0.5)$
- Dept 4: $(x_4, y_4) = (3.5, 0.5)$

**Step 2: Compute Rectilinear Distances (1 mark)**
- $d_{12} = \vert 0.5 - 1.5 \vert = 1.0$
- $d_{13} = \vert 0.5 - 2.5 \vert = 2.0$
- $d_{14} = \vert 0.5 - 3.5 \vert = 3.0$
- $d_{23} = 1.0$, $d_{24} = 2.0$, $d_{34} = 1.0$

**Step 3: Compute Cost for Each Pair (3 marks)**

| Pair | $(f_{ij} + f_{ji})$ | $d_{ij}$ | $c$ | Cost (Rs.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1–2 | 200 + 100 = 300 | 1.0 | 0.05 | $300 \times 1.0 \times 0.05 = 15.00$ |
| 1–3 | 0 + 0 = 0 | 2.0 | 0.05 | $0$ |
| 1–4 | 50 + 50 = 100 | 3.0 | 0.05 | $100 \times 3.0 \times 0.05 = 15.00$ |
| 2–3 | 150 + 100 = 250 | 1.0 | 0.05 | $250 \times 1.0 \times 0.05 = 12.50$ |
| 2–4 | 0 + 0 = 0 | 2.0 | 0.05 | $0$ |
| 3–4 | 100 + 100 = 200 | 1.0 | 0.05 | $200 \times 1.0 \times 0.05 = 10.00$ |

**Step 4: TMHC of Initial Layout (1 mark)**

$$\text{TMHC}_{\text{initial}} = 15.00 + 0 + 15.00 + 12.50 + 0 + 10.00 = \textbf{Rs. 52.50}$$

**Step 5: Identify Best Swap (1 mark)**

We need to find the swap that minimizes TMHC. Since departments 2 and 3 have the **highest combined flow (250)** and are **currently adjacent (distance 1)**, this pair is already well-placed. The **best swap candidate** is to evaluate all pairs and compare ΔC.
