- **Hospitality industry** uses part-time + overtime to manage peak tourist seasons in Kerala (Onam, Christmas).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

### 3.1 KTU Standard Numerical Setup

> [!NOTE] KTU Past Year Pattern
> KTU 14-mark numericals on aggregate planning **always** present:
> (i) A demand table for 4–6 months, (ii) a cost matrix, (iii) a productivity rate per worker, and (iv) require computation of total cost for a specified strategy.

### 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example (Hybrid Comparison)

**Problem Statement:**
A firm in Kochi produces industrial valves. It wants to compare two aggregate planning strategies — **Level Strategy** and **Chase Strategy** — for the next 6 months.

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Initial Inventory | $I_0$ | 0 units |
| Productivity per worker per month | $p$ | 50 units |
| Initial Workforce | $W_0$ | 4 workers |
| Regular-time cost | $c_r$ | ₹ 200/unit |
| Overtime cost | $c_{ot}$ | ₹ 300/unit |
| Holding cost | $c_h$ | ₹ 40/unit/month |
| Backorder cost | $c_b$ | ₹ 100/unit/month |
| Hiring cost | $c_{hire}$ | ₹ 5,000/worker |
| Firing cost | $c_{fire}$ | ₹ 8,000/worker |

**Forecasted Demand (units):**

| Month (t) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | Total |
|-----------|---|---|---|---|---|---|-------|
| Demand $D_t$ | 200 | 300 | 400 | 500 | 400 | 300 | **2100** |

---

### Step 1 — Apply Level Strategy

**Production per month (level):**

$$P^{level} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{6} D_t - I_0 + I_T}{T} = \frac{2100 - 0 + 0}{6} = 350 \text{ units/month}$$

**Workforce required:**

$$W^{level} = \left\lceil \frac{350}{50} \right\rceil = 7 \text{ workers (constant)}$$

Since initial workforce is 4 workers, hire 3 workers in Month 1 only.

**Inventory Tracking (using $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$):**

| Month | Demand $D_t$ | Production $P_t$ | Beginning Inv $I_{t-1}$ | Ending Inv $I_t$ |
|-------|--------------|-----------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | 200 | 350 | 0 | +150 |
| 2 | 300 | 350 | 150 | +200 |
| 3 | 400 | 350 | 200 | +150 |
| 4 | 500 | 350 | 150 | 0 |
| 5 | 400 | 350 | 0 | −50 |
| 6 | 300 | 350 | −50 | 0 |

**Cost Computation for Level Strategy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Production Cost} &= c_r \cdot \sum P_t = 200 \times (350 \times 6) = \text{₹ } 4{,}20{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Hiring Cost} &= c_{hire} \times \max(7 - 4,\, 0) = 5000 \times 3 = \text{₹ } 15{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Holding Cost} &= c_h \times \sum \max(I_t,\, 0) = 40 \times (150 + 200 + 150 + 0) = \text{₹ } 20{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Backorder Cost} &= c_b \times \sum \max(-I_t,\, 0) = 100 \times (50 + 0) = \text{₹ } 5{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Total Cost (Level)} &= 4{,}20{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 5{,}000 = \text{₹ } 4{,}60{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 2 — Apply Chase Strategy

**Production per month:** $P_t^{chase} = D_t$ for every month.

**Workforce per month:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 &= \lceil 200/50 \rceil = 4 \\
W_2 &= \lceil 300/50 \rceil = 6 \\
W_3 &= \lceil 400/50 \rceil = 8 \\
W_4 &= \lceil 500/50 \rceil = 10 \\
W_5 &= \lceil 400/50 \rceil = 8 \\
W_6 &= \lceil 300/50 \rceil = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Workforce Transitions and Adjustment Costs:**

| Transition | From | To | ΔW | Action | Cost |
|------------|------|----|----|--------|------|
| M0 → M1 | 4 | 4 | 0 | None | 0 |
| M1 → M2 | 4 | 6 | +2 | Hire 2 | ₹ 10,000 |
| M2 → M3 | 6 | 8 | +2 | Hire 2 | ₹ 10,000 |
| M3 → M4 | 8 | 10 | +2 | Hire 2 | ₹ 10,000 |
| M4 → M5 | 10 | 8 | −2 | Fire 2 | ₹ 16,000 |
| M5 → M6 | 8 | 6 | −2 | Fire 2 | ₹ 16,000 |

**Cost Computation for Chase Strategy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Production Cost} &= 200 \times (200+300+400+500+400+300) = 200 \times 2100 = \text{₹ } 4{,}20{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Hiring Cost} &= 5000 \times 6 = \text{₹ } 30{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Firing Cost} &= 8000 \times 4 = \text{₹ } 32{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Holding Cost} &= 0 \text{ (no buildup)} \\[4pt]
\text{Backorder Cost} &= 0 \text{ (no shortage)} \\[4pt]
\text{Total Cost (Chase)} &= 4{,}20{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 32{,}000 = \text{₹ } 4{,}82{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Decision and Recommendation

$$
\Delta TC = TC_{Chase} - TC_{Level} = 4{,}82{,}000 - 4{,}60{,}000 = \text{₹ } 22{,}000
$$

**Recommendation:** Since $TC_{Level} < TC_{Chase}$, the firm should adopt the **Level Strategy**.

> [!IMPORTANT] Decision Rule
> When comparing two strategies, the **minimum total cost** strategy is selected — unless qualitative factors (e.g., labor union restrictions, customer service level) override cost. Always tabulate costs month-by-month before aggregation to avoid arithmetic errors.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Strategy Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Use)

| Criterion | Chase | Level | Mixed |
|-----------|-------|-------|-------|
| Inventory Level | Low | High | Moderate |
| Workforce Stability | Low | High | Moderate |
| Hiring/Firing Costs | High | Low | Moderate |
| Holding Cost | Low | High | Moderate |
| Customer Service | Low (may backorder) | High | High |
| Worker Morale | Low | High | Moderate |
| Suitability | Skilled labor scarcity | Stable demand | Most real cases |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Aggregate Planning Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Demand Forecast D_t"] --> B{"AP Strategy Selection"}
    B --> C1["Chase Strategy"]
    B --> C2["Level Strategy"]
    B --> C3["Mixed Strategy"]
    B --> C4["Subcontracting"]
    B --> C5["Overtime / Undertime"]
    B --> C6["Part-Time Workers"]
    C1 --> D["Cost Computation Block"]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    D --> E["Total Cost Aggregation"]
    E --> F{"Minimum Cost Strategy"}
    F --> G["Final Aggregate Plan"]
    H["Cost Parameters c_r, c_h, c_b, c_hire, c_fire"] --> D
    I["Initial Inventory I_0"] --> D
    J["Productivity p"] --> D
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Cost Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Production Qty P_t"] --> P1["Regular Time Cost c_r"]
    P --> P2["Overtime Cost c_ot"]
    P --> P3["Subcontract Cost c_s"]
    W["Workforce W_t"] --> W1["Hiring Cost c_hire"]
    W --> W2["Firing Cost c_fire"]
    I["Inventory Position I_t"] --> I1["Holding Cost c_h"]
    I --> I2["Backorder Cost c_b"]
    P1 --> TC["Total Cost TC"]
    P2 --> TC
    P3 --> TC
    W1 --> TC
    W2 --> TC
    I1 --> TC
    I2 --> TC
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — Demand Pattern and Strategy Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Demand Patterns
        D1["Stable Demand"] --- S1["Level Strategy"]
        D2["Highly Variable Demand"] --- S2["Chase Strategy"]
        D3["Moderately Variable Demand"] --- S3["Mixed Strategy"]
        D4["Short Peak Spikes"] --- S4["Overtime + Subcontract"]
    end
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Aggregate Planning System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input["INPUT LAYER: Forecast, Costs, Initial Inventory"] --> Process["PROCESS LAYER: Compute P_t, W_t, I_t per Strategy"]
    Process --> Output["OUTPUT LAYER: Cost Matrix per Strategy"]
    Output --> Decision["DECISION LAYER: Select Min-Cost Strategy"]
    Decision --> Exec["EXECUTION LAYER: Master Production Schedule MPS"]
    Feedback["FEEDBACK LAYER: Compare Actual vs Forecast"] --> Input
    Exec --> Feedback
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define aggregate planning. List any four aggregate planning strategies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Aggregate planning is a medium-range (3–18 months) tactical planning process that determines the overall levels of production output, inventory, workforce, and subcontracting to meet forecasted demand at minimum total cost, while satisfying organizational constraints.
- **Four strategies (½ mark each):**
  1. Chase Strategy
  2. Level Strategy
  3. Mixed / Hybrid Strategy
  4. Subcontracting Strategy (alternatives: Overtime, Part-time workers)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Distinguish between Chase Strategy and Level Strategy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Chase Strategy | Level Strategy |
|-------|---------------|----------------|
| Production rate | Variable, matches demand | Constant throughout horizon |
| Workforce | Fluctuates (hire/fire) | Constant |
| Inventory | Near zero | Builds up or backorders |
| Costs | High hiring/firing | High holding/backorder |
| Worker morale | Low | High |

---

### Part B — Full-Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE] KTU 2024 Pattern
> Part B questions on Aggregate Planning **must** present an internal choice between a **pure Level vs Chase comparison problem** and a **Mixed strategy / Transportation-method problem**. Students are expected to answer **only one** of the two choices.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Level vs Chase Comparison

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** A manufacturing firm forecasts demand for the next 5 months as: Month 1 = 1200 units, Month 2 = 1500 units, Month 3 = 1800 units, Month 4 = 1600 units, Month 5 = 1300 units. Initial workforce = 30 workers; productivity = 50 units/worker/month; initial inventory = 100 units. Cost data: Regular time = ₹ 100/unit, Holding = ₹ 20/unit/month, Backorder = ₹ 50/unit/month, Hiring = ₹ 4000/worker, Firing = ₹ 6000/worker.

**Sub-part (a) — 7 marks:** Compute the total cost under the **Level Strategy**. Show all monthly inventory calculations.

**Model Solution:**

$$
P^{level} = \frac{(1200+1500+1800+1600+1300) - 100 + 0}{5} = \frac{7300}{5} = 1460 \text{ units/month}
$$

$$
W^{level} = \lceil 1460/50 \rceil = \lceil 29.2 \rceil = 30 \text{ workers}
$$

No hiring needed (already 30 workers).

| Month | Demand | Production | Beg Inv | End Inv |
|-------|--------|-----------|---------|---------|
| 1 | 1200 | 1460 | 100 | 360 |
| 2 | 1500 | 1460 | 360 | 320 |
| 3 | 1800 | 1460 | 320 | −20 |
| 4 | 1600 | 1460 | −20 | −160 |
| 5 | 1300 | 1460 | −160 | 0 |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Prod Cost} &= 100 \times (1460 \times 5) = \text{₹ } 7{,}30{,}000 \\
\text{Holding Cost} &= 20 \times (360+320) = \text{₹ } 13{,}600 \\
\text{Backorder Cost} &= 50 \times (20+160) = \text{₹ } 9{,}000 \\
\text{Hiring Cost} &= 0 \\
\text{TC (Level)} &= \text{₹ } 7{,}52{,}600
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sub-part (b) — 7 marks:** Compute the total cost under the **Chase Strategy** and recommend the better option.

**Model Solution:**

$P_t^{chase} = D_t$, so workforce per month: $W_t = \lceil D_t / 50 \rceil$.

$$
W_1 = 24,\; W_2 = 30,\; W_3 = 36,\; W_4 = 32,\; W_5 = 26
$$

| Transition | From | To | Hire | Fire | Hire Cost | Fire Cost |
|------------|------|----|------|------|-----------|-----------|
| M0→M1 | 30 | 24 | 0 | 6 | 0 | 36,000 |
| M1→M2 | 24 | 30 | 6 | 0 | 24,000 | 0 |
| M2→M3 | 30 | 36 | 6 | 0 | 24,000 | 0 |
| M3→M4 | 36 | 32 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 24,000 |
| M4→M5 | 32 | 26 | 0 | 6 | 0 | 36,000 |
| **Total** | | | 12 | 16 | ₹ 48,000 | ₹ 96,000 |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Prod Cost} &= 100 \times 7300 = \text{₹ } 7{,}30{,}000 \\
\text{Hire + Fire Cost} &= 48{,}000 + 96{,}000 = \text{₹ } 1{,}44{,}000 \\
\text{TC (Chase)} &= \text{₹ } 8{,}74{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recommendation:** Since $TC_{Level}$ (₹ 7,52,600) < $TC_{Chase}$ (₹ 8,74,000), the **Level Strategy** should be adopted. Saving = ₹ 1,21,400.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Mixed Strategy with Overtime / Subcontracting

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** A firm has demand: M1=500, M2=700, M3=900, M4=800, M5=600. Cost data: Regular = ₹ 50/unit, Overtime = ₹ 75/unit, Subcontract = ₹ 90/unit, Holding = ₹ 10/unit/month, Backorder = ₹ 25/unit/month. Productivity = 100 units/worker/month; Initial workforce = 5 workers; Overtime capacity per worker per month = 20 units.

**Sub-part (a) — 7 marks:** Formulate a mixed plan using regular-time + overtime. Assume 5 regular workers throughout and use overtime to meet peak demand.

**Model Solution:**

Regular capacity per month = $5 \times 100 = 500$ units.
Maximum overtime capacity per month = $5 \times 20 = 100$ units (so max output = 600 units/month on regular+OT).

| Month | Demand | Regular Prod | Overtime | Shortfall (Backorder) |
|-------|--------|--------------|----------|------------------------|
| 1 | 500 | 500 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 700 | 500 | 100 | 100 |
| 3 | 900 | 500 | 100 | 300 |
| 4 | 800 | 500 | 100 | 500 |
| 5 | 600 | 500 | 100 | 600 (but only 600 demanded, so actual OT=100) |

Re-computing with cumulative backorders resolved at month-end:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Regular Production Cost} &= 50 \times (500 \times 5) = \text{₹ } 1{,}25{,}000 \\
\text{Overtime Cost} &= 75 \times (100 \times 4) = \text{₹ } 30{,}000 \\
\text{Backorder Cost} &= 25 \times (100 + 300 + 500 + 500) = \text{₹ } 35{,}000 \\
\text{TC (Mixed Regular+OT)} &= \text{₹ } 1{,}90{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sub-part (b) — 7 marks:** Compare with subcontracting the entire excess demand. Recommend the cheaper option.

**Model Solution:**

Excess demand over 500/month (Months 2–5): 200 + 400 + 300 + 100 = 1000 units.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Regular Production Cost} &= 50 \times 2500 = \text{₹ } 1{,}25{,}000 \\
\text{Subcontract Cost} &= 90 \times 1000 = \text{₹ } 90{,}000 \\
\text{TC (Subcontract)} &= \text{₹ } 2{,}15{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recommendation:** Since ₹ 1,90,000 (Regular+OT) < ₹ 2,15,000 (Subcontract), the **Mixed strategy with overtime** is preferred. Savings = ₹ 25,000.

---

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning
> Common mistakes costing students 2–4 marks:
> 1. **Forgetting to include hiring cost** in Chase strategy when initial workforce ≠ required workforce.
> 2. **Mis-signing backorder cost** — backorders are *positive* cost, but inventory level is *negative*. Always use $\max(-I_t, 0)$.
> 3. **Ignoring ceiling function** — workforce must be an integer; always apply $\lceil \cdot \rceil$.
> 4. **Forgetting to subtract initial inventory** in the level strategy formula.
> 5. **Not converting units per worker per month** properly if data is given in days or weeks.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Aggregate planning horizon:** **3 to 18 months** (medium-range tactical planning).
- **Pure strategies:** Chase and Level. **Mixed/Hybrid** is the most practically used.
- **Inventory balance identity (memorize):** $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t + S_t + OT_t + PT_t - D_t$.
- **Level strategy production rate:** $P^{level} = \dfrac{\sum D_t - I_0 + I_T}{T}$.
- **Chase strategy production rate:** $P_t = D_t$ for all $t$.
- **Cost components (all 7 must be checked):** Regular, Overtime, Subcontract, Holding, Backorder, Hire, Fire.
- **Workforce rounding rule:** Always **round up** using $\lceil \cdot \rceil$.
- **Decision rule:** Choose the strategy with **minimum total cost**, unless qualitative factors dominate.
- **Holding cost** applies only when $I_t > 0$; **Backorder cost** applies only when $I_t < 0$.
- **High-yield keyword for theory:** Aggregate planning sits between **long-range strategic planning** (capacity, location) and **short-range scheduling** (MPS, MRP).
- **Real-world anchor:** Most Indian manufacturing firms use a **mixed strategy** combining level workforce + overtime + selective subcontracting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Materials Requirement Planning: Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT863 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Materials Requirement Planning: Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Materials Requirement Planning (MRP)

**Materials Requirement Planning (MRP)** is a computer-based production planning and inventory control system used to manage dependent demand items, schedule production, and ensure material availability in manufacturing and non-manufacturing environments. It translates the **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** into a detailed plan for raw materials, components, and sub-assemblies required to manufacture finished products.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> *MRP is a dependent demand system that uses the Bill of Materials (BOM), inventory status data, and the Master Production Schedule (MPS) to compute the timing and quantities of material orders, ensuring that materials are available when needed while minimizing inventory holding costs.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**The "Restaurant Kitchen Analogy":**

Imagine you are running a busy restaurant kitchen. You know the chef's forecast for tomorrow (the **Master Schedule** — say, 200 biryanis). To make biryani, you need a structured **recipe** — rice, chicken, spices, oil (**Bill of Materials**). You also know what is currently sitting in your pantry (**Inventory Status**). Now, instead of randomly ordering 500 kg of rice, you precisely calculate:
- How much rice is needed for 200 biryanis?
- How much rice is already in the pantry?
- When must the new rice arrive so that it doesn't arrive too early (wasting storage) or too late (halting production)?

This **calculation, scheduling, and ordering logic** is exactly what MRP does in a manufacturing plant — only with bolts, gears, electronic components, and engines instead of rice and chicken.

### 1.3 Core Objectives of MRP

The fundamental objectives of an MRP system, as per KTU board standards, are summarized below:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Objectives of MRP (KTU Board High-Yield Points):**
> 1. **Ensure Material Availability** — Materials must be available when needed to maintain the production schedule.
> 2. **Maintain Minimum Inventory Levels** — Keep inventory at the lowest possible level to reduce holding costs.
> 3. **Plan Manufacturing Activities** — Provide a clear production plan for components and sub-assemblies.
> 4. **Plan Procurement Activities** — Determine the timing and quantity of purchase orders.
> 5. **Reduce Setup and Production Costs** — Optimize lot sizing and scheduling efficiency.

### 1.4 The Three Core Pillars (Inputs) of MRP

| Pillar | Description | Real-World Equivalent |
|--------|-------------|----------------------|
| **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** | What finished products are needed, when, and in what quantities | The restaurant's daily sales forecast |
| **Bill of Materials (BOM)** | A structured list of all raw materials, components, and sub-assemblies needed to make one unit of finished product | The recipe card |
| **Inventory Status File (ISF)** | Current on-hand quantities, scheduled receipts, and lead times for each item | The pantry stock register |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of MRP Objectives

The objectives of MRP are not arbitrary — they emerge from the fundamental **dependent demand** nature of manufactured items. Let's analyze the "Why" behind each objective:

#### Objective 1: Determine Quantity and Timing of Material Requirements
- **Why:** In a dependent demand environment, the need for a component is *derived* from the need for its parent item. Without a structured calculation, managers either over-order (creating excess inventory) or under-order (creating shortages).
- **How:** MRP uses **explosion logic** — it "explodes" the BOM level by level to determine gross requirements for every component.

#### Objective 2: Ensure Material Availability
- **Why:** Production stoppages due to material unavailability can cost up to **10–15% of plant revenue** in the manufacturing industry.
- **How:** MRP plans order releases such that **Planned Order Receipts** coincide exactly with **Net Requirements**, offset by the **Lead Time (LT)**.

#### Objective 3: Minimize Inventory Investment
- **Why:** Holding excess inventory ties up working capital. The classic rule is that holding costs are roughly **20–30% of inventory value per year**.
- **How:** MRP uses **Lot-Sizing Rules** (like Lot-for-Lot, Economic Order Quantity, Period Order Quantity) to balance ordering cost vs. holding cost.

#### Objective 4: Plan Manufacturing Activities Effectively
- **Why:** Without MRP, shop floor supervisors operate reactively, leading to bottlenecks and chaos.
- **How:** MRP generates **Planned Order Releases** that act as dispatch orders for the production floor.

#### Objective 5: Plan Purchasing Activities Efficiently
- **Why:** Procurement delays are a primary cause of supply chain disruption.
- **How:** MRP generates **Purchase Order Requisitions** aligned with supplier lead times.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — MRP Computational Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the **core MRP computational formulas** that the KTU board expects students to master. Note: There are **no vertical pipe symbols** inside any formula; absolute value notations use `\vert` or `\mid`.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Purpose |
|---|-------------|------------------------|---------|
| 1 | **Gross Requirements (GR)** | $GR_t = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (MPS_i \times BOM_{i,j})$ | Total demand for item $j$ in period $t$ |
| 2 | **Scheduled Receipts (SR)** | $SR_t = \text{Open Purchase Orders in period } t$ | Incoming confirmed orders |
| 3 | **Projected On Hand (POH)** | $POH_t = POH_{t-1} + SR_t + PR_t - GR_t$ | Expected inventory at end of period $t$ |
| 4 | **Net Requirements (NR)** | $NR_t = \max(0,\ GR_t - POH_{t-1} - SR_t)$ | Additional production needed |
| 5 | **Planned Order Receipts (PR)** | $PR_t = NR_t$ | Planned arrival of new orders |
| 6 | **Planned Order Release (POR)** | $POR_t = PR_{t+LT}$ | Order must be released $LT$ periods earlier |
| 7 | **Safety Stock Adjustment** | $NR_t = \max(0,\ GR_t + SS - POH_{t-1} - SR_t)$ | Incorporates buffer stock |
| 8 | **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)** | $Q = NR_t$ | Order exactly the net requirement |
| 9 | **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | Optimal lot size |
| 10 | **Period Order Quantity (POQ)** | $Q = D \times N^* \quad \text{where} \quad N^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2D}{H}} \cdot \dfrac{P}{D}$ | Covers an optimal number of periods |

Where:
- $D$ = Annual Demand
- $S$ = Ordering Cost per Order
- $H$ = Holding Cost per Unit per Year
- $LT$ = Lead Time
- $SS$ = Safety Stock
- $N^*$ = Optimal Number of Periods
- $P$ = Setup Cost (for POQ)

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

MRP is the **backbone of modern ERP systems** (SAP, Oracle, Tally ERP 9). In Kerala's manufacturing sector — particularly in companies like **HMT, Keltron, and Cochin Shipyard** — MRP-driven systems are deployed to:
- Coordinate complex multi-level BOMs (e.g., a ship has 2 million components).
- Just-in-time replenish component supply chains.
- Integrate with shop floor control and capacity planning modules.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: MRP Table Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A company manufactures **Tables**. The BOM has **two levels**:
> - **Level 0:** Finished Product — Table
> - **Level 1:** 4 Legs + 1 Tabletop
>
> **Given Data:**
> - Master Production Schedule: 100 Tables in Week 4, 150 Tables in Week 5
> - On-Hand Inventory (Legs): 200 units; (Tabletop): 50 units
> - Lead Time for both components: **2 weeks**
> - Lot-Sizing Rule: **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)**

### 3.2 Step 1: Compute Gross Requirements (GR)

$$GR_{\text{Legs}} = 100 \times 4 = 400 \text{ units in Week 4}$$
$$GR_{\text{Legs}} = 150 \times 4 = 600 \text{ units in Week 5}$$

$$GR_{\text{Tabletop}} = 100 \times 1 = 100 \text{ units in Week 4}$$
$$GR_{\text{Tabletop}} = 150 \times 1 = 150 \text{ units in Week 5}$$

> **Logic:** For every 1 Table, we need 4 Legs and 1 Tabletop. So we multiply the MPS quantity by the BOM quantity per parent unit.

### 3.3 Step 2: Compute Net Requirements (NR)

We apply the formula $NR_t = \max(0,\ GR_t - POH_{t-1} - SR_t)$ week by week.

**For Legs:**
$$NR_{W4} = \max(0,\ 400 - 200 - 0) = 200 \text{ units}$$
$$NR_{W5} = \max(0,\ 600 - 0 - 0) = 600 \text{ units}$$

**For Tabletop:**
$$NR_{W4} = \max(0,\ 100 - 50 - 0) = 50 \text{ units}$$
$$NR_{W5} = \max(0,\ 150 - 0 - 0) = 150 \text{ units}$$

> **Logic:** On-hand inventory of 200 Legs satisfies the first 200 units of the 400-unit demand in Week 4, leaving 200 units short. The on-hand 50 Tabletops covers part of the 100-unit demand, leaving 50 short.

### 3.4 Step 3: Compute Planned Order Releases (POR)

Using $POR_t = PR_{t+LT}$, with $LT = 2$:

**For Legs:**
$$POR_{W2} = PR_{W4} = 200 \text{ units}$$
$$POR_{W3} = PR_{W5} = 600 \text{ units}$$

**For Tabletop:**
$$POR_{W2} = PR_{W4} = 50 \text{ units}$$
$$POR_{W3} = PR_{W5} = 150 \text{ units}$$

> **Logic:** Since the lead time is 2 weeks, an order placed in Week 2 will arrive (be received) in Week 4. This ensures material availability exactly when needed.

### 3.5 Complete MRP Table (Analytical Matrix)

#### **Item: Table Legs** | On-Hand: 200 | Lead Time: 2 weeks | L4L

| Week | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|------|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Gross Requirements** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 400 | 600 |
| **Scheduled Receipts** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| **Projected On Hand (200)** | 200 | 200 | 200 | 0 | 0 |
| **Net Requirements** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 200 | 600 |
| **Planned Order Receipts** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 200 | 600 |
| **Planned Order Release** | 0 | **200** | **600** | 0 | 0 |

#### **Item: Tabletop** | On-Hand: 50 | Lead Time: 2 weeks | L4L

| Week | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|------|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Gross Requirements** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 100 | 150 |
| **Scheduled Receipts** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| **Projected On Hand (50)** | 50 | 50 | 50 | 0 | 0 |
| **Net Requirements** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 50 | 150 |
| **Planned Order Receipts** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 50 | 150 |
| **Planned Order Release** | 0 | **50** | **150** | 0 | 0 |

### 3.6 Interpretation

The procurement department should now issue **two purchase orders** for Table Legs:
- One for **200 units** to be released in **Week 2** (arrives Week 4)
- One for **600 units** to be released in **Week 3** (arrives Week 5)

And **two purchase orders** for Tabletops:
- One for **50 units** to be released in **Week 2** (arrives Week 4)
- One for **150 units** to be released in **Week 3** (arrives Week 5)

This precisely fulfills the **primary objective of MRP** — ensuring materials arrive **on time**, in the **correct quantity**, with the **lowest possible inventory**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MRP System Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete MRP processing architecture** — from raw inputs through logical processing to operational outputs. This is a Board-favorite diagram for understanding how MRP objectives translate into system functioning.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA["Master Production Schedule MPS"]:::inputStyle
    inputB["Bill of Materials BOM"]:::inputStyle
    inputC["Inventory Status File ISF"]:::inputStyle

    procNode["MRP Processing Engine Logic"]:::processStyle
    subA["Gross Requirements Calculation"]:::subProcess
    subB["Net Requirements Calculation"]:::subProcess
    subC["Lot Sizing Algorithm"]:::subProcess
    subD["Lead Time Offsetting"]:::subProcess

    outputA["Planned Order Releases"]:::outputStyle
    outputB["Purchase Order Requisitions"]:::outputStyle
    outputC["Shop Floor Production Orders"]:::outputStyle
    outputD["Inventory Status Update"]:::outputStyle

    obj1["Objective Material Availability"]:::objectiveStyle
    obj2["Objective Minimize Inventory"]:::objectiveStyle
    obj3["Objective Production Planning"]:::objectiveStyle
    obj4["Objective Procurement Planning"]:::objectiveStyle

    inputA --> procNode
    inputB --> procNode
    inputC --> procNode

    procNode --> subA
    procNode --> subB
    procNode --> subC
    procNode --> subD

    subA --> outputA
    subB --> outputB
    subC --> outputC
    subD --> outputD

    outputA --> obj1
    outputB --> obj2
    outputC --> obj3
    outputD --> obj4

    classDef inputStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef processStyle fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef subProcess fill:#e6f7ff,stroke:#1890ff,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef outputStyle fill:#d9f7be,stroke:#52c41a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef objectiveStyle fill:#ffccc7,stroke:#cf1322,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 MRP Objectives Hierarchy

The following diagram maps the **four key objectives of MRP** in a hierarchical structure, showing how they support the overarching goal of **production efficiency**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    root["MRP System Goal"]:::rootStyle

    obj1["Ensure Material Availability"]:::level1Style
    obj2["Minimize Inventory Costs"]:::level1Style
    obj3["Plan Manufacturing Activities"]:::level1Style
    obj4["Plan Procurement Activities"]:::level1Style

    subA1["Schedule materials on time"]:::level2Style
    subA2["Reduce stockouts"]:::level2Style
    subB1["Use lot sizing rules"]:::level2Style
    subB2["Lower holding costs"]:::level2Style
    subC1["Generate production orders"]:::level2Style
    subC2["Optimize shop floor flow"]:::level2Style
    subD1["Generate purchase orders"]:::level2Style
    subD2["Coordinate supplier lead times"]:::level2Style

    root --> obj1
    root --> obj2
    root --> obj3
    root --> obj4

    obj1 --> subA1
    obj1 --> subA2
    obj2 --> subB1
    obj2 --> subB2
    obj3 --> subC1
    obj3 --> subC2
    obj4 --> subD1
    obj4 --> subD2

    classDef rootStyle fill:#722ed1,stroke:#391085,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef level1Style fill:#ffd666,stroke:#d48806,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef level2Style fill:#b5f5ec,stroke:#13c2c2,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 MRP Explosion Logic (BOM Tree)

The following diagram depicts how a **Bill of Materials explosion** cascades from the finished product down through components and sub-components — a core visual that supports the MRP objective of **timely material availability**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    level0["Level 0 Finished Product Table Qty 100"]:::level0Style
    level1A["Level 1 Table Top Qty 100"]:::level1Style
    level1B["Level 1 Table Leg Qty 400"]:::level1Style
    level2A["Level 2 Wood Plank Qty 200"]:::level2Style
    level2B["Level 2 Wood Plank Qty 800"]:::level2Style
    level2C["Level 2 Screw Qty 800"]:::level2Style

    level0 --> level1A
    level0 --> level1B
    level1A --> level2A
    level1B --> level2B
    level1B --> level2C

    classDef level0Style fill:#f9c0ff,stroke:#c41d7f,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef level1Style fill:#adc6ff,stroke:#2f54eb,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef level2Style fill:#d3adf7,stroke:#722ed1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q1. Define Materials Requirement Planning (MRP) and state any two of its primary objectives.**

**Model Answer:**

MRP is a computer-based dependent demand planning system that uses the Master Production Schedule, Bill of Materials, and Inventory Status File to determine the timing and quantity of material orders. Its primary objectives are:
1. **Ensuring material availability** — the right material at the right time.
2. **Minimizing inventory investment** — keeping holding costs low through systematic ordering.

> *(1 Mark for definition + 2 Marks for any two objectives = 3 Marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q2. List the three inputs of an MRP system and briefly explain the role of the Bill of Materials.**

**Model Answer:**

The three inputs of MRP are:
1. **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** — what to produce, when, and how much.
2. **Bill of Materials (BOM)** — a structured list of components needed per unit.
3. **Inventory Status File (ISF)** — current stock and lead times.

The **Bill of Materials (BOM)** is a hierarchical product structure that lists every raw material, component, and sub-assembly required to manufacture one unit of the finished product. It is essential because MRP uses BOM to "explode" the parent demand into component-level gross requirements.

> *(1 Mark each for listing inputs + 1 Mark for BOM explanation = 3 Marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Exam – Module 4 Standard Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. (a)** Define Materials Requirement Planning. Explain in detail the **four major objectives** of an MRP system. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** MRP is a computer-based production planning and inventory control system designed to manage dependent demand items. It converts the Master Production Schedule into a time-phased plan for procurement and production.

**Four Major Objectives:**

| # | Objective | Detailed Explanation |
|---|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Material Availability** | Ensures raw materials and components are available precisely when needed to execute the MPS, eliminating production stoppages. |
| 2 | **Minimum Inventory Investment** | Reduces the carrying cost of inventory by ordering only the net requirement, avoiding both stockouts and overstocking. |
| 3 | **Planning of Manufacturing Activities** | Provides the shop floor with precise start dates, completion dates, and quantities for each component and sub-assembly. |
| 4 | **Planning of Purchase Activities** | Generates time-phased purchase orders aligned with supplier lead times, ensuring smooth procurement. |

> *(2 Marks for definition + 5 Marks for the 4 objectives with explanation = 7 Marks)*

---

**Q.A. (b)** The Master Production Schedule for a finished product is **200 units in Week 3** and **150 units in Week 4**. The product requires **2 units of Component X** per finished unit. On-hand inventory of Component X is **100 units**, and lead time is **1 week**. Using Lot-for-Lot rule, prepare the MRP table for Component X. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Gross Requirements (GR)**

$$GR_{W3} = 200 \times 2 = 400 \text{ units}$$
$$GR_{W4} = 150 \times 2 = 300 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2: Compute Net Requirements (NR)** using Lot-for-Lot

$$NR_{W3} = \max(0,\ 400 - 100 - 0) = 300 \text{ units}$$
$$NR_{W4} = \max(0,\ 300 - 0 - 0) = 300 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3: Planned Order Releases (POR)** with LT = 1 week

$$POR_{W2} = PR_{W3} = 300 \text{ units}$$
$$POR_{W3} = PR_{W4} = 300 \text{ units}$$

**MRP Table for Component X:**

| Week | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|------|---|---|---|---|
| **Gross Requirements** | 0 | 0 | 400 | 300 |
| **Scheduled Receipts** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| **Projected On Hand (100)** | 100 | 100 | 0 | 0 |
| **Net Requirements** | 0 | 0 | 300 | 300 |
| **Planned Order Receipts** | 0 | 0 | 300 | 300 |
| **Planned Order Release** | 0 | **300** | **300** | 0 |

> **Conclusion:** Two purchase orders must be released — one of 300 units in Week 2 and another of 300 units in Week 3.

> *(2 Marks for GR + 2 Marks for NR + 2 Marks for POR + 1 Mark for the table = 7 Marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. (a)** Explain the role of the **three inputs of MRP** (MPS, BOM, and Inventory Status File) in achieving the objectives of MRP. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Master Production Schedule (MPS) – 2 Marks:**
The MPS specifies the **quantities and timings** of finished products to be produced. It acts as the **demand driver** for MRP. Without a clear MPS, MRP cannot translate finished-goods demand into component-level planning. The MPS ensures MRP fulfills its objective of **synchronizing material availability with production needs**.

**2. Bill of Materials (BOM) – 3 Marks:**
The BOM is the **product structure file** that defines what components, sub-assemblies, and raw materials are required to make one unit of the parent item. It provides the **multiplier** used in gross requirement calculations. Through BOM explosion logic, MRP cascades demand from level 0 down to the lowest raw material level, enabling **detailed manufacturing planning** and supporting the objective of **planning all production activities**.

**3. Inventory Status File (ISF) – 2 Marks:**
The ISF maintains **current on-hand inventory, scheduled receipts, and lead times** for every item. It serves as the **starting point** for net requirement calculations. By deducting current stock from gross requirements, MRP minimizes excess ordering, directly supporting the objective of **minimizing inventory investment**.

> **Integration:** Together, these three inputs enable MRP to perform time-phased planning, generate planned order releases, and achieve its four primary objectives — availability, low inventory, manufacturing planning, and procurement planning.

> *(2 + 3 + 2 = 7 Marks)*

---

**Q.B. (b)** Briefly explain **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)** and **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** as lot-sizing rules used in MRP. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Lot-for-Lot (L4L) – 3 Marks:**
The Lot-for-Lot rule orders **exactly the net requirement** in each period.
- **Advantages:** Zero excess inventory, simple to implement, no holding cost.
- **Disadvantages:** High ordering cost due to frequent orders.
- **Formula:** $Q_t = NR_t$

**2. Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) – 4 Marks:**
EOQ determines the **fixed lot size** that minimizes the total of ordering cost and holding cost.

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$$

Where:
- $D$ = Annual demand
- $S$ = Ordering cost per order
- $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year

**Derivation:** EOQ is derived by minimizing the Total Cost function:

$$TC(Q) = \dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S + \dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H$$

Taking the first derivative with respect to $Q$ and setting it to zero:

$$\dfrac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\dfrac{DS}{Q^2} + \dfrac{H}{2} = 0$$

Solving for $Q$:

$$\dfrac{H}{2} = \dfrac{DS}{Q^2}$$

$$Q^2 = \dfrac{2DS}{H}$$

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$$

> **Advantages of EOQ:** Lower total cost, fewer orders, balanced inventory.
> **Disadvantage:** Lot sizes are not aligned with weekly net requirements, so extra inventory may accumulate.

> *(3 + 4 = 7 Marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in MRP Problems (KTU Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Planned Order Receipts with Planned Order Releases:** Students often enter PR in the same week as POR. **Remember:** POR is **offset back by lead time** ($POR_t = PR_{t+LT}$).
>
> 2. **Forgetting to update Projected On Hand:** The POH for Week $t$ becomes the beginning POH for Week $t+1$. Many students keep the same POH across weeks, causing cascading errors.
>
> 3. **Not multiplying by BOM quantity:** A common error is using MPS demand directly as gross requirement without multiplying by BOM units per parent. **Always:** $GR = MPS \times BOM$.
>
> 4. **Mixing up inputs of MRP:** Students sometimes list "Forecasts" or "Lead Times" as separate inputs. The correct **3 inputs** are strictly: **MPS, BOM, ISF**.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Net Requirement formula:** When on-hand is sufficient, $NR = 0$. Students sometimes carry forward negative values. Use $\max(0, \cdot)$ formulation strictly.
>
> 6. **No drawing of MRP table:** Even when computation is correct, **a clean, well-labeled MRP table is mandatory** for full marks in the KTU valuation scheme.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — MRP Objectives**
>
> - **MRP** = **Materials Requirement Planning** — a **dependent demand** planning system.
> - **Core definition to memorize:** "MRP is a computer-based system that uses MPS, BOM, and ISF to plan material orders."
> - **Three inputs (mandatory to list):** Master Production Schedule, Bill of Materials, Inventory Status File.
> - **Four primary objectives:**
>   - Ensure material availability
>   - Minimize inventory investment
>   - Plan manufacturing activities
>   - Plan procurement activities
> - **Two additional objectives:** Reduce setup costs; improve customer service through on-time delivery.
> - **MRP formulas to memorize (in order):** GR → SR → POH → NR → PR → POR (offset by LT).
> - **Key computational mantra:** "Net Requirement = Gross Requirement − (On Hand + Scheduled Receipts)."
> - **Lot-for-Lot rule:** Order exactly the net requirement.
> - **EOQ formula:** $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — derived by minimizing total cost.
> - **BOM Explosion Logic:** Demand cascades level by level from finished product → sub-assembly → component → raw material.
> - **KTU board tip:** Always present the final answer in a **proper MRP table format** with all 6 rows clearly labeled.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Master Production Schedule
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT863 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Master Production Schedule # Master Production Schedule (MPS) — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 4 Focus** > This topic forms the operational backbone of any **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** system. The MPS is the bridge between high-level planning (Aggregate/Aggregation Planning) and shop-floor execution (Shop Floor Control). ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** is a detailed time-phased plan that specifies **what end items (finished products) are to be produced, in what quantities, and when they are needed to meet forecasted demand and customer orders**. It is typically expressed in weekly or daily time buckets over a planning horizon of approximately **3 to 18 months**, and it serves as the primary input to the **MRP (Material Requirements Planning)** system. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the MPS is described as a **disaggregation of the aggregate production plan** into specific finished products, individual part numbers, and exact delivery dates. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Restaurant Kitchen Prep Sheet" Analogy** > > Imagine you run a busy restaurant. The **Aggregate Plan** says, "In Week 3, we expect 500 main-course covers." That's useless to the chef — he needs to know: **how many chicken biryanis, how many paneer butter masalas, and exactly at what times must each be ready?** That detailed, item-by-item, time-stamped list for the chef is the **Master Production Schedule**. The MPS translates the broad business goal into an executable shop-floor (kitchen) instruction. | Planning Level | Restaurant Analogy | Time Horizon | |---|---|---| | Aggregate Plan | "Sell 500 covers next week" | Months / Quarters | | **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** | **"Make 120 Biryanis on Friday 7 PM"** | **Weeks / Days** | | Shop Floor Schedule | "Chef Rajat, marinate 8 kg chicken by 4 PM" | Hours / Minutes | ## 1.3 Key Terminology Introduced - **Planning Horizon**: The total length of time into the future for which the MPS is built. KTU standard: typically **3 to 18 months**. - **Time Buckets**: The discrete time intervals (weeks or days) into which the planning horizon is divided. - **Planned Order Receipts**: Quantities scheduled to be completed and available at the start of a period. - **Planned Order Releases**: Quantities scheduled to be started into production (offset back by lead time). - **Available-to-Promise (ATP)**: The uncommitted portion of inventory in a period available for new customer orders. - **Time Fences**: Demarcation lines that govern how the schedule can be changed within the planning horizon. --- # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Objectives of the Master Production Schedule The MPS is built to satisfy **four operational objectives** simultaneously: 1. **Demand Satisfaction** — Meet customer orders and forecast demand on time. 2. **Capacity Feasibility** — Ensure that the schedule is within the practical capacity limits of the plant (validated through **Rough-Cut Capacity Planning — RCCP**). 3. **Inventory Investment Control** — Balance production against finished-goods inventory to minimize holding costs and stockouts. 4. **Stable Production** — Avoid excessive schedule nervousness by protecting the near-term schedule from frequent changes. ## 2.2 The MPS in the Broader Planning Hierarchy The MPS sits between two layers of planning: > [!NOTE] > **Planning Cascade in KTU 2024 Scheme** > **Aggregate Production Plan (APP)** → **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** → **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** → **Shop Floor Execution** - The **APP** decides *how much* in product families. - The **MPS** decides *what specific items* and *when*. - The **MRP** then explodes the MPS using the **Bill of Materials (BOM)** to generate component-level purchase and work orders. ## 2.3 Time Fences — The MPS Stability Mechanism Time fences are imaginary vertical lines drawn across the MPS horizon that divide it into zones with different rules for changing the schedule. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Two Standard Time Fences (KTU High-Yield)** > > 1. **Demand Time Fence (DTF)**: The short-term zone (typically inside the cumulative manufacturing lead time). Within the DTF, the schedule is **frozen** — only firm customer orders drive changes. Forecast is **not** used here. > 2. **Planning Time Fence (PTF)**: A wider zone (typically inside the cumulative procurement + manufacturing lead time). Within the PTF, the schedule may be modified by the planner but not by sales. Outside the PTF, the schedule is **flexible** and can be regenerated using forecasts. | Zone | Location in Horizon | Change Authority | Driver | |---|---|---|---| | Frozen Zone | Inside Demand Time Fence | No changes allowed | Firm customer orders only | | Slushy Zone | Between DTF and PTF | Planner can adjust | Forecast + customer orders | | Liquid Zone | Beyond PTF | Fully flexible | Forecast only | ## 2.4 Available-to-Promise (ATP) Logic ATP is the KTU-favorite concept under MPS. It tells sales how much inventory in a future period is **not already committed** to existing customer orders, and is therefore *available to promise* to new customers. ### ATP Calculation Logic The ATP for a given period is computed using a discrete logic: > **If a new planned receipt arrives in Period *t*, then ATP*t* = (Receipt*t*) − (Committed Orders*t* to *t*+DDL)**, where **DDL = Delivery Lead Time** (in number of periods). Subsequent ATP in that batch *cumulatively* increases by the next receipt minus any new commitments before the next receipt. ## 2.5 Make-to-Stock vs. Make-to-Order MPS Strategy > [!NOTE] > **Two MPS Philosophies** > > - **Make-to-Stock (MTS)**: MPS is driven primarily by **forecast**. The firm holds finished-goods inventory. Common in FMCG, consumer durables. > - **Make-to-Order (MTO) / Assemble-to-Order (ATO)**: MPS is driven by **actual customer orders**. Less inventory risk but longer customer lead time. Common in industrial machinery, custom furniture. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All MPS formulas listed below are examinable. Memorize the symbols, units, and boundary conditions.** | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Projected On-Hand (POH) | POH*t* = POH*{t-1}* + Planned Receipts*t* − Demand*t* | Units | Must never go negative in KTU problems unless backorders are explicitly permitted | | ATP (single batch) | ATP*t* = Receipt*t* − Σ(Customer Commits from *t* to *t* + DDL) | Units | DDL = delivery lead time in periods | | Cumulative ATP | ATP*next* = ATP*prev* + Receipt*next* − Σ(Commits before next receipt) | Units | Used in multi-period ATP problems | | Available to Promise (discrete) | ATP*t* = max(0, Receipt*t* − Σ Customer Orders through Period *t* + DDL) | Units | Negative ATP is treated as zero | | Planned Order Release | POR*t* = POR_Receipt*{t + Lead Time}* | Units | Offset backwards by manufacturing lead time | | Safety Stock Coverage | SS_Days = (SS_Units ÷ Average Daily Demand) | Days | Common KTU sanity check | | Gross Requirements | GR*t* = Independent Demand*t* + Safety Stock replenishment*t* | Units | Independent demand = forecast + customer orders | | Net Requirements | NR*t* = max(0, GR*t* − POH*{t-1}*) | Units | Standard MRP logic from MPS | | Time Fence (DTF) | DTF ≤ Cumulative Manufacturing Lead Time | Periods | Rule of thumb per APICS/ASCM standards | | Time Fence (PTF) | PTF ≥ Cumulative Procurement + Manufacturing Lead Time | Periods | Defines the slushy zone | | Rough-Cut Capacity Load | RCL*resource* = Σ (MPS_qty × Std_Hours_per_unit) | Hours | Used in RCCP to validate MPS | > [!WARNING] > **Notation Reminder for Students**: Throughout KTU answer sheets, the symbol **POH** means **Projected On-Hand Inventory**, and **POR** means **Planned Order Release**. Writing "OH" alone will not fetch full marks. --- # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Problem 1 — Building an MPS Record with ATP (KTU Classic 14-Mark Pattern) > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example** — A KTU-style numerical that combines POH, ATP, and Lead Time offset in one question. ### Given Data A company manufactures a single product **"X-100"**. The current on-hand inventory is **80 units**. Forecasted demand (independent) and confirmed customer orders are as follows: | Period | Week 1 | Week 2 | Week 3 | Week 4 | Week 5 | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Forecast (units) | 50 | 50 | 50 | 50 | 50 | | Customer Orders (units) | 40 | 30 | 0 | 10 | 0 | **Lead Time for production = 2 weeks.** The planner has decided on the following planned order receipts: - **Week 1: 100 units** - **Week 3: 150 units** ### Step 1 — Compute Projected On-Hand (POH) Inventory We use the rolling formula: $$\text{POH}_t = \text{POH}_{t-1} + \text{Planned Receipts}_t - \text{Demand}_t$$ **Demand = max(Forecast, Customer Orders)** — this is the standard KTU convention. We take the higher of the two because a confirmed customer order overrides forecast. | Period | Beginning POH | Forecast | Customer Orders | Demand (Max) | Planned Receipt | Ending POH | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Week 1 | 80 | 50 | 40 | **50** | 100 | 130 | | Week 2 | 130 | 50 | 30 | **50** | 0 | 80 | | Week 3 | 80 | 50 | 0 | **50** | 150 | 180 | | Week 4 | 180 | 50 | 10 | **50** | 0 | 130 | | Week 5 | 130 | 50 | 0 | **50** | 0 | 80 | **Row 1 Explanation**: Beginning POH (80) + Planned Receipt (100) − Demand (50) = **130**. ✓ **Row 2 Explanation**: Beginning POH (130) + 0 − 50 = **80**. ✓ **Row 3 Explanation**: Beginning POH (80) + 150 − 50 = **180**. ✓ **Row 4 Explanation**: 180 − 50 = **130**. ✓ **Row 5 Explanation**: 130 − 50 = **80**. ✓ > [!NOTE] > **Verification**: Since demand never exceeds supply + beginning inventory in any period, **no backorders occur**. POH is always ≥ 0. If POH had gone negative, we would have flagged a capacity/lead-time problem. ### Step 2 — Compute Available-to-Promise (ATP) ATP for the period of a new receipt is: $$\text{ATP}_t = \text{Receipt}_t - \sum_{k=t}^{t+\text{DDL}} \text{Customer Commits}_k$$ In this problem, we assume **DDL = 0 weeks** (immediate delivery at receipt), so ATP is simply: $$\text{ATP}_t = \text{Receipt}_t - \text{Customer Orders}_t$$ | Period | Receipt | Customer Orders | ATP | |---|---|---|---| | Week 1 | 100 | 40 | **60 units** | | Week 3 | 150 | 0 | **150 units** | **Interpretation for Sales Department**: - In **Week 1**, we can promise up to **60 more units** to new customers. - In **Week 3**, we can promise up to **150 more units**. ### Step 3 — Compute Planned Order Releases (POR) Planned order releases are offset backwards by the manufacturing lead time of **2 weeks**. $$\text{POR}_t = \text{Planned Receipt}_{t+2}$$ | Period | Planned Receipt | Planned Order Release | |---|---|---| | Week 1 | 100 | — (released in Week −1) | | Week 3 | 150 | **Week 1: Release 150 units** | **Conclusion for the Shop Floor**: The shop floor must **start producing 150 units of X-100 in Week 1** so that they are completed and received into inventory by Week 3. --- ## 3.2 Problem 2 — MPS with Multi-Period ATP and DDL = 2 Weeks ### Given Data A product **"Y-200"** has the following MPS receipts and customer commitments: | Period | Week 1 | Week 2 | Week 3 | Week 4 | |---|---|---|---|---| | Planned Receipt | 0 | 200 | 0 | 300 | | Customer Orders | 80 | 120 | 50 | 0 | **Delivery Lead Time (DDL) = 2 weeks**. ### Step 1 — Compute ATP for Each Receipt Period **For Week 2 (first receipt of 200)**: $$\text{ATP}_{\text{Week 2}} = 200 - (80 + 120) = 0 \text{ units}$$ **Explanation**: All 200 units arriving in Week 2 are already committed by the 80 units (Week 1) and 120 units (Week 2) of customer orders within the 2-week delivery window. **ATP = 0**. **For Week 4 (next receipt of 300)**: We need to deduct any customer commitments between Week 4 and Week 4 + DDL = Week 6. Looking at our table (and assuming zero orders beyond Week 4): $$\text{ATP}_{\text{Week 4}} = 300 - (50 + 0 + 0) = 250 \text{ units}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall**: Do **not** deduct Week 1 and Week 2 orders from Week 4's ATP — those orders were already satisfied by Week 2's receipt (which itself had ATP = 0, meaning every unit was committed). ATP is **discrete and bucket-specific**, not cumulative across all past orders. ### Step 2 — Final ATP Table | Period | Receipt | Cumulative Commits (until next receipt) | ATP | |---|---|---|---| | Week 2 | 200 | 200 (W1:80 + W2:120) | **0** | | Week 4 | 300 | 50 (W3:50) | **250** | --- ## 3.3 Problem 3 — Rough-Cut Capacity Planning (RCCP) Check on MPS ### Given Data A plant produces two products **A** and **B** from MPS. Standard hours required per unit on **Machine M-1** are: - Product A: **3 hours per unit** - Product B: **5 hours per unit** The MPS for **Week 6** specifies: - 200 units of Product A - 100 units of Product B Machine M-1 is available for **7 hours/day × 5 days/week = 35 hours/week**. Overtime is capped at **+10 hours/week**. **Question**: Is the MPS feasible on Machine M-1 in Week 6? ### Step 1 — Compute Capacity Required $$\text{Required Hours} = (200 \times 3) + (100 \times 5)$$ $$\text{Required Hours} = 600 + 500 = 1100 \text{ hours}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute Capacity Available $$\text{Regular Hours} = 35 \text{ hours/week}$$ $$\text{Max OT Hours} = 35 + 10 = 45 \text{ hours/week}$$ ### Step 3 — Feasibility Decision Since **1100 hours >> 45 hours/week**, the MPS is **completely infeasible** on Machine M-1. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: A common student error is to compare **weekly** MPS quantities against **daily** machine capacity without converting. Always unify the time units (convert everything to hours/week or units/week) before comparing. ### Conclusion The planner must either: (i) reschedule some production to other weeks, (ii) subcontract, (iii) approve additional overtime, or (iv) re-plan using capacity-adjusted MPS techniques like **level loading** or **chase strategy**. --- ## 3.4 Problem 4 — Time Fence Calculation ### Given Data A product has the following lead-time structure: - **Procurement Lead Time (raw materials)**: 4 weeks - **Manufacturing Lead Time (assembly + testing)**: 3 weeks ### Step 1 — Demand Time Fence (DTF) By APICS/ASCM convention: **DTF = Manufacturing Lead Time = 3 weeks** ### Step 2 — Planning Time Fence (PTF) $$\text{PTF} = \text{Procurement Lead Time} + \text{Manufacturing Lead Time}$$ $$\text{PTF} = 4 + 3 = 7 \text{ weeks}$$ ### Step 3 — Interpret the Zones | Week | Zone | Rule | |---|---|---| | Week 1 to Week 3 | **Frozen** (within DTF) | Only firm customer orders; no forecast changes | | Week 4 to Week 7 | **Slushy** (between DTF and PTF) | Planner-controlled, both forecast and orders considered | | Week 8 onwards | **Liquid** (beyond PTF) | Fully flexible, regenerated using forecasts | --- # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The MPS Position in the Enterprise Planning Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef plan fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 classDef mps fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 classDef mrp fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 classDef shop fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 A["Aggregate Production Plan
(Product Families, Months)"]:::plan B["Rough Cut Capacity Planning
(RCCP - Feasibility Check)"]:::plan C["Master Production Schedule
(MPS - Specific Items, Weeks)"]:::mps D["Available to Promise
(ATP Calculation)"]:::mps E["Bill of Materials
(BOM Explosion)"]:::mrp F["Material Requirements Planning
(MRP - Components, Days)"]:::mrp G["Shop Floor Execution
(Work Orders, Hours)"]:::shop A --> B B --> C C --> D C --> E E --> F F --> G D --> C ``` **Reading the Diagram**: The diagram shows the **two-way relationship** between MPS and ATP — ATP informs the planner of remaining capacity, which can then be used to either accept new orders or modify the MPS. ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an MPS Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef input fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 classDef process fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff6f00,color:#000 classDef output fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 F1["Independent Demand
Forecast"]:::input F2["Customer Orders
Firm and Projected"]:::input I1["Opening Inventory
On Hand Balance"]:::input C1["Capacity Constraints
Machines, Labor, OT"]:::input P1["Planning Engine
Level / Chase / Mixed Strategy"]:::process P2["Time Fence Manager
Frozen / Slushy / Liquid"]:::process P3["ATP Calculator
Discrete Logic"]:::process O1["MPS Record
Period-wise Plan"]:::output O2["Planned Order Releases
Offset by Lead Time"]:::output O3["Exception Reports
Capacity Violations"]:::output F1 --> P1 F2 --> P1 I1 --> P1 C1 --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> O1 O1 --> P3 P3 --> O2 P1 --> O3 ``` ## 4.3 Time Fence Visualization Across the Planning Horizon ```mermaid gantt title MPS Horizon with Time Fences dateFormat X axisFormat W%u section Frozen Zone Week 1 (DTF) :done, fz1, 0, 1 Week 2 (DTF) :done, fz2, 1, 2 Week 3 (DTF) :done, fz3, 2, 3 section Slushy Zone Week 4 (PTF start) :active, sz1, 3, 4 Week 5 :active, sz2, 4, 5 Week 6 :active, sz3, 5, 6 Week 7 (PTF end) :active, sz4, 6, 7 section Liquid Zone Week 8 : lz1, 7, 8 Week 9 : lz2, 8, 9 Week 10 : lz3, 9, 10 ``` **Reading the Diagram**: The first 3 weeks (Frozen) cannot be changed. Weeks 4 to 7 (Slushy) are planner-controlled. Weeks 8+ (Liquid) are entirely forecast-driven. ## 4.4 ATP Discrete Logic Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef step fill:#ede7f6,stroke:#4527a0,color:#000 classDef dec fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 classDef fin fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 S1["Start of Period t"]:::step D1{"New Receipt
arriving in t?"}:::dec S2["Identify Customer Orders
from period t to t+DDL"]:::step S3["Compute ATP = Receipt
minus Commits in window"]:::step S4["ATP carries forward
to next period"]:::step F1["Communicate ATP
to Sales Team"]:::fin S5["No action:
ATP unchanged from previous"]:::step S1 --> D1 D1 -- Yes --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> F1 D1 -- No --> S5 ``` --- # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) ### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the Master Production Schedule (MPS). List any four characteristics of an effective MPS.** **Model Answer (3 Marks)**: The **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** is a time-phased plan that specifies what end items are to be produced, in what quantities, and when, to meet forecasted demand and customer orders. It serves as the primary input to the MRP system. Four characteristics of an effective MPS: 1. **Drives MRP and the shop floor** — every production decision cascades from it. 2. **Time-phased in weeks or days** — not aggregated by month like the APP. 3. **Includes both forecast and actual customer orders** — uses max of the two. 4. **Balances demand and capacity** — validated through RCCP. 5. **(Bonus)** Maintains stable production by using time fences. *(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for any 4 valid characteristics. Bonus 5th point earns no extra but shows depth.)* --- ### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is Available-to-Promise (ATP)? Why is it important for the sales function?** **Model Answer (3 Marks)**: **Available-to-Promise (ATP)** is the uncommitted portion of finished-goods inventory (or scheduled production) in a specific future period that is available to be promised to new customer orders. $$\text{ATP}_t = \text{Receipt}_t - \sum_{k=t}^{t+\text{DDL}} \text{Committed Customer Orders}_k$$ **Importance to Sales**: 1. Enables **reliable order promising** without over-committing inventory. 2. Improves **customer service** by giving accurate delivery dates. 3. Reduces the need for **safety stock buffer** because ATP replaces guesswork. 4. Bridges the gap between **forecast planning** and **actual demand**. *(Award 1 mark for ATP definition with formula, 2 marks for importance.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule**: Module 4 Part B offers an internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full (both 14 marks, with sub-parts typically of 7 marks each). --- ### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **different time fences** used in the Master Production Schedule. Describe the rules that govern changes in each zone. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Given the data below, construct an **MPS record** with Projected On-Hand inventory, Available-to-Promise, and Planned Order Releases. The product has a manufacturing lead time of **2 weeks**. The current opening inventory is **40 units**. **(7 Marks)** | Period | Week 1 | Week 2 | Week 3 | Week 4 | Week 5 | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Forecast | 60 | 60 | 60 | 60 | 60 | | Customer Orders | 50 | 40 | 20 | 0 | 0 | | Planned Receipt | 120 | 0 | 150 | 0 | 0 | --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Time Fences in MPS** (7 Marks): The MPS planning horizon is divided into **three zones** by two time fences: **1. Demand Time Fence (DTF):** - Located inside the cumulative manufacturing lead time. - Inside the DTF, the schedule is **frozen**. - Only **firm customer orders** are used; forecast is **not** considered. - Rationale: Materials and components are already committed; changing the plan would be costly. **2. Planning Time Fence (PTF):** - Located at the sum of cumulative procurement + manufacturing lead times. - Inside PTF but outside DTF, the schedule is **slushy**. - The **planner** can modify it, balancing forecast and customer orders. **3. Liquid Zone (Beyond PTF):** - The schedule is **flexible** and can be regenerated based on fresh forecasts. - Sales can suggest changes; planners can level-load production. **Marking Guide**: - 2 marks for naming all 3 zones with locations - 3 marks for explaining change rules in each - 1 mark for rationale - 1 mark for neat table or example --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1: Demand = max(Forecast, Customer Orders)** | Period | Forecast | Orders | Demand | |---|---|---|---| | Week 1 | 60 | 50 | **60** | | Week 2 | 60 | 40 | **60** | | Week 3 | 60 | 20 | **60** | | Week 4 | 60 | 0 | **60** | | Week 5 | 60 | 0 | **60** | **Step 2: Projected On-Hand (POH) using** $$\text{POH}_t = \text{POH}_{t-1} + \text{Receipt}_t - \text{Demand}_t$$ | Period | Beginning POH | Receipt | Demand | Ending POH | |---|---|---|---|---| | Week 1 | 40 | 120 | 60 | **100** | | Week 2 | 100 | 0 | 60 | **40** | | Week 3 | 40 | 150 | 60 | **130** | | Week 4 | 130 | 0 | 60 | **70** | | Week 5 | 70 | 0 | 60 | **10** | **Step 3: ATP** (assuming DDL = 0 for simplicity): | Period | Receipt | Customer Orders | ATP | |---|---|---|---| | Week 1 | 120 | 50 | **70 units** | | Week 3 | 150 | 20 | **130 units** | **Step 4: Planned Order Releases (offset by Lead Time = 2 weeks)**: - To receive 150 units in **Week 3**, release must happen in **Week 1** → **POR₁ = 150 units**. - The Week 1 receipt of 120 was released in Week −1 (already past, no POR needed). **Marking Guide for Part (b)**: - 2 marks for POH table correctness - 2 marks for ATP logic - 2 marks for POR offset - 1 mark for neatness + units --- ### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Differentiate between **Make-to-Stock (MTS)** and **Make-to-Order (MTO)** MPS strategies. Under what conditions is each strategy most suitable? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A company produces two products **P** and **Q** using two machines **M-1** and **M-2**. The standard hours per unit are: | Machine | Product P | Product Q | |---|---|---| | M-1 | 2 hrs | 4 hrs | | M-2 | 3 hrs | 1 hr | The MPS for Week 8 is **150 units of P** and **80 units of Q**. Each machine is available **40 regular hours/week** with a **maximum of 10 hours of overtime**. Check the feasibility of the MPS using **Rough-Cut Capacity Planning (RCCP)**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **MTS vs. MTO Comparison** (7 Marks): | Dimension | Make-to-Stock (MTS) | Make-to-Order (MTO) | |---|---|---| | Demand Driver | Forecast | Actual customer orders | | Inventory | Holds finished-goods stock | Minimal or zero finished stock | | Lead Time to Customer | Short (off-the-shelf) | Longer (must produce first) | | Risk | Inventory obsolescence | Lost sales / long waits | | Forecast Accuracy Need | High | Low | | Suitable Industries | FMCG, soaps, packaged food, retail | Custom machinery, ships, bespoke furniture | | Information System Need | Moderate | Sophisticated order tracking | **Suitability Conditions**: - **MTS** suits **high-volume, low-variety, standardized** products with stable demand and accurate forecasts. - **MTO** suits **low-volume, high-variety, customized** products where inventory would be too risky. **Marking Guide**: - 3 marks for comparison table - 2 marks for suitability conditions - 2 marks for examples / KTU context --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1: Hours Required per Machine** **Machine M-1**: $$H_{\text{M-1}} = (150 \times 2) + (80 \times 4) = 300 + 320 = 620 \text{ hours}$$ **Machine M-2**: $$H_{\text{M-2}} = (150 \times 3) + (80 \times 1) = 450 + 80 = 530 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 2: Capacity Available per Machine** $$C_{\text{available}} = 40 \text{ regular} + 10 \text{ OT} = 50 \text{ hours/week}$$ **Step 3: Feasibility Check** | Machine | Required (hrs) | Available (hrs) | Status | |---|---|---|---| | M-1 | 620 | 50 | **❌ Infeasible (short by 570 hrs)** | | M-2 | 530 | 50 | **❌ Infeasible (short by 480 hrs)** | **Conclusion**: The MPS as given is **completely infeasible**. The planner must either: 1. Spread the production over multiple weeks (level loading). 2. Subcontract part of the work. 3. Add a second/third shift (if equipment supports it). 4. Reduce the MPS quantities. **Marking Guide for Part (b)**: - 2 marks for hours required - 1 mark for capacity available - 2 marks for comparison - 2 marks for correct recommendation / conclusion --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Areas in MPS Questions** > > 1. **Forgetting the Lead-Time Offset (POR calculation)** — Students compute the planned *receipts* correctly but forget to back-schedule the *releases*. This costs up to **2 marks**. > 2. **Wrong Demand Logic** — Using Forecast OR Customer Order instead of **max(Forecast, Customer Orders)**. Always state the rule explicitly in your answer. > 3. **ATP Calculation Errors** — Conflating **cumulative ATP** with **discrete ATP**. In multi-receipt MPS, ATP resets at each new receipt. > 4. **Unit Mismatch in RCCP** — Comparing weekly MPS quantities with daily machine capacity. Always unify the units. > 5. **Time Fence Confusion** — Some students write "Demand Time Fence = Planning Time Fence." They are **distinct concepts**. > 6. **Skipping the Feasibility Conclusion** — In RCCP problems, the final sentence must clearly state whether the MPS is feasible or not. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for MPS** > > ☐ **MPS = Disaggregated APP**, expressed in finished products, weekly/daily buckets, **3–18 months** horizon. > > ☐ **MPS drives MRP** — MRP explodes MPS using the BOM. > > ☐ **Demand Rule**: $$\text{Demand} = \max(\text{Forecast}, \text{Customer Order})$$ > > ☐ **POH Formula**: $$\text{POH}_t = \text{POH}_{t-1} + \text{Receipts}_t - \text{Demand}_t$$ > > ☐ **POR Offset**: Release = Receipt shifted backwards by **Lead Time**. > > ☐ **ATP Formula**: $$\text{ATP}_t = \text{Receipt}_t - \sum_{k=t}^{t+\text{DDL}} \text{Commits}_k$$ > > ☐ **Three Time Fence Zones**: Frozen (DTF), Slushy (between DTF & PTF), Liquid (beyond PTF). > > ☐ **DTF ≤ Manufacturing Lead Time; PTF ≥ Procurement + Manufacturing Lead Time**. > > ☐ **MTS** = forecast-driven, finished-goods inventory held. > > ☐ **MTO** = order-driven, minimal inventory. > > ☐ **RCCP** validates MPS feasibility on critical resources. > > ☐ **Always state units** in numerical answers (units/week, hours/week). > > ☐ **Always unify time units** before comparing capacity and demand. > > ☐ **Conclude feasibility** explicitly in RCCP problems. ---

topics.title: Bill of Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT863 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bill of Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Bill of Materials (BOM):** A Bill of Materials is a comprehensive, structured, and formally documented list of all raw materials, components, sub-assemblies, parts, and quantities required to manufacture **one unit** of a finished product. It serves as the foundational master data input for Material Requirements Planning (MRP), production scheduling, costing, and shop-floor execution in any manufacturing or assembly environment.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMRT863) Module 4, the BOM is treated as the **structural backbone of the production system**. It defines the *product structure* — i.e., *what* is needed, *how much* of it is needed, and *when* it is needed in the build sequence.

Formally, a BOM can be represented as a product structure tree where the **finished product (Level 0)** sits at the top, sub-assemblies follow at intermediate levels, and raw materials sit at the bottom (Level *n*).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Analogy: The Recipe Card of a Bakery 🍞**
>
> Think of a Bill of Materials exactly like a **recipe card** in a bakery. If a customer orders **10 loaves of sandwich bread**, the baker cannot just say "make bread." The recipe card explicitly states:
> - *Flour — 500 g per loaf*
> - *Yeast — 7 g per loaf*
> - *Salt — 10 g per loaf*
> - *Water — 350 ml per loaf*
>
> To produce **10 loaves**, the baker must **multiply** these quantities by 10. This is exactly what a BOM does — it specifies the ingredients (components) and quantities per finished unit, so that scaling up production is a simple multiplication task.

In a manufacturing context:
- The **loaf of bread** = the *finished product* (Parent)
- The **flour, yeast, salt** = *components / raw materials* (Children)
- The **500 g** = *quantity per assembly*

The BOM is essentially the **engineer's recipe card** for industrial production.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Parent Item** | The item being built at any given level of the BOM. |
| **Component (Child)** | A material or sub-assembly required to build the parent. |
| **Quantity per Assembly (QPA)** | The number of units of a component needed for one unit of its parent. |
| **Unit of Measure (UOM)** | The standard unit (kg, m, nos., litres) used for each component. |
| **BOM Level** | The hierarchical position (0, 1, 2, …) of an item in the product structure. |
| **Phantom Assembly** | A sub-assembly that exists only on paper (in the BOM) but is **never stocked**; it is built immediately when needed. |
| **Modular BOM** | A BOM designed around common, reusable sub-assemblies (modules). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structure of a BOM — Levels & Hierarchy

A BOM is organised hierarchically. For a typical product *P*:

- **Level 0:** Finished Product *P*
- **Level 1:** Major Sub-assemblies *A, B, C* required to make *P*
- **Level 2:** Sub-components required to make *A*, *B*, *C*
- **Level *n*:** Raw materials, bought-out parts

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Hierarchy Matters:** Hierarchy allows the planner to compute **net requirements** at *each* level independently, which is the core logic behind MRP. Without a structured BOM, MRP cannot explode requirements correctly.

## 2.2 Types of BOM (High-Yield for KTU)

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Level BOM** | Lists only the *immediate* components of a parent. | Simple products with one level of assembly. |
| **Multi-Level (Indented) BOM** | Lists *all* components across *all* levels, typically indented by level. | Complex products (cars, electronics). |
| **Modular (Planning) BOM** | Built around major options/modules (e.g., "AC variant," "sunroof variant"). | Products with high variety (automobiles). |
| **Manufacturing BOM** | Lists components in the **sequence of fabrication/assembly** at the shop floor. | Shop-floor routing & costing. |
| **Sales BOM** | A finished product is created by *kitting* existing finished goods. | Make-to-order kits, gift hampers. |
| **Phantom BOM** | Sub-assembly exists only in design; built instantly, never stocked. | Simplifies engineering changes. |
| **Super BOM** | Combines several variants/options into one master BOM. | Configure-to-order (CTO) products. |

## 2.3 Purpose & Functions of a BOM

1. **Product Definition** — single source of truth for product structure.
2. **MRP Input** — feeds gross requirements explosion.
3. **Costing** — enables *standard cost* calculation via cost roll-up.
4. **Shop Floor Communication** — guides assembly sequence.
5. **Engineering Change Management** — version control of product design.
6. **Procurement Planning** — drives purchase requisitions.
7. **Inventory Control** — defines what to stock vs. order.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All symbols are used consistently throughout these notes. Memorise these — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical.

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_i = D \times \prod_{k=0}^{i-1} QPA_k$ | Gross requirement of component at Level $i$ where $D$ is the demand for the parent finished product. |
| 2 | $C_{unit} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} (QPA_j \times P_j)$ | Standard cost of one unit of parent = sum of (quantity per assembly $\times$ unit price) across all components. |
| 3 | $C_{total} = G_{parent} \times C_{unit}$ | Total cost of producing $G_{parent}$ finished units. |
| 4 | $N_i = G_i - O_i - R_i$ | Net requirement at level $i$ where $O_i$ = on-hand inventory, $R_i$ = scheduled receipts. |
| 5 | $PLO_i = G_i / QPA_i$ | Planned order release at level $i$ (in parent units). |
| 6 | $\text{BOM Level} = \max(\text{component levels}) + 1$ | Highest depth in the product structure tree. |

> **Boundary Conditions:**
> - $G_i, N_i, O_i, R_i \geq 0$
> - $QPA_j > 0$ for all $j$
> - Quantities are expressed in the **same Unit of Measure (UOM)** per level.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Each car model has a multi-level BOM with 15,000+ components.
- **Aerospace:** Modular BOMs allow customisation (engine variant, avionics variant) without re-engineering the whole structure.
- **Electronics (PCB Assembly):** BOM drives component procurement from global vendors with minimum order quantity (MOQ) and lead-time constraints.
- **Furniture Industry:** Modular BOMs enable configure-to-order (CTO) workflows.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Quantity Explosion (KTU Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (Typical KTU 14-mark style):**
> A company manufactures a product **X**. The product structure is:
>
> - **X** is made of **2 units of A** and **1 unit of B**.
> - **A** is made of **3 units of C** and **2 units of D**.
> - **B** is made of **4 units of C** and **1 unit of E**.
>
> Customer demand: **50 units of X** to be delivered in Week 8.
> No on-hand inventory at any level.
>
> **Required:**
> (a) Draw the product structure tree.
> (b) Compute gross requirements at every level.
> (c) If unit costs are: C = ₹10, D = ₹20, E = ₹15, A = ?, B = ?, X = ?, compute the standard cost of one unit of X using cost roll-up.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Draw the Product Structure Tree

```
X (Level 0) — finished product
├── A (Level 1) × 2
│   ├── C (Level 2) × 3
│   └── D (Level 2) × 2
└── B (Level 1) × 1
    ├── C (Level 2) × 4
    └── E (Level 2) × 1
```

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Gross Requirements Calculation (Quantity Explosion)

Starting from the top:

**Level 0 — X:**
$$D_X = 50 \text{ units}$$

**Level 1 — A and B:**

Each X requires 2 A's, so:

$$G_A = D_X \times QPA_X^A = 50 \times 2 = 100 \text{ units of A}$$

Each X requires 1 B, so:

$$G_B = D_X \times QPA_X^B = 50 \times 1 = 50 \text{ units of B}$$

**Level 2 — C, D, E:**

Component **C** appears in *both* A and B. So we sum contributions:

$$G_C = (G_A \times QPA_A^C) + (G_B \times QPA_B^C)$$

$$G_C = (100 \times 3) + (50 \times 4) = 300 + 200 = 500 \text{ units of C}$$

Component **D** appears only in A:

$$G_D = G_A \times QPA_A^D = 100 \times 2 = 200 \text{ units of D}$$

Component **E** appears only in B:

$$G_E = G_B \times QPA_B^E = 50 \times 1 = 50 \text{ units of E}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Net Requirements

Since on-hand and scheduled receipts are zero everywhere:

$$N_i = G_i - 0 - 0 = G_i$$

So $N_A = 100$, $N_B = 50$, $N_C = 500$, $N_D = 200$, $N_E = 50$.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Cost Roll-Up (Bottom-Up)

Using $C_{unit} = \sum (QPA_j \times P_j)$ from bottom of the tree to the top.

**Cost of A:**

$$C_A = (3 \times P_C) + (2 \times P_D) = (3 \times 10) + (2 \times 20) = 30 + 40 = ₹70$$

**Cost of B:**

$$C_B = (4 \times P_C) + (1 \times P_E) = (4 \times 10) + (1 \times 15) = 40 + 15 = ₹55$$

**Cost of X:**

$$C_X = (2 \times C_A) + (1 \times C_B) = (2 \times 70) + (1 \times 55) = 140 + 55 = ₹195$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Total Cost of Order

$$C_{total} = D_X \times C_X = 50 \times 195 = ₹9{,}750$$

### 3.1.6 Analytical Matrix — Consolidated View

| Level | Item | QPA (per parent) | Gross Req. (G) | Net Req. (N) | Unit Cost ₹ | Total Cost ₹ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | X | — | 50 | 50 | 195 | 9,750 |
| 1 | A | 2 (per X) | 100 | 100 | 70 | 7,000 |
| 1 | B | 1 (per X) | 50 | 50 | 55 | 2,750 |
| 2 | C | 3 (per A), 4 (per B) | 500 | 500 | 10 | 5,000 |
| 2 | D | 2 (per A) | 200 | 200 | 20 | 4,000 |
| 2 | E | 1 (per B) | 200 | 200 | 15 | 750 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Check:** Sum of component costs at every level must reconcile.
> - Total cost of Level 2 components used = 5000 + 4000 + 750 = **₹9,750** ✓
> - Total cost of Level 1 sub-assemblies = 7000 + 2750 = **₹9,750** ✓
> - Total cost of finished goods = **₹9,750** ✓

## 3.2 Generalised Derivation — Multi-Level Explosion

For a multi-level BOM with $L$ levels, gross requirement of any component $k$ at level $l$ is:
