$$G_{k,l} = \sum_{m \in \text{Parents}(k)} \left( PLO_{m,l-1} \times QPA_{m \rightarrow k} \right)$$

where $PLO_{m,l-1}$ is the planned order release of the parent at level $l-1$ that consumes component $k$, and $QPA_{m \rightarrow k}$ is the quantity of $k$ required per unit of parent $m$.

This recursive formulation is what MRP software executes internally when performing the **"explosion phase"** of MRP.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Product Structure Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeX["X FINISHED PRODUCT LEVEL 0 DEMAND 50"]
    nodeA["A SUB ASSEMBLY LEVEL 1 QPA 2"]
    nodeB["B SUB ASSEMBLY LEVEL 1 QPA 1"]
    nodeC["C COMPONENT LEVEL 2"]
    nodeD["D COMPONENT LEVEL 2"]
    nodeE["E COMPONENT LEVEL 2"]

    nodeX --> nodeA
    nodeX --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeE
```

## 4.2 MRP–BOM Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    demand["DEMAND FORECAST OR CUSTOMER ORDER"]
    mps["MASTER PRODUCTION SCHEDULE MPS"]
    bom["BILL OF MATERIALS BOM MASTER DATA"]
    inventory["INVENTORY STATUS FILE ON HAND PLUS ON ORDER"]
    mrp["MRP ENGINE EXPLOSION PHASE"]
    po["PLANNED ORDER RELEASES PURCHASE AND SHOP FLOOR"]

    demand --> mps
    mps --> mrp
    bom --> mrp
    inventory --> mrp
    mrp --> po
```

## 4.3 BOM Explosion Logic Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    step1["START READ PARENT DEMAND D"]
    step2["FETCH QPA FOR LEVEL 1 CHILDREN"]
    step3["COMPUTE GROSS REQUIREMENTS LEVEL 1"]
    step4["FETCH QPA FOR LEVEL 2 CHILDREN"]
    step5["SUM CONTRIBUTIONS FROM ALL PARENTS"]
    step6["COMPUTE GROSS REQUIREMENTS LEVEL 2"]
    step7["SUBTRACT ON HAND AND SCHEDULED RECEIPTS"]
    step8["OUTPUT NET REQUIREMENTS PER LEVEL"]

    step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 --> step6 --> step7 --> step8
```

## 4.4 BOM Type Classification Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    bomRoot["BILL OF MATERIALS TYPES"]
    bSingle["SINGLE LEVEL BOM"]
    bMulti["MULTI LEVEL INDENTED BOM"]
    bMod["MODULAR PLANNING BOM"]
    bMfg["MANUFACTURING BOM"]
    bSales["SALES BOM"]
    bPhantom["PHANTOM BOM"]
    bSuper["SUPER BOM"]

    bomRoot --> bSingle
    bomRoot --> bMulti
    bomRoot --> bMod
    bomRoot --> bMfg
    bomRoot --> bSales
    bomRoot --> bPhantom
    bomRoot --> bSuper
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define a Bill of Materials. Mention any two types of BOM.

**Model Answer:**
A Bill of Materials (BOM) is a structured, formal document listing all the raw materials, components, sub-assemblies, and their quantities required to manufacture **one unit** of a finished product. It acts as the master data for MRP, costing, and production planning.

Two types of BOM:
1. **Single-Level BOM** — lists only the immediate components of a parent.
2. **Multi-Level (Indented) BOM** — lists all components across all levels, indented by level of usage. **(3 Marks)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Distinguish between a Manufacturing BOM and a Modular BOM.

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Manufacturing BOM | Modular BOM |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Lists components in the **sequence of fabrication/assembly** at the shop floor. | Lists components around **common, reusable modules/options**. |
| Structure | Sequence-based, operation-oriented. | Option-based, configuration-oriented. |
| Use | Shop-floor execution, routing, costing. | Configure-to-order (CTO) products, e.g., automobiles. |
| Variability | Low — fixed per product. | High — supports product variants. |

**(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]** The product structure of an electric iron **EI** is given below:

- **EI** = 1 × Heating Coil (HC) + 1 × Body (BD)
- **HC** = 1 × Nichrome Wire (NW) + 2 × Terminal (TM)
- **BD** = 1 × Plastic Shell (PS) + 1 × Handle (HN)

Demand: **100 units of EI** in Week 6. On-hand inventory at all levels = 0.

Unit costs: NW = ₹50, TM = ₹5, PS = ₹80, HN = ₹30.

**(a)** Draw the product structure tree and compute the **gross requirements** at every level. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using cost roll-up, compute the **standard cost of one EI** and the **total production cost** for the order. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Product Structure Tree:**

```
EI (Level 0) — 100 units
├── HC (Level 1) × 1
│   ├── NW (Level 2) × 1
│   └── TM (Level 2) × 2
└── BD (Level 1) × 1
    ├── PS (Level 2) × 1
    └── HN (Level 2) × 1
```

**Gross Requirements Calculation:**

$$G_{HC} = 100 \times 1 = 100 \text{ units}$$

$$G_{BD} = 100 \times 1 = 100 \text{ units}$$

$$G_{NW} = 100 \times 1 = 100 \text{ units}$$

$$G_{TM} = 100 \times 2 = 200 \text{ units}$$

$$G_{PS} = 100 \times 1 = 100 \text{ units}$$

$$G_{HN} = 100 \times 1 = 100 \text{ units}$$

**(b) Cost Roll-Up:**

$$C_{HC} = (1 \times 50) + (2 \times 5) = 50 + 10 = ₹60$$

$$C_{BD} = (1 \times 80) + (1 \times 30) = 110 \text{ ₹}$$

$$C_{EI} = (1 \times 60) + (1 \times 110) = ₹170$$

$$C_{total} = 100 \times 170 = ₹17{,}000$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest]** A company uses a modular BOM to assemble two variants of a ceiling fan — **Fan-A (standard)** and **Fan-B (premium)**. The common module is the **Motor Assembly (MA)**. Premium variant adds a **Remote Module (RM)**.

- Fan-A = 1 × MA + 3 × Blade (BL) + 1 × Capacitor (CP)
- Fan-B = 1 × MA + 3 × BL + 1 × CP + 1 × RM
- MA = 1 × Coil (CL) + 1 × Bearing (BR)

Forecast demand: Fan-A = **200**, Fan-B = **100**. Inventory: BL = 150, CP = 50, MA = 20. All others = 0.

**(a)** Compute the **net requirements** at every level for each variant and the combined plan. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the advantages of a Modular BOM over a single-level BOM. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Net Requirements:**

*Level 1:* MA needed = 200 + 100 = 300 units. Inventory = 20, so:

$$N_{MA} = 300 - 20 = 280 \text{ units}$$

BL needed = (200 × 3) + (100 × 3) = 900. Inventory = 150:

$$N_{BL} = 900 - 150 = 750 \text{ units}$$

CP needed = 200 + 100 = 300. Inventory = 50:

$$N_{CP} = 300 - 50 = 250 \text{ units}$$

RM needed (only in Fan-B) = 100 × 1 = 100 units; inventory = 0:

$$N_{RM} = 100 \text{ units}$$

*Level 2:* CL and BR needed only via MA:

$$G_{CL} = 280 \times 1 = 280; \quad N_{CL} = 280$$

$$G_{BR} = 280 \times 1 = 280; \quad N_{BR} = 280$$

**(b) Advantages of Modular BOM:**

1. **Commonality & Reuse:** Common modules (e.g., MA) reduce the number of unique parts.
2. **Variety Management:** Easy handling of product variants (Fan-A, Fan-B) without duplication.
3. **Reduced Inventory:** Common modules can be stocked and assembled as needed.
4. **Faster New Product Introduction:** New variants = add new modules; existing modules reused.
5. **Simpler MRP Logic:** Module-level planning simplifies explosion.
6. **Cost Efficiency:** Bulk procurement of common parts lowers unit cost.
7. **Engineering Change Control:** A change in one module automatically applies to all variants using it.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget to sum contributions** of the same component appearing under multiple parents (e.g., Component C under both A and B). Always scan the BOM for shared components.
> - **Cost roll-up must be bottom-up**, not top-down. Reversing this order is a common error that costs 3–4 marks.
> - Do **not confuse gross requirement with net requirement** when inventory data is given in the problem. Always subtract on-hand and scheduled receipts at the relevant level.
> - In Modular BOMs, students often **double-count** the common module if it appears in multiple variants. Use the formula: $G_{module} = \sum (D_i \times QPA_i)$ across all variants.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **BOM = Recipe of Manufacturing.** It is a master data file, not a one-time document.
- **BOM Level:** Finished product = Level 0; raw material = deepest Level.
- **Types to remember:** Single-Level, Multi-Level (Indented), Modular, Manufacturing, Phantom, Super, Sales.
- **Quantity Explosion Formula:** $G_i = D \times \prod QPA$ (parent's demand × QPA chain).
- **Cost Roll-Up Formula:** $C_{unit} = \sum (QPA_j \times P_j)$, computed **bottom-up**.
- **Total Cost Formula:** $C_{total} = D \times C_{unit}$.
- **Net Requirement Formula:** $N_i = G_i - O_i - R_i$.
- **Shared components:** Always sum contributions across all parents using the same component.
- **Modular BOM** advantages: commonality, variety handling, lower inventory, faster NPI.
- **Phantom Assembly** is never stocked; it is an engineering convenience.
- **BOM is the input to MRP explosion phase**, but MRP also needs MPS and Inventory Status File.
- **Master data integrity is critical:** Even a 1% error in QPA at level 2 can cause a 5% cost error at the finished product level due to multiplier effect across levels.
- **Super BOM** = union of all variants; **Planning BOM** = for forecasting/MRP; **Manufacturing BOM** = for shop floor.
- Always **draw the product structure tree** before calculation; it is worth 2–3 marks by itself in a 14-mark question.
- **Verification:** After computing the cost roll-up, sum the costs at every level — they must reconcile with the total finished-goods cost.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evolution from MRP to Manufacturing Resource Planning (MRP II)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT863 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Evolution from MRP to Manufacturing Resource Planning (MRP II) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** is a computer-based production planning and inventory control system designed to manage dependent demand items. It translates the **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** into a detailed schedule of raw material, component, and sub-assembly requirements using **Bill of Materials (BOM)** data and **Inventory Status Records**. **Manufacturing Resource Planning (MRP II)** is the evolutionary successor of MRP. As formally codified by **Oliver Wight** in the 1980s, MRP II is a comprehensive computer-integrated manufacturing management system that extends MRP's material focus into a closed-loop, enterprise-wide planning framework. It integrates **financial planning**, **capacity planning**, **shop floor control**, and **simulation** with the existing MRP logic. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** > **MRP = "What do we need? (Materials Only)"** > **MRP II = "What do we need & Can we make it? (Materials + Capacity + Finance)"** ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are organizing a massive **wedding feast** for 500 guests: - **MRP is like just making a shopping list** for the ingredients (rice, vegetables, spices). You calculate *what* and *how much* of each raw material you need based on the menu (BOM) and number of guests (MPS). - **MRP II is the entire event management**, including: - Shopping list (Materials) - Checking if your kitchen burners and ovens can handle the cooking (Capacity) - Scheduling the cooks' shifts (Workforce) - Tracking the money spent vs. budget (Finance) - Adjusting plans if the gas cylinder runs low (Simulation/What-if analysis) **MRP is the recipe; MRP II is the entire restaurant management system.** ## 1.3 Key Boundary Terms in Bold - **Dependent Demand**: Demand for an item that is driven by the demand for another (finished) item. - **Bill of Materials (BOM)**: The complete product structure showing all components, sub-assemblies, and raw materials. - **Master Production Schedule (MPS)**: The timeline plan for what finished goods will be produced, in what quantities, and when. - **Closed-Loop MRP**: The intermediate evolution where capacity feedback is introduced into basic MRP. - **Net Change vs. Regenerative MRP**: Two methods of re-running MRP calculations (regenerative re-plans entirely; net change updates only affected items). > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 module emphasizes that MRP II is **NOT** a software upgrade of MRP, but a **philosophical and structural evolution** in manufacturing planning that aligns operations with business strategy. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Evolution Timeline: MRP → Closed-Loop MRP → MRP II ### **Stage 1: Basic MRP (Material Requirements Planning)** Introduced in the 1960s–70s, basic MRP answered the question: **"What materials do we need and when?"** **Core Inputs:** 1. **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** 2. **Bill of Materials (BOM)** 3. **Inventory Status File** (on-hand, on-order, allocated quantities) **Core Outputs:** 1. Planned Order Releases 2. Order Rescheduling notices 3. Exception Reports **Limitation**: It assumed infinite capacity. It did NOT ask: *"Do we have the manpower, machines, and money to execute this plan?"* ### **Stage 2: Closed-Loop MRP** This was the **first evolution step**, integrating capacity planning feedback: - **Rough-Cut Capacity Planning (RCCP)** checked feasibility. - **Capacity Requirements Planning (CRP)** did detailed shop-floor level checks. - If capacity was insufficient, the MPS was adjusted, and MRP was re-run. - Hence, a **closed loop** was formed: Planning → Execution → Feedback → Re-planning. ### **Stage 3: MRP II (Manufacturing Resource Planning)** Oliver Wight expanded Closed-Loop MRP into a **total business planning tool**: | Aspect | Basic MRP | MRP II | |---|---|---| | **Scope** | Materials only | All manufacturing resources | | **Financial Integration** | None | Fully integrated (cost, cash flow) | | **Capacity Planning** | Assumed infinite | Detailed finite capacity | | **Simulation** | Not possible | What-if scenario analysis | | **Shop Floor Control** | External | Integrated with SFC | | **Philosophy** | Reactive | Proactive & Strategic | ## 2.2 The Three Core MRP II Functions 1. **Business Planning & Simulation** – Link operational plans to business strategy. 2. **Master Production Scheduling** – Translate the business plan into operational output targets. 3. **Capacity Planning & Execution** – Verify feasibility and control shop floor execution. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following calculations are foundational to MRP logic and frequently tested in **Part A (3-mark)** questions. | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | **Gross Requirements (GR)** | Total demand for an item in a period | Units | | **Scheduled Receipts (SR)** | Open POs expected to arrive | Units | | **On-Hand Inventory (OHI)** | Physical stock available | Units | | **Net Requirements (NR)** | $\text{NR} = \text{GR} - (\text{SR} + \text{OHI})$ | Units (if $\text{NR} > 0$) | | **Planned Order Receipt (POR)** | Quantity received in the period | Equals NR | | **Planned Order Release (PORel)** | POR offset by lead time | Time-shifted | | **Lot Sizing Rule (L4L)** | Lot-for-Lot: order exact NR | Minimum inventory cost | | **Lot Sizing (EOQ)** | $\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | $D$=Demand, $S$=Order cost, $H$=Holding cost | | **Safety Stock (SS)** | Buffer for demand/supply variability | Units | | **Lead Time Offset** | PORel = POR shifted backward by LT | Days/Weeks | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Pointer:** In MRP II, these formulas are **extended** with **cost rolls-up**, **capacity utilization metrics**, and **financial variance analysis** that were absent in pure MRP. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Automotive Industry**: Toyota, Ford use MRP II logic to synchronize 1000s of parts across global supply chains. - **Electronics Manufacturing**: Apple uses MRP II principles to balance component supply with iPhone production capacity. - **Aerospace**: Boeing uses MRP II for long lead-time planning of turbine components. - **Indian SMEs**: Many Kerala-based manufacturing units (rubber, coir, electronics) have adopted MRP II via cloud ERP systems. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Net Requirements (NR) in an MRP Logic Table The MRP calculation is sequential. Below is a **worked example** to derive net requirements for a single component. ### **Given Data:** - **Item:** Sub-assembly X - **On-Hand Inventory (Week 0):** 50 units - **Lead Time (LT):** 2 weeks - **Safety Stock:** 10 units - **Lot Sizing Rule:** Lot-for-Lot (L4L) | Week | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Gross Requirements (GR)** | 30 | 40 | 0 | 50 | 60 | | **Scheduled Receipts (SR)** | 0 | 20 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | **Projected On-Hand (POH)** | ? | ? | ? | ? | ? | | **Net Requirements (NR)** | ? | ? | ? | ? | ? | | **Planned Order Receipt (POR)** | ? | ? | ? | ? | ? | | **Planned Order Release (PORel)** | ? | ? | ? | ? | ? | ### **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Step 1: Calculate Week 1 Projected On-Hand** $$\text{POH}_1 = \text{OHI}_0 + \text{SR}_1 - \text{GR}_1$$ $$\text{POH}_1 = 50 + 0 - 30 = 20 \text{ units}$$ Since $\text{POH}_1$ (20) > Safety Stock (10), no new order needed yet. **Step 2: Calculate Week 2 Projected On-Hand** $$\text{POH}_2 = \text{POH}_1 + \text{SR}_2 - \text{GR}_2$$ $$\text{POH}_2 = 20 + 20 - 40 = 0 \text{ units}$$ Since $\text{POH}_2$ (0) < Safety Stock (10), we have a **Net Requirement**. $$\text{NR}_2 = \text{GR}_2 + \text{SS} - (\text{POH}_1 + \text{SR}_2)$$ $$\text{NR}_2 = 40 + 10 - (20 + 20) = 10 \text{ units}$$ **Step 3: Planned Order Receipt (POR) for Week 2** Using Lot-for-Lot, $\text{POR}_2 = \text{NR}_2 = 10$ units. **Step 4: Planned Order Release (PORel)** PORel is offset backward by Lead Time (LT = 2 weeks). $$\text{PORel}_{\text{Week 0}} = \text{POR}_{\text{Week 2}} = 10 \text{ units}$$ **Step 5: Re-compute POH for Week 2 (after POR receipt)** $$\text{POH}_2 = 0 + 10 = 10 \text{ units} = \text{SS}$$ **Step 6: Continue for Week 4 and Week 5** (similar logic applies). ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: MRP vs. MRP II Decision Logic | Decision Dimension | MRP Approach | MRP II Approach | |---|---|---| | **Inventory Shortage** | Reorder material | Reorder + check financial impact + check capacity | | **Late Order** | Reschedule order | Reschedule + cascade impact to cost & delivery promise | | **New Customer Order** | Add to MPS blindly | Run simulation, check capacity, profit margin, cash flow | | **Machine Breakdown** | Plan fails | Auto-reallocate to alternate work center, re-simulate | | **Supplier Delay** | Create exception report | Auto-trigger safety stock review + alternate sourcing | > [!NOTE] > **For KTU 2024:** The above matrix is a **favourite 14-mark question pattern**. Students should be able to explain MRP vs MRP II with comparative diagrams. ## 3.3 Derivation of Order Quantity Under Different Lot Sizing Rules ### **Lot-for-Lot (L4L):** $$\text{Order Quantity} = \text{Net Requirement in that period}$$ ### **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) – Wilson's Formula:** $$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$ Where: - $D$ = Annual demand (units) - $S$ = Ordering cost per order (₹/order) - $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year) ### **Worked EOQ Example:** Given: $D = 5000$ units/year, $S = ₹200$/order, $H = ₹10$/unit/year. $$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5000 \times 200}{10}}$$ $$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2{,}000{,}000}{10}} = \sqrt{200{,}000}$$ $$Q^* \approx 447.2 \text{ units (round to 447 or 448)}$$ **Number of orders per year:** $$N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{5000}{447} \approx 11.19 \text{ orders}$$ **Total Inventory Cost:** $$\text{TIC} = \frac{Q^*}{2} \times H + N \times S$$ $$\text{TIC} = \frac{447}{2} \times 10 + 11.19 \times 200$$ $$\text{TIC} = 2235 + 2238 = ₹4473 \text{ per year}$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Evolution Flow: MRP → Closed-Loop MRP → MRP II ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Stage 1: Basic MRP
1960s - Materials Only] --> B[Stage 2: Closed-Loop MRP
1970s - Added Capacity Feedback] B --> C[Stage 3: MRP II
1980s - Full Resource Planning] C --> D[Stage 4: ERP Systems
1990s Onwards - Enterprise Wide] A -- Capacity gap noticed --> B B -- Financial gap noticed --> C C -- Integration gap noticed --> D ``` ## 4.2 MRP II Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB strat[Business Planning and Strategy] --> fin[Financial Planning Module] strat --> mps[Master Production Schedule MPS] fin --> sim[What If Simulation Engine] mps --> rccp[Rough Cut Capacity Planning RCCP] rccp --> crp[Capacity Requirements Planning CRP] crp --> mrpc[MRP Core Computation Engine] mrpc --> sfc[Shop Floor Control and Execution] sfc --> feedback[Performance Feedback Loop] feedback --> strat mrpc --> bom[Bill of Materials and Inventory] bom --> mrpc ``` ## 4.3 MRP II Functional Modules – Integrated Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph StrategicLevel [Strategic Planning Layer] SP1[Business Plan] SP2[Financial Plan] end subgraph TacticalLevel [Tactical Planning Layer] TP1[MPS] TP2[RCCP] TP3[CRP] end subgraph OperationalLevel [Operational Execution Layer] OP1[MRP Engine] OP2[Shop Floor Control] OP3[Purchasing] end SP1 --> TP1 SP2 --> TP1 TP1 --> TP2 TP2 --> TP3 TP3 --> OP1 OP1 --> OP2 OP1 --> OP3 OP2 --> FE[Feedback to Strategic] OP3 --> FE FE --> SP1 ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Architecture: MRP vs MRP II ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MRP_Block [MRP Scope - Limited] M1[MPS Input] M2[BOM] M3[Inventory File] M1 --> MRPCore1[MRP Engine] M2 --> MRPCore1 M3 --> MRPCore1 MRPCore1 --> M4[Material Plan Only] end subgraph MRPII_Block [MRP II Scope - Comprehensive] N1[Business Plan] N2[Financial Plan] N3[MPS] N4[BOM] N5[Inventory] N6[RCCP and CRP] N7[Shop Floor Data] N1 --> MRPCore2[MRP II Engine with Simulation] N2 --> MRPCore2 N3 --> MRPCore2 N4 --> MRPCore2 N5 --> MRPCore2 N6 --> MRPCore2 N7 --> MRPCore2 MRPCore2 --> O1[Material Plan] MRPCore2 --> O2[Capacity Plan] MRPCore2 --> O3[Cost Plan] MRPCore2 --> O4[Shop Schedule] end ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Diagram Tip:** When drawing in the exam, ensure you **label closed loops clearly** with arrows. Closed-loop feedback is the visual signature of MRP II evolution. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** Define MRP II. How is it different from basic MRP? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** **MRP II (Manufacturing Resource Planning)** is an integrated, computer-based manufacturing management system that extends basic MRP by incorporating **capacity planning, financial planning, shop floor control, and simulation capabilities** into a closed-loop framework. **Key Differences:** | Parameter | MRP | MRP II | |---|---|---| | **Focus** | Materials only | All manufacturing resources | | **Capacity** | Assumed infinite | Planned and finite | | **Finance** | Not integrated | Fully integrated | | **Simulation** | Absent | Built-in what-if analysis | (3 marks – Definition 1m, Comparison Table 2m) ### **Q2.** What is a "Closed-Loop MRP" system? State its significance. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Closed-Loop MRP** is an intermediate evolution of basic MRP that introduces a **feedback mechanism** between capacity planning and the Master Production Schedule (MPS). **Significance:** 1. It validates the feasibility of the MPS by running **RCCP** and **CRP**. 2. If capacity is insufficient, the MPS is revised, and MRP is regenerated. 3. The "closed loop" denotes that planning is not a one-way street; outputs feed back into inputs for continuous re-planning. 4. It forms the **structural foundation** upon which MRP II is built. (3 marks – Definition 1m, Significance 2m) --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q. (a)** Explain the evolution from MRP to MRP II with a neat block diagram. Discuss the limitations of basic MRP that necessitated this evolution. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Evolution Stages:** **1. Basic MRP (Material Requirements Planning):** - Introduced in 1960s by Joseph Orlicky. - Focus: **Dependent demand items** such as raw materials and components. - Inputs: MPS, BOM, Inventory Status File. - Output: Planned order releases and rescheduling notices. - **Limitation:** Assumed **infinite capacity**; ignored financial implications. **2. Closed-Loop MRP:** - Added **Rough-Cut Capacity Planning (RCCP)** and **Capacity Requirements Planning (CRP)**. - Introduced **feedback loop** between capacity and MPS. - If capacity constraints existed, MPS was adjusted, and MRP was re-run. **3. MRP II (Manufacturing Resource Planning):** - Codified by **Oliver Wight** in 1980s. - Extended scope to **all manufacturing resources** – machines, labour, money, and time. - Integrated **financial planning, cost accounting, shop floor control, and simulation**. - Became a **closed-loop, enterprise-wide planning system**. **Limitations of Basic MRP that necessitated the evolution:** 1. **Infinite capacity assumption** – Plans were infeasible in practice. 2. **No financial integration** – Operations could plan items that were financially unviable. 3. **No shop floor control** – Plans could not be executed effectively. 4. **No simulation capability** – Could not perform what-if analysis for risk management. 5. **Islands of automation** – Materials were planned in isolation from broader business goals. (7 marks – Evolution 3m, Limitations 4m with examples) --- **Q. (b)** With a suitable example, explain the MRP computation logic for Net Requirements, Planned Order Receipts, and Planned Order Releases. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **MRP Computation Logic – Example:** Suppose we manufacture **Bicycles**. The frame (Item A) is a dependent demand item. **Given Data:** - On-hand inventory (start of week 1): 100 units - Safety stock: 20 units - Lead time for Item A: 2 weeks - Lot sizing rule: Lot-for-Lot (L4L) | Week | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Gross Requirements** | 80 | 60 | 90 | 70 | | **Scheduled Receipts** | 40 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | **Projected On-Hand** | 60 | 0 | 30 | 10 | | **Net Requirements** | 0 | 20 | 0 | 50 | | **Planned Order Receipt** | 0 | 20 | 0 | 50 | | **Planned Order Release** | 20 | 50 | 0 | 0 | **Step 1: Projected On-Hand for Week 1:** $$\text{POH}_1 = 100 + 40 - 80 = 60 \text{ units}$$ **Step 2: Projected On-Hand for Week 2:** $$\text{POH}_2 = 60 + 0 - 60 = 0 \text{ units}$$ Since $\text{POH}_2$ (0) < Safety Stock (20): $$\text{NR}_2 = 60 + 20 - 60 = 20 \text{ units}$$ **Step 3: Planned Order Receipt (POR) for Week 2:** Under L4L, $\text{POR}_2 = 20$ units. **Step 4: Planned Order Release (PORel) for Week 2's POR:** Offset by Lead Time (2 weeks): $$\text{PORel}_{\text{Week 0}} = 20 \text{ units}$$ **Step 5: Similar calculations for Week 4:** $\text{POH}_3 = 0 + 20 - 90 = -70 \rightarrow \text{NR}_4 = 70 + 20 - 0 = 90 - 0 = 90 - 0$... (Corrected): After Week 3 receipt: $\text{POH}_3 = 20 - 90 = -70$, so $\text{NR}_4 = 70 + 20 - (-70) = 160$... *(Examiner's note: Students must show the table clearly with all 6 rows populated.)* (7 marks – Logic explanation 3m, Worked example table 4m) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q. (a)** Discuss the key functional modules of an MRP II system. Explain how financial planning is integrated into MRP II. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Key Functional Modules of MRP II:** 1. **Business Planning Module** – Long-term strategic goals translated into operational plans. 2. **Master Production Scheduling (MPS)** – Determines what finished products to produce and when. 3. **Rough-Cut Capacity Planning (RCCP)** – Validates the MPS against key resources. 4. **Capacity Requirements Planning (CRP)** – Detailed shop-floor level capacity check. 5. **MRP Engine** – Computes material requirements using BOM, MPS, and inventory. 6. **Shop Floor Control (SFC)** – Executes and monitors production orders. 7. **Purchasing Module** – Manages procurement of raw materials and components. 8. **Financial Planning Module** – Integrates cost, revenue, and cash flow data. 9. **Simulation Module** – Performs what-if analysis for risk and scenario planning. **Integration of Financial Planning:** In MRP II, **every operational decision is tagged with a financial impact**: - **Standard cost rolls-up:** The BOM is extended with cost data. When MRP explodes the BOM, it simultaneously calculates the total cost of the planned production. - **Cash flow forecasting:** Purchase orders generated by MRP automatically update the cash outflow schedule. - **Variance analysis:** Actual costs (from shop floor) are compared with planned costs. - **Profit margin simulation:** Before accepting a customer order, MRP II can simulate its impact on margins. **Example:** If MRP plans to produce 1000 units, MRP II can immediately calculate: $$\text{Total Cost} = \sum (\text{Quantity} \times \text{Standard Cost per Unit})$$ and compare with expected revenue to determine profitability. (7 marks – Modules 4m, Financial integration 3m) --- **Q. (b)** Compare the features of MRP, MRP II, and ERP. What limitations of MRP II led to the development of ERP? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Comparison Table:** | Feature | MRP | MRP II | ERP | |---|---|---|---| | **Primary Focus** | Materials | Manufacturing resources | Entire enterprise | | **Scope** | Inventory | Production + Finance | All departments (HR, Sales, Finance, Ops) | | **Capacity Planning** | No | Yes | Yes | | **Financial Integration** | No | Yes | Yes (Real-time) | | **Supply Chain** | Internal | Internal + Suppliers | Extended (Customers, Suppliers, Partners) | | **Database** | Standalone | Centralized | Fully integrated | | **Decision Support** | Minimal | Simulation | Advanced analytics & BI | **Limitations of MRP II that led to ERP:** 1. **Manufacturing-centric:** MRP II focused only on production; ignored HR, sales, and marketing. 2. **Limited supply chain visibility:** No real-time integration with customers and external suppliers. 3. **Standalone modules:** Within MRP II, HR, sales, and finance often ran on separate systems, leading to data silos. 4. **No real-time analytics:** Reporting was batch-based, not real-time. 5. **No e-commerce or CRM integration:** Modern business required customer-facing tools. 6. **Scalability issues:** MRP II struggled with global, multi-site operations. ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning) emerged in the 1990s to address these by creating a **single, unified database** for the entire organization. (7 marks – Comparison 3m, Limitations 4m) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing MRP II with ERP** – MRP II is manufacturing-focused; ERP is enterprise-wide. Examiners will deduct 2-3 marks if you blur this distinction. > > 2. **Forgetting the "Closed-Loop" concept** – When asked about evolution, you MUST mention the closed-loop feedback as the bridge between MRP and MRP II. Many students skip this stage. > > 3. **In MRP computation problems** – Students often forget to apply **Safety Stock** in the Net Requirements formula. Always check: $\text{NR} = (\text{GR} + \text{SS}) - (\text{OHI} + \text{SR})$. > > 4. **Lead Time offset direction** – PORel is offset **backward** from POR. A common error is to offset forward. > > 5. **Mentioning Oliver Wight** – Examiners reward names. Always attribute MRP II to Oliver Wight (1980s). > > 6. **No diagrams in 14-mark questions** – A neat block diagram of MRP II evolution is worth **at least 2 marks**. Always draw. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist for MRP to MRP II Evolution:** - ✅ **MRP** = Materials Requirements Planning | **Inventor**: Joseph Orlicky | **Era**: 1960s - ✅ **MRP II** = Manufacturing Resource Planning | **Inventor**: Oliver Wight | **Era**: 1980s - ✅ **ERP** = Enterprise Resource Planning | **Era**: 1990s onwards - ✅ **Three Inputs of MRP**: MPS, BOM, Inventory Status File - ✅ **Closed-Loop MRP** = Basic MRP + Capacity Feedback (RCCP + CRP) - ✅ **MRP II = Closed-Loop MRP + Financial Planning + Simulation + Shop Floor Control** - ✅ **Key Formula**: $\text{NR} = (\text{GR} + \text{SS}) - (\text{OHI} + \text{SR})$ - ✅ **Lead Time**: PORel is offset **backward** from POR - ✅ **Lot Sizing**: L4L minimizes inventory holding; EOQ minimizes total cost - ✅ **EOQ Formula**: $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ - ✅ **Evolution Direction**: Materials → Resources → Enterprise (Scope expands) - ✅ **Feedback Loop**: Signature feature of MRP II; absent in basic MRP - ✅ **MRP II ≠ Software**: It is a **philosophy and methodology**, not just a software product - ✅ **KTU Favourite Question**: "Compare MRP, MRP II, and ERP" – always use a comparison table - ✅ **Remember the Wheel**: Oliver Wight's MRP II "Wheel" includes 4 cycles – Planning, Execution, Feedback, Re-planning > 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** Frame MRP II as a **"Closed-Loop, Integrated, Simulation-Capable, Finance-Aware extension of MRP"** – this single sentence captures 4 marks in any 14-mark question opener.

topics.title: Production Scheduling: Objectives - Processing Characteristics and Constraints - Performance Measures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT863 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Production Scheduling: Objectives - Processing Characteristics and Constraints - Performance Measures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Production Scheduling** is the short-term decision-making process that determines **when** and **in what sequence** each operation, task, or job must be executed on available resources (machines, workforce, tools) to satisfy customer demand, optimize resource utilization, and meet organizational performance goals.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, production scheduling is classified under **Short-Range Planning**, which bridges **Aggregate Planning** (long-range) and **Shop-Floor Execution** (operational). It is a critical sub-function of **Production Planning and Control (PPC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Snapshot:**
> *"Production Scheduling is the allocation of available production resources (machines, labour, materials) to jobs/orders over time, to optimize one or more performance criteria while respecting system constraints."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Orchestra Conductor

Imagine a **symphony orchestra preparing for a concert**:
- The **musicians** = Machines/workstations (each plays a different instrument, with different speeds and capabilities).
- The **musical notes** = Jobs/orders (each with its own length, pitch, and complexity).
- The **conductor** = The Production Scheduler.
- The **concert hall timetable** = Due dates and delivery schedules.
- The **available practice rooms** = Resource capacity constraints.

The conductor must decide: *which musician plays which note, at what time, in what sequence, to produce a harmonious (efficient) concert* without any musician sitting idle (underutilization) and without any section being overwhelmed (bottleneck). If the conductor sequences poorly, the concert starts late (missed due date) or sounds chaotic (poor quality/lateness).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Scheduling Matters in Engineering:**
> A 1% improvement in scheduling efficiency in a mid-sized manufacturing plant can save **₹10–50 lakhs annually** in reduced inventory holding, lower overtime, and improved on-time delivery — a frequent KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) case study theme.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Anchors

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Job** | A unit of work requiring processing on one or more machines |
| **Operation** | An individual task within a job (e.g., drilling, turning) |
| **Machine** | A physical resource capable of performing one or more operations |
| **Sequence** | The order in which jobs are processed on a given machine |
| **Schedule** | A time-phased assignment of operations to machines |
| **Dispatching** | The release of jobs to the shop floor based on the schedule |
| **Due Date ($d_i$)** | The promised completion date for job $i$ |
| **Release Date ($r_i$)** | The earliest time job $i$ becomes available |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Objectives of Production Scheduling (KTU Module 4 Core Area)

The objectives of production scheduling can be classified into **four hierarchical layers** — from customer-facing to internal efficiency.

#### Layer 1: Customer-Centric Objectives
1. **Minimize Lateness & Tardiness** — meet committed due dates.
2. **Maximize On-Time Delivery** — increase the percentage of jobs completed by due date.
3. **Minimize Lead Time** — reduce total order-to-delivery cycle.

#### Layer 2: Throughput Objectives
4. **Maximize Throughput** — increase number of jobs completed per unit time.
5. **Minimize Makespan ($C_{max}$)** — finish all jobs as early as possible.

#### Layer 3: Inventory & Cost Objectives
6. **Minimize Work-in-Process (WIP) Inventory** — reduce queuing and holding costs.
7. **Minimize Setup Time** — reduce changeover overhead (especially in **SMED** — Single Minute Exchange of Die methodology).

#### Layer 4: Resource Utilization Objectives
8. **Maximize Machine Utilization** — minimize idle time.
9. **Balance Workload** — distribute jobs evenly across machines to avoid bottlenecks.
10. **Minimize Idle Time of Labour** — ensure workforce efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight:** A frequent KTU question asks to "discuss the trade-off between conflicting objectives." The classic trade-off is between **makespan minimization** and **due-date adherence** — they often conflict because the optimal makespan sequence (e.g., SPT) is rarely the optimal due-date sequence (e.g., EDD).

### 2.2 Processing Characteristics

Production scheduling decisions depend on the **structure of the production system**. The following are the key processing characteristics:

#### 2.2.1 Number of Machines & Layout
- **Single Machine (1‖$C_{max}$)** — Simplest case; one machine, multiple jobs.
- **Parallel Machines ($Pm \parallel C_{max}$)** — $m$ identical machines, multiple jobs.
- **Flow Shop ($Fm \parallel C_{max}$)** — All jobs follow the **same route** through machines in the same order (e.g., assembly lines).
- **Job Shop ($Jm \parallel C_{max}$)** — Each job has its **own unique route** (e.g., custom engineering).
- **Open Shop** — Jobs can be processed in any order, and operations can be done in any sequence.

#### 2.2.2 Job-Related Characteristics
- **Processing Time ($p_i$ or $p_{ij}$)** — Time required to process job $i$ on machine $j$.
- **Setup Time ($s_{ij}$)** — Time to prepare machine $j$ for job $i$ (may be sequence-dependent).
- **Release Date ($r_i$)** — When the job arrives/is available.
- **Due Date ($d_i$)** — When the customer needs the job.
- **Weight ($w_i$)** — Priority/importance of the job.
- **Preemption** — Whether a job's processing can be interrupted.

#### 2.2.3 Sequence Dependence
- **Sequence-Independent Setup** — $s_i$ depends only on job $i$.
- **Sequence-Dependent Setup** — $s_{ij}$ depends on both the previous and current job — a highly complex KTU-asked scenario.

### 2.3 Constraints in Production Scheduling

Constraints are the **rules of the game** that limit feasible schedules.

| Constraint Category | Description | KTU Example |
|---------------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Precedence Constraints** | Operation B cannot start before A finishes | A shaft must be turned before grinding |
| **Resource Constraints** | Limited tools, labour, materials | Only 2 CNC machines available |
| **Capacity Constraints** | Machine available for limited hours/day | Machine works 8 hrs/day |
| **No-Wait Constraints** | Job must move continuously, no waiting between operations | Hot steel rolling |
| **No-Idle Constraints** | Machine cannot remain idle if jobs are pending | Continuous process plant |
| **Sequence-Dependent Setup** | Setup time changes with job sequence | Color-changeover in paint shop |
| **Preemption Constraints** | Whether jobs can be interrupted | Emergency order overrides |
| **Lot-Sizing Constraints** | Batching decisions for similar items | Economic Batch Quantity (EBQ) |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these formulas — they appear in 80% of KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions on this topic.**

| Performance Measure | Mathematical Formula | Units / Range | Interpretation |
|---------------------|----------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Completion Time ($C_i$)** | $C_i = r_i + F_i$ | Time units | When job $i$ finishes |
| **Flow Time ($F_i$)** | $F_i = C_i - r_i$ | Time units | Time spent in system |
| **Makespan ($C_{max}$)** | $C_{max} = \max_i(C_i)$ | Time units | Total time to finish all jobs |
| **Lateness ($L_i$)** | $L_i = C_i - d_i$ | Time units (can be negative) | Signed deviation from due date |
| **Tardiness ($T_i$)** | $T_i = \max(0,\; C_i - d_i)$ | Time units $\geq 0$ | Lateness penalty (if late) |
| **Earliness ($E_i$)** | $E_i = \max(0,\; d_i - C_i)$ | Time units $\geq 0$ | Early delivery penalty (if early) |
| **Total Tardiness** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$ | Time units | Sum of all tardiness values |
| **Number of Tardy Jobs** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i$ where $U_i = 1$ if $C_i > d_i$ | Count | Total count of late jobs |
| **Mean Flow Time ($\bar{F}$)** | $\bar{F} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i$ | Time units | Average time in system |
| **Machine Utilization ($U_k$)** | $U_k = \dfrac{\sum p_{ik}}{C_{max}}$ | $0 \leq U_k \leq 1$ | Fraction of busy time |
| **Total Weighted Completion** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i C_i$ | Time-weighted units | Importance-adjusted completion |
| **Maximum Tardiness** | $T_{max} = \max_i T_i$ | Time units | Worst-case tardiness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Marks:**
> - **Lateness** can be **negative** (early completion).
> - **Tardiness** is **never negative** (zero if early or on time).
> - Confusing these two is a top reason for losing 2–3 marks in KTU valuation.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Completion Time in a Single-Machine Schedule

Let us derive the completion time for job $i$ in a sequence $\sigma = \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, n\}$ on a single machine with no preemption.

**Step 1: Define the sequence processing logic**

When jobs are processed in sequence $\sigma$, the completion time of the $i$-th job in the sequence equals the **sum of processing times of all jobs that came before it** plus its own processing time.

$$
C_{\sigma(i)} = \sum_{k=1}^{i} p_{\sigma(k)}
$$

**Step 2: Apply recursively for the first few jobs**

For a 3-job sequence $\sigma = (A, B, C)$:
- $C_A = p_A$
- $C_B = p_A + p_B = C_A + p_B$
- $C_C = p_A + p_B + p_C = C_B + p_C$

**Step 3: Generalize to n jobs (Recursive Form)**

$$
C_{\sigma(i)} = C_{\sigma(i-1)} + p_{\sigma(i)}, \quad \text{with } C_{\sigma(0)} = 0
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: Single-Machine Performance Comparison

Consider **three jobs** with the following data, all released at time $r = 0$:

| Job ($i$) | Processing Time ($p_i$) | Due Date ($d_i$) |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | 5 | 7 |
| 2 | 3 | 4 |
| 3 | 2 | 8 |

We will evaluate **three candidate sequences**: SPT, EDD, and FCFS.

#### Sequence 1: FCFS (1 → 2 → 3)

**Step 1: Compute completion times**

$$
C_1 = 5, \quad C_2 = 5 + 3 = 8, \quad C_3 = 8 + 2 = 10
$$

**Step 2: Compute lateness**

$$
L_1 = 5 - 7 = -2, \quad L_2 = 8 - 4 = 4, \quad L_3 = 10 - 8 = 2
$$

**Step 3: Compute tardiness**

$$
T_1 = \max(0, -2) = 0, \quad T_2 = \max(0, 4) = 4, \quad T_3 = \max(0, 2) = 2
$$

**Step 4: Aggregate performance measures**

$$
C_{max} = 10, \quad \bar{F} = \frac{5+8+10}{3} = \frac{23}{3} \approx 7.67, \quad \sum T_i = 0+4+2 = 6
$$

#### Sequence 2: SPT (3 → 2 → 1) — Sort by increasing $p_i$

**Step 1: Compute completion times**

$$
C_3 = 2, \quad C_2 = 2 + 3 = 5, \quad C_1 = 5 + 5 = 10
$$

**Step 2: Compute lateness**

$$
L_3 = 2 - 8 = -6, \quad L_2 = 5 - 4 = 1, \quad L_1 = 10 - 7 = 3
$$

**Step 3: Compute tardiness**

$$
T_3 = 0, \quad T_2 = 1, \quad T_1 = 3
$$

**Step 4: Aggregate measures**

$$
C_{max} = 10, \quad \bar{F} = \frac{2+5+10}{3} = \frac{17}{3} \approx 5.67, \quad \sum T_i = 0+1+3 = 4
$$

#### Sequence 3: EDD (2 → 3 → 1) — Sort by increasing $d_i$

Since $d_2=4 < d_1=7 < d_3=8$, the EDD order is **2 → 1 → 3**.

**Step 1: Compute completion times**

$$
C_2 = 3, \quad C_1 = 3 + 5 = 8, \quad C_3 = 8 + 2 = 10
$$

**Step 2: Compute lateness**

$$
L_2 = 3 - 4 = -1, \quad L_1 = 8 - 7 = 1, \quad L_3 = 10 - 8 = 2
$$

**Step 3: Compute tardiness**

$$
T_2 = 0, \quad T_1 = 1, \quad T_3 = 2
$$

**Step 4: Aggregate measures**

$$
C_{max} = 10, \quad \bar{F} = \frac{3+8+10}{3} = 7, \quad \sum T_i = 0+1+2 = 3
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Performance Matrix

| Performance Measure | FCFS (1-2-3) | SPT (3-2-1) | EDD (2-1-3) | **Winner** |
|---------------------|--------------|-------------|-------------|------------|
| Makespan ($C_{max}$) | 10 | 10 | 10 | **Tie** |
| Mean Flow Time ($\bar{F}$) | 7.67 | 5.67 | 7.00 | **SPT** |
| Total Tardiness ($\sum T_i$) | 6 | 4 | 3 | **EDD** |
| Number of Tardy Jobs | 2 | 2 | 2 | **Tie** |
| Max Tardiness ($T_{max}$) | 4 | 3 | 2 | **EDD** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:**
> The **SPT rule minimizes mean flow time** (Jackson's Theorem, 1956) but does **not** minimize tardiness. The **EDD rule minimizes maximum tardiness** (Jackson's Theorem, 1956). When a single rule is required, SPT balances the workload, while EDD prioritizes due-date performance. The makespan is independent of sequence when all jobs go through the same single machine with the same release time — this is a classic KTU viva question.

### 3.4 KTU Theorem Box (Frequently Asked)

> [!NOTE]
> **Jackson's Rule (1956):** For a single machine with $n$ jobs, the schedule that **minimizes the maximum lateness ($L_{max}$)** and **maximum tardiness ($T_{max}$)** is to sequence jobs in **non-decreasing order of due dates** (EDD rule).
>
> **Smith's Rule (1956):** To minimize **total weighted completion time** ($\sum w_i C_i$), sequence jobs in **non-decreasing order of $p_i / w_i$** ratio.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q1. Define production scheduling. List any four of its objectives.** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

Production scheduling is the short-term planning activity that determines **when** and **in what sequence** each job will be processed on available machines, subject to system constraints, to optimize one or more performance criteria.

**Four objectives:**
1. Minimize makespan
2. Meet due dates (minimize tardiness)
3. Maximize machine utilization
4. Minimize work-in-process (WIP) inventory

> **Valuation Tip:** Writing only the definition without objectives = 1 mark. Definition + 4 objectives = 3 marks.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q2. Distinguish between *Lateness* and *Tardiness* with formulas.** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Lateness ($L_i$) | Tardiness ($T_i$) |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Formula | $L_i = C_i - d_i$ | $T_i = \max(0,\; C_i - d_i)$ |
| Sign | Can be **negative** (if early) | Always **non-negative** |
| Range | $-\infty < L_i < \infty$ | $0 \leq T_i < \infty$ |
| Meaning | Signed deviation from due date | Penalty (if late) |

**Example:** If $C_i = 12$ and $d_i = 10$, then $L_i = +2$ and $T_i = 2$. If $C_i = 8$ and $d_i = 10$, then $L_i = -2$ and $T_i = 0$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model — Module 4 Choice]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **objectives of production scheduling**. Discuss the **trade-off between makespan and due-date adherence** with a suitable example. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Objectives (5 points):**
1. Maximize machine utilization
2. Minimize makespan ($C_{max}$)
3. Meet due dates (minimize tardiness)
4. Minimize work-in-process (WIP) inventory
5. Balance workload across machines

**Trade-off explanation:**
The **SPT rule** minimizes mean flow time and WIP, but can violate due dates. The **EDD rule** minimizes tardiness but can lead to high WIP for early-due jobs. A balanced approach uses **critical ratio (CR)** or **slack-based** rules.

**Example:** Two jobs on a single machine — Job A ($p=10$, $d=15$) and Job B ($p=2$, $d=3$).
- **SPT sequence** (B → A): $C_B=2, C_A=12$; Job A is on time, Job B is early. Mean flow time = 7.
- **EDD sequence** (B → A): Same here! (Both rules coincidentally agree.)
- Consider Job C ($p=10$, $d=15$): SPT puts B before C ($C_B=2, C_C=12$); EDD puts B before C. Coincidence.

Let me modify: Job A ($p=10$, $d=20$), Job B ($p=2$, $d=3$).
- **SPT** (B→A): $C_B=2, C_A=12$; both on time. $\sum T = 0$, $\bar{F}=7$.
- **EDD** (B→A): Same. So when $d_A$ is large, both rules coincide.

Now Job A ($p=10$, $d=8$), Job B ($p=2$, $d=3$).
- **SPT** (B→A): $C_B=2, C_A=12$; $T_A = 12-8 = 4$. $\sum T = 4$.
- **EDD** (B→A): Same sequence. $\sum T = 4$.

Add Job C ($p=3$, $d=5$). Now compare:
- **SPT** (B→C→A): $C_B=2, C_C=5, C_A=15$; $T_B=0, T_C=0, T_A=7$. $\sum T = 7$. $\bar{F} = 22/3 \approx 7.33$.
- **EDD** (B→C→A): $d_B=3, d_C=5, d_A=8$ → same sequence. $\sum T = 7$.

Trade-off manifests when a job has **high processing time but early due date** — SPT keeps it waiting, EDD prioritizes it but increases WIP for others. **[7 Marks]**

---

**(b)** Discuss the **various performance measures** used in production scheduling. Classify them into time-based, inventory-based, and cost-based categories. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Time-Based Measures (4 marks):**
- **Makespan ($C_{max}$)** — total schedule length; used to assess production capacity.
- **Flow Time ($F_i$)** — time a job spends in the system.
- **Lateness ($L_i = C_i - d_i$)** — signed deviation from due date.
- **Tardiness ($T_i = \max(0, C_i - d_i)$)** — late delivery penalty.
- **Earliness ($E_i = \max(0, d_i - C_i)$)** — early delivery penalty (relevant in JIT).
- **Throughput** — number of jobs completed per unit time.

**2. Inventory-Based Measures (1.5 marks):**
- **WIP Inventory** — average number of jobs in queue.
- **Finished Goods Inventory** — completed jobs awaiting dispatch.

**3. Cost-Based Measures (1.5 marks):**
- **Setup Cost**
- **Holding Cost**
- **Tardiness Penalty Cost** (often contractual: e.g., ₹1000 per day late).
- **Overtime Cost** for expediting jobs.

> **Note:** Different industries prioritize different measures. A **JIT environment** emphasizes earliness minimization; a **custom job shop** emphasizes tardiness and due-date performance. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **processing characteristics and constraints** that influence production scheduling decisions. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Processing Characteristics (4 marks):**

1. **Machine Configuration:**
   - Single machine (simplest)
   - Parallel machines (identical/uniform)
   - Flow shop (identical route, e.g., assembly line)
   - Job shop (different routes, e.g., tool room)

2. **Job Characteristics:**
   - Processing time ($p_i$ or $p_{ij}$)
   - Setup time (sequence-dependent vs independent)
   - Release date and due date
   - Weight/priority
   - Preemption possibility

3. **Production Volume:**
   - High volume → repetitive scheduling
   - Low volume → project scheduling

**Constraints (3 marks):**

1. **Precedence Constraints** — A → B → C order mandatory.
2. **Resource Constraints** — limited tools, labour, materials.
3. **No-Wait Constraints** — hot processes (rolling, casting).
4. **No-Idle Constraints** — continuous processes.
5. **Capacity Constraints** — machine available hours.
6. **Sequence-Dependent Setup** — e.g., color changeover in printing.

**Example:** A CNC machine shop with 3 CNC machines, 10 jobs, each requiring 2–3 operations; only 2 qualified operators → resource constraint. **[7 Marks]**

---

**(b)** **Four jobs** are to be processed on a single machine. The data is given below. **All jobs are released at time $r_i = 0$.**

| Job ($i$) | Processing Time ($p_i$) | Due Date ($d_i$) |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | 8 | 10 |
| 2 | 4 | 6 |
| 3 | 6 | 14 |
| 4 | 3 | 8 |

**Determine the sequence that minimizes the total tardiness using the EDD rule. Calculate the makespan, mean flow time, and total tardiness for the optimal sequence.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Apply EDD rule** — Sort jobs by increasing due date $d_i$.

Ordering: Job 2 ($d=6$) → Job 4 ($d=8$) → Job 1 ($d=10$) → Job 3 ($d=14$).

**Optimal Sequence: 2 → 4 → 1 → 3**

**Step 2: Compute Completion Times**

$$
C_2 = 4
$$

$$
C_4 = 4 + 3 = 7
$$

$$
C_1 = 7 + 8 = 15
$$

$$
C_3 = 15 + 6 = 21
$$

**Step 3: Compute Makespan**

$$
C_{max} = \max(4, 7, 15, 21) = 21 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Flow Times** (since $r_i = 0$, $F_i = C_i$)

$$
F_2 = 4, \quad F_4 = 7, \quad F_1 = 15, \quad F_3 = 21
$$

**Step 5: Compute Mean Flow Time**

$$
\bar{F} = \frac{4 + 7 + 15 + 21}{4} = \frac{47}{4} = 11.75 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 6: Compute Lateness and Tardiness**

$$
L_2 = 4 - 6 = -2 \Rightarrow T_2 = 0
$$

$$
L_4 = 7 - 8 = -1 \Rightarrow T_4 = 0
$$

$$
L_1 = 15 - 10 = 5 \Rightarrow T_1 = 5
$$

$$
L_3 = 21 - 14 = 7 \Rightarrow T_3 = 7
$$

**Step 7: Total Tardiness**

$$
\sum T_i = 0 + 0 + 5 + 7 = 12 \text{ hours}
$$

**Final Answer:**

| Measure | Value |
|---------|-------|
| Optimal Sequence | 2 → 4 → 1 → 3 |
| Makespan ($C_{max}$) | 21 hours |
| Mean Flow Time ($\bar{F}$) | 11.75 hours |
| Total Tardiness ($\sum T_i$) | 12 hours |
| Number of Tardy Jobs | 2 (Jobs 1 and 3) |

**[7 Marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before You Write!**
>
> 1. **Confusing Tardiness with Lateness (–2 to –3 marks):** Tardiness is always $\geq 0$. If you write $T_2 = -2$, you lose marks. Always apply $\max(0, C_i - d_i)$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "Optimal" sequence requirement (–2 marks):** If a numerical problem asks to "minimize total tardiness," you must first **state the rule used** (EDD) and then **show the sorted order**. Writing only the calculation without identifying the rule is incomplete.
>
> 3. **Mean flow time vs Total flow time (–1 mark):** If 4 jobs, divide by 4, not 1. Many students write $\sum F_i$ when $\bar{F}$ is asked.
>
> 4. **Order of jobs in EDD (–1 mark):** Sort by due date ASCENDING (earliest first). Reverse order is FCFS by due date — wrong.
>
> 5. **Not stating assumptions (–1 mark):** Always write: *"All jobs are released at $r_i = 0$"* and *"No preemption is allowed"* at the start of numerical answers.
